Closed reduction with adequate analgesia/sedation followed by immobilization in a well-molded cast, with post-reduction films
stages.explanation: A displaced, angulated Salter-Harris II distal radius fracture is best treated with closed reduction under adequate analgesia/procedural sedation and immobilization in a well-molded cast, with post-reduction radiographs to confirm alignment; the distal radius has excellent remodeling potential in a skeletally immature child. Open reduction with plating is reserved for irreducible, unstable, or open fractures, or failed closed treatment, and unnecessarily disturbs the physis. Physeal bar resection treats an established growth arrest, not an acute fracture. A sling alone neither corrects angulation nor protects the reduction and risks malunion. Repeated forceful manipulations should be avoided to limit physeal injury.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the most appropriate follow-up and counseling for the family?
options: Return only if the cast becomes uncomfortable; no imaging required with appropriate follow-up
Orthopedic follow-up with repeat radiographs in about 1 week to confirm maintained reduction,
No follow-up needed once casted; remove the cast at home in 2 weeks with appropriate follow-up
Schedule a CT every month to monitor the physis with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: After reduction and casting, the child should follow up with orthopedics with repeat radiographs in about a week to confirm that the reduction is maintained, since displaced fractures can re-displace within the cast; families should be counseled that, although growth arrest is uncommon in Salter-Harris II injuries, late growth disturbance can occur and warrants surveillance. Removing a cast at home or having no follow-up risks missed loss of reduction or malunion. Returning only for discomfort ignores the need to verify alignment. Monthly CT delivers excessive radiation; periodic plain films are the appropriate surveillance tool.

cases.diagnosis: Salter-Harris type II fracture of the distal radial physis
pearls: Salter-Harris II is the most common physeal fracture and is identified by a metaphyseal fragment (Thurston-Holland sign).
Higher Salter-Harris grade correlates with higher risk of growth arrest; types III and IV cross the epiphysis and joint and carry the greatest risk.
Most displaced distal radius physeal fractures are managed with closed reduction and a well-molded cast given excellent remodeling; avoid repeated forceful manipulations.
Confirm maintained reduction with repeat films at about one week and counsel families on the small but real risk of late growth disturbance.

cases.title: Persistent Knee Pain Months After a Replacement
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening right knee pain, swelling, and warmth four months after a total knee replacement.
cases.history: A 71-year-old woman is admitted from clinic for evaluation of a painful right total knee arthroplasty performed four months ago for osteoarthritis. Her early postoperative course was complicated by superficial wound drainage that was treated with a short oral antibiotic course. She never achieved a pain-free knee: it remained swollen and warm, and pain is present at rest and at night, unlike her preoperative mechanical pain. Over the past two weeks she has had low-grade fevers and increasing difficulty bearing weight. She denies recent dental work but had a urinary tract infection a month ago. Her medical history includes type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 8.4%), obesity (BMI 34), and hypertension. Medications are metformin 1000 mg twice daily, lisinopril 20 mg daily, and acetaminophen as needed. She has no drug allergies. She is a lifelong nonsmoker, drinks rarely, and lives with her husband. Family history is notable for diabetes. She is frustrated that the knee has 'never felt right' and is concerned the implant may be failing.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable but not toxic. The right knee is warm, diffusely swollen, and erythematous over the surgical incision, which is well healed without a current sinus or active drainage. There is a moderate effusion with a positive ballottement sign. Range of motion is limited to 10-70 degrees by pain and swelling. There is tenderness along the joint line and over the patella. No crepitus suggestive of component loosening is appreciated on gentle motion. Distal pulses are intact, the foot is warm and well perfused, and sensation is intact. There is no calf tenderness or asymmetric swelling to suggest deep vein thrombosis. The contralateral knee and the remainder of the musculoskeletal exam are unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first set of investigations to evaluate this painful prosthetic knee?
options: Immediate empiric IV vancomycin before any diagnostic testing
MRI of the knee with contrast as the first step today
Bone scan as the initial screening test
Serum ESR and CRP plus plain radiographs of the knee
stages.explanation: When periprosthetic joint infection (PJI) is suspected, the recommended first-line workup is serum inflammatory markers (ESR and CRP) combined with plain radiographs; these are sensitive screening tests and guide the decision to aspirate. Starting empiric antibiotics before obtaining cultures, including synovial fluid, can compromise the ability to identify the organism and should be avoided unless the patient is septic. MRI is degraded by metal artifact and is not the test of choice for prosthetic infection. A bone scan is nonspecific in the first year after arthroplasty because normal periprosthetic remodeling causes uptake, so it is not a good initial screen.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
ESR 78 mm/hr
CRP 9.2 mg/dL
WBC 11.8 ×10³/µL
HbA1c 8.4%
Knee radiographs: Components in acceptable position; no obvious loosening, but periprosthetic lucency is equivocal. No fracture.
Synovial fluid aspirate: WBC 32,000 cells/µL with 88% neutrophils
Synovial fluid alpha-defensin: positive
Gram stain: pending; cultures sent

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on these results, which diagnosis is most strongly supported?
options: Aseptic loosening of the prosthesis
Chronic periprosthetic joint infection
Simple postoperative hemarthrosis
Crystal-induced (gout) flare of the prosthetic knee
stages.explanation: Elevated ESR and CRP, a synovial WBC well above the chronic PJI threshold (roughly >3,000 cells/µL with >65-80% neutrophils for a prosthetic joint), and a positive alpha-defensin together meet criteria for periprosthetic joint infection presenting in a chronic/delayed pattern beyond the early postoperative window. Aseptic loosening would not produce a markedly inflammatory synovial fluid or elevated alpha-defensin and typically shows progressive radiolucency. A gout flare can elevate cell counts but would show crystals and would not explain fevers with positive alpha-defensin. A simple hemarthrosis is bloody, not purulent, and does not cause systemic inflammatory signs four months out.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this chronic periprosthetic infection (symptoms >4 weeks, well-healed but chronically infected implant), what is the most appropriate definitive surgical strategy?
options: Debridement, antibiotics, and implant retention (DAIR) as the definitive treatment as initial therapy
Two-stage revision: removal of components, placement of an antibiotic spacer with IV antibiotics, then reimplantation once infection is cleared
Long-term oral antibiotics alone without surgery as initial therapy
Immediate above-knee amputation as initial therapy
stages.explanation: For chronic PJI with symptoms beyond about 3-4 weeks and a biofilm-laden implant, the gold standard in North America is two-stage revision: explant all hardware and cement, place an antibiotic-impregnated spacer, deliver organism-directed IV antibiotics (commonly about 6 weeks), confirm eradication, and then reimplant. Antibiotics alone cannot eradicate biofilm on retained hardware and will fail. DAIR is reserved for acute infections with a well-fixed implant and a short symptom duration, not chronic biofilm-established infection, where it has high failure rates. Amputation is a last resort for unsalvageable limbs, not first-line for a treatable chronic PJI.

stages.label: PERIOPERATIVE
stages.question: Which modifiable factor in this patient most importantly increases her risk of treatment failure and should be optimized?
options: Her use of lisinopril
Her social alcohol use
Her family history of diabetes
Her glycemic control
stages.explanation: Poorly controlled diabetes (HbA1c 8.4%) impairs wound healing and immune function and is a well-established modifiable risk factor for periprosthetic infection and for failure of treatment; optimizing glycemic control improves outcomes and is a priority in this patient. Lisinopril is appropriate for her hypertension and is not a relevant infection risk factor. Minimal social alcohol use is not the dominant driver here. Family history of diabetes is non-modifiable and does not itself alter perioperative risk. Addressing hyperglycemia, along with her obesity, gives the best chance of successful eradication and reimplantation.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Before reimplantation, which finding would best indicate that the infection has been adequately cleared?
options: A single negative blood culture during antibiotic therapy as the leading concern
Patient preference to proceed without further testing as the leading concern
Resolution of pain alone, regardless of laboratory values as the leading concern
Normalizing or substantially improving ESR/CRP trends together with a negative repeat aspirate after an antibiotic-free interval
stages.explanation: Eradication is best judged by a combination of downtrending or normalized inflammatory markers (ESR/CRP) and a reassuring repeat synovial aspiration, ideally after an antibiotic-free 'holiday' so that residual infection is not masked. Pain resolution alone is unreliable, as low-grade biofilm infection can be relatively painless. A single negative blood culture drawn while on antibiotics does not assess the local joint and is easily falsely negative. Proceeding on patient preference without objective confirmation risks reimplanting into an infected field and recurrent failure. Objective, multimodal confirmation is the standard before second-stage reimplantation.

cases.diagnosis: Chronic periprosthetic joint infection of a total knee arthroplasty
pearls: Rest/night pain and a chronically warm, swollen prosthetic joint that 'never felt right' should raise suspicion for periprosthetic infection rather than aseptic loosening.
Initial workup is ESR/CRP plus radiographs; synovial aspiration with cell count, differential, alpha-defensin, and culture confirms the diagnosis.
Chronic PJI with a biofilm-laden implant is treated with two-stage revision; DAIR is only for acute infection with a well-fixed implant and short symptom duration.
Optimize modifiable risks (especially glycemic control and obesity) and confirm eradication with improving markers and a negative aspirate before reimplantation.

cases.title: Groin Pain on the First Steps of the Day
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive right groin and thigh pain that is worst when he first stands up, years after a hip replacement.
cases.history: A 74-year-old man presents to the orthopedic clinic with two years of gradually worsening right groin pain. He underwent a right total hip arthroplasty twelve years ago for osteoarthritis and did excellently for the first decade, returning to golf and daily walks. Over the past two years he has developed 'start-up' pain: sharp groin and anterior thigh discomfort with the first few steps after sitting or upon rising in the morning, which eases somewhat after he walks a bit, then returns with prolonged activity. He now uses a cane. He denies fevers, chills, night sweats, or any wound problems, and his postoperative course years ago was uncomplicated with no infection. He denies recent trauma. His medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. Medications are amlodipine 5 mg daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily, and acetaminophen as needed. He has no allergies. He is a retired teacher, drinks a glass of wine with dinner, and quit smoking 30 years ago. Family history is unremarkable. He is concerned his 'new hip is wearing out' and wants to know whether he needs another operation.
cases.exam: Comfortable at rest, ambulates with an antalgic gait favoring the right leg and uses a cane. The healed posterolateral hip incision is clean with no erythema, warmth, drainage, or sinus tract. Groin pain is reproduced with axial loading and with rotation of the hip; log-roll elicits groin discomfort. Hip flexion is to about 95 degrees, with pain at end range of internal and external rotation. There is no leg-length discrepancy of note and no instability or apprehension. Thigh pain is reproduced with resisted straight-leg raise against the femur. Neurovascular exam of the limb is normal, with intact pulses, sensation, and motor function. There is no calf tenderness. The spine and contralateral hip exams are unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial investigation for this painful, previously well-functioning hip replacement?
options: MRI of the lumbar spine first as the first step today
Empiric corticosteroid injection into the hip as the first step today
Serial plain radiographs of the hip , ideally compared with prior films, plus ESR/CRP
Immediate revision surgery without imaging as the first step today
stages.explanation: The first step in a painful arthroplasty is serial plain radiographs compared with prior films to assess component position, radiolucent lines, osteolysis, and migration, along with ESR/CRP to screen for infection, which can mimic loosening. Lumbar spine MRI may be considered if a spinal source is suspected, but the exam localizes pain to the hip with reproduction on axial loading and rotation, so hip imaging comes first. Proceeding to revision without diagnostic imaging is inappropriate. An empiric steroid injection neither diagnoses the problem nor is appropriate before infection is excluded, and could obscure or worsen an undiagnosed septic process.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
ESR 14 mm/hr
CRP 0.5 mg/dL
WBC 6.9 ×10³/µL
AP pelvis and lateral hip radiographs: Progressive radiolucent lines >2 mm around the entire femoral stem compared with films from 3 years ago. Subsidence of the stem by approximately 4 mm. Focal periprosthetic osteolysis in the proximal femur. Acetabular component appears well fixed. No fracture. No periosteal reaction.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best explains the start-up pain and these radiographic findings?
options: Aseptic loosening of the femoral stem
Greater trochanteric bursitis
Lumbar radiculopathy
Periprosthetic joint infection
stages.explanation: Progressive circumferential radiolucent lines, component subsidence, and periprosthetic osteolysis in the setting of normal inflammatory markers and no infectious features point to aseptic loosening of the femoral stem; the classic symptom is 'start-up' (thigh/groin) pain on initiating weight-bearing that eases then returns with activity. Infection is made unlikely by normal ESR/CRP and the absence of fever, drainage, or sinus, though it must always be excluded first. Trochanteric bursitis causes lateral hip tenderness over the trochanter, not axial-load groin pain with radiographic loosening. Lumbar radiculopathy produces dermatomal leg pain with neuro findings, absent here, and would not cause stem subsidence.

stages.label: MECHANISM
stages.question: What is the most likely underlying mechanism of the osteolysis driving this loosening?
options: Vitamin D deficiency causing osteomalacia as the immediate next step
Metastatic malignancy to the proximal femur as the immediate next step
Particle-induced osteolysis triggering a macrophage-mediated inflammatory response
Bacterial biofilm eroding bone as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: The dominant mechanism of late aseptic loosening is particle-induced osteolysis: micron-sized wear debris (classically polyethylene, also metal or cement particles) is phagocytosed by macrophages, which release inflammatory cytokines (e.g., TNF-alpha, IL-1, RANKL-driven signaling) that activate osteoclasts and resorb periprosthetic bone, leading to lucency and loosening. Bacterial biofilm causes septic, not aseptic, loosening and would typically elevate markers. Metastatic disease can cause focal lysis but would be an unusual explanation for symmetric peri-stem lucency with subsidence and normal markers in this context. Vitamin D deficiency/osteomalacia causes generalized bone softening, not the focal periprosthetic osteolysis pattern seen around the implant.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given symptomatic aseptic loosening with subsidence and osteolysis, what is the most appropriate definitive treatment?
options: Intra-articular corticosteroid injections every 3 months
Revision total hip arthroplasty of the loose femoral component
Hip resurfacing of the existing implant as initial therapy
Indefinite activity restriction and analgesics only
stages.explanation: Symptomatic aseptic loosening with progressive subsidence and osteolysis is definitively treated with revision arthroplasty of the loose component, restoring fixation and addressing bone loss before further osteolysis compromises bone stock or causes periprosthetic fracture. Activity restriction and analgesics may temporize but do not halt mechanical loosening or bone destruction and leave the patient disabled. Resurfacing is not a remedy for a loose, subsided stem. Repeated steroid injections do not treat mechanical loosening, provide only transient relief at best, and carry infection risk around an implant. Timely revision preserves bone and function.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If this loosening is left untreated and osteolysis progresses, which complication is he most at risk of developing?
options: Deep vein thrombosis from immobility alone as the leading concern
Periprosthetic femoral fracture through the weakened, osteolytic bone
Avascular necrosis of the contralateral hip as the leading concern
Heterotopic ossification of the abductors as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Progressive periprosthetic osteolysis weakens the femoral bone stock supporting the stem, markedly increasing the risk of a periprosthetic femoral fracture, which is technically difficult to manage and may necessitate complex revision. While prolonged immobility can contribute to DVT risk generally, that is not the specific consequence of untreated osteolysis. Contralateral avascular necrosis is unrelated to ipsilateral loosening. Heterotopic ossification is an early postoperative phenomenon, not a consequence of late osteolysis. The principal hazard of waiting is loss of bone stock culminating in fracture, which is why timely revision is recommended.

cases.diagnosis: Aseptic loosening of the femoral component of a total hip arthroplasty
pearls: Start-up groin/thigh pain that eases after a few steps and returns with activity in a previously well-functioning hip replacement is the hallmark of femoral component loosening.
Always exclude infection first with ESR/CRP; aseptic loosening shows normal markers with progressive radiolucent lines, subsidence, and osteolysis on serial films.
The mechanism is particle (wear-debris) induced, macrophage-mediated osteolysis with osteoclast activation, not infection.
Symptomatic loosening is treated with revision arthroplasty; delaying risks osteolytic bone loss and periprosthetic fracture.

cases.title: Sudden Breathlessness Three Days After Surgery
cases.chiefComplaint: Acute shortness of breath and a racing heart on postoperative day 3 after a knee replacement.
cases.history: A 67-year-old woman on the orthopedic ward develops sudden shortness of breath and palpitations on the third day after an elective right total knee arthroplasty for osteoarthritis. The surgery and early recovery were uneventful, and she had been participating in physical therapy. She received mechanical prophylaxis with sequential compression devices and was started on aspirin 81 mg twice daily for chemoprophylaxis per the surgeon's protocol. Twenty minutes ago she felt abrupt breathlessness while transferring to a chair, with mild right-sided pleuritic chest discomfort and light-headedness. She denies hemoptysis, cough, or fever. Her medical history includes hypertension, obesity (BMI 33), and prior tobacco use (quit 5 years ago, 25 pack-years). She takes lisinopril 10 mg daily, atorvastatin 20 mg daily, and the postoperative aspirin and acetaminophen. She has no drug allergies. She had a prior episode of 'a clot in the leg' after a long flight a decade ago but cannot recall details. Family history includes a brother with a pulmonary embolism. She is anxious and feels she 'cannot catch her breath.'

vitals.SpO2: 89% on room air, 95% on 4 L nasal cannula

cases.exam: Anxious, tachypneic, using accessory muscles. She is alert and oriented. Cardiac exam reveals tachycardia with a regular rhythm and a possible accentuated pulmonic component of S2; no murmur or rub. Lungs are clear to auscultation bilaterally without crackles or wheeze. There is no jugular venous distension at 45 degrees, though it is borderline. The right lower extremity has the expected postoperative dressing; the calf is mildly more swollen than the left with some tenderness, but no overt cord. Distal pulses are intact and the foot is warm. No chest wall tenderness is reproduced. The abdomen is soft. There is no focal neurologic deficit. Capillary refill is normal centrally.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate action while you begin the diagnostic workup?
options: Administer a fluid bolus of 2 liters and recheck in an hour as the first step today
Withhold all interventions until imaging is complete as the first step today
Start nebulized bronchodilators for presumed COPD exacerbation as the first step today
Provide supplemental oxygen, obtain an ECG
stages.explanation: The immediate priorities in acute postoperative dyspnea with hypoxia and tachycardia are to support oxygenation, place the patient on continuous monitoring, obtain an ECG, and assess hemodynamic stability, which determines the diagnostic and therapeutic pathway. Withholding all supportive care until imaging is unsafe in a hypoxic patient. A large fluid bolus can worsen right ventricular strain if this is a pulmonary embolism and is not the reflexive answer; cautious fluids may be used judiciously, but 2 liters empirically is wrong. Nebulizers target obstructive lung disease, but her clear lungs and risk profile point away from a COPD exacerbation, so empiric bronchodilators do not address the likely diagnosis.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
ECG: Sinus tachycardia at 120 bpm; new S1Q3T3 pattern; right axis deviation
ABG (4 L NC): pH 7.49, PaCO2 30 mmHg, PaO2 68 mmHg (hypoxemia with respiratory alkalosis)
Troponin: mildly elevated
BNP: elevated
D-dimer: markedly elevated
Chest radiograph: No infiltrate, no pneumothorax, no effusion
Bedside echo: Right ventricular dilation with mild hypokinesis

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best unifies these findings?
options: Postoperative pneumonia
Acute myocardial infarction
Acute pulmonary embolism
Pulmonary edema from fluid overload
stages.explanation: Sudden postoperative dyspnea with pleuritic pain, hypoxemia with hypocapnia (respiratory alkalosis), sinus tachycardia, an S1Q3T3 pattern, right ventricular dilation/strain on echo, mildly elevated troponin/BNP from RV strain, and a clear chest film in a high-risk patient with a swollen tender calf is the classic picture of acute pulmonary embolism. Myocardial infarction usually shows ischemic ECG changes and regional wall-motion abnormalities, not isolated RV strain with clear lungs. Pneumonia would show an infiltrate and fever. Pulmonary edema would show crackles and vascular congestion/infiltrates on radiograph, none of which are present here.

stages.label: RISK STRATIFICATION
stages.question: Her BP is 108/68 with HR 122 but she is maintaining perfusion; troponin and BNP are elevated with RV strain on echo. How is this PE best classified?
options: Massive (high-risk) PE with shock
Submassive (intermediate-risk) PE
Chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension
Low-risk (non-massive) PE in this setting
stages.explanation: This is a submassive (intermediate-risk) PE: the patient is normotensive (no sustained hypotension or shock) but has evidence of right ventricular dysfunction (RV dilation/hypokinesis on echo) and myocardial injury (elevated troponin/BNP), which together define intermediate risk and warrant closer monitoring. Low-risk PE has neither RV dysfunction nor biomarker elevation. Massive (high-risk) PE requires sustained hypotension or shock, which she does not have. Chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension is a chronic condition causing progressive dyspnea over months to years, not an acute postoperative event with acute RV strain.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She is hemodynamically stable with submassive PE. Considering she is postoperative day 3 from major joint surgery, what is the most appropriate management?
options: Place an IVC filter and give no anticoagulation as initial therapy
Continue aspirin alone and observe as initial therapy
Therapeutic anticoagulation with close monitoring for deterioration, with consideration of catheter-directed therapy if she destabilizes
Immediate systemic full-dose thrombolysis (tPA) as initial therapy
stages.explanation: For stable submassive PE, the mainstay is prompt therapeutic anticoagulation with close monitoring; escalation to catheter-directed or systemic thrombolysis is reserved for hemodynamic deterioration, because thrombolysis carries major bleeding risk that is especially concerning so soon after major surgery. Immediate systemic full-dose thrombolysis is not indicated in a normotensive patient and is hazardous postoperatively. Aspirin is inadequate treatment for an established PE. An IVC filter without anticoagulation is reserved for patients with an absolute contraindication to anticoagulation; she can be anticoagulated, so a filter alone is inappropriate and does not treat the existing clot.

stages.label: PREVENTION
stages.question: On review, which prophylaxis issue most plausibly contributed to this event, and what is the lesson?
options: No prophylaxis is needed after knee replacement as the immediate next step
Aspirin is never acceptable VTE prophylaxis after arthroplasty as the immediate next step
Aspirin may be adequate only for low-risk patients
Mechanical prophylaxis alone is superior to any chemoprophylaxis as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Guidelines accept aspirin as VTE prophylaxis for selected low-risk arthroplasty patients, but a patient with a prior VTE and a strong family history is high-risk, and such patients generally warrant a potent anticoagulant agent (e.g., low-molecular-weight heparin or a direct oral anticoagulant) rather than aspirin alone. Saying aspirin is never acceptable overstates the evidence. Mechanical prophylaxis alone is generally inferior to pharmacologic prophylaxis in high-risk surgical patients. No prophylaxis is unacceptable after major lower-extremity arthroplasty, which carries substantial VTE risk. The lesson is risk-stratified prophylaxis selection.

cases.diagnosis: Submassive (intermediate-risk) acute pulmonary embolism after total knee arthroplasty
pearls: Sudden postoperative dyspnea with hypoxemia, tachycardia, pleuritic pain, and clear lungs should prompt immediate evaluation for pulmonary embolism.
S1Q3T3, right axis deviation, RV dilation on echo, and elevated troponin/BNP indicate right heart strain and define submassive (intermediate-risk) PE in a normotensive patient.
Stable submassive PE is treated with therapeutic anticoagulation and close monitoring; thrombolysis is reserved for hemodynamic deterioration, especially given high postoperative bleeding risk.
Match VTE prophylaxis to patient risk: aspirin may suffice for low-risk arthroplasty patients, but prior VTE or strong family history warrants potent anticoagulant prophylaxis.

cases.title: Neck Pain After a Fall in an Older Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Neck pain after tripping and falling onto his face at home.
cases.history: A 78-year-old man presents to the ED after a ground-level fall: he tripped over a rug and struck his forehead on a coffee table, hyperextending his neck. He did not lose consciousness and has midline posterior neck pain that worsens with movement. He denies arm or leg weakness, numbness, or tingling, and has had no bowel or bladder incontinence. He walked into triage with his daughter. His medical history includes hypertension, atrial fibrillation, and osteoporosis. Medications include apixaban 5 mg twice daily, metoprolol 50 mg daily, amlodipine 5 mg daily, and a calcium/vitamin D supplement. He has no allergies. He lives independently, uses a cane outdoors, drinks rarely, and quit smoking decades ago. Family history is noncontributory. His daughter notes he has had two minor falls in the past year. He is alert, fully oriented, and not intoxicated. He is anxious about being on a blood thinner and asks whether he 'broke something in his neck,' noting the pain is mostly high up near the base of his skull.
cases.exam: Alert, oriented x3, conversant, in a soft collar placed by EMS. There is a forehead abrasion and midline tenderness over the upper cervical spine, more pronounced at the C1-C2 level, with painful, guarded range of motion (not formally tested given tenderness). There is no step-off deformity. Motor strength is 5/5 in all four extremities, sensation is intact to light touch throughout, and reflexes are symmetric without clonus or a Babinski sign. Gait was grossly intact on arrival but limited by pain. There is no Lhermitte phenomenon elicited. Cranial nerves are intact. The remainder of the trauma survey is unremarkable: chest, abdomen, pelvis, and extremities are non-tender with no other injuries identified.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Applying the Canadian C-Spine Rule, why can this patient NOT be cleared clinically without imaging?
options: Because he can rotate his neck 45 degrees as the first step today
Because he had no dangerous mechanism as the first step today
Because he is over 65 years old, a high-risk factor that mandates imaging
Because he has no midline tenderness as the first step today
stages.explanation: Under the Canadian C-Spine Rule, the presence of any high-risk factor mandates imaging, and age 65 or older is a high-risk factor (as are a dangerous mechanism and paresthesias in the extremities). This patient is 78, so he cannot be clinically cleared and requires imaging regardless of other features. Being able to rotate the neck 45 degrees would only matter after high-risk factors are excluded and a low-risk factor permitting safe range-of-motion testing is present. He does in fact have midline tenderness. Although a ground-level fall is not a classic dangerous mechanism, his age alone already triggers imaging.

stages.label: IMAGING CHOICE
stages.question: What is the most appropriate imaging study to evaluate his cervical spine?
options: MRI of the cervical spine as the first study
Plain cervical radiographs (three views) only
No imaging; observe overnight in this setting
Non-contrast CT of the cervical spine
stages.explanation: Non-contrast CT of the cervical spine is the imaging modality of choice in adults with suspected cervical injury after trauma, particularly in older patients, because it is far more sensitive than plain films for fractures, including subtle odontoid and upper cervical injuries that are poorly seen on radiographs. Plain three-view radiographs miss a significant fraction of fractures, especially at C1-C2 and the cervicothoracic junction, and are inadequate here. MRI is excellent for ligamentous and cord injury and is added when neurologic deficit or ligamentous injury is suspected, but CT is the appropriate first bony study. Observation without imaging violates the decision rule given his age.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Non-contrast CT cervical spine: Type II odontoid (dens) fracture through the base of the dens at its junction with the C2 body. Approximately 3 mm of posterior displacement of the dens. No fracture of the C1 ring. No facet dislocation. Spinal canal patent at this level. Mild multilevel degenerative changes elsewhere.
CT head (given anticoagulation and facial impact): No acute intracranial hemorrhage.

stages.label: CLASSIFICATION
stages.question: This is an odontoid fracture through the base of the dens. By the Anderson and D'Alonzo classification, which type is it, and why is it clinically important?
options: Type III (extends into the C2 body); rarely heals in this setting
Type II ; prone to nonunion due to a tenuous blood supply
Type IV; involves the C1 arch as the immediate next step
Type I (tip avulsion); generally stable and benign
stages.explanation: A fracture through the base of the dens at its junction with the C2 body is an Anderson and D'Alonzo type II odontoid fracture, the most common type, and it is clinically important because the fracture line sits at a watershed area with a tenuous blood supply, giving it the highest rate of nonunion, especially in elderly patients and with displacement. Type I is an avulsion of the tip and is usually stable and benign. Type III extends into the cancellous C2 body and generally has a good healing potential because of its broad vascularized surface. There is no type IV in this classification, and the C1 arch is intact here.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this displaced type II odontoid fracture in a 78-year-old on apixaban, what management approach is most appropriate to discuss?
options: Spine surgery consultation, immobilization ,
Lumbar puncture to assess for cord injury as initial therapy
Immediate cervical traction with heavy weights in the ED as initial therapy
Discharge in a soft collar with no follow-up as initial therapy
stages.explanation: A displaced type II odontoid fracture warrants spine surgery consultation and immobilization, with an individualized decision: elderly patients have high nonunion rates with collar treatment yet also carry surgical risk, so management is tailored, weighing operative stabilization (e.g., posterior C1-C2 fusion or anterior odontoid screw) against rigid collar immobilization, and reversal/holding of anticoagulation is coordinated for any procedure. A soft collar with no follow-up is unsafe for an unstable upper cervical fracture. Heavy ED traction is not the standard for an odontoid fracture and risks neurologic harm. A lumbar puncture has no role in evaluating cord injury and could be dangerous.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which additional consideration is most important in this specific patient's acute management?
options: Encouraging early aggressive neck range-of-motion exercises
Managing his anticoagulation around any planned intervention
Starting high-dose corticosteroids for the fracture with appropriate follow-up
Immediate MRI of the entire spine before any stabilization in this clinical context
stages.explanation: A central acute consideration is his anticoagulation: apixaban must be managed (held and possibly reversed) before any surgical intervention, and his recurrent falls and osteoporosis must be addressed to prevent further injury, including a falls workup and bone-health optimization. Corticosteroids are not indicated for an isolated bony cervical fracture without cord injury and carry risk without benefit here. Early aggressive range-of-motion exercises are contraindicated with an unstable upper cervical fracture requiring immobilization. While MRI may be useful to assess ligaments or the cord, it is not a prerequisite that supersedes immobilization and addressing his anticoagulation in the acute setting.

cases.diagnosis: Type II odontoid (C2 dens) fracture
pearls: Age 65 or older is a high-risk factor in the Canadian C-Spine Rule that mandates imaging even after a low-energy fall.
CT, not plain radiographs, is the first-line study for cervical spine fractures in adults; upper cervical injuries are easily missed on plain films.
Anderson and D'Alonzo type II odontoid fractures occur at the dens base, are the most common, and have the highest nonunion rate due to a tenuous blood supply.
Management in the elderly is individualized (operative vs rigid collar), and anticoagulation and recurrent falls must be addressed concurrently.

cases.title: Back Pain and Leg Weakness After a Fall From a Roof
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe mid-back pain and new lower-extremity weakness after a fall from height.
cases.history: A 29-year-old construction worker is admitted to the ICU after falling approximately 4 meters from a roof and landing on his feet, then onto his back. He has severe pain at the thoracolumbar junction and reports that both legs feel weak and 'tingly.' He denies head injury and had no loss of consciousness. He notes difficulty initiating urination since the fall. He has no significant past medical history, takes no medications, and has no allergies. He does not smoke, drinks alcohol on weekends, and uses no recreational drugs. He is otherwise fit. There is no family history of bone disease. On scene, EMS maintained spinal precautions with a backboard and collar and noted he could not lift his legs against gravity. He is alert and frightened, asking whether he will be able to walk again. The trauma team has completed the primary survey; he is hemodynamically stable with no other immediately life-threatening injuries identified, though a full secondary survey and imaging are in progress.
cases.exam: Alert, oriented, in spinal precautions. There is midline tenderness and a palpable focal kyphotic prominence at the thoracolumbar junction (around T12-L1). Motor exam: hip flexion 3/5, knee extension 4/5, ankle dorsiflexion and plantarflexion 2/5 bilaterally; upper extremities 5/5. Sensation is diminished to pinprick below the umbilicus bilaterally, with preserved light touch in some dermatomes. Patellar and Achilles reflexes are diminished; no sustained clonus. Rectal exam shows decreased but present voluntary sphincter tone and intact perianal sensation; bulbocavernosus reflex is present. There is no priapism. Distal pulses are intact and the feet are warm. The abdomen is soft and non-distended. There are no long-bone deformities. He has urinary retention requiring catheterization.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: After ensuring ABCs and spinal precautions, what is the most appropriate next diagnostic step for his spinal injury?
options: Discharge after pain control as the first step today
Plain radiographs of the thoracolumbar spine only as the first step today
CT of the thoracolumbar spine to characterize the bony injury, with MRI to assess the cord
Lumbar puncture as the first step today
stages.explanation: In a high-energy axial-load injury with neurologic deficit, CT precisely defines the fracture pattern and degree of canal compromise, while MRI evaluates the spinal cord, conus, ligamentous integrity, and epidural hematoma, which are critical when a neurologic deficit is present. Plain radiographs alone are insufficient to characterize a burst fracture or canal retropulsion and underestimate injury. Discharge is unsafe in a patient with progressive neurologic deficit and urinary retention. Lumbar puncture has no diagnostic role here and could be hazardous in the setting of spinal injury and possible cord compression.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
CT thoracolumbar spine: Comminuted fracture of the L1 vertebral body with involvement of both the anterior and middle columns. Retropulsion of a bony fragment into the spinal canal with approximately 50% canal compromise. Increased interpediculate distance. Loss of vertebral body height ~40%.
MRI: Spinal cord/conus compression at L1 by the retropulsed fragment; T2 signal change within the conus. Posterior ligamentous complex disruption (high signal in the interspinous region).

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which fracture pattern do these findings describe?
options: Chance (flexion-distraction) fracture without bony involvement
Thoracolumbar burst fracture
Isolated transverse process fracture as the primary diagnosis
Simple anterior wedge compression fracture (anterior column only)
stages.explanation: Failure of both the anterior and middle columns from an axial load, with retropulsion of bone into the canal, increased interpediculate distance, and vertebral body height loss, defines a burst fracture. A simple wedge compression fracture involves only the anterior column without middle-column failure or retropulsion. An isolated transverse process fracture is a minor, stable injury that does not compromise the canal. A Chance fracture is a flexion-distraction injury through the posterior elements and body (classically a seatbelt mechanism) and is distinct from an axial-load burst; while this case also has posterior ligamentous injury, the defining feature is the burst pattern with retropulsion.

stages.label: STABILITY
stages.question: Using the concept of the Thoracolumbar Injury Classification and Severity (TLICS) framework, why is this injury considered unstable and likely operative?
options: Because it involves only the anterior column as the immediate next step
Because there is a neurologic deficit
Because plain films looked normal as the immediate next step
Because the patient is young as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: In the TLICS scheme, points accrue for injury morphology, integrity of the posterior ligamentous complex (PLC), and neurologic status; a burst morphology plus a disrupted PLC plus an incomplete neurologic deficit yields a high score that favors surgical management. Involvement of only the anterior column would describe a stable compression fracture, not this two-column burst with PLC injury. The patient's age does not determine stability. The appearance of plain films is irrelevant to the validated stability assessment; the determination rests on morphology, PLC status, and neurology, all of which here point to instability and operative treatment.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given the incomplete neurologic deficit, canal compromise with cord/conus compression, and PLC disruption, what is the most appropriate management?
options: Urgent surgical decompression and stabilization
Bed rest with a thoracolumbosacral orthosis (TLSO) brace alone
Outpatient physical therapy referral as initial therapy
High-dose corticosteroids as definitive treatment with no surgery
stages.explanation: An unstable burst fracture with an incomplete neurologic deficit and ongoing canal/cord compression and PLC disruption is managed with surgical decompression and stabilization to relieve neural compression and restore alignment, giving the best chance for neurologic recovery and a stable spine. A TLSO brace is appropriate for stable burst fractures without neurologic deficit or PLC injury, not for this unstable, deficit-associated injury. Outpatient PT alone is inappropriate for an unstable spine with cord compression. Corticosteroids are not a substitute for decompression in this setting and are controversial in acute traumatic spinal cord injury; they are not definitive therapy.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: While awaiting the operating room, which physiologic complication must you anticipate and monitor for, given the level and severity of his injury?
options: Neurogenic shock with hypotension and bradycardia
Immediate hyperkalemic cardiac arrest from a single dose of any medication
Acute pancreatitis as the leading concern
Hypertensive emergency from cord injury
stages.explanation: Significant spinal cord injury, particularly at higher thoracic levels but possible with severe injuries, can produce neurogenic shock from loss of sympathetic tone, characterized by hypotension with paradoxical bradycardia, which requires vigilant monitoring and supportive management (fluids and vasopressors as needed). A hypertensive emergency (autonomic dysreflexia) is a chronic phenomenon seen later in patients with injuries at or above T6, not an acute concern in this immediate phase. Hyperkalemic arrest is a specific risk with succinylcholine in the subacute/chronic phase of denervation, not an immediate single-dose certainty now. Acute pancreatitis is unrelated to this spinal injury.

cases.diagnosis: L1 thoracolumbar burst fracture with retropulsion and incomplete neurologic deficit
pearls: Axial-load (fall onto feet/back) injuries cause burst fractures from anterior and middle column failure with canal retropulsion; get CT for bone and MRI for cord/ligaments.
Burst fractures are distinguished from simple wedge compression fractures by middle-column failure, retropulsion, and widened interpediculate distance.
TLICS integrates injury morphology, posterior ligamentous complex integrity, and neurologic status; high scores (deficit plus PLC disruption) favor surgery.
Unstable burst fractures with neurologic deficit and cord compression warrant surgical decompression and stabilization; watch for neurogenic shock (hypotension with bradycardia).

cases.title: Crushed Pelvis and Falling Blood Pressure
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe pelvic pain and hemodynamic instability after a high-speed motorcycle crash.
cases.history: A 36-year-old man is brought to the trauma bay and then the ICU after a high-speed motorcycle collision in which he was thrown from the bike and struck a guardrail. He complains of severe pelvic and lower abdominal pain. He is in distress and increasingly drowsy. There was no helmet loss of consciousness reported by EMS, but he has become progressively pale and clammy en route. He has no known past medical history, takes no medications, and has no allergies. He smokes occasionally and drinks socially. There is no family history of bleeding disorders. EMS noted an unstable-feeling pelvis on initial assessment and applied no binder. On arrival, the trauma team is running the primary survey; the airway is patent, breath sounds are equal, but he is tachycardic and hypotensive with cool extremities. Two large-bore IVs are in place and a massive transfusion protocol is being considered. He is responsive to voice but anxious and asking what happened. There is blood at the urethral meatus noted by the nurse.
cases.exam: Pale, diaphoretic, anxious, responsive to voice. Airway patent; chest with equal breath sounds and no flail segment. Abdomen is mildly distended with lower-quadrant tenderness. The pelvis is tender and feels unstable; there is bruising over the perineum and scrotal swelling, with blood at the urethral meatus. Gentle assessment suggests pelvic widening. Distal pulses in the legs are weak but present; feet are cool. There is no obvious long-bone deformity. Neurologic exam grossly shows moving all extremities. A FAST exam is being performed. Given the urethral blood, a digital rectal exam is done showing a high-riding, boggy prostate; no gross rectal blood. The skin over the pelvis is intact with no open wounds.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In this hypotensive patient with a clinically unstable pelvis, what is the most appropriate immediate intervention?
options: Take the patient immediately to MRI as the first step today
Insert a Foley catheter right away to monitor urine output as the first step today
Apply a pelvic binder and begin resuscitation
Repeatedly spring and rock the pelvis to confirm instability as the first step today
stages.explanation: In a hemodynamically unstable patient with a suspected unstable pelvic ring injury, applying a pelvic binder centered over the greater trochanters provides circumferential compression that reduces pelvic volume and helps tamponade venous bleeding while resuscitation proceeds. Repeatedly springing/rocking the pelvis can dislodge clot and worsen hemorrhage and should be avoided once instability is suspected. MRI is slow, inaccessible for an unstable patient, and inappropriate in this acute setting. Inserting a Foley is contraindicated here because blood at the meatus and a high-riding prostate suggest urethral injury; a retrograde urethrogram should precede catheterization.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
FAST: No significant intraperitoneal free fluid
Hemoglobin 8.1 g/dL (down from 12.5 on arrival)
Lactate 5.2 mmol/L
Base deficit -8
INR 1.4
Pelvic radiograph: Widening of the pubic symphysis to ~3.5 cm with diastasis of the right sacroiliac joint ('open-book' pattern)
After binder placement: BP improves transiently to 96/60, then drifts down again
Retrograde urethrogram: contrast extravasation consistent with urethral injury

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which pelvic ring injury pattern do these findings indicate?
options: Isolated acetabular fracture
Lateral compression injury
Anteroposterior compression injury
Stable pubic ramus fracture
stages.explanation: Widening of the pubic symphysis with sacroiliac joint diastasis is the classic 'open-book' pattern of an anteroposterior compression (APC) injury, in which external rotation forces spring the pelvis open, increasing pelvic volume and disrupting the posterior ligaments, predisposing to major venous (and sometimes arterial) bleeding. Lateral compression injuries cause internal rotation with overlapping or impacted rami and a reduced pelvic volume. An isolated acetabular fracture involves the joint surface rather than the ring's anterior and posterior elements opening. A stable single pubic ramus fracture would not produce symphyseal widening, SI diastasis, or this degree of instability and hemorrhage.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He responds only transiently to the binder and ongoing transfusion (a 'transient responder'), and FAST is negative. What is the most appropriate next step to control pelvic hemorrhage?
options: Immediate definitive open reduction and internal fixation of the pelvis as the first hemorrhage maneuver as initial therapy
Discharge to the ward for monitoring as initial therapy
Observe and continue crystalloid only as initial therapy
Pursue hemorrhage control via angiographic embolization and/or preperitoneal pelvic packing, coordinated with the trauma team
stages.explanation: A transient responder with a negative FAST and an open-book pelvis whose bleeding source is the pelvis requires active hemorrhage control: angiographic embolization addresses arterial bleeding, and preperitoneal pelvic packing (often with external fixation) controls venous/bony bleeding; the choice and sequence are coordinated by the trauma team and institutional protocol. Crystalloid alone does not control surgical hemorrhage and worsens coagulopathy. Definitive ORIF is not the initial hemorrhage-control maneuver in an unstable patient; temporary stabilization plus embolization/packing comes first. Discharge to the ward is dangerous in an actively bleeding, unstable patient.

stages.label: RESUSCITATION
stages.question: Given his hemoglobin drop, lactate, base deficit, and INR with ongoing bleeding, what resuscitation principle should guide his care?
options: Balanced massive transfusion with permissive hypotension
Withhold all blood products until the source is confirmed angiographically in this setting
Diuresis to protect the kidneys as the immediate next step
Large-volume crystalloid resuscitation to a high-normal blood pressure in this setting
stages.explanation: Hemorrhagic shock from pelvic trauma is best managed with damage-control resuscitation: balanced transfusion of red cells, plasma, and platelets in roughly equal ratios, judicious permissive hypotension until surgical control, early tranexamic acid (within 3 hours), and correction of coagulopathy. Large-volume crystalloid dilutes clotting factors, worsens coagulopathy and acidosis, and drives the lethal triad. Diuresis is harmful in a hypovolemic, bleeding patient. Withholding blood until angiographic source confirmation would let the patient exsanguinate; resuscitation must proceed concurrently with hemorrhage control efforts.

stages.label: ASSOCIATED INJURY
stages.question: Blood at the meatus and a high-riding prostate with urethrogram extravasation indicate which associated injury, and what is the implication?
options: No urologic injury; place a Foley as usual as the immediate next step
Ureteral transection; obtain immediate IV pyelogram in this setting
Bladder rupture; proceed directly to suprapubic tube without further evaluation
Posterior urethral injury
stages.explanation: Blood at the urethral meatus, a high-riding/boggy prostate, and contrast extravasation on retrograde urethrogram indicate a posterior urethral injury, which classically accompanies pelvic ring fractures; the key implication is to avoid blind urethral catheterization (which can convert a partial to a complete tear) and to involve urology, who may place a suprapubic catheter or perform delayed repair. Assuming bladder rupture and placing a suprapubic tube without evaluation skips diagnosis and may be premature. Ureteral injuries are less typical of this mechanism and are not what the urethrogram shows. Concluding there is no injury and placing a Foley ignores clear evidence of urethral disruption.

cases.diagnosis: Open-book (anteroposterior compression) pelvic ring fracture with hemorrhagic shock and associated posterior urethral injury
pearls: For a hemodynamically unstable patient with an unstable pelvis, apply a pelvic binder over the greater trochanters early and avoid repeated manipulation that can dislodge clot.
Symphyseal widening with sacroiliac diastasis is the open-book (APC) pattern, which increases pelvic volume and bleeds heavily.

Transient responders with pelvic bleeding need active hemorrhage control via angioembolization and/or preperitoneal packing, alongside balanced massive transfusion, permissive hypotension, and early tranexamic acid.
Blood at the meatus, a high-riding prostate, and urethrogram extravasation mean posterior urethral injury: do not place a blind Foley; involve urology.

cases.title: Deep Groin Pain in a Young Athlete
cases.chiefComplaint: Several months of activity-related anterior groin pain that catches and clicks.
cases.history: A 27-year-old recreational soccer player and yoga enthusiast presents to clinic with about eight months of deep right anterior groin pain. The pain is worse with prolonged sitting, getting out of a low car, pivoting, and deep squats, and she sometimes feels a catching or clicking sensation in the hip. She localizes the pain by cupping her hand over the front of the hip in a 'C' around the joint (a positive C-sign). There is no history of acute trauma, though she recalls a gradual onset over a season of increased training. She denies night pain, fevers, weight loss, or low back pain radiating down the leg. Her medical history is unremarkable; menses are regular and she takes only an oral contraceptive and occasional ibuprofen. She has no allergies. She does not smoke, drinks rarely, and works as a graphic designer (prolonged sitting). Family history is noncontributory. She is frustrated that activity modification and NSAIDs have only partially helped and worries this is 'early arthritis' that will end her sports.
cases.exam: Comfortable, with a normal non-antalgic gait. She demonstrates a positive C-sign, grasping the anterolateral hip to indicate the pain location. The FADIR test (flexion, adduction, internal rotation, i.e., the anterior impingement test) reproduces her deep groin pain on the right. Internal rotation of the flexed hip is reduced compared with the left. The FABER (flexion, abduction, external rotation) test produces some groin discomfort. Hip flexion strength is full but mildly uncomfortable. There is no tenderness over the greater trochanter, no pain with resisted straight-leg raise suggesting a different pathology, and a negative log-roll for gross arthritis crepitus. Neurovascular exam of the limb is normal. The lumbar spine exam is unremarkable with a negative straight-leg raise.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which physical exam maneuver is most specific for anterior hip impingement and labral pathology, and is positive here?
options: Trendelenburg test as the first step today
Thomas test as the first step today
FADIR anterior impingement test
Ober test as the first step today
stages.explanation: The FADIR (anterior impingement) test, which combines flexion, adduction, and internal rotation, mechanically approximates the femoral neck against the acetabular rim and is the most sensitive/provocative maneuver for femoroacetabular impingement (FAI) and associated labral tears, reproducing deep groin pain when positive, as in this patient. The Trendelenburg test assesses hip abductor (gluteus medius) strength, not impingement. The Thomas test evaluates hip flexor contracture. The Ober test assesses iliotibial band/tensor fasciae latae tightness. None of the latter three specifically provoke anterior labral/impingement pain the way FADIR does.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial imaging study for suspected femoroacetabular impingement?
options: Non-contrast CT of the pelvis as the immediate next step
MRI arthrogram of the hip as the very first test in this setting
Bone scan as the immediate next step
Plain radiographs of the pelvis/hip
stages.explanation: Plain radiographs (an AP pelvis plus a lateral view such as a Dunn or cross-table lateral) are the appropriate first imaging step to evaluate for bony morphology of FAI, namely a cam lesion (aspherical femoral head-neck junction with an increased alpha angle) or a pincer lesion (acetabular overcoverage), and to exclude arthritis or dysplasia. MR arthrography is excellent for visualizing labral tears and chondral damage but is generally obtained after radiographs when surgical planning or diagnostic confirmation is needed, not as the very first test. CT details bone but adds radiation and is reserved for surgical planning. A bone scan is nonspecific and not indicated for this mechanical problem.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
AP pelvis and Dunn lateral radiographs: Aspherical femoral head-neck junction on the right with a prominent 'bump,' alpha angle measured at 68 degrees (elevated; cam morphology). Acetabular coverage normal with a normal lateral center-edge angle (no pincer overcoverage, no dysplasia). Joint space preserved with no significant osteoarthritis.
MR arthrogram (obtained for confirmation): Anterosuperior acetabular labral tear with adjacent paralabral cyst; mild adjacent chondral signal change.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on the history, exam, and imaging, what is the diagnosis?
options: Greater trochanteric pain syndrome (bursitis) as the primary diagnosis
Hip osteoarthritis as the primary diagnosis
Acetabular labral tear secondary to cam-type femoroacetabular impingement
Lumbar radiculopathy referring to the groin as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: A young athlete with activity-related deep groin pain, a positive C-sign, a positive FADIR test with reduced internal rotation, cam morphology (elevated alpha angle, aspherical head-neck junction) on radiographs, and an anterosuperior labral tear on MR arthrogram has a labral tear secondary to cam-type FAI. Greater trochanteric pain syndrome causes lateral hip tenderness over the trochanter, not deep groin pain with a positive FADIR. Established osteoarthritis would show joint-space narrowing and osteophytes, which are absent. Lumbar radiculopathy produces dermatomal radiating pain with neurologic signs and a positive straight-leg raise, none of which are present; the spine exam is normal.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: She has had several months of partial relief with activity modification and NSAIDs. What is the most appropriate next management step?
options: A structured course of physical therapy focused on core/hip stabilization
Complete and permanent cessation of all physical activity as initial therapy
Long-term opioid analgesia as initial therapy
Immediate total hip arthroplasty as initial therapy
stages.explanation: First-line treatment of symptomatic FAI with a labral tear is a structured, supervised physical therapy program emphasizing core and hip stabilization, neuromuscular control, and activity/movement modification; if a meaningful trial of conservative management fails, hip arthroscopy to address the cam lesion (femoral osteochondroplasty) and repair the labrum is considered. Total hip arthroplasty is for end-stage arthritis, not a young patient with a preserved joint. Long-term opioids are inappropriate for a chronic mechanical condition and carry dependence risk. Permanent cessation of all activity is neither necessary nor therapeutic; targeted rehabilitation and selective activity modification are preferred over total deconditioning.

stages.label: PROGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most important reason to address cam-type FAI rather than ignore it long-term?
options: It causes systemic inflammatory arthritis throughout the body as the immediate next step
Cam morphology is associated with increased risk of progressive labral
It will spontaneously remodel and disappear with rest as the immediate next step
It invariably leads to avascular necrosis of the femoral head as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Cam-type FAI is associated with repetitive abutment of the aspherical femoral head-neck junction against the acetabular rim, which over time can drive progressive labral tearing and chondral delamination and is linked to the development of early hip osteoarthritis; addressing symptomatic mechanical impingement aims to relieve pain and may help protect the joint. Cam morphology is a bony deformity that does not remodel away with rest. It does not invariably cause avascular necrosis, which has different risk factors. FAI is a focal mechanical/structural problem, not a systemic inflammatory arthritis affecting multiple joints.

cases.diagnosis: Acetabular labral tear secondary to cam-type femoroacetabular impingement
pearls: Young adults with deep activity-related groin pain, a positive C-sign, and a positive FADIR test should be evaluated for femoroacetabular impingement and labral tear.
Start with plain radiographs (AP pelvis plus a lateral) to assess cam (elevated alpha angle) and pincer morphology; MR arthrogram best demonstrates labral tears.
First-line treatment is physical therapy and activity modification; hip arthroscopy (osteochondroplasty plus labral repair) is considered if conservative care fails.
Cam morphology is associated with progressive chondrolabral damage and early hip osteoarthritis, which is why symptomatic impingement is worth addressing.

cases.title: Burning Pain in the Ball of the Foot
cases.chiefComplaint: Burning forefoot pain with a sensation of 'walking on a pebble' between the toes.
cases.history: A 46-year-old woman presents to clinic with several months of burning, sometimes electric pain in the ball of her right foot, radiating into the third and fourth toes. She describes a sensation of a wadded-up sock or a pebble under the forefoot and occasional numbness of the adjacent sides of those toes. Symptoms are worse in tight, narrow dress shoes and high heels she wears for work, and are relieved by removing the shoe and massaging the foot. There is no history of acute trauma. She denies fevers, redness, or swelling, and has no morning stiffness or other joint pains. Her medical history is unremarkable; she takes only a daily multivitamin and occasional acetaminophen, and has no allergies. She works in retail management and stands much of the day in fashionable footwear. She does not smoke, drinks socially, and exercises with running on weekends. Family history is noncontributory. She is concerned this could be a stress fracture or nerve disease and wants relief so she can keep working on her feet.
cases.exam: Comfortable, in no distress. The forefoot has no erythema, warmth, or swelling and no obvious deformity. There is focal tenderness in the third web space (between the third and fourth metatarsal heads). Palpating this interspace while squeezing the metatarsal heads together with the other hand produces a palpable, sometimes audible click with reproduction of her pain (a positive Mulder sign). There is reduced sensation along the contiguous sides of the third and fourth toes. There is no tenderness directly over the metatarsal shafts or heads to suggest a stress fracture, and no metatarsophalangeal joint synovitis. Pulses are 2+, capillary refill is brisk, and the foot is warm. Ankle and subtalar motion are normal. The remainder of the foot exam, including the plantar fascia and Achilles, is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which examination finding is most characteristic of this condition?
options: Pain on resisted great-toe plantarflexion as the first step today
A positive Tinel sign at the tarsal tunnel as the first step today
A positive Thompson test as the first step today
A positive Mulder click on mediolateral compression of the metatarsal heads
stages.explanation: The Mulder click, elicited by squeezing the metatarsal heads together while palpating the symptomatic web space, reproduces pain and a palpable/audible click and is characteristic of a Morton (interdigital) neuroma. The Thompson test evaluates Achilles tendon continuity, not forefoot interdigital pathology. A positive Tinel sign at the tarsal tunnel indicates tarsal tunnel syndrome (posterior tibial nerve), which causes plantar/medial foot symptoms rather than focal interdigital web-space pain with a click. Pain on resisted great-toe plantarflexion points to flexor hallucis longus pathology, not an interdigital neuroma between the third and fourth toes.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Morton neuroma of the third web space
Metatarsal stress fracture in this clinical context
Metatarsophalangeal joint osteoarthritis
Gouty arthritis of the forefoot
stages.explanation: Burning, electric forefoot pain radiating into the third and fourth toes with a pebble sensation, numbness of the contiguous toe surfaces, relief with shoe removal and massage, worsening in tight shoes, and a positive Mulder click localizing to the third web space is the classic presentation of a Morton interdigital neuroma (perineural fibrosis of the common digital nerve). A metatarsal stress fracture causes focal bony tenderness over the shaft and pain on weight-bearing, not web-space neuritic symptoms with a click. MTP osteoarthritis causes joint-line pain and stiffness with motion. Gout causes acute red, hot, swollen joint inflammation, which is absent here.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Morton neuroma is largely a clinical diagnosis. If imaging is used to confirm or exclude alternatives, which modality is most appropriate?
options: Ultrasound to confirm the neuroma
Nerve conduction studies of the common digital nerve as the standard confirmatory test
CT angiography of the foot as the immediate next step
Plain radiographs alone are sufficient to visualize the neuroma in this setting
stages.explanation: When confirmation is needed or the diagnosis is uncertain, ultrasound and MRI are the imaging modalities of choice; ultrasound is inexpensive and can visualize the hypoechoic interdigital mass, while MRI delineates the neuroma and excludes alternatives. Plain radiographs do not show the soft-tissue neuroma, though they are reasonable to rule out a bony cause such as a stress fracture or arthritis. CT angiography evaluates vasculature and is irrelevant to this neuritic interdigital problem. Nerve conduction studies are not the standard confirmatory test for Morton neuroma; the diagnosis is primarily clinical, supported by ultrasound or MRI when needed.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line management?
options: Long-term oral opioids as initial therapy
Below-knee amputation as initial therapy
Conservative measures: wide toe-box footwear, a metatarsal pad/offloading orthotic,
Immediate surgical excision of the neuroma as initial therapy
stages.explanation: First-line treatment of Morton neuroma is conservative: switching to wide toe-box, low-heeled shoes, using a metatarsal pad or orthotic to offload and spread the metatarsal heads, and modifying activity, which relieves the mechanical compression that drives symptoms. Surgical excision (neurectomy) is reserved for cases that fail conservative care and injections. Long-term opioids are inappropriate for this chronic mechanical/neuritic condition and risk dependence. Amputation is never indicated for a benign interdigital neuroma. Starting with footwear and offloading addresses the root mechanical cause and resolves or improves symptoms in many patients before any procedure is considered.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Her symptoms persist despite several months of appropriate footwear changes and a metatarsal pad. What is the most reasonable next step before considering surgery?
options: A corticosteroid injection into the affected web space , counseling on risks
Systemic high-dose corticosteroids for one month as the immediate next step
Proceed directly to amputation of the toes as the immediate next step
Begin chronic gabapentin indefinitely with no other measures before discharge planning
stages.explanation: When footwear modification and offloading fail, a corticosteroid injection into the affected interspace (often with local anesthetic, which can also be diagnostic) is a reasonable next step that frequently provides relief, with counseling about risks such as fat-pad atrophy and depigmentation. Systemic high-dose steroids are not used for a localized neuroma and carry unnecessary systemic risk. Proceeding directly to amputation is never appropriate for a benign neuroma. While neuropathic agents like gabapentin may adjunctively help symptoms, indefinite gabapentin alone without addressing the lesion is not the standard escalation; a targeted injection is the typical next step, with neurectomy reserved for refractory cases.

cases.diagnosis: Morton (interdigital) neuroma of the third web space
pearls: Morton neuroma presents with burning forefoot pain radiating into adjacent toes, a 'pebble in the shoe' sensation, and is worsened by tight shoes; the third web space is most common.
The Mulder click (palpable/audible click reproducing pain on metatarsal head compression) is the characteristic exam finding; the diagnosis is largely clinical.
Ultrasound or MRI confirms the neuroma and excludes alternatives when needed; plain films help rule out stress fracture or arthritis.
First-line care is wide toe-box shoes and a metatarsal pad/orthotic; escalate to corticosteroid injection, then surgical neurectomy for refractory cases.

cases.title: Sudden Shoulder Pain After a Tackle
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe left shoulder pain and inability to move the arm after a collision during a rugby match.
cases.history: A 24-year-old recreational rugby player presents to the ED 40 minutes after being tackled while his left arm was extended overhead to catch the ball. He felt a sudden 'pop' and immediate severe pain, and has been holding the arm slightly away from his body, supporting it with his right hand. He cannot bring the arm to his side or rotate it inward without excruciating pain. He denies head injury, loss of consciousness, neck pain, or paresthesias, though he notes the shoulder 'feels out of place.' This is his first such injury. PMH is unremarkable; he takes no medications and has no allergies. He drinks socially, does not smoke, and uses no recreational drugs. He works as a graphic designer and is right-hand dominant. Family history is noncontributory. He took 400 mg ibuprofen before arrival with minimal relief. He is anxious about whether he will need surgery and how long he will be out of sport.
cases.exam: The left arm is held in slight abduction and external rotation; the patient resists adduction and internal rotation. There is loss of the normal rounded deltoid contour with a prominent acromion and a palpable fullness anteriorly and inferiorly. Sensation is intact over the lateral deltoid (axillary nerve regimental badge area) and throughout the hand; radial pulse is 2+ and capillary refill is brisk. Distal motor function in the hand is intact. No open wounds, no neck tenderness, and no other extremity deformity. The contralateral shoulder is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step before attempting any reduction maneuver?
options: Immediate closed reduction at bedside without imaging as the first step today
MRI of the shoulder to assess the labrum as the first step today
Apply a sling and discharge with orthopedic follow-up as the first step today
Obtain pre-reduction radiographs including an axillary or scapular-Y view
stages.explanation: Pre-reduction radiographs (AP plus an axillary or scapular-Y view) confirm the direction of dislocation and exclude an associated fracture before manipulation; a documented neurovascular exam (especially axillary nerve) is mandatory before and after reduction. Immediate reduction without imaging risks missing a fracture-dislocation and displacing fragments. MRI is not part of the acute workup and delays care; the labrum is evaluated electively if instability persists. A sling alone leaves the joint dislocated, threatening cartilage and the axillary nerve, and is inappropriate management of an acute dislocation.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
AP shoulder radiograph: humeral head displaced anteriorly and inferiorly, lying beneath the coracoid (subcoracoid position)
Scapular-Y view: humeral head anterior to the glenoid 'Y' center
No Hill-Sachs lesion or bony Bankart fracture identified
No glenoid, humeral neck, or greater tuberosity fracture
Clavicle and acromioclavicular joint intact

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which finding is most consistent with the diagnosis of an anterior shoulder dislocation?
options: Inability to abduct the arm with a positive drop-arm sign and intact contour
Humeral head in a subcoracoid position with the arm held in abduction
Arm held in adduction and internal rotation with posterior fullness in this clinical context
Squared-off shoulder with the arm locked in internal rotation after a seizure
stages.explanation: Anterior dislocation, the most common type (~95%), classically presents with the arm held in slight abduction and external rotation and the humeral head displaced anteriorly to a subcoracoid position, exactly as imaged. Adduction/internal rotation with posterior fullness describes a posterior dislocation. A drop-arm sign with preserved contour suggests a rotator cuff tear, not dislocation. The arm 'locked in internal rotation' after a seizure with a squared-off shoulder is the classic posterior dislocation pattern (seizures, electrocution), which is frequently missed on AP film—hence the need for the axillary view.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After confirming an anterior dislocation without fracture, what is the most appropriate definitive acute management?
options: Closed reduction with procedural analgesia/sedation, followed by post-reduction radiographs
Open reduction in the operating room as initial therapy
Long-arm casting with the elbow flexed at 90 degrees as initial therapy
Intra-articular corticosteroid injection and physical therapy referral as initial therapy
stages.explanation: An acute anterior dislocation without fracture is treated with prompt closed reduction using adequate analgesia (intra-articular lidocaine or procedural sedation) and a gentle technique (e.g., scapular manipulation, traction-countertraction, or external rotation). Post-reduction films confirm relocation and exclude iatrogenic fracture, and the neurovascular exam is repeated. Open reduction is reserved for irreducible dislocations, fracture-dislocations, or button-holed soft tissue. Corticosteroid injection has no role in acute dislocation. Casting does not address the dislocation and is not used; after reduction a sling with early controlled motion is standard.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After successful reduction with relief of pain and restored contour, what is the most appropriate disposition and counseling?
options: Admit for observation and repeat reduction every 8 hours with appropriate follow-up
Refer for emergent surgical stabilization tonight with appropriate follow-up
Discharge without immobilization and clear him for return to contact sport in 3 days
Immobilize in a sling, repeat neurovascular check,
stages.explanation: After reduction, place the arm in a sling for comfort, re-document the neurovascular exam (axillary nerve), and arrange orthopedic follow-up with early progressive rehabilitation; prolonged rigid immobilization offers no recurrence benefit. He must avoid contact sport until strength and stability return—not within 3 days—because recurrence risk is high in young athletes. Routine admission and serial re-reductions are unnecessary for a stable, reduced joint. Emergent surgery is not indicated for a first-time, uncomplicated, successfully reduced dislocation; surgical stabilization is considered electively for recurrent instability or high-demand athletes after discussion.

cases.diagnosis: Anterior glenohumeral (shoulder) dislocation
pearls: Anterior dislocation accounts for ~95% of shoulder dislocations; the arm is held in abduction and external rotation with the head in a subcoracoid position.
Always assess axillary nerve function (sensation over the lateral deltoid) before and after reduction—it is the most commonly injured nerve.
Obtain an axillary or scapular-Y view: posterior dislocations are easily missed on a single AP film and classically follow seizures or electrocution.
Young athletes have a high recurrence rate; early rehabilitation is favored over prolonged immobilization, with elective stabilization for recurrent instability.

cases.title: Aching Shoulder With Overhead Activity
cases.chiefComplaint: Several months of right shoulder pain that worsens with reaching overhead.
cases.history: A 50-year-old right-hand-dominant warehouse supervisor presents with 4 months of gradually worsening right shoulder pain. The pain is worst when he reaches overhead to stock high shelves and when he lies on that side at night, occasionally waking him. He localizes a dull ache to the lateral upper arm. There was no acute injury. He denies neck pain, numbness, tingling, or weakness in the hand, and denies fevers or weight loss. He has been taking ibuprofen 400 mg as needed with partial relief. PMH includes well-controlled hypertension on lisinopril 10 mg daily and borderline hyperlipidemia. He has no allergies. He played recreational tennis until the pain began.
