He smokes half a pack of cigarettes daily for 20 years and drinks two beers on weekends. Family history is notable for osteoarthritis in his mother. He is worried the pain will limit his ability to do his job, which requires frequent overhead lifting.
cases.exam: Right shoulder shows no deformity, erythema, or atrophy. There is tenderness over the greater tuberosity and the anterolateral subacromial space. A painful arc is present between roughly 60 and 120 degrees of active abduction. Neer (passive forward flexion) and Hawkins (forward flexion to 90 degrees with internal rotation) maneuvers reproduce the pain. Active and passive range of motion are preserved, though active motion is limited by pain. Rotator cuff strength is grossly preserved without a drop-arm sign. Distal neurovascular exam is intact, and cervical spine motion does not reproduce the shoulder pain.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which combination of exam findings best supports the suspected diagnosis?
options: Painful arc from 60–120 degrees with positive Neer
Positive drop-arm sign with profound external-rotation weakness as the first step today
Reproduction of pain with Spurling maneuver radiating to the hand as the first step today
Loss of passive external rotation with global stiffness as the first step today
stages.explanation: Subacromial impingement/rotator cuff tendinopathy classically produces a painful arc (≈60–120°) with positive Neer and Hawkins impingement signs, while passive motion is preserved—matching this patient. Loss of passive external rotation with global stiffness is adhesive capsulitis. A positive drop-arm sign with marked weakness suggests a full-thickness cuff tear. Spurling-provoked radiating pain to the hand indicates cervical radiculopathy. Distinguishing these clusters is high yield because management diverges substantially among impingement, frozen shoulder, cuff tear, and cervical disease.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial diagnostic approach for this presentation?
options: Immediate MRI of the shoulder as the primary diagnosis
A clinical diagnosis
Electromyography and nerve conduction studies as the primary diagnosis
CT arthrogram of the glenohumeral joint as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Subacromial impingement/rotator cuff tendinopathy is largely a clinical diagnosis; plain radiographs are a reasonable first imaging study to assess for subacromial spurring, acromial morphology, or calcific tendinitis, especially if symptoms persist. MRI is not first-line and is reserved for suspected full-thickness tears, failure of conservative therapy, or preoperative planning. EMG/NCS evaluates nerve pathology such as radiculopathy, which the exam does not support. CT arthrogram is an advanced test used when MRI is contraindicated and is unnecessary in an initial uncomplicated presentation.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line management?
options: Relative activity modification, NSAIDs,
Arthroscopic subacromial decompression now as initial therapy
Complete immobilization in a sling for 6 weeks as initial therapy
Systemic oral corticosteroid taper as initial therapy
stages.explanation: First-line treatment is conservative: activity modification (avoiding aggravating overhead work), NSAIDs for analgesia, and a structured physical therapy program emphasizing rotator cuff and scapular stabilizer strengthening and posture. Most patients improve without surgery. Arthroscopic decompression is reserved for those who fail months of well-conducted conservative care. Prolonged sling immobilization risks stiffness and adhesive capsulitis and is contraindicated. Systemic corticosteroids are not standard; a targeted subacromial corticosteroid injection is an option for refractory symptoms but is not the initial step over therapy.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After 10 weeks of adherent physical therapy and NSAIDs, he has persistent night pain and a positive painful arc but no weakness or drop-arm sign. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Offer a subacromial corticosteroid injection
Reassure and discharge with no further intervention before discharge planning
Start chronic opioid therapy as the immediate next step
Refer immediately for rotator cuff repair as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: When a well-conducted conservative trial fails but there is no objective weakness or drop-arm sign, a subacromial corticosteroid injection can provide relief and facilitate continued rehabilitation; MRI is appropriate if symptoms persist or new weakness suggests a cuff tear. Immediate cuff repair is premature without evidence of a significant tear or failed comprehensive management. Chronic opioids are inappropriate for a benign musculoskeletal condition. Discharging without addressing ongoing night pain abandons a treatable problem and misses evolving pathology.

cases.diagnosis: Subacromial impingement syndrome with rotator cuff tendinopathy
pearls: A painful arc (60–120°) with positive Neer and Hawkins signs and preserved passive motion is the classic impingement pattern.
Impingement/tendinopathy is a clinical diagnosis; reserve MRI for suspected tears or failed conservative therapy.
First-line care is activity modification, NSAIDs, and rotator cuff/scapular strengthening—most patients improve without surgery.
A drop-arm sign or significant external-rotation weakness should raise concern for a full-thickness rotator cuff tear.

cases.title: A Lump on the Back of the Wrist
cases.chiefComplaint: A painless bump on the back of the wrist that has changed in size over a few months.
cases.history: A 28-year-old graphic designer presents with a 3-month history of a smooth lump on the dorsal aspect of her right wrist. She first noticed it after a period of heavy keyboard and tablet use. The lump is mostly painless but causes a mild aching when she fully flexes or extends the wrist, and she reports it has gotten smaller and then larger again over the weeks. She denies redness, warmth, fever, numbness, or weakness in the hand, and there has been no trauma. She has no significant PMH, takes only an oral contraceptive pill, and has no allergies. She does not smoke and drinks occasionally. Family history is unremarkable. She is primarily concerned that the mass could be cancerous and is anxious because it seems to fluctuate in size, which she finds alarming. She asks whether it should simply be cut out.
cases.exam: On the dorsal wrist, over the scapholunate region, there is a firm, smooth, well-circumscribed, mobile mass approximately 1.5 cm in diameter. It is non-tender and not warm or erythematous, and the overlying skin is normal. The mass transilluminates brightly when a penlight is applied. It becomes more prominent with wrist flexion. There is no overlying ulceration. Range of motion is full though slightly uncomfortable at end-range. Distal sensation, capillary refill, and grip strength are intact. Allen test is normal, and there is no Tinel sign over the carpal tunnel or Guyon canal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which bedside finding most strongly supports the suspected diagnosis?
options: The mass is fixed to underlying bone and stony hard
The mass is warm, fluctuant, and acutely tender
The mass transilluminates brightly with a penlight
The mass is pulsatile and has an audible bruit
stages.explanation: Bright transillumination indicates a fluid-filled cystic structure and is the classic bedside sign of a ganglion cyst, the most common soft-tissue mass of the hand and wrist. A pulsatile mass with a bruit suggests a vascular lesion (e.g., aneurysm). A stony-hard mass fixed to bone raises concern for a bony or solid neoplasm. A warm, fluctuant, tender mass suggests an abscess or septic process. These distinguishing features guide whether reassurance, imaging, or urgent intervention is needed.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis and its origin?
options: Dorsal wrist ganglion cyst arising from the scapholunate joint/tendon sheath
Lipoma arising from subcutaneous fat as the primary diagnosis
Rheumatoid nodule as the primary diagnosis
Giant cell tumor of the tendon sheath as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: A dorsal wrist ganglion is a mucin-filled cyst that most commonly arises from the scapholunate joint or adjacent tendon sheath via a one-way valve, explaining its fluctuation in size and bright transillumination—exactly this presentation. A lipoma is soft, lobulated, and does not transilluminate brightly. A giant cell tumor of the tendon sheath is solid, does not transilluminate, and is more common on the volar/digital surfaces. A rheumatoid nodule occurs over extensor/pressure surfaces in patients with established rheumatoid arthritis and is solid, not cystic.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The patient is reassured but asks about treatment for this asymptomatic-to-mildly-aching cyst. What is the most appropriate initial recommendation?
options: Striking the cyst with a heavy book to rupture it ('Bible therapy')
Reassurance and observation, as many ganglia resolve spontaneously
Begin oral antibiotics and warm compresses as initial therapy
Urgent surgical excision to prevent malignant transformation
stages.explanation: Ganglion cysts are benign; for asymptomatic or minimally symptomatic cysts, reassurance and observation are appropriate because a substantial proportion resolve spontaneously. Surgical excision is reserved for persistent pain, functional limitation, or cosmetic concern—not to prevent malignancy, since ganglia do not become malignant. The old 'Bible therapy' of striking the cyst is no longer recommended due to risk of injury and high recurrence. Antibiotics are unnecessary because this is not an infection; there is no warmth, erythema, or tenderness to suggest one.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: If the cyst became persistently painful and limited her work, which intervention carries the lowest recurrence rate?
options: Corticosteroid injection into the cyst in this setting
Repeated manual compression as the immediate next step
Needle aspiration alone as the immediate next step
Surgical excision including the cyst stalk
stages.explanation: Surgical excision that removes the cyst along with its stalk and a cuff of the underlying joint capsule has the lowest recurrence rate of the options, because incomplete removal of the stalk is the main driver of recurrence. Needle aspiration is simple and useful diagnostically but has a high recurrence rate. Repeated manual compression is ineffective and not recommended. Corticosteroid injection does not reliably eliminate the cyst and also has high recurrence. Surgery is still reserved for cysts that are persistently symptomatic or functionally limiting.

cases.diagnosis: Dorsal wrist ganglion cyst
pearls: Ganglion cysts are the most common soft-tissue masses of the hand/wrist and classically transilluminate and fluctuate in size.
The dorsal wrist ganglion typically arises from the scapholunate joint; volar ganglia near the radial artery warrant an Allen test before aspiration.
Most are benign and many resolve spontaneously—reassurance and observation are first-line for asymptomatic cysts.
When intervention is needed, surgical excision of the cyst and its stalk has a lower recurrence rate than aspiration.

cases.title: A Finger That Catches and Locks
cases.chiefComplaint: Her ring finger catches when she bends it and sometimes locks in a bent position.
cases.history: A 55-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes presents with several weeks of her right ring finger 'catching' when she makes a fist. The finger snaps or clicks as she straightens it, and twice it locked in flexion, requiring her to pull it straight with the other hand, which was painful. Symptoms are worst in the morning. She denies trauma, redness, fever, or numbness. PMH includes type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 8.2%) on metformin 1000 mg twice daily, and hypothyroidism on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. She has no allergies. She works as a seamstress, involving repetitive gripping. She does not smoke and drinks rarely. Her mother had carpal tunnel syndrome. She is frustrated that the locking interferes with her sewing work and is worried she is developing arthritis. She reports no other joint swelling and no constitutional symptoms.
cases.exam: There is a palpable, tender nodule over the volar aspect of the right ring finger at the level of the metacarpophalangeal joint (A1 pulley) over the distal palmar crease. Active flexion produces a palpable and audible catch, and the finger transiently locks in flexion before snapping into extension. There is no erythema, warmth, or joint effusion at the PIP or DIP joints. Sensation is intact in all digits, and there is no Tinel sign at the wrist. The other fingers move smoothly. Heberden and Bouchard nodes are absent. Grip strength is mildly reduced secondary to discomfort.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Stenosing flexor tenosynovitis at the A1 pulley
Osteoarthritis of the PIP joint
Flexor tendon rupture as the primary diagnosis
Dupuytren contracture as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: The catching, locking, and a tender nodule at the A1 pulley over the distal palmar crease are pathognomonic for trigger finger (stenosing flexor tenosynovitis), in which a thickened tendon/nodule cannot glide smoothly under the A1 pulley—especially common in diabetics. Dupuytren contracture produces painless palmar cords and fixed flexion without triggering. PIP osteoarthritis causes bony Bouchard nodes and joint-line tenderness, not a snapping tendon. Flexor tendon rupture causes loss of active flexion, the opposite of the locking-in-flexion seen here.

stages.label: RISK FACTORS
stages.question: Which feature of her history is the most important predisposing risk factor for this condition?
options: Her occasional alcohol use
Her use of levothyroxine
Her age of 55 in this setting
Her diabetes mellitus
stages.explanation: Diabetes mellitus is a well-established and strong risk factor for trigger finger; diabetics develop it more frequently, often in multiple digits, and respond less reliably to corticosteroid injection. Repetitive gripping and female sex are associated, but diabetes is the dominant modifiable medical risk factor here. Levothyroxine itself is not causative, though hypothyroidism is a minor association. Age in the 40s–60s is typical but is a demographic association rather than a strong independent driver. Alcohol use is not a recognized risk factor.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line treatment for symptomatic trigger finger?
options: Corticosteroid injection into the flexor tendon sheath
Immediate surgical A1 pulley release as initial therapy
Amputation of the affected digit as initial therapy
Six weeks of broad-spectrum oral antibiotics as initial therapy
stages.explanation: First-line treatment for symptomatic trigger finger is a corticosteroid injection into the flexor tendon sheath, often combined with activity modification, NSAIDs, and night splinting; this resolves symptoms in a majority of patients, though diabetics respond somewhat less reliably and may need repeat injection. Surgical A1 pulley release is effective but reserved for cases that fail one to two injections or that present with fixed locking. Antibiotics have no role absent infection. Amputation is never indicated for this benign, treatable condition.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: She returns 3 months later after two corticosteroid injections with recurrent painful locking. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Long-term oral corticosteroids in this setting
Refer for surgical A1 pulley release
A third corticosteroid injection every month indefinitely
MRI of the hand to exclude malignancy
stages.explanation: When trigger finger persists or recurs despite one to two corticosteroid injections, surgical A1 pulley release is the appropriate definitive treatment, providing high success and durable relief. Repeated indefinite injections risk tendon and soft-tissue complications and diminishing returns, particularly in diabetics. Long-term systemic corticosteroids are inappropriate for a localized mechanical problem and would worsen glycemic control. MRI is unnecessary because the diagnosis is clinical and there is no clinical suspicion of a tumor; advanced imaging would only delay definitive care.

cases.diagnosis: Trigger finger (stenosing flexor tenosynovitis)
pearls: Trigger finger is caused by a mismatch between a thickened flexor tendon/nodule and the A1 pulley, producing catching and locking.
A palpable tender nodule at the distal palmar crease that moves with the tendon is the key exam finding.
Diabetes is a strong risk factor; diabetics often have multiple digits involved and respond less reliably to injection.
First-line treatment is corticosteroid injection into the tendon sheath; surgical A1 pulley release is used for refractory cases.

cases.title: A Stiff Shoulder That Won't Loosen Up
cases.chiefComplaint: Progressive left shoulder pain and stiffness making it hard to dress and reach behind her back.
cases.history: A 54-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes presents with 5 months of left shoulder pain and progressive stiffness. It began as a deep ache without injury and has evolved so that she now cannot raise her arm overhead, fasten her bra, or reach into her back pocket. Pain was severe early on, including at night, but has plateaued while stiffness has worsened. She denies numbness, tingling, hand weakness, fevers, or weight loss. PMH includes type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 7.9%) on metformin 1000 mg twice daily and empagliflozin 10 mg daily, plus hypothyroidism on levothyroxine 100 mcg daily. She has no allergies. She is a right-handed office administrator, does not smoke, and drinks rarely. Her sister also had a 'frozen shoulder.' She is concerned that the stiffness is permanent and worried she has a torn rotator cuff that needs surgery. She has tried ibuprofen with limited relief.
cases.exam: The left shoulder has no deformity, erythema, swelling, or atrophy. There is diffuse tenderness without a focal painful arc. The hallmark finding is marked restriction of BOTH active and passive range of motion in multiple planes, most pronounced in external rotation and abduction; passive external rotation is limited to about 10 degrees with a firm end-feel. Rotator cuff strength, when testable within the limited arc, is preserved without a drop-arm sign. Neurovascular exam of the limb is intact, and cervical spine motion does not reproduce the shoulder symptoms.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which exam finding most distinguishes this diagnosis from rotator cuff disease?
options: Tenderness localized to the greater tuberosity only as the primary diagnosis
A positive drop-arm sign as the primary diagnosis
A positive painful arc between 60 and 120 degrees as the primary diagnosis
Loss of BOTH active and passive range of motion, especially external rotation
stages.explanation: Adhesive capsulitis (frozen shoulder) is defined by global loss of BOTH active AND passive range of motion, classically with the greatest restriction in external rotation due to capsular contracture—the key discriminator from rotator cuff disease, where passive motion is typically preserved. A painful arc and isolated greater tuberosity tenderness point to impingement/tendinopathy. A drop-arm sign indicates a full-thickness cuff tear. The combined active-and-passive restriction, particularly of external rotation with a firm end-feel, is the signature of capsular pathology.

stages.label: RISK FACTORS
stages.question: Which of her conditions most increases her risk for this disorder?
options: Diabetes mellitus
Hypertension in this setting
Use of an SGLT2 inhibitor
Right-hand dominance
stages.explanation: Diabetes mellitus is the strongest medical risk factor for adhesive capsulitis; diabetics develop it more often, sometimes bilaterally, and may have a more protracted course. Thyroid disease is also associated, but diabetes is the dominant risk here. Hypertension is not a recognized risk factor. SGLT2 inhibitors do not cause adhesive capsulitis. Hand dominance does not determine which shoulder is affected. Recognizing the diabetes association helps anticipate a longer recovery and the need for optimized glycemic control.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial investigation?
options: Bone scan as the immediate next step
Emergent MRI to look for a labral tear as the immediate next step
Plain radiographs of the shoulder to exclude other causes of stiffness
Diagnostic shoulder arthroscopy as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Adhesive capsulitis is a clinical diagnosis, but plain radiographs are an appropriate first study to exclude mimics such as glenohumeral osteoarthritis, calcific tendinitis, or a locked posterior dislocation that can also restrict motion; films are typically normal in frozen shoulder. MRI is not required initially and is reserved for atypical features or suspected cuff/labral pathology. A bone scan is nonspecific and not indicated. Diagnostic arthroscopy is invasive and not a first-line evaluation for a clinically evident frozen shoulder.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Immediate open capsular release as initial therapy
Prolonged sling immobilization as initial therapy
Patient education on the self-limited course, analgesia/NSAIDs,
Manipulation under anesthesia today as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Initial management is conservative: educate the patient that adhesive capsulitis is typically self-limited over months to a couple of years, provide analgesia/NSAIDs, and begin a gentle range-of-motion/stretching physical therapy program; an intra-articular corticosteroid injection can reduce pain and speed recovery, especially in the painful phase. Manipulation under anesthesia and surgical capsular release are reserved for refractory cases after failed conservative therapy. Sling immobilization worsens stiffness and is contraindicated. Optimizing glycemic control is also advisable given her diabetes.

cases.diagnosis: Adhesive capsulitis (frozen shoulder)
pearls: Frozen shoulder is defined by loss of BOTH active and passive motion, with external rotation typically the most restricted.
Diabetes is the strongest risk factor and predicts a more protracted, sometimes bilateral course.
Radiographs are usually normal and serve mainly to exclude osteoarthritis, calcific tendinitis, or a locked dislocation.
It is generally self-limited; treatment centers on analgesia, physical therapy, and selective intra-articular corticosteroid, reserving surgery for refractory cases.

cases.title: Front-of-Knee Pain on the Stairs
cases.chiefComplaint: Aching pain in the front of both knees, worse on stairs and after sitting.
cases.history: A 22-year-old college student and recreational runner presents with several months of dull, aching pain around the front of both knees, left worse than right. The pain worsens when descending stairs, squatting, and after sitting through long lectures—she has to straighten her legs to relieve it (the 'theater sign'). She recently increased her running mileage to train for a 10K. She denies locking, true giving-way, significant swelling, fever, or a specific injury. PMH is unremarkable; she takes only a combined oral contraceptive and has no allergies. She does not smoke and drinks socially. Family history is noncontributory. She is worried she has 'worn out' her knee cartilage and may need surgery, which is affecting her motivation to keep training. She reports occasional grinding sensations under the kneecap but no instability.
cases.exam: Gait is normal. There is no effusion, warmth, or erythema. Tenderness is reproduced on palpation of the medial patellar facet and with compression of the patella against the femoral trochlea during quadriceps contraction (positive patellofemoral grind). Pain is reproduced with squatting. The patellar tracking shows mild lateral maljustment, and there is tightness of the iliotibial band and hamstrings. The joint lines are non-tender, McMurray test is negative, and Lachman, anterior drawer, and varus/valgus stress tests are stable. Distal neurovascular exam is intact. Hip range of motion is normal.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Patellofemoral pain syndrome
Medial meniscus tear
Anterior cruciate ligament tear
Septic arthritis of the knee
stages.explanation: Anterior knee pain worse with stairs, squatting, and prolonged sitting (theater sign), with a positive patellofemoral compression test and no effusion or instability, is classic for patellofemoral pain syndrome, common in young active women. A meniscus tear typically causes joint-line tenderness, locking, and a positive McMurray—absent here. An ACL tear presents with an acute pop, hemarthrosis, and a positive Lachman, none of which are present. Septic arthritis would cause a hot, swollen, exquisitely painful joint with fever, which is not the picture.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial diagnostic approach?
options: MRI of both knees now as the immediate next step
Arthrocentesis with synovial fluid analysis
A clinical diagnosis
Whole-body bone scan as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Patellofemoral pain syndrome is a clinical diagnosis based on history and exam; routine imaging is not required at first presentation in a young patient without effusion, locking, instability, or red flags. MRI is reserved for atypical features, mechanical symptoms, or failure of conservative management. Arthrocentesis is indicated only when an effusion suggests infection or crystal disease, neither present here. A bone scan is nonspecific and inappropriate. Avoiding unnecessary imaging spares cost and prevents over-treatment of incidental findings.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the cornerstone of first-line treatment?
options: Long-term opioid analgesia as initial therapy
Activity modification plus physical therapy emphasizing quadriceps
Knee immobilization in an extension brace for 6 weeks as initial therapy
Arthroscopic lateral release as initial therapy
stages.explanation: The cornerstone of treatment is conservative: relative activity modification (reducing aggravating load while staying active) plus a physical therapy program targeting quadriceps strengthening—particularly the vastus medialis obliquus—and hip abductor/external-rotator strengthening to improve patellar tracking; NSAIDs and stretching of the IT band/hamstrings are adjuncts. Arthroscopic lateral release is rarely indicated and only after failed comprehensive therapy with specific maltracking. Prolonged immobilization causes quadriceps atrophy and worsens the problem. Long-term opioids are inappropriate for this benign condition.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: She asks about her prognosis and return to running. What is the most accurate counseling?
options: Continued high-mileage running with no changes is the best therapy as the immediate next step
She has irreversible cartilage destruction and must stop running permanently as the immediate next step
The condition usually responds well to a structured rehabilitation program with gradual return to running
She will almost certainly require knee replacement within 5 years as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Patellofemoral pain syndrome generally has a good prognosis and responds to a structured rehabilitation program, with gradual, graded return to running once strength and mechanics improve and load is progressed sensibly. It is not irreversible cartilage destruction, and reassurance is important to maintain adherence. Knee replacement is not a consequence of this condition in a healthy young adult. Continuing high-mileage training without modification or strengthening typically perpetuates symptoms; graded loading—not unmodified overload—is the goal.

cases.diagnosis: Patellofemoral pain syndrome
pearls: Anterior knee pain worse with stairs, squatting, and prolonged sitting (theater sign) is the hallmark of patellofemoral pain syndrome.
It is a clinical diagnosis—imaging is unnecessary at first presentation absent effusion, locking, instability, or red flags.
Treatment centers on quadriceps (VMO) and hip strengthening plus activity modification, not immobilization or surgery.
Prognosis is good with structured rehab and graded return to activity.

cases.title: Heel-Cord Pain in a Weekend Runner
cases.chiefComplaint: Pain and stiffness in the back of the lower leg that worsens with running.
cases.history: A 42-year-old accountant who runs on weekends presents with 6 weeks of pain and stiffness in the back of his right lower leg, a few centimeters above the heel. The pain is worst with the first steps in the morning and at the start of a run, sometimes easing as he warms up before returning afterward. He recently increased his weekend distance and started hill workouts. He denies a sudden 'pop,' an inability to walk, or an acute injury. He has had no fevers and no calf swelling beyond the tender area. PMH is unremarkable. He took a 7-day course of a fluoroquinolone for a urinary infection 2 months ago. He takes no other medications and has no allergies. He does not smoke and drinks 3–4 beers per week. Family history is noncontributory. He is concerned the tendon might rupture and wants to know if he should keep running.
cases.exam: There is focal, fusiform thickening and tenderness of the Achilles tendon approximately 4 cm proximal to its calcaneal insertion (mid-portion). Pain increases with resisted plantarflexion and single-leg heel raises, which he can perform but with discomfort. There is no palpable gap or defect in the tendon. The Thompson (calf-squeeze) test is intact—squeezing the calf produces normal passive plantarflexion. There is no calcaneal posterior-superior tenderness to suggest insertional disease, no warmth or erythema, and no ankle effusion. Distal pulses and sensation are intact.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Mid-portion Achilles tendinopathy
Complete Achilles tendon rupture
Posterior ankle septic arthritis
Gastrocnemius (calf) muscle tear
stages.explanation: Gradual-onset pain with fusiform thickening and tenderness 2–6 cm above the calcaneal insertion, morning stiffness, pain at the start of activity, and an intact Thompson test are classic for mid-portion Achilles tendinopathy, typically from a recent training-load increase. A complete rupture causes a sudden pop, a palpable gap, and a positive Thompson test (no plantarflexion on calf squeeze)—absent here. Septic arthritis would produce a hot, swollen, painful joint with systemic signs. A gastrocnemius tear presents acutely higher in the calf with bruising, not a chronically thickened tendon.

stages.label: HISTORY
stages.question: Which element of his history is the most relevant additional risk factor for tendinopathy and rupture?
options: His occupation as an accountant
His age of 42 in this setting
Recent fluoroquinolone antibiotic use
Moderate beer consumption
stages.explanation: Fluoroquinolone antibiotics are associated with Achilles tendinopathy and tendon rupture, an important and frequently tested risk factor; recent use should heighten caution and prompt counseling to avoid fluoroquinolones going forward and to load the tendon carefully. A sedentary office job and moderate alcohol intake are not significant contributors. Age in the 40s is a mild demographic association but far less specific than the medication exposure. Recognizing this drug effect changes counseling and future prescribing.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first-line management?
options: Relative rest/load management, NSAIDs for analgesia,
Cast immobilization in plantarflexion for 8 weeks as initial therapy
Corticosteroid injection directly into the Achilles tendon as initial therapy
Surgical debridement of the tendon as initial therapy
stages.explanation: First-line management of Achilles tendinopathy is conservative: relative rest with load management, short-term NSAIDs for pain, and—most importantly—a structured eccentric calf-strengthening (heel-drop) program, which has strong evidence for improving mid-portion tendinopathy. Surgery is reserved for refractory cases after months of failed rehabilitation. Corticosteroid injection directly into the Achilles tendon is contraindicated because it increases rupture risk. Prolonged cast immobilization causes deconditioning and is not standard; controlled loading promotes tendon healing.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: What is the most appropriate counseling regarding injections and activity?
options: Avoid corticosteroid injection into the tendon
Resume hill sprints immediately at full intensity as the immediate next step
Stop all activity permanently to avoid any risk as the immediate next step
Get monthly corticosteroid injections to enable immediate full-distance running
stages.explanation: He should be counseled to avoid intratendinous corticosteroid injections (rupture risk) and to avoid fluoroquinolones, while progressing eccentric loading and returning to running gradually as symptoms and capacity allow. Monthly tendon corticosteroid injections are dangerous and inappropriate. Resuming hill sprints at full intensity reproduces the overload that caused the problem and risks rupture. Complete permanent cessation is unnecessary and counterproductive; graded loading is therapeutic, so a sensible, progressive return is the goal.

cases.diagnosis: Mid-portion Achilles tendinopathy
pearls: Mid-portion Achilles tendinopathy presents with fusiform thickening 2–6 cm above the insertion, morning stiffness, and start-up pain after a load increase.
An intact Thompson (calf-squeeze) test and absence of a palpable gap distinguish tendinopathy from rupture.
Fluoroquinolones and corticosteroids increase the risk of tendinopathy and rupture—avoid intratendinous steroid injection.
Eccentric calf-strengthening (heel-drop) exercises are the evidence-based cornerstone of treatment.

cases.title: A Fingertip That Won't Straighten
cases.chiefComplaint: The tip of his finger droops and he cannot straighten it after a ball hit it.
cases.history: A 29-year-old man presents the day after a basketball game in which a pass struck the tip of his right middle finger, jamming it. He felt immediate pain and now notices the very end of the finger droops and he cannot actively straighten the last joint. He can bend it. There was mild swelling and a small amount of pain over the back of the fingertip. He denies an open wound, numbness, or other injuries. PMH is unremarkable; he takes no medications and has no allergies. He works in IT and is right-hand dominant. He does not smoke and drinks socially. Family history is noncontributory. He initially taped the finger to the adjacent one and took ibuprofen. He is concerned about permanent deformity and asks whether it needs surgery, noting he must type for work.
cases.exam: The distal interphalangeal (DIP) joint of the right middle finger rests in approximately 35 degrees of flexion (a droop). The patient cannot actively extend the DIP joint, but passive extension is full and painless. There is mild dorsal swelling and tenderness over the DIP joint without an open wound, and the skin is intact. Active and passive flexion of the DIP are preserved, and proximal interphalangeal and metacarpophalangeal joint motion is normal. Sensation and capillary refill of the fingertip are intact. There is no volar swelling or DIP joint instability.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Jersey finger (flexor digitorum profundus avulsion)
Mallet finger
DIP joint dislocation as the primary diagnosis
Boutonnière deformity as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Inability to actively extend the DIP with a resting flexion droop after an axial/forced-flexion blow to the fingertip, with intact passive extension, is classic mallet finger—disruption of the terminal extensor tendon (with or without a dorsal avulsion fracture). Jersey finger is loss of active DIP flexion from FDP avulsion, the opposite deficit. Boutonnière deformity involves PIP flexion with DIP hyperextension from central slip injury. A DIP dislocation would show gross joint deformity and abnormal alignment, not an isolated extension lag with normal passive motion.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial investigation?
options: Plain radiograph of the finger to assess for an avulsion fracture
MRI of the finger as the immediate next step
No imaging is ever needed as the immediate next step
Ultrasound of the flexor tendon as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: A plain radiograph (including a true lateral) is the appropriate initial study to determine whether the mallet injury is purely tendinous (soft-tissue) or involves a dorsal avulsion fracture, and to assess for volar subluxation of the distal phalanx—findings that influence whether surgical referral is needed. MRI is unnecessary for this clinical diagnosis. Ultrasound of the flexor tendon evaluates the wrong structure. Saying imaging is never needed is incorrect because bony involvement and subluxation change management.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Lateral radiograph of the middle finger: extension lag at the DIP joint
Small dorsal avulsion fragment at the base of the distal phalanx involving <30% of the articular surface
No volar subluxation of the distal phalanx
DIP joint otherwise congruent; no other fracture

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given a small avulsion (<30% articular surface) without subluxation, what is the most appropriate management?
options: Immediate open reduction and internal fixation as monotherapy
Splint the PIP joint while leaving the DIP free as monotherapy
Buddy taping with immediate return to full DIP motion
Continuous splinting of the DIP in full extension for 6–8 weeks
stages.explanation: Most mallet fingers—including those with a small avulsion fragment (<~30% of the joint) and no subluxation—are treated nonoperatively with continuous splinting of the DIP joint in full extension for 6–8 weeks (often longer if a fracture is present), keeping the PIP free to move. Splinting the PIP is wrong; the PIP must stay mobile and the DIP immobilized. Surgery is reserved for large fragments (often >30–50%) or joint subluxation. Buddy taping with free DIP motion does not maintain extension and risks a persistent droop; uninterrupted extension splinting is essential.

stages.label: COUNSELING
stages.question: What is the most important counseling point about the splinting regimen?
options: The DIP must remain in continuous extension
He may remove the splint at night since the finger rests anyway in this setting
Splinting beyond two weeks is unnecessary as the immediate next step
He should actively flex the DIP several times daily to prevent stiffness
stages.explanation: Success of nonoperative mallet finger treatment depends on uninterrupted DIP extension; if the joint is allowed to drop into flexion at any point—including splint changes—the healing process must essentially restart, so patients are taught to support the fingertip in extension whenever the splint is off. Removing the splint at night allows inadvertent flexion and is incorrect. Actively flexing the DIP defeats the purpose and would perpetuate the deformity. Two weeks is insufficient; 6–8 weeks (or more with a fracture) of continuous extension is required.

cases.diagnosis: Mallet finger (terminal extensor tendon avulsion)
pearls: Mallet finger is loss of active DIP extension after a forced-flexion blow; passive extension remains full.
Obtain a lateral radiograph to identify an avulsion fracture and exclude volar subluxation, which would prompt surgical referral.
Most cases, including small avulsions without subluxation, are treated with continuous DIP extension splinting for 6–8 weeks.
Any interruption of DIP extension during splinting restarts healing—patient education on uninterrupted splinting is critical.

cases.title: A Swollen Elbow Tip in a Plumber
cases.chiefComplaint: A soft, swollen lump on the point of his elbow that appeared over a week.
cases.history: A 47-year-old plumber presents with a 10-day history of a soft swelling over the tip of his right elbow. He frequently leans on his elbows while working under sinks. The swelling enlarged gradually and is mildly uncomfortable but not severely painful. He denies fever, chills, or a break in the skin, though he recalls minor scrapes on that elbow in the past. He has full use of the arm. PMH includes gout (last flare 1 year ago) and hypertension on amlodipine 5 mg daily. He has no allergies. He drinks 4–5 beers most nights and does not smoke. Family history is noncontributory. He is worried it might be infected and need antibiotics, and he wants to keep working. He has not had a fever and feels otherwise well.
cases.exam: Over the olecranon there is a well-circumscribed, fluctuant, fluid-filled swelling roughly 4 cm in diameter. The overlying skin is intact with no break, and there is no significant erythema, marked warmth, or surrounding cellulitis. The swelling is mildly tender. Elbow flexion and extension are full; passive and active range of motion do not significantly increase the pain (no pain on motion at the joint itself). Distal neurovascular exam is intact. There is no axillary or epitrochlear lymphadenopathy. The contralateral elbow is normal.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Septic arthritis of the elbow
Olecranon bursitis
Fracture of the olecranon
Rheumatoid synovitis of the elbow joint
stages.explanation: A fluctuant, fluid-filled swelling localized over the olecranon with full, relatively painless elbow motion is characteristic of olecranon bursitis, often from repetitive pressure/microtrauma ('plumber's/student's elbow'). Septic arthritis of the elbow JOINT causes severe pain on any joint motion and a hot effusion within the joint—not an isolated superficial bursal swelling with preserved motion. Rheumatoid synovitis would involve the joint with systemic features and other joints. An olecranon fracture follows trauma and produces bony tenderness and inability to extend against gravity, not a soft fluctuant mass.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature is most important to assess to distinguish septic from aseptic bursitis?
options: The exact diameter of the swelling as the first step today
Whether the patient is right- or left-handed as the first step today
Signs of infection
The color of the overlying skin hair as the first step today
stages.explanation: Distinguishing septic from aseptic olecranon bursitis hinges on signs of infection: significant warmth, spreading erythema/cellulitis, fever, marked tenderness, or a portal of entry (skin breach); when uncertain, bursal aspiration with Gram stain, culture, cell count, and crystal analysis differentiates infection from inflammatory or crystal-induced bursitis. The exact diameter alone does not establish infection. Handedness and skin hair color are irrelevant. Because septic bursitis requires drainage and antibiotics whereas aseptic bursitis is managed conservatively, this distinction directly drives management.

stages.label: ASPIRATION & LABS
stages.content:
Bursal fluid aspirate: slightly cloudy, straw-colored
WBC (bursal fluid): 900 /µL (low)
Gram stain: no organisms seen
Crystals: none identified under polarized light
Blood: WBC 7.1 ×10³/µL, Temp 36.9 °C, no leukocytosis
Follow-up fluid culture (final): no growth

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The aspirate and exam indicate aseptic (non-infected) olecranon bursitis. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Urgent surgical bursectomy as initial therapy
Begin allopurinol and colchicine for an acute crystal flare as initial therapy
Conservative care: activity/pressure modification, elbow padding, NSAIDs,
Start IV vancomycin and admit for septic bursitis as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Aseptic olecranon bursitis (no infection signs, low bursal WBC, negative Gram stain/culture, no crystals) is managed conservatively: avoid leaning on the elbow, use padding, take NSAIDs for discomfort, apply compression, and provide reassurance; aspiration may be repeated if recurrent. IV vancomycin and admission are for confirmed septic bursitis, which this is not. Surgical bursectomy is reserved for chronic, refractory, or complicated cases. Starting gout pharmacotherapy is unwarranted because no crystals were found and there is no acute crystal arthropathy on this analysis.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: What is the most appropriate return-precaution counseling at discharge?
options: No follow-up is needed regardless of symptoms with appropriate follow-up
Take prophylactic antibiotics for two weeks to be safe with appropriate follow-up
Return immediately if he develops fever, spreading redness, increasing warmth, or worsening pain suggesting infection
Apply heat continuously and resume leaning on the elbow to drain it with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Because aseptic bursitis can become secondarily infected, the patient should be told to return promptly if he develops fever, spreading erythema, increasing warmth, or worsening pain—red flags for septic bursitis requiring drainage and antibiotics. Saying no follow-up is ever needed ignores this risk. Continuously leaning on the elbow perpetuates the microtrauma that caused the bursitis and should be avoided. Prophylactic antibiotics are not indicated for aseptic bursitis and contribute to resistance and side effects without benefit.

cases.diagnosis: Aseptic olecranon bursitis
pearls: Olecranon bursitis is a superficial fluctuant swelling with preserved, relatively painless elbow motion—unlike septic arthritis, which causes severe pain on joint motion.
Distinguish septic from aseptic bursitis by warmth, spreading erythema, fever, or a skin breach; aspirate for Gram stain, culture, cell count, and crystals when uncertain.
Aseptic bursitis is managed conservatively with padding, pressure avoidance, NSAIDs, and compression.
Counsel on return precautions, as aseptic bursitis can become secondarily infected.

cases.title: Neck Pain Shooting to the Middle Finger
cases.chiefComplaint: Neck pain radiating down the arm to the middle finger with tingling.
cases.history: A 49-year-old office manager presents with 3 weeks of right-sided neck pain that radiates down the back of his arm and forearm into his middle finger. He describes a burning, electric quality with intermittent tingling in the middle finger. The pain worsens when he tilts and turns his head toward the right and improves when he rests his hand on top of his head. There was no acute trauma, though he recalls sleeping awkwardly before onset. He denies bowel or bladder dysfunction, gait imbalance, bilateral symptoms, fever, or weight loss. PMH includes hypertension on hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily. He has no allergies. He does desk work, does not smoke, and drinks socially. Family history is noncontributory. He has tried acetaminophen with partial relief and is worried he has a 'pinched nerve' that might need surgery. He reports no weakness in the legs and no clumsiness.
cases.exam: The cervical paraspinal muscles are tender on the right with reduced range of motion. The Spurling maneuver—extension, ipsilateral rotation, and axial compression—reproduces the radiating pain into the arm and middle finger. The shoulder abduction (relief) sign is present. Motor exam shows mild weakness of elbow extension (triceps) and wrist flexion on the right; the triceps reflex is diminished. Sensation is reduced over the middle finger. Lower-extremity strength, reflexes, and gait are normal, with no Hoffman or Babinski sign and no clonus. Distal pulses are intact, and there is no Tinel sign at the wrist or elbow.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on the pain distribution and exam, which nerve root is most likely involved?
options: C6 as the primary diagnosis
C5 as the primary diagnosis
C7
C8 as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: C7 radiculopathy classically causes pain/sensory change radiating to the middle finger, triceps and wrist-flexor weakness, and a diminished triceps reflex—precisely this exam—and is the most commonly affected cervical root. C5 affects the deltoid/shoulder and biceps reflex. C6 affects the thumb/index finger, biceps/wrist extensors, and the brachioradialis reflex. C8 affects the little finger and intrinsic hand muscles/finger flexors. Matching dermatome, myotome, and reflex pinpoints the level and explains the middle-finger localization.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the significance of the positive Spurling and shoulder-abduction signs?
options: They indicate a rotator cuff tear as the first step today
They are signs of cervical myelopathy requiring emergent surgery
They confirm carpal tunnel syndrome in this clinical context
They support cervical nerve root compression/radiculopathy
stages.explanation: The Spurling test (extension, ipsilateral rotation, axial load reproducing radicular pain) and the shoulder-abduction relief sign (relief when the hand is placed on the head, off-loading the root) both support cervical nerve root compression—i.e., radiculopathy. They are not tests for carpal tunnel (Tinel/Phalen at the wrist) or rotator cuff disease (Neer/Hawkins). They are not myelopathy signs; myelopathy is suggested by Hoffman/Babinski signs, hyperreflexia, clonus, gait disturbance, and bowel/bladder dysfunction—none present here.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: In the absence of red flags, what is the most appropriate initial management approach?
options: Immediate cervical fusion as the immediate next step
Conservative management
Long-course oral antibiotics as the immediate next step
Emergent cervical MRI and neurosurgical consultation as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Most cervical radiculopathy without red flags (no myelopathy, no severe/progressive deficit, no signs of infection or malignancy) improves with conservative care: NSAIDs/analgesia, activity modification, and physical therapy, with MRI reserved for symptoms that persist beyond several weeks, progress, or develop concerning features. Emergent MRI and neurosurgical consult are warranted for red flags such as myelopathy or major motor deficit, absent here. Immediate fusion is premature without a failed conservative trial and clear surgical indication. Antibiotics have no role in mechanical/degenerative radiculopathy.

stages.label: RED FLAGS
stages.question: Which new finding on follow-up would most warrant urgent MRI and specialist referral?
options: Development of gait imbalance, hyperreflexia with Hoffman/Babinski signs, or bowel/bladder dysfunction
Continued neck stiffness in the morning as the immediate next step
Mild persistent middle-finger tingling as the immediate next step
Temporary worsening of pain after physical therapy as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Signs of cervical myelopathy—gait imbalance, hyperreflexia, positive Hoffman/Babinski signs, clonus, or bowel/bladder dysfunction—indicate spinal cord involvement and warrant urgent MRI and specialist referral, as these can require timely surgical decompression. Mild persistent tingling and morning stiffness are common in uncomplicated radiculopathy and do not by themselves mandate urgent imaging. A transient post-therapy pain flare is expected and not a red flag. Recognizing the transition from radiculopathy to myelopathy is the key safety check during conservative management.

cases.diagnosis: C7 cervical radiculopathy
pearls: C7 is the most commonly affected cervical root: pain to the middle finger, triceps/wrist-flexor weakness, and a diminished triceps reflex.
A positive Spurling test and shoulder-abduction relief sign support nerve root compression.
Most radiculopathy without red flags improves with conservative care; reserve MRI for persistent, progressive, or red-flag presentations.
Watch for myelopathy (Hoffman/Babinski, hyperreflexia, gait or bladder changes)—these mandate urgent imaging and referral.

cases.title: Low Back Pain Shooting Down the Leg
cases.chiefComplaint: Sharp low back pain radiating down the back of my right leg for the past two weeks.
cases.history: A 40-year-old warehouse worker presents with two weeks of low back pain that now radiates down the posterior right thigh and calf into the sole and lateral foot. Symptoms began the morning after he lifted a heavy box with a twisting motion. The pain is sharp and electric, worse with sitting, coughing, sneezing, and bending forward, and somewhat relieved by lying down. He reports tingling along the outer foot but denies saddle anesthesia, urinary retention, incontinence, or fever. There is no weight loss or night pain. PMH is unremarkable. He takes only ibuprofen 400 mg as needed with modest relief. No allergies. He smokes half a pack daily, drinks socially, and lifts heavy loads at work daily. Family history is noncontributory. He has not had prior back injury or surgery and has never had imaging of his spine. He is otherwise healthy and active, with no recent infections or IV drug use.
cases.exam: Alert, ambulating with an antalgic gait, favoring the right leg. Lumbar paraspinal tenderness without midline spinal tenderness or step-off. Straight-leg raise on the right reproduces radiating pain at 40 degrees; crossed straight-leg raise is negative. Sensation is decreased over the lateral right foot and sole. Plantarflexion (toe-walk) is weak on the right; dorsiflexion is intact. Right Achilles reflex is diminished compared with the left; patellar reflexes are symmetric. No saddle anesthesia, normal rectal tone, no clonus, and downgoing toes bilaterally. Distal pulses are intact.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Based on the history and exam, which nerve root is most likely involved?
options: L3 as the first step today
S1
L5 as the first step today
L4 as the first step today
stages.explanation: S1 is correct. Pain radiating down the posterior leg to the sole and lateral foot, decreased sensation over the lateral foot, weak plantarflexion, and a diminished Achilles reflex are the classic S1 signature, typically from an L5-S1 disc herniation. L3 (anterior thigh pain, weak knee extension, reduced patellar reflex) is wrong because reflexes and quadriceps are intact. L4 (medial leg/shin sensory loss, weak dorsiflexion at the ankle, reduced patellar reflex) is wrong since dorsiflexion and patellar reflex are preserved. L5 (dorsal foot/great toe sensation, weak great-toe extension and foot dorsiflexion, no consistent reflex change) is wrong because his deficit is plantarflexion and the Achilles reflex, not dorsiflexion.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step in evaluation for this patient?
options: Order an urgent MRI of the lumbar spine
Refer for emergent surgical decompression
No imaging now; treat conservatively and reassess
Order lumbar spine radiographs to rule out fracture
stages.explanation: No imaging now with conservative management is correct. In acute radiculopathy without red flags (no trauma, fever, cancer, weight loss, saddle anesthesia, or bladder/bowel dysfunction), guidelines recommend deferring imaging for 4-6 weeks because most herniations improve and early imaging does not change outcomes. Urgent MRI is reserved for red flags such as cauda equina syndrome or progressive/severe neurologic deficit, none present here. Plain radiographs do not visualize discs or nerve roots and add little when fracture is not suspected. Emergent surgical decompression is unwarranted without cauda equina or a major progressive deficit; his single mildly weak myotome with intact bladder function does not meet that threshold.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which initial management strategy is most appropriate?
options: NSAIDs, activity as tolerated,
A 6-day oral corticosteroid taper as monotherapy
Immediate epidural steroid injection
Strict bed rest for one week as initial therapy
stages.explanation: NSAIDs with continued activity as tolerated and physical therapy is the evidence-based first-line approach for acute lumbar radiculopathy. Staying active speeds recovery, and most patients improve within 6 weeks. Strict bed rest is harmful, prolongs recovery, and is no longer recommended. Oral corticosteroid tapers provide little durable benefit for sciatica and carry side effects, so they are not first line. Epidural steroid injection may offer short-term relief for refractory radicular pain but is not an initial treatment; it is considered after conservative measures fail and is best guided by imaging confirming the level.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Symptoms persisted beyond 6 weeks; MRI lumbar spine obtained:
Right paracentral disc herniation at L5-S1 contacting the traversing right S1 nerve root
Mild loss of disc height at L5-S1
No central canal stenosis; no cauda equina compression
No fracture, infection, or bony destructive lesion
Normal vertebral marrow signal

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which new finding at follow-up would most urgently warrant emergent MRI and surgical consultation?
options: Worsening pain only when sitting
Persistent S1 pain unchanged at 3 weeks
New urinary retention with saddle numbness
Mild residual numbness of the lateral foot
stages.explanation: New urinary retention with saddle anesthesia signals cauda equina syndrome, a surgical emergency requiring emergent MRI and immediate decompression to prevent permanent bladder, bowel, and sexual dysfunction. Persistent unchanged S1 pain at 3 weeks is common and still within the window for continued conservative care or elective imaging if it lingers. Pain worse with sitting is typical of discogenic radiculopathy and not alarming. Mild residual lateral-foot numbness often resolves slowly and does not indicate an emergency. The teaching point: monitor for cauda equina red flags (saddle anesthesia, urinary retention/incontinence, bilateral leg symptoms, fecal incontinence) at every visit.

cases.diagnosis: Lumbar disc herniation (L5-S1) with S1 radiculopathy (sciatica)
pearls: S1 radiculopathy: posterior leg pain to the lateral foot/sole, weak plantarflexion, diminished Achilles reflex; L5-S1 is the most common herniation level.
In acute radiculopathy without red flags, defer imaging 4-6 weeks; most herniations resolve with conservative care.
Crossed straight-leg raise (pain in the affected leg when raising the unaffected leg) is less sensitive but more specific for herniation.
Cauda equina syndrome (saddle anesthesia, urinary retention, bilateral deficits) demands emergent MRI and surgical decompression.

cases.title: Twisted Ankle After a Stumble
cases.chiefComplaint: I rolled my ankle stepping off a curb and now I can't put any weight on it.
cases.history: A 55-year-old woman presents to the ED after stepping off a curb, rolling her right ankle, and falling. She heard a crack and felt immediate severe pain. She has been unable to bear weight since the injury two hours ago. The ankle is markedly swollen and deformed. She denies numbness or color change in the foot but reports throbbing pain rated 8/10. PMH includes hypertension and type 2 diabetes, well controlled. Medications are lisinopril 20 mg daily and metformin 1000 mg twice daily. She is postmenopausal and takes no hormone therapy. No known drug allergies. She drinks wine occasionally and does not smoke. Family history includes osteoporosis in her mother. She has no prior ankle injuries or surgeries. She works as a teacher and is normally fully ambulatory without aids. Last tetanus booster was more than 10 years ago; the skin over the ankle is intact.
cases.exam: Right ankle is grossly swollen with an obvious deformity and ecchymosis over both malleoli. There is tenderness directly over the lateral malleolus and the medial malleolus. The skin is intact with no open wound or tenting. She cannot bear weight and cannot take four steps. Dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses are palpable and symmetric; capillary refill is brisk. Sensation is intact over all toes and the dorsum of the foot, and toe flexion/extension are intact. There is no tenderness at the proximal fibula, midfoot, or base of the fifth metatarsal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: According to the Ottawa Ankle Rules, why does this patient require ankle radiographs?
options: She is over age 50 as the first step today
She has isolated lateral soft-tissue swelling only
She has malleolar tenderness
She has diabetes and hypertension in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Bony tenderness at the posterior edge or tip of either malleolus AND inability to bear weight for four steps both at the time of injury and in the ED each independently mandate ankle radiographs under the Ottawa Ankle Rules. This patient has both, making imaging clearly indicated. Isolated soft-tissue swelling without bony tenderness does not require films. Age over 50 is not part of the ankle rule (it is part of some other decision rules but not here). Comorbidities like diabetes and hypertension are not criteria for the Ottawa rules. The rules are highly sensitive for clinically significant fractures and reduce unnecessary imaging.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Right ankle radiographs (AP, lateral, mortise):
Transverse fracture of the lateral malleolus at the level of the joint line
Oblique fracture of the medial malleolus
Medial clear space widened to 6 mm
Talar shift laterally within the mortise
No posterior malleolus fracture; no proximal fibula fracture

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: How is this fracture pattern best characterized?
options: Isolated medial malleolus avulsion
Stable isolated lateral malleolus fracture
Unstable bimalleolar fracture with talar shift
Maisonneuve fracture as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: This is an unstable bimalleolar fracture: both the medial and lateral malleoli are fractured, and the widened medial clear space with lateral talar shift confirms loss of mortise integrity. Disruption of two of the three malleolar/ligamentous supports makes the ankle unstable. An isolated lateral malleolus fracture would be stable only if the medial side (malleolus and deltoid ligament) is intact, which is not the case here. A Maisonneuve fracture involves a proximal fibula fracture with syndesmotic and medial injury, but the proximal fibula is intact. An isolated medial malleolus avulsion does not explain the lateral fracture or talar shift.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate definitive management?
options: Closed reduction and short-leg cast as definitive care
Compression wrap and crutches with outpatient follow-up
Walking boot and immediate weight-bearing as initial therapy
Open reduction and internal fixation (ORIF)
stages.explanation: ORIF is the standard of care for unstable bimalleolar fractures with talar displacement. Restoring an anatomic mortise is essential; even small residual talar shift dramatically increases contact stress and the risk of post-traumatic arthritis, so surgical fixation is required. A walking boot with weight-bearing is inappropriate for an unstable, displaced fracture. Closed reduction and casting alone cannot reliably maintain anatomic alignment of a bimalleolar injury with talar shift and is not definitive. A compression wrap with crutches is treatment for a sprain, not an unstable fracture. Pre-operatively the ankle should be reduced and splinted with neurovascular checks while awaiting surgery.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: While awaiting surgery, which finding should prompt the most urgent intervention?
options: Mild persistent swelling as the leading concern
Bruising spreading to the heel as the leading concern
Pain controlled with oral analgesics
Tense fracture blisters
stages.explanation: A dusky, pulseless foot with tense skin signals vascular compromise or critical skin/soft-tissue tension, often from a displaced fracture tenting the skin or a malreduction; this requires urgent reduction and reassessment to restore perfusion and prevent skin necrosis or limb-threatening ischemia. Mild swelling is expected after an ankle fracture. Pain controlled with oral analgesics is reassuring. Bruising tracking to the heel reflects normal dependent spread of hematoma. The key teaching point is that displaced ankle fractures can compromise the skin and neurovascular structures, so prompt reduction and serial neurovascular checks are mandatory.

cases.diagnosis: Unstable bimalleolar ankle fracture with talar shift requiring ORIF
pearls: Ottawa Ankle Rules: image if there is bony tenderness at either malleolus or inability to bear weight 4 steps both at injury and in the ED.
Bimalleolar fractures are unstable; a widened medial clear space and talar shift confirm loss of mortise integrity.
Unstable, displaced ankle fractures need ORIF to restore an anatomic mortise and prevent post-traumatic arthritis.
Always document neurovascular status and inspect skin for tenting/blisters; reduce and splint promptly while awaiting surgery.

cases.title: Swollen Hand After Punching a Wall
cases.chiefComplaint: I punched a wall last night and now the side of my hand is swollen and hurts.
cases.history: A 21-year-old man presents after punching a wall in anger during an argument the previous night. He felt immediate pain over the small-finger side of his hand, which is now swollen and bruised. He has difficulty making a tight fist. He denies any laceration or cut to the knuckles and specifically denies that his fist struck another person's mouth or teeth. There is no numbness. PMH is unremarkable; he takes no medications and has no allergies. He drinks alcohol on weekends and admits to drinking heavily the night of the injury; he does not smoke or use drugs. He is right-hand dominant and works as a barista. Family history is noncontributory. Tetanus immunization is up to date. He has no prior hand injuries and reports no prior episodes of impulsive aggression, though he says he was very angry last night.
cases.exam: Right hand with swelling and ecchymosis over the dorsal ulnar aspect at the fifth metacarpal. There is tenderness and a depressed, less prominent fifth knuckle with loss of the normal MCP contour. The overlying skin is intact with no laceration, abrasion, or tooth-mark wound over the knuckles. Pain is reproduced with axial loading of the small finger. When the patient makes a fist, the small finger crosses over (overlaps) the ring finger, indicating rotational malalignment. Capillary refill is brisk; sensation is intact in all digits; no signs of flexor tenosynovitis.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the mechanism and findings, which injury is most likely?
options: Distal radius fracture
Bennett fracture of the thumb
Fifth metacarpal neck fracture
Scaphoid fracture as the first step today
stages.explanation: A fifth metacarpal neck fracture (boxer's fracture) is the classic injury from punching with a closed fist; the axial force fractures the metacarpal neck, producing dorsal swelling, a depressed knuckle, and apex-dorsal angulation. A scaphoid fracture causes anatomic-snuffbox tenderness after a fall on an outstretched hand, not knuckle deformity from a punch. A Bennett fracture is an intra-articular fracture-dislocation at the thumb base, inconsistent with small-finger findings. A distal radius fracture presents with wrist deformity and tenderness, not isolated fifth-knuckle depression. The depressed fifth knuckle and ulnar-sided dorsal swelling point clearly to a boxer's fracture.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which examination finding is most important to assess before treatment because it changes management?
options: Degree of dorsal swelling in this setting
Presence of nail-bed bruising in this setting
Rotational alignment of the small finger
Skin temperature of the hand in this setting
stages.explanation: Rotational alignment is critical: malrotation causes the finger to scissor over its neighbor during flexion and is poorly tolerated, so any rotational deformity mandates reduction and often surgical fixation regardless of angulation. This patient's small finger overlaps the ring finger on fist-making, indicating malrotation that must be corrected. Dorsal swelling is expected and does not by itself dictate management. Skin temperature is a perfusion check but is normal here and not the deciding factor. Nail-bed bruising is incidental. Assessing rotation (by viewing the planes of the fingernails and the cascade on flexion) is essential because even small rotational errors produce significant functional impairment.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Why is it important that his fist did NOT strike another person's mouth in this case?
options: It determines whether a cast is required as the primary diagnosis
A 'fight bite' over the MCP would risk septic arthritis
It changes the radiographic views needed as the primary diagnosis
It rules out a scaphoid fracture as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: A laceration over the MCP from striking teeth is a 'fight bite' (clenched-fist injury) that inoculates oral flora, including Eikenella corrodens, into the joint and extensor tendon, risking septic arthritis and deep-space infection; such wounds require irrigation, debridement/washout, and antibiotics (typically amoxicillin-clavulanate) and should never be closed primarily. Confirming the absence of a knuckle laceration is therefore essential. It does not affect the likelihood of a scaphoid fracture, the radiographic views obtained, or whether casting is needed. The history and intact, unbroken skin over the knuckles reassuringly exclude a fight bite in this patient.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Right hand radiographs (PA, oblique, lateral):
Fracture through the neck of the fifth metacarpal
Apex-dorsal angulation of approximately 25 degrees on the lateral view
No intra-articular extension into the MCP joint
No associated carpal or other metacarpal fracture
No radiographic foreign body

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: His radiograph shows 25 degrees of apex-dorsal angulation with NO rotational deformity after re-examination. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Buddy taping alone with no immobilization
Open reduction and internal fixation
Ulnar gutter splint and orthopedic follow-up
Long-arm cast for 6 weeks as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Closed reduction if needed and immobilization in an ulnar gutter splint with outpatient orthopedic follow-up is appropriate for an isolated boxer's fracture without malrotation; the fifth metacarpal neck tolerates substantial angulation (commonly up to about 40-70 degrees is accepted given the mobile CMC joint), so 25 degrees without rotation can be managed nonoperatively. ORIF is reserved for malrotation, excessive angulation, or intra-articular involvement, none of which apply. Buddy taping alone provides inadequate support for a neck fracture. A long-arm cast is excessive immobilization; an ulnar gutter splint placing the MCP in slight flexion is the standard. Rotational deformity, not this degree of angulation, would mandate surgery.

cases.diagnosis: Fifth metacarpal neck fracture (boxer's fracture) without malrotation
pearls: Always assess rotational alignment (nail-plane and finger cascade) in metacarpal fractures; any scissoring mandates reduction/fixation.
The fifth metacarpal neck tolerates significant angulation; rotation and intra-articular extension drive the need for surgery, not angle alone.
A laceration over the MCP after a punch is a fight bite: irrigate, give amoxicillin-clavulanate, do not close primarily, consider washout.
Treat isolated boxer's fractures without malrotation in an ulnar gutter splint with the MCP slightly flexed and arrange orthopedic follow-up.

cases.title: Severe Leg Pain After a Motorbike Crash
cases.chiefComplaint: My lower leg is killing me after I crashed my motorcycle.
cases.history: A 27-year-old man is brought to the ED after a motorcycle collision in which his lower leg struck the curb at high speed. He has severe pain and deformity of the left shin and cannot bear weight. He denies head injury or loss of consciousness and was wearing a helmet. He reports the pain is severe and worsening, out of proportion to what he expected, and increasingly affects the entire calf. There is no open wound visible. PMH is unremarkable. He takes no medications and has no allergies. He is a nonsmoker and drinks socially; he denies drug use. He works in construction. Family history is noncontributory. Tetanus is up to date. On arrival he is in obvious distress, holding his leg still. He denies abdominal, chest, or pelvic pain, and primary survey is otherwise unremarkable with a normal secondary trauma survey except for the leg.
cases.exam: Left lower leg with obvious mid-shaft deformity, swelling, and tense, firm compartments to palpation. Skin is intact (closed injury). There is exquisite tenderness over the mid-tibia. Passive dorsiflexion and plantarflexion of the toes elicit severe, disproportionate pain. Sensation is beginning to feel diminished in the first web space. Dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses are still palpable but require firm palpation. Capillary refill is 3 seconds. The knee and ankle are stable to inspection. Pain is rated 10/10 and not relieved by initial IV opioids.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial imaging for the suspected fracture?
options: Bone scan as the first step today
Plain radiographs of the tibia/fibula including the knee
MRI of the lower leg as the first step today
Ultrasound of the calf as the first step today
stages.explanation: Plain radiographs of the tibia and fibula that include the joints above and below (knee and ankle) are the correct initial imaging; they confirm the fracture, characterize displacement and comminution, and detect associated proximal/distal injuries. Imaging the adjacent joints is a fundamental rule in long-bone trauma to avoid missing a second fracture or dislocation. MRI is not used acutely for diagnosing a shaft fracture and delays care. Ultrasound is not the test for a suspected tibial fracture. A bone scan is for occult or stress fractures and chronic processes, not acute high-energy trauma. Radiographs are fast, available, and definitive for diagnosis here.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Left tibia/fibula radiographs (including knee and ankle):
Transverse, mildly comminuted fracture of the mid-tibial shaft with 100% displacement
Associated fibular shaft fracture
No intra-articular extension at the knee or ankle
Soft-tissue swelling throughout the leg
No radiographic foreign body or air in tissues

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: His pain is severe, worsening, out of proportion, and aggravated by passive toe stretch with tense compartments. What complication must be diagnosed urgently?
options: Septic arthritis of the ankle
Acute compartment syndrome
Fat embolism syndrome
Deep vein thrombosis
stages.explanation: Acute compartment syndrome is the urgent diagnosis. The hallmark early sign is pain out of proportion to injury, worsened by passive stretch of the muscles in the compartment, with tense compartments; paresthesias follow, while pulselessness and pallor are late and unreliable. Tibial shaft fractures are the most common cause. DVT does not cause disproportionate pain on passive stretch acutely after a fracture and would not explain tense compartments. Fat embolism causes hypoxia, confusion, and petechiae days after long-bone fractures, not focal leg findings. Septic arthritis is an infection over days, inconsistent with this acute post-traumatic picture. Recognizing the '6 Ps' early (especially pain and paresthesia) is lifesaving for the limb.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Elevate the leg above the heart and observe as monotherapy
Measure compartment pressures
Administer therapeutic anticoagulation as initial therapy
Apply ice and a tight compression wrap as initial therapy
stages.explanation: When compartment syndrome is suspected clinically, the limb is threatened, and the definitive treatment is emergent fasciotomy; compartment pressure measurement (especially a delta pressure of diastolic minus compartment pressure of 30 mmHg or less) supports the diagnosis but should not delay surgery in a clear clinical case. Elevation above the heart can worsen ischemia by lowering perfusion pressure; the limb should be kept at heart level, not elevated. Ice and tight wraps further compromise perfusion and are contraindicated. Anticoagulation treats thrombosis, not compartment syndrome, and would be harmful before surgery. Time is critical: irreversible muscle and nerve damage occurs within hours, so emergent fasciotomy and fracture stabilization are required.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After fasciotomy, what is the definitive fixation of choice for this displaced tibial shaft fracture?
options: Long-leg cast based on current risk
External fixation as permanent treatment
Functional bracing only
Intramedullary nailing
stages.explanation: Intramedullary nailing is the definitive treatment of choice for displaced tibial shaft fractures in adults, providing stable fixation, early weight-bearing, and high union rates. A long-leg cast cannot adequately control a 100%-displaced unstable shaft fracture and is unsuitable, particularly after fasciotomy with open wounds. External fixation is useful as a temporizing measure (for example in severe soft-tissue injury or as part of damage control) but is generally not the permanent solution here. Functional bracing is reserved for select stable, minimally displaced fractures, not a fully displaced one. Definitive fixation also helps protect the limb and is coordinated with soft-tissue/fasciotomy wound management.

cases.diagnosis: Displaced tibial shaft fracture complicated by acute compartment syndrome
pearls: Tibial shaft fractures are the most common cause of acute compartment syndrome; maintain high vigilance.
Earliest reliable signs are pain out of proportion and pain on passive stretch; pulselessness/pallor are late and unreliable.
Suspected compartment syndrome is a clinical diagnosis warranting emergent fasciotomy; keep the limb at heart level, never elevated or iced.
Image the joints above and below any long-bone fracture; intramedullary nailing is the definitive fixation for displaced tibial shaft fractures.

cases.title: Knee That Locks After a Pivot
cases.chiefComplaint: My knee catches and locks up, and the inside hurts after I twisted it playing soccer.
cases.history: A 33-year-old recreational soccer player presents with three weeks of right knee pain after he planted his foot and twisted while turning to chase the ball.
