pearls: Painless or mildly painful limp in a 4-8-year-old with restricted hip abduction/internal rotation suggests Perthes—image the hips.
Hip pathology refers pain to the thigh and knee; a normal knee exam does not rule out hip disease.
Early management centers on containment: preserve range of motion, activity modification, NSAIDs, and physical therapy.
Younger age at onset and maintained ROM predict the best prognosis; older onset and greater head involvement do worse.

cases.title: Newborn Hip 'Clunk' on Routine Exam
cases.chiefComplaint: Asymmetric leg creases and an abnormal hip exam noted at a well-child visit.
cases.history: A 6-week-old girl is brought to clinic for her routine well-child visit. The parents have no specific complaints, but the pediatrician notes asymmetric thigh skin creases and an abnormal hip exam. The baby was born at term via breech presentation (frank breech) and was the mother's first child; pregnancy was complicated by oligohydramnios. Birth weight was 3.6 kg. She feeds well, has normal stools and urine output, and is meeting early milestones. PMH: uncomplicated nursery course. Medications: vitamin D drops. Allergies: none. Social: lives with both parents; she is swaddled tightly with legs together for much of the day. Family history: the mother had 'a hip problem as a baby' that required a brace. Review of systems is otherwise normal—no irritability with diaper changes, no fever, and normal movement of all extremities. The parents mention she seems to keep one leg slightly turned out.
cases.exam: Well-appearing, vigorous infant. Hip exam: with the hips and knees flexed, gentle abduction with anterior pressure on the greater trochanter produces a palpable clunk as the left femoral head relocates into the acetabulum (positive Ortolani). Posterior pressure with adduction produces a palpable clunk of dislocation (positive Barlow) on the left. There are asymmetric thigh and gluteal skin folds and apparent shortening of the left thigh with the hips/knees flexed and feet flat (positive Galeazzi sign). The right hip is stable with symmetric abduction. No spinal dimple or hair tuft; neurologic exam, tone, and lower-extremity movement are otherwise normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given a positive Ortolani/Barlow in a 6-week-old, what is the most appropriate confirmatory study?
options: MRI of the pelvis under sedation
Ultrasound of the hips
Plain radiographs of the pelvis
CT of the hips in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Hip ultrasound is the confirmatory imaging study of choice for developmental dysplasia of the hip in infants under about 4-6 months, because the largely cartilaginous femoral head is not yet ossified and is poorly seen on radiographs; ultrasound assesses acetabular morphology and femoral-head position dynamically. Plain radiographs become useful only after the femoral head ossifies (around 4-6 months). MRI is unnecessary, costly, and would require sedation. CT delivers ionizing radiation and is not indicated for routine DDH evaluation. In a 6-week-old, ultrasound provides the needed structural and dynamic assessment without radiation or sedation.

stages.label: HIP ULTRASOUND
stages.content:
Left hip: shallow acetabulum with alpha angle 48° (normal >60°)
Decreased femoral head coverage; head subluxable/dislocatable with stress maneuvers
Right hip: alpha angle 64°, normal coverage, stable
Impression: developmental dysplasia of the left hip

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which feature in this infant's history is the strongest known risk factor for this condition?
options: Being a term infant
Tight swaddling with legs extended
Maternal vitamin D supplementation
Breech presentation
stages.explanation: Breech presentation is among the strongest risk factors for developmental dysplasia of the hip, along with female sex, first-born status, family history, and oligohydramnios—several of which this infant has. Tight swaddling with the legs extended/adducted is also a real and modifiable risk factor, but breech positioning carries a stronger, well-established association and is the classic answer. Maternal vitamin D supplementation does not cause DDH (and is recommended for the infant). Being born at term is not a risk factor. Recognizing breech, female sex, family history, and first-born status helps target screening.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial treatment for confirmed DDH in this 6-week-old?
options: Closed reduction and hip spica casting under anesthesia
Observation and re-examination at 6 months as monotherapy
Pavlik harness to maintain the hips in flexion
Triple diapering to abduct the hips as initial therapy
stages.explanation: For a confirmed dislocatable/dysplastic hip in an infant under ~6 months, the Pavlik harness is the first-line treatment: it holds the hips flexed and abducted to keep the femoral head reduced and concentrically positioned, allowing the acetabulum to develop normally. Observation alone risks persistent dislocation and later degenerative disease. Closed reduction with spica casting under anesthesia is reserved for older infants or when harness treatment fails. Triple diapering is ineffective and not recommended. Early Pavlik harness treatment achieves high success rates and avoids surgery when started promptly.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: What is the most serious complication associated with improper or overly aggressive abduction treatment of DDH?
options: Avascular necrosis of the femoral head
Permanent sciatic nerve transection
Septic arthritis in this presentation
Femoral shaft fracture
stages.explanation: Avascular necrosis of the femoral head is the most serious complication of DDH treatment, occurring when excessive or forced abduction (e.g., a harness applied too tightly or improper positioning) compromises the blood supply to the developing femoral head. This is why the Pavlik harness is applied carefully with appropriate flexion and limited abduction and monitored closely. Femoral shaft fracture is not a typical treatment complication. Septic arthritis is unrelated to harness use. Sciatic nerve injury from positioning is possible but transection is not a recognized routine complication; femoral nerve palsy from excessive flexion can occur but is usually reversible. AVN is the feared, potentially permanent outcome.

cases.diagnosis: Developmental dysplasia of the hip (left)
pearls: Positive Ortolani (relocation clunk) and Barlow (dislocation clunk) with asymmetric creases/Galeazzi sign suggest DDH.
Risk factors: breech presentation, female sex, first-born, family history, oligohydramnios, tight leg-extended swaddling.
Confirm with hip ULTRASOUND under ~4-6 months (head not ossified); use radiographs only after ossification.
First-line treatment under 6 months is the Pavlik harness; avascular necrosis is the feared complication of over-abduction.

cases.title: Toddler Suddenly Refusing to Use Her Arm
cases.chiefComplaint: A 3-year-old who will not move her left arm after being pulled up by the hand.
cases.history: A 3-year-old girl is brought to the ED because she suddenly stopped using her left arm. According to her father, she was about to step off a curb when he grabbed her hand and pulled upward to keep her from falling; she immediately cried and has since held the arm limp at her side, refusing to use it. There was no fall, no direct blow, and no audible snap. She stopped crying within a few minutes but will not reach for toys with the left hand and resists having the arm moved. She is otherwise well, eating and playing normally with the right hand. PMH: healthy, immunizations up to date, normal development. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Social: lives with both parents and an older sibling; no concerns for maltreatment, and the history is consistent and plausible. Family history: noncontributory. Review of systems is negative for fever, swelling, bruising, or prior similar episodes. The father feels guilty and worried she has 'broken her arm.'
cases.exam: Comfortable, playful child who is consolable and not in acute distress while the arm is left alone. She holds the left arm slightly flexed at the elbow and pronated, close to her body, and resists active or passive supination of the forearm. There is no visible swelling, deformity, ecchymosis, or warmth at the elbow, forearm, wrist, or clavicle, and no point tenderness over the bones when palpated gently. She moves the fingers, wrist, and shoulder when distracted. Distal pulses are 2+, capillary refill is brisk, and sensation appears intact. She uses the right arm normally to reach and grasp.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Based on the classic mechanism and exam, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Apply a long-arm cast and arrange orthopedic follow-up as the first step today
Order an urgent skeletal survey for nonaccidental trauma as the first step today
Perform a reduction maneuver since the presentation is classic
Obtain radiographs of the elbow and forearm before any intervention
stages.explanation: The history of an axial pull on a pronated, extended arm in a young child, with the arm held pronated and no swelling, deformity, or focal bony tenderness, is classic for radial head subluxation (nursemaid's elbow). When the presentation is classic and there is no clinical concern for fracture, a reduction maneuver (supination-flexion or hyperpronation) can be performed without imaging. Routine radiographs are not required for a classic presentation and would delay relief; they are reserved for atypical history, swelling, deformity, or focal tenderness, or after failed reduction. Casting is not indicated and would immobilize a reducible subluxation. A skeletal survey is unwarranted here—the history is consistent and there are no red flags for abuse.

stages.label: PROCEDURE
stages.question: Which reduction technique is appropriate for radial head subluxation?
options: Longitudinal traction with the arm extended as the immediate next step
Pronating the forearm and extending the elbow fully in this setting
Applying figure-of-eight bandaging across the shoulders in this setting
Supinating the forearm
stages.explanation: Reduction of radial head subluxation is accomplished by either the supination-flexion technique—supinate the forearm with the elbow at 90° and then fully flex it—or the hyperpronation technique, often feeling a palpable click over the radial head as the annular ligament repositions. Longitudinal traction with the arm extended does not reduce the subluxation. Pronating and fully extending the elbow is essentially the injury mechanism and will not reduce it. Figure-of-eight bandaging is used for clavicle fractures, not elbow subluxation. After a successful maneuver the child typically resumes using the arm within minutes.

stages.label: POST-REDUCTION
stages.content:
A palpable click is felt over the radial head during supination-flexion
Within 10-15 minutes the child spontaneously reaches for a sticker with the left hand
Full active range of motion at the elbow with no distress
No swelling or deformity
Neurovascular exam intact

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: The child is now using the arm normally. What is the most appropriate disposition and counseling?
options: Admit for observation and serial exams with appropriate follow-up
Obtain post-reduction radiographs in all cases with appropriate follow-up
Discharge home with reassurance
Place in a sling for two weeks and refer to orthopedics with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Once the child is comfortably using the arm with full range of motion, she can be discharged home with reassurance and anticipatory guidance: avoid pulling or lifting her by the hands/wrists, and lift from the trunk/axillae instead, because recurrence is common until the annular ligament strengthens with age. Admission is unnecessary for a successfully reduced, well-appearing child. A sling and orthopedic referral are not needed for an uncomplicated, reduced subluxation. Routine post-reduction radiographs are not required when reduction is clinically successful and the child resumes normal use; imaging is reserved for failed reduction or atypical features. Reassurance and prevention counseling are key.

cases.diagnosis: Nursemaid's elbow (radial head subluxation)
pearls: Classic story: axial pull on a young child's pronated, extended arm; child holds it pronated and refuses to use it.
With a classic presentation and no deformity/focal tenderness, reduce without imaging (supination-flexion or hyperpronation).
Successful reduction is confirmed by a click and the child resuming spontaneous use within minutes.
Counsel caregivers to lift from the trunk, not by the hands/wrists; recurrence is common until the child is older.

cases.title: Elbow Injury After a Fall from the Monkey Bars
cases.chiefComplaint: Right elbow pain, swelling, and refusal to move the arm after a playground fall.
cases.history: A 6-year-old boy is brought to the ED after falling from the monkey bars onto his outstretched right hand with the elbow extended. He had immediate pain and swelling at the elbow and has refused to move the arm since. There was no loss of consciousness and no other injuries. PMH: healthy, fully immunized, normal development. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Social: lives with both parents; in kindergarten; the witnessed mechanism is consistent with the injury. Family history: noncontributory. He last ate 2 hours ago. Review of systems is negative for numbness that the child can articulate, but he is guarding and tearful. The parents report he landed hard with the arm straight out and that the elbow 'looked bent the wrong way' before swelling obscured the contour. He is anxious and reluctant to let anyone touch the arm.
cases.exam: Tearful, guarding child holding the right arm. There is marked swelling and tenderness about the distal humerus/elbow with an effusion; gross deformity is partially obscured by swelling and the elbow is held in slight flexion. Significant pain limits range of motion. Neurovascular check: radial pulse is present but slightly diminished compared with the left; capillary refill ~2-3 seconds; the hand is warm. He cannot make the 'OK' sign—weak thumb interphalangeal and index distal interphalangeal flexion (anterior interosseous nerve distribution)—and has decreased sensation over the volar index finger and thumb. Wrist extension and finger spread are intact. Skin is intact with no open wound.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: After analgesia, what is the most appropriate immediate step in this child with a deformed, swollen elbow?
options: Forcefully reduce the elbow at the bedside as the first step today
Perform and document a careful neurovascular exam and obtain elbow radiographs
Order an MRI of the elbow as the first step today
Apply a tight circumferential cast to stabilize the elbow in this clinical context
stages.explanation: The immediate priorities are a careful, documented neurovascular examination (motor, sensory, pulses, perfusion) and AP/lateral elbow radiographs to characterize the injury—essential because supracondylar fractures carry significant risk of neurovascular injury. Forceful bedside reduction without imaging or, in displaced fractures, without orthopedic involvement risks worsening neurovascular damage. A tight circumferential cast can compromise an already swollen limb and precipitate compartment syndrome; splinting in a position of comfort is preferred initially. MRI is not the appropriate initial study for an acute bony injury in the ED. Documenting neurovascular status before and after any intervention is critical.

stages.label: RADIOGRAPHS
stages.content:
AP and lateral right elbow:
Displaced supracondylar fracture of the distal humerus (extension-type)
Posterior displacement of the distal fragment
Elevated anterior and posterior fat pads (sail sign / posterior fat pad sign)
Anterior humeral line does NOT intersect the middle third of the capitellum
No other fractures; growth plates otherwise intact

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The child cannot make the 'OK' sign and has weak thumb IP/index DIP flexion. Which structure is most likely injured?
options: Radial nerve proper as the primary diagnosis
Posterior interosseous nerve
Ulnar nerve as the primary diagnosis
Anterior interosseous nerve
stages.explanation: Inability to make the 'OK' sign with weak flexion of the thumb interphalangeal joint (flexor pollicis longus) and index distal interphalangeal joint (flexor digitorum profundus to the index) is the hallmark of an anterior interosseous nerve (AIN) injury, the most commonly injured nerve in extension-type supracondylar fractures. The AIN is a pure motor branch of the median nerve, so sensation is often spared, though this patient also has some median sensory changes. Ulnar nerve injury affects finger abduction/adduction and small-finger sensation. The posterior interosseous nerve and radial nerve affect wrist/finger extension, which are intact here. The AIN deficit fits the classic supracondylar pattern.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: The radial pulse is diminished and capillary refill is borderline. Which vascular structure is at greatest risk, and what is the concern?
options: Axillary artery transection risking shoulder ischemia
Radial artery spasm with no clinical significance
Cephalic vein thrombosis risking arm swelling
Brachial artery injury risking Volkmann ischemic contracture
stages.explanation: The brachial artery is at greatest risk in displaced extension-type supracondylar fractures because the sharp proximal fragment can injure or tether it; compromised perfusion can lead to forearm compartment syndrome and Volkmann ischemic contracture, a devastating permanent flexion deformity from muscle ischemia and fibrosis. This makes a diminished pulse, poor capillary refill, or a cool/pale hand an emergency requiring urgent reduction and orthopedic/vascular involvement. Cephalic vein thrombosis is not the concern here. Axillary artery injury occurs with proximal/shoulder injuries, not the distal humerus. Dismissing the diminished pulse as benign 'spasm' is dangerous; perfusion must be restored and monitored closely.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate definitive management of this displaced supracondylar fracture with neurovascular findings?
options: Long-arm cast in full extension and discharge as initial therapy
Outpatient sling with follow-up in one week as initial therapy
Immediate above-elbow amputation as initial therapy
Urgent orthopedic consultation for closed reduction
stages.explanation: A displaced supracondylar humerus fracture, particularly with neurovascular compromise, requires urgent orthopedic consultation for operative treatment—closed reduction and percutaneous pinning (CRPP)—to restore alignment, relieve pressure on neurovascular structures, and stabilize the fracture. Casting in full extension does not address displacement, can worsen swelling and vascular compromise, and is inappropriate. Outpatient sling management delays urgent care of a high-risk injury. Amputation is absurd in this scenario—the goal is timely reduction and fixation, after which most nerve injuries (including AIN palsy) recover spontaneously. Prompt surgical management protects the limb and neurovascular function.

cases.diagnosis: Displaced (extension-type) supracondylar humerus fracture with anterior interosseous nerve injury
pearls: Supracondylar fractures follow a FOOSH with the elbow extended; check the anterior humeral line and fat pad signs on X-ray.
AIN injury ('OK' sign weakness: thumb IP and index DIP flexion) is the most common nerve injury and is usually motor-only.
The brachial artery is at risk—diminished pulse/poor perfusion raises concern for Volkmann ischemic contracture.
Displaced fractures (especially with neurovascular findings) need urgent ortho for closed reduction and percutaneous pinning.

cases.title: Knee Pain Below the Kneecap in a Young Athlete
cases.chiefComplaint: Several months of pain and a tender bump just below the right kneecap.
cases.history: A 13-year-old boy presents to clinic with a 3-month history of anterior right knee pain localized to a bump below the kneecap. The pain worsens with running, jumping, kneeling, and going up and down stairs, and it improves with rest. He is an avid soccer player who recently increased practice frequency. He denies any single injury, locking, giving-way, or true swelling of the joint. He has had a recent growth spurt. PMH: healthy; no prior injuries. Medications: ibuprofen occasionally with relief. Allergies: none. Social: plays club soccer 5 days a week; lives with both parents. Family history: noncontributory. Review of systems is negative for fever, night pain that wakes him, weight loss, or other joint involvement. He is frustrated because the pain limits his play, and his parents are worried about a serious problem after a teammate was diagnosed with 'a bone issue.' Symptoms are bilateral but worse on the right.
cases.exam: Healthy-appearing, well-developed adolescent. The right knee shows a prominent, tender, mildly swollen tibial tubercle with localized firmness; tenderness is focal over the tubercle at the insertion of the patellar tendon. Pain is reproduced by resisted knee extension and by deep squatting. There is no joint-line tenderness, no effusion, and no warmth or erythema over the joint. Ligamentous exam (Lachman, anterior/posterior drawer, varus/valgus) is stable, and McMurray is negative. Range of motion is full. There is tightness of the quadriceps and hamstrings. The left tibial tubercle is mildly tender but less prominent. Gait is normal; distal neurovascular exam is intact.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the classic presentation, what is the most appropriate approach to diagnosis?
options: Diagnose clinically
Order a bone scan to assess the tubercle
Obtain MRI of both knees to confirm as the first step today
Aspirate the knee joint for analysis as the first step today
stages.explanation: Osgood-Schlatter disease is a clinical diagnosis in an active, skeletally immature adolescent with focal pain and tenderness at the tibial tubercle worsened by activity; in a typical presentation, no imaging is required. Radiographs may be obtained to exclude other pathology when the presentation is atypical (e.g., night pain, rest pain, systemic symptoms, or a discrete mass), and can show tubercle fragmentation, but they are not necessary to confirm a classic case. MRI is overkill and unnecessary. A bone scan is nonspecific and inappropriate. Joint aspiration is for suspected effusion/infection, which is absent here. Recognizing the classic syndrome avoids unnecessary testing.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the underlying pathophysiology of this condition?
options: Bacterial infection of the proximal tibia as the primary diagnosis
Meniscal degeneration as the primary diagnosis
Avascular necrosis of the patella as the primary diagnosis
Traction apophysitis of the tibial tubercle from repetitive quadriceps/patellar tendon pull during growth
stages.explanation: Osgood-Schlatter disease is a traction apophysitis: repetitive, forceful contraction of the quadriceps transmitted through the patellar tendon places stress on the still-cartilaginous tibial tubercle apophysis during the adolescent growth spurt, causing microavulsions, inflammation, and the characteristic tender bony prominence. It is an overuse phenomenon, not an infection—there are no systemic or local signs of infection. It is not avascular necrosis of the patella (that would be Sinding-Larsen-Johansson at the inferior pole, a related but distinct condition). It does not involve meniscal degeneration, which would cause joint-line symptoms and mechanical signs. The mechanism explains why it occurs in jumping/running athletes mid-growth.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Long-leg cast immobilization for 6 weeks as initial therapy
Surgical excision of the tibial tubercle ossicle as initial therapy
Activity modification, relative rest, ice, NSAIDs,
Intra-articular corticosteroid injection as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Osgood-Schlatter disease is self-limited and managed conservatively: relative rest and activity modification (reducing aggravating jumping/running), ice after activity, NSAIDs for pain, and a stretching/strengthening program for the quadriceps and hamstrings; a patellar tendon strap may help. Symptoms typically resolve with skeletal maturity as the apophysis fuses. Long-leg casting is unnecessary and causes deconditioning. Surgery (excision of a persistent ossicle) is reserved for the rare adult with refractory symptoms after skeletal maturity, not initial management. Intra-articular corticosteroid injection is inappropriate—this is an extra-articular apophysitis, and steroid injection into or around the tendon risks tendon/skin complications. Conservative care is first-line.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: How should the family be counseled regarding prognosis and sports participation?
options: This will inevitably require surgery with appropriate follow-up
The condition is usually self-limited
He must stop all sports permanently to avoid disability with appropriate follow-up
A persistent bump indicates a missed malignancy with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Families should be reassured that Osgood-Schlatter is benign and self-limited: the adolescent can usually continue sports as tolerated within pain limits, and symptoms typically resolve as the tibial tubercle apophysis fuses at skeletal maturity, sometimes leaving a painless residual bony prominence. Permanent cessation of all sport is unnecessary and overly restrictive—activity is modulated by symptoms, not eliminated. Surgery is rarely required and only for refractory cases after maturity. A persistent bump is the expected residual finding, not a sign of malignancy; red-flag features (night pain, rest pain, systemic symptoms, rapidly enlarging mass) would prompt further workup, but none are present here. Reassurance with activity guidance is appropriate.

cases.diagnosis: Osgood-Schlatter disease (tibial tubercle apophysitis)
pearls: Osgood-Schlatter is a clinical diagnosis: focal tibial tubercle pain/tenderness in an active, growing adolescent—no imaging needed if typical.
It is a traction apophysitis from repetitive quadriceps/patellar tendon pull during the growth spurt, common in jumpers/runners.
Treat conservatively: relative rest, ice, NSAIDs, and quad/hamstring stretching; activity as tolerated within pain limits.
It is self-limited and resolves with skeletal maturity, often leaving a painless bony bump; surgery is rarely needed.

cases.title: Uneven Shoulders in a Preteen Girl
cases.chiefComplaint: Her mother noticed her shoulders and waistline look uneven when she tries on clothes.
cases.history: A 12-year-old girl is brought to the pediatric clinic after her mother noticed asymmetric shoulders and a prominence on one side of her back while shopping for dresses. The patient denies back pain, leg weakness, numbness, bowel or bladder changes, or difficulty walking. She has no history of trauma. She began breast development about a year ago and reached menarche two months ago. She is otherwise healthy, takes no daily medications, and uses occasional acetaminophen for headaches. She has no known drug allergies. She plays recreational soccer without limitation. Developmental milestones were normal and she has no history of neurologic disease, cardiac murmur, or connective-tissue disorder. Family history is notable for a maternal aunt who wore a back brace as a teenager. Review of systems is otherwise negative, with no café-au-lait macules, no foot deformity, and no recent growth spurt-related complaints. She is in the seventh grade and doing well academically.
cases.exam: Well-appearing girl, Tanner stage 3. Standing, the right shoulder sits higher than the left and the right scapula is more prominent; the waist creases are asymmetric. On Adam's forward-bend test, a right-sided thoracic rib hump is visible and measures 8° by scoliometer. Spine is non-tender without midline step-off or skin lesions. Shoulders, hips, and pelvis are otherwise level when seated. Neurologic exam shows 5/5 strength in all extremities, normal sensation, symmetric 2+ reflexes, downgoing toes, and no clonus. Abdominal reflexes are symmetric. Gait is normal; she can heel- and toe-walk. No pectus, joint hypermobility, or pes cavus.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which physical examination finding is most specific for confirming a structural spinal curvature in this patient?
options: A rib hump on Adam's forward-bend test
Asymmetric waist creases in this clinical context
Asymmetric shoulder height while standing
Leg-length discrepancy on supine measurement
stages.explanation: The Adam's forward-bend test elicits a rib hump (or paraspinal/lumbar prominence) caused by the vertebral rotation that accompanies a true structural scoliosis, making it the most specific bedside maneuver; a scoliometer quantifies it. Asymmetric shoulder height and waist creases are suggestive but nonspecific and occur with postural asymmetry alone. A leg-length discrepancy causes a functional (non-rotational) curve that resolves on forward bending or when the discrepancy is corrected, so it points away from structural scoliosis rather than confirming it. Rotation that persists on forward flexion is the hallmark distinguishing structural from postural curves.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next step to evaluate the curvature?
options: MRI of the entire spine as the primary diagnosis
Reassurance and re-examination in one year
Standing full-length posteroanterior
CT scan of the thoracolumbar spine in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Standing full-length PA and lateral radiographs are the standard initial imaging to measure the Cobb angle, which defines severity and guides management. A scoliometer reading of 7° or more (hers is 8°) warrants radiographs. MRI is reserved for atypical features—left thoracic curves, rapid progression, pain, neurologic signs, or onset before age 10—none of which she has, so it is not first-line. CT delivers excessive radiation and is unnecessary for routine curve measurement. Reassurance without imaging is inappropriate once a scoliometer threshold is crossed, because an unmeasured curve cannot be risk-stratified for progression.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Standing PA spine radiograph: Right thoracic curve, apex T8
Cobb angle: 22°
Risser sign: 1 (minimal iliac apophysis ossification)
Lateral film: Normal sagittal alignment, no spondylolysis
No congenital vertebral anomalies; pedicles symmetric
Triradiate cartilage: open

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given a 22° curve, a Risser sign of 1, and recent menarche, what is the most appropriate management?
options: Thoracolumbosacral orthosis (TLSO) bracing now
Observation with repeat radiographs in 4–6 months
Physical therapy and core-strengthening as definitive treatment
Posterior spinal fusion as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Curves of 10–24° are managed with observation and serial radiographs every 4–6 months during the growth spurt, because most such curves do not progress to a threshold requiring intervention. Her skeletal immaturity (Risser 1, open triradiate cartilage) means she needs close surveillance, but the curve is below the bracing threshold. Bracing is indicated for curves of roughly 25–45° in a skeletally immature patient to prevent progression—she has not yet reached it. Surgery (fusion) is reserved for curves above ~45–50°. Physical therapy and core work do not halt curve progression and are not a substitute for surveillance.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Eighteen months later her curve has progressed to 30° and her Risser sign is now 2. What is the most appropriate intervention?
options: Continue observation with annual films
Initiate TLSO brace wear
Refer for posterior spinal fusion in this setting
Begin scoliosis-specific exercises in place of bracing
stages.explanation: A curve of 25–45° in a still-growing patient (Risser 2 indicates ongoing skeletal immaturity) meets criteria for bracing, the goal of which is to prevent progression to a surgical magnitude—greater daily wear time correlates with better outcomes. Continued observation is inadequate now that the curve has crossed 25° in a growing spine with documented progression. Surgery is not yet indicated because the curve is below ~45–50° and she retains growth potential where bracing can still work. Scoliosis-specific exercises may be an adjunct but are not an accepted replacement for bracing at this magnitude and maturity.

cases.diagnosis: Adolescent idiopathic scoliosis (right thoracic curve)
pearls: A scoliometer reading of 7° or more on Adam's forward-bend test is the usual threshold to obtain standing PA/lateral spine radiographs.
The Cobb angle plus skeletal maturity (Risser sign, triradiate cartilage, menarchal status) drives management: <25° observe, 25–45° brace, >45–50° fuse.
Bracing aims to halt progression, not to correct the existing curve; outcomes improve with longer daily wear.
Atypical features—left thoracic curve, pain, rapid progression, neurologic deficits, or onset before age 10—mandate spine MRI to exclude an underlying cause.

cases.title: Sudden Back Pain With Trouble Urinating
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe low back pain radiating down both legs with numbness when he sits and inability to urinate.
cases.history: A 44-year-old warehouse worker presents to the ED with two days of worsening low back pain radiating down both legs, now accompanied by numbness around the buttocks and genitals and an inability to urinate for the past eight hours despite feeling full. He has a 6-month history of intermittent sciatica and lifted a heavy pallet three days ago, after which the pain abruptly intensified. Today he noticed his legs feel weak climbing stairs and he could not feel toilet paper when wiping. He has had no fever, chills, or weight loss. He has no history of cancer, IV drug use, or recent infection. Past medical history is unremarkable. Medications: ibuprofen 600 mg as needed and a cyclobenzaprine course from his primary doctor. No known drug allergies. He smokes half a pack daily, drinks socially, and denies recreational drugs. Family history is noncontributory. On further questioning he reports decreased sensation when passing the small amount of urine he can produce and worsening difficulty achieving an erection over the last day.
cases.exam: Uncomfortable, shifting on the gurney. Lumbar spine is tender to palpation without midline step-off or warmth. Straight-leg raise is positive bilaterally at 30°. Strength: ankle dorsiflexion 4/5 bilaterally, plantarflexion 4/5, hip flexion 5/5. Sensation is diminished to light touch over the perineum, posterior thighs, and both soles (saddle distribution). Ankle reflexes are absent bilaterally; patellar reflexes 1+. Anal sphincter tone is reduced on digital rectal exam and the bulbocavernosus reflex is absent. Bladder is palpable to the umbilicus. Post-void residual by bladder scan is 650 mL. No fever, no spinal warmth, no rash.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which constellation of findings is most concerning and should drive the immediate workup?
options: Erectile dysfunction and bilateral leg pain in this clinical context
Absent ankle reflexes and a smoking history in this clinical context
Bilateral positive straight-leg raise and lumbar tenderness
Saddle anesthesia, urinary retention,
stages.explanation: Saddle (perineal) anesthesia, urinary retention with a large post-void residual, and decreased anal sphincter tone together constitute the classic red-flag triad of cauda equina syndrome, a surgical emergency requiring urgent imaging and decompression. Bilateral positive straight-leg raise and lumbar tenderness indicate nerve-root irritation but are nonspecific and common in ordinary disc disease. Absent ankle reflexes and smoking are relevant but not the emergency-defining features. Erectile dysfunction and leg pain are concerning when part of the cauda equina picture but are not, in isolation, the findings that mandate emergent intervention—the retention and saddle sensory loss are the key drivers.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
options: Plain lumbar radiographs
Emergent MRI of the lumbosacral spine
Outpatient MRI within one week
CT of the lumbar spine without contrast
stages.explanation: Emergent MRI of the lumbosacral spine is the test of choice because it directly visualizes the cauda equina, disc herniation, and degree of canal compromise, and the diagnosis is time-sensitive. Plain radiographs cannot show neural compression and would only delay care. Non-contrast CT is inferior to MRI for soft tissue and neural detail and is reserved for when MRI is contraindicated or unavailable. An outpatient MRI within a week is dangerously slow: cauda equina syndrome is a surgical emergency where delayed decompression risks permanent bladder, bowel, and sexual dysfunction, so imaging must be obtained now.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI lumbosacral spine: Large central L4–L5 disc extrusion
Severe canal stenosis with compression of the cauda equina nerve roots
No epidural collection, no abnormal marrow signal, no enhancing mass
Post-void residual on repeat bladder scan: 700 mL
Neurosurgery consulted at bedside

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the definitive management for this patient?
options: Epidural steroid injection as initial therapy
Indwelling urinary catheter and discharge with neurosurgery follow-up
High-dose IV corticosteroids and observation
Urgent surgical decompression (discectomy)
stages.explanation: Cauda equina syndrome from a compressive disc requires urgent surgical decompression (e.g., wide laminectomy/discectomy); earlier surgery is associated with better recovery of bladder, bowel, and motor function, so it should not be delayed. High-dose steroids have a role in malignant or inflammatory cord compression but do not address a mechanical disc compressing the cauda equina and are not definitive here. Catheterizing and discharging the patient ignores a progressive surgical emergency and risks permanent deficits. An epidural steroid injection treats radicular pain in stable patients and is contraindicated as management of acute cauda equina compression.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: While awaiting the operating room, which immediate bedside intervention is most appropriate?
options: Administer a fluid bolus and recheck the bladder scan in 4 hours
Place a urinary (Foley) catheter to decompress the bladder
Start tamsulosin to relax the bladder neck
Encourage oral fluids to promote spontaneous voiding
stages.explanation: With acute urinary retention and a bladder volume near 700 mL, placing a Foley catheter relieves the obstruction, prevents bladder overdistension injury, and allows accurate output monitoring while the patient awaits decompression. Encouraging oral fluids would worsen overdistension in a bladder that cannot empty. Repeating the scan in four hours after a fluid bolus needlessly delays decompression of a dangerously full bladder. Tamsulosin treats functional bladder-outlet obstruction (e.g., BPH) and has no meaningful role in the neurogenic retention of cauda equina syndrome, where the problem is interrupted sacral nerve function rather than prostatic obstruction.

cases.diagnosis: Cauda equina syndrome from a central L4–L5 disc herniation
pearls: Saddle anesthesia, urinary retention with elevated post-void residual, and reduced anal sphincter tone form the red-flag triad of cauda equina syndrome.
Emergent MRI of the lumbosacral spine is the diagnostic test of choice; do not delay for plain films.
Treatment is urgent surgical decompression—earlier decompression yields better recovery of bladder, bowel, and motor function.
Painless urinary retention with overflow incontinence (high post-void residual) is more reliable than urinary frequency for identifying cauda equina compromise.

cases.title: Fever and Back Pain in a Man Who Injects Drugs
cases.chiefComplaint: Worsening mid-back pain with fevers and new leg weakness over the past week.
cases.history: A 56-year-old man with active intravenous heroin use presents to the ED with one week of progressively severe mid-thoracic back pain, now constant and worse at night, accompanied by subjective fevers and chills. Over the past two days he has noticed both legs feeling weak and heavy and difficulty starting his urine stream. He denies recent trauma. He has type 2 diabetes (HbA1c last checked at 9.2%) treated with metformin 1000 mg twice daily, and hepatitis C. He injects into his arms and groin and shares equipment intermittently. He has no known drug allergies. He is unhoused, smokes tobacco, and drinks alcohol heavily. Family history is noncontributory. He reports drenching night sweats and decreased appetite. He has not had recent dental work or skin abscess drainage but points to a healing injection-site sore on his left forearm. Review of systems is positive for malaise and the back/leg symptoms above and negative for headache, neck stiffness, chest pain, and cough.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing, diaphoretic man. There is exquisite point tenderness over the mid-thoracic spinous processes with overlying paraspinal muscle spasm; no fluctuant mass or overlying erythema. Lower-extremity strength is 4/5 in hip flexion and knee extension bilaterally; sensation is mildly reduced below a mid-thoracic level. Patellar reflexes are brisk (3+) with bilateral upgoing toes. Anal sphincter tone is preserved. A healing track-mark sore is present on the left forearm without active drainage. Cardiac exam reveals tachycardia without a murmur. No nuchal rigidity. Gait is unsteady but the patient is ambulatory with assistance.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given fever, focal spinal tenderness, IV drug use, and new neurologic deficits, which diagnosis must be excluded most urgently?
options: Spinal epidural abscess
Mechanical lumbar strain
Diabetic peripheral neuropathy
Acute pyelonephritis
stages.explanation: The triad of back pain, fever, and neurologic deficit in a patient with IV drug use, diabetes, and hepatitis C is the classic presentation of a spinal epidural abscess, which can rapidly cause irreversible cord compression and must be excluded emergently. Mechanical strain does not cause fever, focal spinal point tenderness with systemic illness, or upper-motor-neuron signs. Pyelonephritis causes fever and flank pain but not spinal point tenderness with myelopathy. Diabetic peripheral neuropathy is chronic and produces distal sensory loss with reduced reflexes, not acute fever with brisk reflexes and a sensory level—features that point to spinal cord compression.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate next diagnostic step?
options: Bone scan of the thoracolumbar spine
Plain thoracic spine radiographs
MRI of the spine with
Lumbar puncture for CSF analysis
stages.explanation: Contrast-enhanced MRI of the spine is the diagnostic gold standard for epidural abscess: it defines the abscess extent, the level and degree of cord compression, and associated vertebral osteomyelitis/discitis. Plain radiographs are insensitive early and cannot show the epidural collection or cord. Lumbar puncture is contraindicated because the needle can seed infection into the subarachnoid space and offers little diagnostic value here; it also risks neurologic worsening if there is a pressure gradient from cord compression. A bone scan lacks the anatomic resolution needed for surgical planning and is far inferior to MRI for soft tissue and neural structures.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 16.8 ×10³/µL (neutrophil predominant)
ESR 92 mm/hr
CRP 184 mg/L
Blood cultures ×2 drawn before antibiotics: pending
HbA1c 9.4%
MRI spine with contrast: Rim-enhancing posterior epidural collection T5–T8 with cord compression and adjacent T6–T7 discitis/osteomyelitis

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Empiric antibiotic coverage for this infection should most importantly include activity against which organism?
options: Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus
Anaerobes such as Bacteroides fragilis
Pseudomonas aeruginosa only as monotherapy
Candida species as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Staphylococcus aureus—frequently MRSA in people who inject drugs—is by far the most common cause of spinal epidural abscess, so empiric therapy must include MRSA coverage (e.g., vancomycin) plus a broad gram-negative agent given the IV-drug-use risk for Pseudomonas. Covering Pseudomonas alone would miss the predominant staphylococcal pathogen and is incomplete. Anaerobes are uncommon in hematogenous spinal abscess and are not the priority for empiric coverage. Candida is a rare cause, seen mainly in certain injection practices or profound immunosuppression, and would not be targeted empirically up front. Crucially, blood cultures should be obtained before antibiotics so therapy can be narrowed once the organism and sensitivities return.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: He has progressive lower-extremity weakness and a sensory level with significant cord compression on MRI. What is the most appropriate management?
options: IV antibiotics alone with serial neurologic checks
Urgent surgical decompression
Corticosteroids and physical therapy based on current risk
CT-guided aspiration and oral antibiotics
stages.explanation: With a neurologic deficit and significant cord compression, urgent surgical decompression and drainage combined with prolonged IV antibiotics is the standard of care; surgery relieves compression and provides source control plus culture material. Antibiotics alone may suffice only in select patients without neurologic deficit and without significant compression—not this progressing, compressed patient. CT-guided aspiration with oral antibiotics is inadequate for an epidural collection causing myelopathy and does not achieve reliable source control. Corticosteroids and physical therapy do not address the infection or the mechanical compression and would allow the deficit to progress toward irreversible paralysis; they have no role as primary therapy here.

cases.diagnosis: Spinal epidural abscess with discitis/osteomyelitis (T5–T8)
pearls: Suspect spinal epidural abscess with the triad of back pain, fever, and neurologic deficit—especially in IV drug users, diabetics, and the immunocompromised.
Contrast-enhanced MRI is the diagnostic gold standard; avoid lumbar puncture, which risks seeding the subarachnoid space.
Staphylococcus aureus (often MRSA) is the leading pathogen—obtain blood cultures, then start empiric vancomycin plus gram-negative coverage.
Neurologic deficit with cord compression mandates urgent surgical decompression in addition to prolonged IV antibiotics.

cases.title: Sudden Mid-Back Pain After a Small Stumble
cases.chiefComplaint: Sharp mid-back pain that started after she sat down hard in a chair.
cases.history: A 75-year-old woman presents to clinic with five days of sharp, band-like mid-back pain that began the moment she lowered herself heavily into a chair. The pain is worse with standing, coughing, and rolling over in bed, and is relieved by lying still. She denies any high-energy trauma. She reports she has lost about two inches of height over recent years and her family says she is becoming more stooped. There is no leg weakness, numbness, saddle sensory change, or bowel or bladder dysfunction. Past medical history includes hypothyroidism and a prior wrist fracture from a low fall at age 68. She had early menopause at 45 and took prednisone for two years in her sixties for polymyalgia rheumatica. Medications: levothyroxine 75 mcg daily and a calcium/vitamin D supplement. No known drug allergies. She is a lifelong nonsmoker, drinks one glass of wine nightly, and lives alone. Her mother had a hip fracture in her seventies. She has never had a bone density scan.
cases.exam: Thin, mildly stooped woman with an exaggerated thoracic kyphosis. There is focal midline tenderness to percussion over a lower thoracic spinous process that reproduces her pain; paraspinal muscles are tense. No overlying erythema, warmth, or palpable mass. Lower-extremity strength is 5/5 throughout, sensation is intact, reflexes are symmetric 2+, and toes are downgoing. Straight-leg raise is negative. Gait is slow but steady; she rises from the chair guarding her back. No saddle anesthesia and normal rectal tone. Abdomen is soft and nontender. No constitutional appearance of cachexia.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most likely cause of this patient's acute focal thoracic back pain?
options: Metastatic spinal cord compression
Lumbar disc herniation with radiculopathy
Osteoporotic vertebral compression fracture
Ankylosing spondylitis flare
stages.explanation: Acute, mechanical, focal thoracic pain after trivial trauma in an older woman with multiple osteoporosis risk factors (early menopause, prior fragility wrist fracture, prolonged glucocorticoid use, height loss, progressive kyphosis) is the classic presentation of an osteoporotic vertebral compression fracture. A lumbar disc herniation causes radicular leg pain and a positive straight-leg raise, both absent here. Metastatic cord compression typically brings constitutional symptoms, night pain unrelieved by rest, and neurologic deficits—she has none and no cancer history. Ankylosing spondylitis presents in young adults with inflammatory back pain (morning stiffness improving with activity), not acute mechanical pain in a 75-year-old.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial imaging study?
options: MRI of the thoracic spine in this clinical context
Whole-body bone scan as the primary diagnosis
Standing plain radiographs of the thoracic spine
Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DXA) of the hip and spine
stages.explanation: Plain radiographs of the thoracic spine are the appropriate first study to confirm a compression fracture, showing anterior wedging and loss of vertebral body height. They are inexpensive, fast, and adequate to diagnose most acute fractures. MRI is reserved for when malignancy or infection is suspected, neurologic deficits are present, or fracture acuity/age must be determined—none of which is established yet. A bone scan is nonspecific and not the initial test. DXA is essential to assess underlying osteoporosis and guide therapy, but it does not diagnose the acute fracture and is performed alongside or after the fracture is confirmed, not as the first imaging for acute pain.

stages.label: IMAGING & LABS
stages.content:
Thoracic spine radiograph: Anterior wedge compression fracture of T11, ~35% height loss
No posterior cortical retropulsion; no other acute fracture
DXA (performed same visit): Lumbar spine T-score −3.1; femoral neck T-score −2.8
Calcium 9.4 mg/dL; 25-OH vitamin D 22 ng/mL; TSH 2.1 mIU/L
Serum protein electrophoresis: no monoclonal spike

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this neurologically intact, stable compression fracture, what is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Strict bed rest for 6 weeks as initial therapy
Urgent vertebroplasty as initial therapy
Analgesia, early mobilization,
Surgical decompression and instrumented fusion
stages.explanation: Most osteoporotic compression fractures are stable and treated conservatively with adequate analgesia, early mobilization, and physical therapy, which preserves bone and muscle mass and reduces deconditioning. Vertebroplasty/kyphoplasty is considered only for refractory, severe pain that fails conservative care over several weeks; it is not first-line. Strict prolonged bed rest worsens bone loss, muscle atrophy, and thromboembolic and pneumonia risk in an older patient and should be avoided. Surgical decompression and fusion are reserved for unstable fractures, retropulsion with neurologic compromise, or deformity—none present here, where the fracture is stable and she is neurologically intact.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: To reduce her risk of future fragility fractures, which long-term pharmacologic therapy is most appropriate?
options: A bisphosphonate plus calcium
Calcium and vitamin D supplementation alone in this setting
Calcitonin nasal spray as the sole agent in this setting
Continuous combined estrogen–progestin therapy in this setting
stages.explanation: She has established osteoporosis (T-score ≤ −2.5) with a fragility fracture, so a bisphosphonate is first-line antiresorptive therapy and should be paired with correction of her vitamin D insufficiency and adequate calcium. Calcium and vitamin D alone are necessary but insufficient to reduce fracture risk in someone with a fracture and a T-score of −3.1. Systemic estrogen–progestin carries cardiovascular and breast-cancer risks and is not recommended for fracture prevention in a 75-year-old. Calcitonin has weak efficacy and is not used as primary therapy. Vitamin D should be repleted before or alongside starting a bisphosphonate to avoid hypocalcemia.

cases.diagnosis: Osteoporotic vertebral compression fracture (T11)
pearls: Acute mechanical back pain after minimal trauma with height loss and kyphosis in an older adult signals an osteoporotic compression fracture until proven otherwise.
Plain radiographs (anterior wedging, height loss) confirm most fractures; reserve MRI for suspected malignancy, infection, or neurologic deficit.
Manage stable, neurologically intact fractures conservatively—analgesia, early mobilization, PT—avoiding prolonged bed rest.
A fragility fracture defines osteoporosis regardless of T-score; start a bisphosphonate and ensure calcium/vitamin D repletion to prevent recurrence.

cases.title: Leg Pain That Eases When Leaning on the Cart
cases.chiefComplaint: Aching, heavy legs when he walks that go away when he sits or leans forward.
cases.history: A 70-year-old retired mail carrier presents to clinic with one year of gradually worsening bilateral buttock, thigh, and calf aching, heaviness, and tingling that come on after walking about one block. He notices the symptoms vanish when he sits down or leans forward over a shopping cart, and he can walk much farther in the grocery store leaning on the cart than he can walking upright outdoors. Walking downhill is harder than uphill. He denies rest pain, night cramps, or symptoms while standing still without walking. There is no weakness reported and no bowel or bladder dysfunction. Past medical history includes hypertension and well-controlled type 2 diabetes. Medications: lisinopril 20 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg twice daily, atorvastatin 40 mg daily, and aspirin 81 mg daily. No known drug allergies. He is a former smoker (quit 15 years ago, 20 pack-years). He drinks rarely. Pedal pulses have always been documented as normal. Family history includes coronary disease. Review of systems is otherwise negative.
cases.exam: Comfortable at rest. Lumbar spine shows reduced extension that reproduces his leg symptoms, while forward flexion relieves them. Strength is 5/5 in both legs at rest; sensation is mildly diminished in both feet in a stocking pattern (consistent with mild diabetic neuropathy). Deep tendon reflexes are 1+ and symmetric. Straight-leg raise is negative bilaterally. Dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses are 2+ and symmetric; feet are warm and well-perfused with normal capillary refill and no hair loss or skin atrophy. Gait is wide-based but nonataxic; he stands with a slightly flexed posture. No spinal tenderness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The relief of his leg symptoms with forward flexion (the 'shopping-cart sign') and worsening with extension most strongly suggests which mechanism?
options: Atherosclerotic peripheral arterial disease
Diabetic peripheral neuropathy alone as the first step today
Hip osteoarthritis as the first step today
Lumbar spinal canal narrowing relieved by flexion
stages.explanation: Symptoms triggered by walking and lumbar extension but relieved by sitting or forward flexion are characteristic of neurogenic claudication from lumbar spinal stenosis: flexion enlarges the canal and decompresses the cauda equina, while extension narrows it. This positional, posture-dependent pattern—and the ability to walk farther leaning on a cart or going uphill (flexed)—distinguishes it from vascular claudication, which is relieved simply by stopping (rest) regardless of posture and has diminished pulses. Hip osteoarthritis produces groin pain with hip motion, not posture-dependent leg symptoms. Diabetic neuropathy gives constant distal sensory loss, not exertional, posture-modulated claudication.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which feature best distinguishes his neurogenic claudication from vascular claudication?
options: Pain is reproduced by walking as the primary diagnosis
Symptoms are relieved by stopping and standing still
Symptoms occur in the calves as the primary diagnosis
Symptoms are relieved by lumbar flexion rather than by rest alone
stages.explanation: The key discriminator is that neurogenic claudication is relieved by lumbar flexion (sitting, leaning on a cart, walking uphill), whereas vascular claudication is relieved simply by stopping the exertion regardless of posture. In vascular disease, standing still after stopping relieves pain; in spinal stenosis, the patient often must flex to gain relief and can remain symptomatic if standing upright. Walking-induced pain and calf involvement occur in both and therefore do not distinguish them. The positional/postural dependence, plus his normal symmetric pulses and warm well-perfused feet, points firmly to a neurogenic rather than vascular cause.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.content:
Ankle-brachial index: 1.05 bilaterally (normal)
Lumbar spine radiographs: multilevel degenerative changes, facet hypertrophy, disc space narrowing L3–L4 and L4–L5
MRI lumbar spine: severe central canal stenosis at L3–L4 and L4–L5 with ligamentum flavum hypertrophy; no disc extrusion, no cord/conus lesion
HbA1c 6.8%

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: He has no neurologic deficit and has not yet tried structured nonoperative care. What is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Physical therapy , activity modification,
Long-term opioid therapy as initial therapy
Lumbar fusion at L3–L5 as initial therapy
Immediate surgical decompressive laminectomy as initial therapy
stages.explanation: For lumbar spinal stenosis without a progressive or disabling neurologic deficit, first-line treatment is conservative: physical therapy emphasizing flexion-based exercises and core strengthening, activity modification, and analgesics like NSAIDs, with epidural steroid injections as a possible adjunct. Most patients improve or stabilize without surgery. Immediate decompressive laminectomy is reserved for those who fail conservative care or have significant or progressive neurologic deficits, neither present here. Long-term opioids are inappropriate for a chronic degenerative condition given limited benefit and substantial harms. Fusion is not indicated for stenosis without instability or deformity and adds morbidity over decompression alone.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which clinical development would most strongly justify referral for surgical decompression?
options: An incidental finding of mild foraminal narrowing on MRI with appropriate follow-up
Progressive lower-extremity weakness
A single episode of leg tingling after a long walk with appropriate follow-up
Persistent mild aching after two weeks of NSAIDs with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Surgery (decompressive laminectomy) is indicated when a patient develops progressive neurologic deficits such as worsening weakness, or has disabling symptoms that persist despite an adequate trial of conservative management—both signal that ongoing nonoperative care is unlikely to suffice. Persistent mild aching after only two weeks does not constitute a failed conservative trial, which typically spans several weeks to months. An incidental imaging finding without correlating disability is not a surgical indication—treat the patient, not the scan. A single transient episode of tingling after exertion is expected with stenosis and does not warrant operative referral.

cases.diagnosis: Lumbar spinal stenosis with neurogenic claudication
pearls: Neurogenic claudication is relieved by lumbar flexion (sitting, leaning on a cart, walking uphill); vascular claudication is relieved by stopping exertion regardless of posture.
Normal symmetric pedal pulses and a normal ABI argue against vascular claudication and support a spinal etiology.
MRI is the imaging of choice and shows central canal narrowing, often from ligamentum flavum and facet hypertrophy.
Start with conservative care (flexion-based PT, activity modification, analgesics); reserve decompressive surgery for progressive deficits or disabling, refractory symptoms.

cases.title: A Teen Gymnast's Nagging Low Back Pain
cases.chiefComplaint: Low back pain that gets worse with arching backward during tumbling and gymnastics.
cases.history: A 17-year-old competitive gymnast presents to clinic with four months of dull low back pain that has steadily worsened. The pain is localized to the lower lumbar region, worsens with back extension maneuvers (back-walkovers, dismounts, and landing) and prolonged standing, and improves with rest and forward bending. She denies leg pain, numbness, weakness, or bowel or bladder changes. There was no single injury. She trains roughly 20 hours per week with frequent hyperextension and repetitive loading. She is otherwise healthy, takes only occasional ibuprofen, and has no known drug allergies. Menarche was at 13 and her periods are regular. She is a nonsmoker, does not drink, and uses no supplements beyond a multivitamin. Family history is noncontributory. She has missed several meets because the pain limits her arching. She denies fever, weight loss, or night pain. Review of systems is otherwise negative. She is anxious about whether she can continue elite training this season.
cases.exam: Fit adolescent. Lumbar extension reproduces her pain, and the single-leg hyperextension (stork) test is positive, eliciting focal lower lumbar pain. There is tenderness over the lower lumbar spinous processes and tight hamstrings limiting forward flexion. A subtle palpable step-off is appreciated at the lumbosacral junction. Strength is 5/5 throughout both legs, sensation is intact, deep tendon reflexes are symmetric 2+, and toes are downgoing. Straight-leg raise is negative bilaterally. Gait is normal and she walks without a limp. No saddle anesthesia, normal rectal tone, and no spinal warmth, erythema, or constitutional appearance of illness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given activity-related extension pain and a positive single-leg hyperextension test in a young athlete, which condition is most likely?
options: Pars interarticularis stress injury , possibly with spondylolisthesis
Ankylosing spondylitis as the first step today
Lumbar disc herniation as the first step today
Vertebral osteomyelitis as the first step today
stages.explanation: Repetitive hyperextension and axial loading in adolescent athletes (gymnasts, divers, dancers, football linemen) classically cause a pars interarticularis stress injury—spondylolysis—which can progress to forward slippage of the vertebra (isthmic spondylolisthesis). Pain worsened by extension and a positive single-leg hyperextension (stork) test fit this perfectly. A disc herniation produces radicular leg pain and a positive straight-leg raise, both absent. Ankylosing spondylitis causes inflammatory back pain with prolonged morning stiffness that improves with activity, not mechanical extension pain in an athlete. Vertebral osteomyelitis presents with fever, night pain, and constitutional symptoms, none of which she has.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial imaging study?
options: Non-contrast CT of the pelvis in this clinical context
MRI of the lumbar spine as the primary diagnosis
Whole-body bone scan as the primary diagnosis
Plain lumbar radiographs
stages.explanation: Plain lumbar radiographs are the appropriate first study: a lateral film readily demonstrates anterolisthesis and grades the slip, and the pars defect can be seen as the classic 'collar' on the Scotty dog on oblique views. Films are low-cost and often sufficient to make the diagnosis. MRI is valuable for detecting early stress reactions before a frank fracture and for assessing nerve roots, but it is not the routine first study here. A bone scan/SPECT can identify an active stress lesion when films are normal but suspicion persists—an adjunct, not the initial test. CT of the pelvis images the wrong region for a lumbar pars defect.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Lateral lumbar radiograph: bilateral pars interarticularis defects at L5
Grade I anterolisthesis of L5 on S1 (~20% slip)
Oblique view: lucency across the 'neck' of the Scotty dog (pars defect / collar sign)
No high-grade slip, no disc-space infection, no congenital vertebral anomaly
MRI (obtained for completeness): active pars stress signal at L5, no nerve-root compression

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this low-grade (Grade I) isthmic spondylolisthesis without neurologic deficit, what is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Activity restriction , physical therapy with core
Long-term opioid analgesia and continued full training as initial therapy
Immediate posterior spinal fusion as initial therapy
Lumbar epidural steroid injection as first-line therapy as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Low-grade spondylolysis/spondylolisthesis without neurologic deficit is managed conservatively: relative rest with avoidance of aggravating hyperextension, a course of physical therapy emphasizing core stabilization and hamstring flexibility, and analgesics; activity-modifying bracing is used in some athletes to promote healing and symptom relief. Most adolescents improve and can return to sport. Posterior fusion is reserved for high-grade slips, progression, or refractory symptoms with neurologic involvement—not a Grade I slip. Long-term opioids with continued full training ignores the stress injury and risks progression. An epidural steroid injection treats radicular pain and is not first-line for a mechanical pars stress injury without radiculopathy.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which finding on follow-up would most strongly warrant referral for surgical evaluation?
options: Progression to a high-grade slip or new neurologic deficits/radiculopathy despite conservative care
Persistent tight hamstrings after two weeks of therapy with appropriate follow-up
An incidental finding of a healed pars defect with appropriate follow-up
Mild residual ache after returning to gymnastics with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Surgical referral is warranted when the slip progresses to a high grade, when there is new or progressive neurologic compromise (radiculopathy, weakness), or when disabling symptoms persist despite adequate conservative treatment—these indicate that nonoperative care is failing. Persistent tight hamstrings after only two weeks reflects an incomplete therapy course, not a surgical indication. An incidentally healed pars defect is a good outcome, not a reason for surgery. Mild residual ache after return to sport is common and managed with ongoing conservative measures rather than operative intervention. The decision hinges on slip progression and neurologic status, not minor lingering symptoms.

cases.diagnosis: Isthmic spondylolisthesis (L5–S1, Grade I) from bilateral pars defects
pearls: Adolescent athletes with repetitive hyperextension (gymnasts, divers, linemen) are at risk for pars stress injury (spondylolysis) and isthmic spondylolisthesis.
Extension-provoked pain and a positive single-leg hyperextension (stork) test point to a pars lesion; the oblique radiograph shows the 'Scotty dog collar' defect.
Start with plain radiographs; use MRI or SPECT to catch an early stress reaction when films are unrevealing.
Low-grade slips without deficit are treated conservatively (activity modification, core/hamstring PT, analgesia); reserve surgery for high-grade or progressive slips and neurologic deficits.

cases.title: A Pop in the Ankle During Weekend Basketball
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden pain in the back of the ankle after he felt like someone kicked him during a pickup game.
cases.history: A 45-year-old accountant presents to the ED after a sudden injury during a weekend pickup basketball game. While pushing off to sprint for a loose ball, he felt a sharp blow to the back of his right ankle and thought a teammate had kicked him, but no one was near him. He heard an audible pop, immediately lost push-off power, and could not continue playing. He can bear weight flat-footed but cannot rise onto his toes or run. He denies numbness, prior ankle injuries, or chronic Achilles pain. He has been mostly sedentary and plays sports only occasionally. Two weeks ago his primary doctor started him on ciprofloxacin for a urinary tract infection. Other medications: none regular. He has no known drug allergies. He drinks socially, does not smoke, and has no diabetes. Family history is noncontributory. He recalls a brief period of heel stiffness last month that he ignored. Review of systems is otherwise negative.
cases.exam: Right ankle with posterior swelling and ecchymosis above the heel. There is a palpable tender gap in the tendon roughly 4 cm proximal to its calcaneal insertion. Active plantarflexion is weak but present (long toe flexors and posterior tibialis intact), and he cannot perform a single-leg heel raise. Resting ankle posture shows decreased plantarflexion compared with the uninjured side when prone. With the patient prone and knee flexed, squeezing the calf produces no plantarflexion of the foot. Sensation is intact, dorsalis pedis pulse is 2+, and the foot is well-perfused. The ankle joint itself is stable to ligamentous stress and nontender over the malleoli.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which bedside test best confirms the suspected diagnosis?
options: Homans sign in this clinical context
Anterior drawer test of the ankle
Thompson test
Talar tilt test
stages.explanation: The Thompson test—squeezing the calf with the patient prone and knee flexed—normally produces passive plantarflexion via an intact gastrocnemius-soleus-Achilles unit; absence of plantarflexion indicates a complete Achilles tendon rupture, exactly what is seen here. The anterior drawer and talar tilt tests assess lateral ankle ligament stability (sprains), not the Achilles. Homans sign is a (poorly sensitive) maneuver for deep vein thrombosis and is irrelevant to tendon integrity. Note that weak active plantarflexion can persist after rupture because of the long toe flexors and tibialis posterior, which is why a positive Thompson test, palpable gap, and inability to heel-raise are more reliable.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which element of his history is the most relevant predisposing risk factor for this injury?
options: His sedentary lifestyle with occasional sport
Recent initiation of a fluoroquinolone
His occupation as an accountant
Social alcohol use as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Fluoroquinolones such as ciprofloxacin are a well-recognized cause of tendinopathy and tendon rupture, with the Achilles most often affected; recent initiation is a clinically important, modifiable risk factor and the drug should be stopped. The classic patient is also a middle-aged 'weekend warrior' making an explosive push-off, so deconditioning with intermittent sport is a contributing background factor—but among the listed options, the fluoroquinolone is the specific pharmacologic precipitant most directly tied to rupture. Social alcohol use and a desk job are not established independent risk factors for Achilles rupture and do not explain this presentation.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Ankle radiographs: No fracture; no malleolar or calcaneal injury
Soft-tissue view: loss of the normal Kager fat-pad contour posteriorly
Bedside ultrasound: full-thickness discontinuity of the Achilles tendon ~4 cm above the calcaneal insertion with a hypoechoic gap
Neurovascular exam unchanged: intact sensation, 2+ dorsalis pedis

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial management in the ED?
options: Place the ankle in a splint/boot in plantarflexion , keep non-weight-bearing,
Apply a neutral-position weight-bearing cast as initial therapy
Discharge with an elastic wrap and full weight-bearing as tolerated as initial therapy
Immediate surgical repair in the ED as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Initial ED management of an acute Achilles rupture is immobilization in equinus (plantarflexion), which approximates the torn tendon ends, with the patient kept non-weight-bearing and referred promptly to orthopedics to decide between operative and nonoperative (functional rehab) treatment. A neutral-position cast pulls the tendon ends apart and is incorrect for the acute setting. Surgical repair is not performed emergently in the ED; it is scheduled after specialist evaluation. Discharging with only an elastic wrap and full weight-bearing fails to protect the healing tendon and risks gap formation, re-rupture, and poor outcome.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: When counseling him about definitive treatment options, which statement is most accurate?
options: Both surgical repair and functional nonoperative management are accepted options
Surgery is mandatory because nonoperative care always leads to re-rupture with appropriate follow-up
Treatment choice does not affect return to sport or re-rupture rate at all with appropriate follow-up
Nonoperative management requires permanent casting and no rehabilitation with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Both operative repair and nonoperative management with a structured early functional-rehabilitation protocol are accepted; modern functional rehab has narrowed the re-rupture gap considerably, while surgery still tends to modestly reduce re-rupture at the cost of wound-healing and infection risks. The choice is individualized to patient activity level, comorbidities, and preferences. It is false that surgery is mandatory or that nonoperative care always re-ruptures. Nonoperative management is not permanent casting—it uses progressive functional rehabilitation. And treatment choice does meaningfully affect re-rupture and recovery, so the claim that it is irrelevant is incorrect.

cases.diagnosis: Acute Achilles tendon rupture
pearls: A 'felt-a-kick' sensation with an audible pop, a palpable tendon gap, and inability to do a single-leg heel raise are hallmarks of Achilles rupture.
A positive Thompson (calf-squeeze) test—no passive plantarflexion—confirms a complete rupture; weak active plantarflexion can persist via long toe flexors.
Fluoroquinolones predispose to Achilles tendinopathy and rupture—stop the drug and counsel patients on the risk.
Initial management is equinus (plantarflexed) immobilization and non-weight-bearing; definitive care can be operative or functional nonoperative rehabilitation.

cases.title: Pain on the Outside of the Foot After Rolling the Ankle
cases.chiefComplaint: Pain and swelling along the outer edge of his foot after twisting it while jogging.
cases.history: A 22-year-old recreational runner presents to the ED after he stepped off a curb and his foot rolled inward (inversion) while the ankle was plantarflexed during an evening jog. He felt immediate sharp pain along the outer border of his right midfoot and has had difficulty bearing weight since. He denies a pop in the back of the ankle, numbness, or prior foot injuries. He reports no preceding foot pain. He is otherwise healthy, takes a daily multivitamin and occasional ibuprofen, and has no known drug allergies. He is a college student, drinks on weekends, does not smoke, and uses no recreational drugs. He has no history of stress fractures, eating disorder, or metabolic bone disease. Family history is noncontributory. He runs roughly 15 miles per week in worn shoes. Review of systems is negative for fever, ankle instability symptoms, and constitutional complaints. He recalls the pain is maximal over a bony bump on the lateral side of his foot rather than over the ankle ligaments.
cases.exam: Focal tenderness, swelling, and mild ecchymosis over the base of the fifth metatarsal on the lateral border of the right foot. The point of maximal tenderness is at the proximal metatarsal diaphysis just distal to the styloid (tuberosity). There is no tenderness over the navicular, the anterior talofibular ligament, the lateral malleolus, or the base of the second metatarsal. The midfoot is stable without gross deformity. Weight-bearing is painful but possible with a limp. Sensation is intact, dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses are 2+, and capillary refill is brisk. No skin tenting or open wound. Ankle range of motion is preserved though uncomfortable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial diagnostic step?
options: MRI of the foot as the first step today
Reassurance and an elastic wrap without imaging
Ultrasound of the lateral ankle ligaments
Plain radiographs of the foot
stages.explanation: Three-view plain radiographs of the foot are the appropriate first study: tenderness over the base of the fifth metatarsal is an Ottawa foot rule indication for imaging, and films readily show fifth-metatarsal fractures and localize them by zone. MRI is unnecessary for an acute, radiographically diagnosable fracture and is reserved for occult stress injuries or nonunion evaluation. Ultrasound of lateral ankle ligaments addresses a sprain, but his maximal tenderness is over bone at the metatarsal base, not the ligaments. Reassurance without imaging is inappropriate when a bony point of maximal tenderness meets criteria for radiographs and a fracture is likely.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Foot radiographs (AP, lateral, oblique):
Transverse fracture at the base of the fifth metatarsal located at the metaphyseal–diaphyseal junction, ~1.7 cm distal to the proximal tip
Fracture line extends into the fourth–fifth intermetatarsal articulation region
No fracture of the tuberosity (styloid) itself
No displacement of major fragments; no other midfoot fracture or Lisfranc malalignment

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on the fracture location, which diagnosis is most accurate?
options: Fifth metatarsal shaft (dancer's) fracture
Jones fracture at the metaphyseal–diaphyseal junction
Lisfranc fracture-dislocation in this clinical context
Pseudo-Jones (tuberosity avulsion) fracture
