stages.explanation: A transverse fracture at the metaphyseal–diaphyseal junction of the fifth metatarsal—within roughly 1.5–2 cm of the proximal tip, often extending toward the fourth–fifth intermetatarsal joint—is a Jones fracture. The key contrast is with a pseudo-Jones (avulsion) fracture, which involves the proximal tuberosity/styloid where the peroneus brevis attaches and is more proximal; here the tuberosity is intact. A dancer's (spiral) fracture occurs in the distal shaft, not the proximal junction, and follows a different mechanism. Lisfranc injury involves the tarsometatarsal joint complex with characteristic malalignment, which is explicitly absent on these films. Correctly distinguishing a true Jones fracture from a pseudo-Jones avulsion is essential because their healing and management differ substantially.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Why does the Jones fracture warrant more cautious management than a proximal tuberosity avulsion fracture?
options: It occurs in a vascular watershed zone, giving it a high risk of delayed union or nonunion
It heals faster and needs no immobilization as initial therapy
It is usually grossly displaced and requires reduction as initial therapy
It always involves the tarsometatarsal joint as initial therapy
stages.explanation: The Jones fracture sits in a relative vascular watershed at the metaphyseal–diaphyseal junction, where blood supply from competing nutrient sources is tenuous, so it carries a notably higher risk of delayed union and nonunion than a tuberosity avulsion fracture, which lies in well-vascularized bone and typically heals with symptomatic care. Jones fractures are often minimally displaced, so displacement is not the defining concern here. They do not, by definition, involve the tarsometatarsal joint—that is a Lisfranc injury. And they do not heal faster; the watershed location makes healing slower and less predictable, which is precisely why management is more conservative or operative and why follow-up matters.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: He is a young, active runner. Which initial management plan is most appropriate for this nondisplaced Jones fracture?
options: Hard-soled shoe with immediate full weight-bearing with appropriate follow-up
Elastic wrap and return to running in one week with appropriate follow-up
Non-weight-bearing short-leg cast or boot with orthopedic follow-up
Immediate amputation referral due to nonunion risk with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: A nondisplaced Jones fracture is initially managed with non-weight-bearing immobilization (short-leg cast or boot) and prompt orthopedic follow-up; because of the high nonunion risk, intramedullary screw fixation is frequently offered to active patients and athletes to speed reliable union and return to sport. A hard-soled shoe with immediate full weight-bearing is appropriate for a tuberosity avulsion, not a Jones fracture, where early loading raises nonunion risk. An elastic wrap with rapid return to running grossly undertreats it. Amputation is absurd—nonunion is managed with fixation and bone healing strategies, never amputation for an acute fracture.

cases.diagnosis: Jones fracture of the fifth metatarsal (metaphyseal–diaphyseal junction)
pearls: A Jones fracture is at the metaphyseal–diaphyseal junction (~1.5–2 cm distal to the tip); a pseudo-Jones avulsion is at the proximal tuberosity where peroneus brevis inserts.
The Jones fracture lies in a vascular watershed zone, conferring a high risk of delayed union and nonunion.
Tuberosity avulsion fractures heal well with a hard-soled shoe and weight-bearing as tolerated; Jones fractures need non-weight-bearing immobilization.
Offer intramedullary screw fixation for Jones fractures in athletes/active patients to achieve faster, more reliable union and return to play.

cases.title: A Swollen Midfoot After a Misstep on the Stairs
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe midfoot pain and swelling after his foot got caught and twisted going down the stairs.
cases.history: A 30-year-old man presents to the ED after his foot caught on a step and twisted beneath him as he descended a staircase; he felt his body weight drive through a downward-pointing (plantarflexed) foot. He has severe pain across the top and middle of the foot, cannot bear weight, and the foot has swollen markedly over a few hours. He denies a posterior ankle pop, numbness, or prior foot problems. He is otherwise healthy, takes no regular medications, and has no known drug allergies. He works in construction, drinks occasionally, does not smoke, and uses no recreational drugs. Family history is noncontributory. On arrival he is distressed by pain that seems out of proportion to the modest bruising initially visible on the top of the foot, prompting the staff to examine the sole, where they find a band of bruising. He recalls being able to take a couple of steps right after the injury before pain and swelling made weight-bearing impossible.
cases.exam: Diffuse midfoot swelling with tenderness focused over the tarsometatarsal (Lisfranc) joint complex. There is plantar ecchymosis across the medial midfoot/arch. Pain is reproduced by passive pronation–abduction of the forefoot with the hindfoot fixed and by piano-key stress of the metatarsals. There is tenderness and a sense of instability at the base of the first and second metatarsals; the medial border of the second metatarsal does not line up smoothly with the medial border of the middle cuneiform on visual inspection. Distal pulses are 2+, sensation is intact, and the foot is warm—but compartments are tense. No open wound. Pain appears out of proportion to the visible dorsal bruising.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which physical finding should most strongly raise suspicion for a tarsometatarsal (Lisfranc) injury?
options: Tenderness over the lateral malleolus
A palpable gap behind the heel
Tenderness over the navicular tuberosity
Plantar ecchymosis of the midfoot
stages.explanation: Plantar (sole-side) ecchymosis of the midfoot is a classic and relatively specific sign of a Lisfranc injury, reflecting disruption of the tarsometatarsal ligamentous complex with bleeding tracking to the plantar surface—and pain out of proportion to dorsal findings should heighten suspicion. Lateral malleolar tenderness points to an ankle sprain or fibular fracture, not the midfoot. A palpable retrocalcaneal gap suggests an Achilles rupture. Navicular tuberosity tenderness localizes to a different midfoot structure and a different injury pattern. Recognizing plantar bruising is crucial because Lisfranc injuries are frequently and dangerously missed on first evaluation, and a delayed diagnosis predisposes to chronic instability and post-traumatic arthritis.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial imaging?
options: MRI as the first-line study as the primary diagnosis
Weight-bearing AP, lateral,
Non-weight-bearing AP foot film only in this clinical context
Ankle radiographs as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Weight-bearing radiographs (AP, lateral, oblique), ideally with comparison views of the contralateral foot, are the best initial imaging because loading stresses the Lisfranc complex and unmasks subtle diastasis and malalignment that non-weight-bearing films miss. The diagnostic clue is loss of alignment between the medial border of the second metatarsal and the medial border of the middle cuneiform, or a fleck (avulsion) sign. A single non-weight-bearing AP view is insensitive and a common reason these injuries are missed. MRI/CT are valuable adjuncts—CT for fracture detail, MRI for ligament integrity—but are not the routine first study. Ankle films image the wrong region.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Weight-bearing AP foot radiograph: 3 mm diastasis between the bases of the first and second metatarsals
Malalignment of the medial border of the 2nd metatarsal with the medial border of the middle cuneiform
Small bony 'fleck sign' avulsion in the first intermetatarsal space
Lateral view: dorsal displacement of the metatarsal bases
CT obtained for surgical planning: comminuted fracture at the 2nd metatarsal base with tarsometatarsal incongruity

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: The foot is tensely swollen with pain out of proportion to exam and worsening pain on passive toe extension. What complication must be evaluated and managed urgently?
options: Deep vein thrombosis
Cellulitis as the leading concern
Acute compartment syndrome of the foot
Gout flare as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Pain out of proportion, a tensely swollen foot, and pain with passive stretch of the toes are hallmark warning signs of acute compartment syndrome, a limb-threatening emergency that requires urgent compartment pressure measurement and emergent fasciotomy if confirmed. High-energy midfoot injuries like Lisfranc fracture-dislocations can precipitate it. DVT does not cause pain on passive toe stretch acutely after trauma and is not the immediate threat. A gout flare would be atypical in this traumatic context and would not explain the mechanism or fleck sign. Cellulitis is an infection that develops over days with fever and erythema, not within hours of trauma.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Once compartment syndrome is addressed, what is the definitive treatment for this displaced Lisfranc injury?
options: Closed reduction and a short-leg cast alone as initial therapy
Elastic wrap and outpatient physical therapy as initial therapy
Open reduction and internal fixation to restore tarsometatarsal alignment
Walking boot with weight-bearing as tolerated as initial therapy
stages.explanation: A displaced or unstable Lisfranc injury requires surgical anatomic restoration—open reduction with internal fixation (or primary arthrodesis in certain comminuted/ligamentous patterns)—because precise tarsometatarsal alignment is essential to prevent chronic instability, deformity, and post-traumatic arthritis. A walking boot with weight-bearing would allow the displacement to persist and lead to disabling arthrosis. Closed reduction with casting alone rarely maintains the reduction of an unstable midfoot and is inadequate for displacement of this degree. An elastic wrap with PT grossly undertreats a surgical injury. Only a truly nondisplaced, stable injury could be considered for nonoperative immobilization—not this case.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which complication should he be counseled about as the most common long-term sequela even after appropriate anatomic fixation?
options: Post-traumatic midfoot arthritis
Avascular necrosis of the talus
Permanent loss of all foot sensation
Recurrent compartment syndrome
stages.explanation: Post-traumatic arthritis of the tarsometatarsal joints is the most common long-term sequela of Lisfranc injuries; even with anatomic reduction and fixation, a meaningful proportion of patients develop midfoot arthrosis, and non-anatomic reduction markedly increases this risk—hence the emphasis on precise alignment and the option of primary arthrodesis. Permanent global sensory loss is not an expected outcome of an isolated midfoot ligamentous/bony injury with intact nerves. Compartment syndrome is an acute, one-time emergency, not a recurrent chronic complication. Avascular necrosis of the talus follows talar neck fractures and disrupted talar blood supply, not a Lisfranc tarsometatarsal injury, so it is not the relevant long-term risk here for this patient.

cases.diagnosis: Lisfranc (tarsometatarsal) fracture-dislocation
pearls: Plantar midfoot ecchymosis and pain out of proportion to dorsal findings are red flags for a Lisfranc injury, which is easily missed on standard non-weight-bearing films.
Look for malalignment of the second metatarsal base with the middle cuneiform and a 'fleck sign'; obtain weight-bearing and comparison views.
High-energy midfoot injuries can precipitate acute compartment syndrome—pain out of proportion and pain on passive toe stretch warrant urgent evaluation.
Displaced or unstable Lisfranc injuries require anatomic surgical fixation (ORIF or primary arthrodesis) to prevent post-traumatic arthritis and chronic instability.

cases.title: Both Heels Crushed After a Fall From a Roof
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe heel pain and inability to stand after landing on his feet from a roof fall.
cases.history: A 38-year-old roofer presents to the ED after falling approximately 12 feet from a roof and landing on his feet on concrete. He has severe pain in both heels and his lower back, and he cannot bear weight. He denies head injury or loss of consciousness and has no abdominal pain, but he reports mid-lower back pain in addition to the heel pain. He is otherwise healthy, takes no regular medications, and has no known drug allergies. He works in construction, smokes half a pack daily, drinks occasionally, and uses no recreational drugs. Family history is noncontributory. He was wearing work boots at the time. He has had no prior foot or spine injuries. On secondary survey he confirms tenderness in his thoracolumbar spine that he initially did not mention because the heel pain was dominant. Review of systems is otherwise negative for chest pain, dyspnea, numbness, or weakness in the legs.
cases.exam: Both heels are markedly swollen, ecchymotic, and tender with a broadened, flattened heel contour; ecchymosis extends to the soles and arches. Palpation of the calcaneus is exquisitely painful bilaterally; the patient cannot bear weight. There is focal tenderness on palpation of the lower thoracic/upper lumbar spinous processes. Lower-extremity strength is 5/5, sensation is intact, and pulses are 2+ with brisk capillary refill; no skin tenting or open wounds. Compartments of the feet are swollen but soft on initial exam. Straight-leg raise is deferred due to pain. No saddle anesthesia and normal rectal tone. Abdomen is soft and nontender.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given the axial-loading mechanism (fall from height onto the feet) and his back tenderness, which associated injury must be specifically evaluated?
options: Patellar fracture
Cervical spine fracture
Distal radius fracture
Thoracolumbar spine fracture
stages.explanation: Axial loading from a fall onto the feet transmits force up the skeleton, so calcaneal fractures are classically associated with thoracolumbar spine fractures (especially burst fractures); his lumbar tenderness mandates dedicated spine evaluation and imaging. Cervical fractures are more typical of head-first or hyperflexion mechanisms, not a feet-first axial load. Distal radius fractures occur with outstretched-hand falls, not feet-first landings. Patellar fractures result from direct knee trauma or forceful quadriceps contraction, not this feet-first mechanism. Failing to screen the spine after a calcaneal fracture is a classic missed-injury pitfall, so any patient with a calcaneal fracture from a fall warrants dedicated thoracolumbar evaluation.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate imaging to confirm and characterize the heel injury?
options: Ultrasound of the heel as the primary diagnosis
Bone scan as the primary diagnosis
MRI of the foot as the first study as the primary diagnosis
Foot radiographs , with CT to characterize the fracture
stages.explanation: Plain radiographs—a lateral view (to measure Böhler angle) and an axial (Harris) view—are the initial study for a suspected calcaneal fracture, and CT is the standard follow-up to characterize intra-articular involvement and plan surgery. The lateral film lets you measure Böhler angle, which flattens (decreases) with depressed calcaneal fractures. MRI is not first-line for acute bony fractures and adds little for fracture classification here. Ultrasound cannot adequately assess this complex bone. A bone scan is for occult or stress injuries, not an obvious acute fracture, and lacks the anatomic detail needed for management.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Lateral foot radiograph: depressed intra-articular calcaneal fracture
Böhler angle 12° (normal ~20–40°), indicating loss of calcaneal height
Axial (Harris) view: widened, shortened heel with comminution
CT calcaneus: comminuted intra-articular fracture involving the posterior facet (Sanders type II)
Thoracolumbar CT: L1 compression fracture without retropulsion or neurologic compromise

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: A reduced Böhler angle on the lateral radiograph indicates what?
options: An avulsion of the Achilles insertion only
A ligamentous ankle sprain without fracture
Loss of calcaneal height from a depressed/comminuted fracture
A normal, reassuring finding as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Böhler angle is formed by lines along the posterior and anterior aspects of the calcaneus on the lateral view; it normally measures about 20–40°. A decreased angle (here 12°) reflects collapse/depression of the posterior facet and loss of calcaneal height, signaling a significant intra-articular calcaneal fracture. It is not specific for an isolated Achilles avulsion, which produces a different bony-fragment pattern. A flattened angle is the opposite of normal and is definitely not reassuring. A ligamentous sprain involves no bony angle change. Measuring Böhler angle is a quick, high-yield way to detect calcaneal fractures that might otherwise be underappreciated.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Beyond fracture-specific care, which immediate complication of these high-energy calcaneal fractures must be monitored for?
options: Foot compartment syndrome
Plantar fasciitis as monotherapy
Charcot neuroarthropathy
Septic arthritis of the ankle
stages.explanation: High-energy calcaneal fractures cause substantial swelling and are associated with foot compartment syndrome, which can lead to clawing of the toes and chronic disability if missed; serial neurovascular checks and a low threshold for compartment pressure measurement are essential. Septic arthritis is an infectious complication that develops over time and is not the immediate post-traumatic concern in a closed injury. Charcot neuroarthropathy is a chronic, neuropathy-driven process (e.g., in diabetics), not an acute traumatic complication. Plantar fasciitis is a chronic overuse condition unrelated to acute fracture management. Soft-tissue swelling also dictates timing of any operative fixation.

cases.diagnosis: Intra-articular calcaneal fracture (with associated L1 vertebral fracture)
pearls: Calcaneal fractures result from axial loading (falls from height) and are classically associated with thoracolumbar spine fractures—always image the spine.
Measure Böhler angle on the lateral radiograph; a value below ~20° indicates loss of calcaneal height from a depressed fracture.
CT characterizes intra-articular (Sanders) involvement and guides operative planning.
Watch for foot compartment syndrome after high-energy calcaneal fractures, and time surgery to soft-tissue swelling.

cases.title: Locked, Painful Shoulder After a Seizure
cases.chiefComplaint: He has severe right shoulder pain and cannot turn his arm outward after a witnessed seizure this morning.
cases.history: A 42-year-old man with a 12-year history of epilepsy is brought to the ED after a generalized tonic-clonic seizure at home witnessed by his wife. On waking he noted immediate right shoulder pain and an inability to move the arm normally. He denies prior shoulder dislocation or injury and reports no direct fall or blow during the event. Past medical history includes epilepsy, managed with levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily; he admits to missing two recent doses. He has no prior shoulder surgery. He takes no anticoagulants. He has no known drug allergies. He works as an accountant, drinks alcohol socially, and does not use tobacco or recreational drugs. Family history is noncontributory. He reports the arm is held tightly against his body and feels "stuck." There is no chest pain, headache, tongue laceration noted on his own report, or new weakness in the legs. He is right-hand dominant and is very anxious about the deformity and pain, rating it 8/10 and worse with any attempted movement.
cases.exam: The right arm is held adducted and internally rotated against the trunk. The anterior shoulder appears flattened with a prominent posterior fullness and a prominent coracoid anteriorly. Active and passive external rotation are completely blocked; the patient cannot supinate the forearm with the elbow flexed at the side. There is no anterior axillary fullness. The deltoid contour is asymmetric. Distal pulses are intact and the hand is warm. Sensation over the lateral deltoid (axillary nerve) is intact, and finger flexion/extension and intrinsic function are normal. No open wounds. The contralateral shoulder is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which examination finding most strongly suggests a POSTERIOR rather than anterior glenohumeral dislocation?
options: The arm is held in slight abduction and external rotation in this clinical context
The patient can passively externally rotate but not abduct
There is prominent anterior axillary fullness with a palpable humeral head
The arm is locked in internal rotation with complete loss of external rotation
stages.explanation: Posterior dislocations classically lock the arm in adduction and internal rotation with a mechanical block to external rotation and supination, exactly as seen here. This pattern, especially after a seizure or electrocution, is the hallmark. An arm held in abduction/external rotation (option B) and anterior axillary fullness with a palpable head (option D) describe ANTERIOR dislocation, the far more common type. Preserved passive external rotation (option E) would argue against a posterior dislocation, since the locked internal rotation is the key block. Posterior dislocations are missed in over half of cases on initial presentation because the AP film can look deceptively normal, making recognition of this exam pattern critical.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which imaging view is MOST important to confirm the suspected posterior dislocation?
options: AP view of the shoulder alone
Axillary view of the shoulder
Standing chest radiograph
AP view of the clavicle
stages.explanation: The axillary lateral view (or a scapular-Y view if pain prevents abduction) directly shows the humeral head sitting posterior to the glenoid and is the single most useful film for confirming posterior dislocation. The AP view alone (option A) frequently misses posterior dislocations or shows only subtle signs such as the 'lightbulb sign' (fixed internal rotation makes the head look round/symmetric) and the 'rim sign' (widened glenohumeral space). A clavicle film (option C) and a chest radiograph (option D) do not adequately profile the glenohumeral joint. CT is the gold standard for defining an associated reverse Hill-Sachs lesion but the axillary view is the key plain-film step.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
AP shoulder radiograph: humeral head appears rounded and symmetric ('lightbulb sign') in fixed internal rotation; glenohumeral joint space appears widened (rim sign >6 mm)
Axillary view: humeral head lies posterior to the glenoid, confirming posterior dislocation
Reverse Hill-Sachs (McLaughlin) impaction defect noted on the anteromedial humeral head, estimated <20% of articular surface
No associated proximal humerus fracture
Neurovascular structures grossly intact on exam

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The reduction is most appropriately performed how, given the imaging and an acute injury with a small (<20%) reverse Hill-Sachs lesion?
options: Sling immobilization alone without reduction and outpatient follow-up as initial therapy
Closed reduction under procedural sedation with axial traction, gentle adduction,
Immediate open surgical reduction in the operating room as initial therapy
Closed reduction by forced external rotation with the arm abducted to 90° as initial therapy
stages.explanation: An acute posterior dislocation with a small impaction defect should undergo prompt closed reduction under procedural sedation: apply axial traction on the adducted arm, gently lift/push the humeral head anteriorly back into the glenoid, then carefully externally rotate once disengaged. Open reduction (option A) is reserved for irreducible dislocations, large reverse Hill-Sachs lesions (>25-50%), or associated fractures. Doing nothing and immobilizing (option C) leaves a locked, unstable joint and risks chronic dislocation and avascular changes. Forced external rotation in abduction (option D) is the maneuver pattern for anterior dislocation and can fracture the humeral head when applied to a locked posterior dislocation engaged on the glenoid rim.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After successful reduction with a concentric, stable joint, what is the most appropriate immobilization and disposition?
options: Admit for emergent surgical stabilization regardless of stability with appropriate follow-up
Immobilize in slight external rotation/neutral, confirm reduction with post-reduction films, optimize seizure control,
No immobilization; begin immediate aggressive active range-of-motion with appropriate follow-up
Immobilize in a standard internal-rotation sling for 6 weeks and discharge with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: After reduction, immobilize in neutral to slight external rotation (a standard internal-rotation sling can re-engage the defect and promote re-dislocation in posterior instability), obtain post-reduction radiographs to confirm a concentric joint, and address the underlying seizure trigger by optimizing antiepileptic therapy and counseling on adherence. Routine internal-rotation slinging (option A) is suboptimal for posterior instability. No immobilization with immediate aggressive motion (option C) risks recurrent dislocation before soft tissues heal. Emergent surgery regardless of stability (option D) is unnecessary when the joint is concentric and stable; surgery is reserved for instability, large lesions, or fractures. Recurrence is high if seizures remain uncontrolled.

cases.diagnosis: Posterior glenohumeral (shoulder) dislocation following a seizure
pearls: Suspect posterior shoulder dislocation in any patient with a seizure, electrocution, or high-energy trauma whose arm is locked in internal rotation with no external rotation.
The AP film can look near-normal: look for the 'lightbulb sign' (head fixed in internal rotation), the rim sign, and an empty glenoid; always get an axillary or scapular-Y view.
A reverse Hill-Sachs (McLaughlin) lesion is the posterior-dislocation analog of the Hill-Sachs lesion; large defects (>25%) may require surgical reconstruction.
Immobilize in neutral/external rotation after reduction and treat the underlying cause (seizure control) to prevent recurrence.

cases.title: Bump on the Top of the Shoulder After a Bike Crash
cases.chiefComplaint: He has pain and a visible bump at the top of his left shoulder after falling off his mountain bike onto the shoulder.
cases.history: A 26-year-old recreational cyclist presents to the ED two hours after a mountain-bike crash in which he went over the handlebars and landed directly on the point of his left (nondominant) shoulder with the arm tucked at his side. He felt immediate pain over the top of the shoulder and noticed a bump developing. He denies head strike, loss of consciousness, neck pain, or numbness/tingling in the arm. Pain is 6/10, worse with reaching across his body and with lifting the arm overhead. Past medical history is unremarkable. He takes no regular medications and used ibuprofen 400 mg once before arrival. He has no allergies. He works as a graphic designer, drinks alcohol on weekends, does not smoke, and uses no recreational drugs. Family history is noncontributory. He is otherwise healthy and active, plays in a recreational soccer league, and is anxious about how long he will be out of sport. He can make a fist and move his fingers without difficulty and has no chest wall pain or shortness of breath.
cases.exam: There is a visible, tender step-off at the left acromioclavicular (AC) joint with prominence of the distal clavicle and localized swelling. The distal clavicle is tender to palpation; cross-body adduction reproduces pain over the AC joint. The clavicle reduces with downward pressure but springs back up when released. The skin over the prominence is intact and not tented or threatened. There is no sternoclavicular tenderness. Distal neurovascular exam is intact: radial pulse 2+, capillary refill brisk, normal sensation and motor function in all distributions. Shoulder active elevation is limited by pain but the glenohumeral joint is reducible and stable. No chest wall crepitus.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which mechanism and finding best fit an acromioclavicular joint separation?
options: A fall onto an outstretched hand with anterior shoulder fullness as the first step today
A direct blow to the point of the shoulder with a tender step-off at the distal clavicle
A seizure with the arm locked in internal rotation as the first step today
A high-energy chest impact with sternoclavicular tenderness and dysphagia
stages.explanation: AC joint separations classically result from a direct downward force on the point (acromion) of the shoulder with the arm adducted, driving the scapula down relative to the clavicle and disrupting the AC and coracoclavicular ligaments; the hallmark is a tender step-off over the distal clavicle, exactly the described scenario. A fall onto an outstretched hand with anterior fullness (option A) suggests anterior glenohumeral dislocation. A seizure with the arm locked in internal rotation (option C) suggests posterior shoulder dislocation. A chest impact with sternoclavicular tenderness and dysphagia (option D) suggests a posterior sternoclavicular dislocation, a separate and potentially airway-threatening injury.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial imaging to evaluate and grade this injury?
options: Bilateral AC joint radiographs , comparing both sides
Ultrasound of the biceps tendon as the primary diagnosis
CT angiography of the chest as the primary diagnosis
MRI of the shoulder as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Plain radiographs of both AC joints, including a dedicated Zanca view (10-15° cephalic tilt), are the standard first step; comparing the injured to the uninjured side lets you measure the coracoclavicular (CC) distance and assess displacement for Rockwood grading. MRI (option A) is rarely needed acutely and adds cost without changing initial management. CT angiography of the chest (option C) is for suspected vascular injury or posterior sternoclavicular dislocation, not this isolated AC injury. Ultrasound of the biceps tendon (option D) evaluates a different structure entirely and would not grade an AC separation.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Zanca view, left shoulder: distal clavicle elevated relative to the acromion with a visible step-off
Coracoclavicular distance: 13 mm on the left vs 8 mm on the right (increased, but <25% increase relative to normal range)
AC joint widening present; clavicle does not appear posteriorly displaced into the trapezius on axillary view
No associated clavicle, coracoid, or proximal humerus fracture
Glenohumeral joint congruent

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Using the Rockwood classification, the increased AC space and CC distance with an elevated distal clavicle that reduces with pressure, no posterior displacement, and CC widening less than 25% over normal is most consistent with which grade?
options: Type IV (posterior displacement of the clavicle into the trapezius)
Type I (sprain, normal radiographs) as the primary diagnosis
Type III (complete AC and CC disruption with 25-100% CC widening)
Type II (AC ligament disruption, CC intact, slight elevation)
stages.explanation: A Type II injury involves disruption of the AC ligaments with the CC ligaments intact, producing slight superior elevation of the distal clavicle and mild widening but less than the 25% CC increase that defines Type III. Type I (option A) shows a sprain with normal radiographs and no step-off. Type III (option B) involves complete AC and CC disruption with CC widening of 25-100% and a more obvious, less reducible deformity. Type IV (option D) is defined by posterior displacement of the clavicle into or through the trapezius, seen on the axillary view, which is absent here. Grading drives management decisions.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management for this Rockwood Type II AC separation?
options: Long-arm cast immobilization for 6 weeks as initial therapy
Sling for comfort, ice
Urgent open reduction and ligament reconstruction as initial therapy
Closed reduction under sedation and figure-of-eight bracing as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Type I and II AC separations are managed nonoperatively: a sling for comfort, ice, analgesia, and progressive range-of-motion as pain subsides, with return to activity once strength and motion normalize, usually within weeks. Most do well without surgery. Urgent surgery (option A) is reserved for Types IV-VI and selected Type III injuries in high-demand patients; it is not indicated for Type II. Closed reduction with figure-of-eight bracing (option C) is a clavicle-shaft-fracture technique and does not hold an AC reduction. Long-arm casting (option D) immobilizes the wrong joints, risks stiffness, and provides no benefit for an AC separation.

cases.diagnosis: Acromioclavicular joint separation, Rockwood Type II
pearls: AC separations follow a direct blow to the point of the shoulder with the arm adducted; the tender distal-clavicle step-off and painful cross-body adduction are key signs.
Order bilateral AC (Zanca) views and measure the coracoclavicular distance against the uninjured side to apply the Rockwood classification.
Types I-II (and most Type III) are managed nonoperatively with a sling and early motion; Types IV-VI (and select Type III) warrant surgical referral.
Always check the axillary view for posterior clavicle displacement (Type IV), which mandates surgery and can be missed on the AP film.

cases.title: A Sudden Bulge in the Arm After Lifting a Box
cases.chiefComplaint: He felt a pop in his upper arm while lifting and now has a bulge in the muscle with mild soreness.
cases.history: A 55-year-old warehouse supervisor presents to clinic three days after feeling a sudden 'pop' in his right (dominant) upper arm while lifting a heavy box from a low shelf. He felt brief sharp pain, then noticed a bulge in the lower part of his upper arm and some bruising. The acute pain has largely resolved to a dull ache; he has mild weakness with twisting motions but can still use the arm for daily tasks. He has a several-month history of intermittent anterior shoulder aching, which he attributed to 'getting older.' Past medical history includes well-controlled hypertension (lisinopril 10 mg daily) and hyperlipidemia (atorvastatin 20 mg daily). He took a 7-day course of a fluoroquinolone for a urinary infection two months ago. He has no allergies. He smokes half a pack of cigarettes daily, drinks beer on weekends, and denies recreational drugs. Family history is noncontributory. He is concerned about the deformity and asks whether he needs surgery. He denies numbness, tingling, or hand weakness, and the elbow moves normally.
cases.exam: There is a soft-tissue bulge of the biceps muscle belly in the distal anterior arm that becomes more prominent with elbow flexion ('Popeye' deformity). Ecchymosis tracks down the medial arm. The anterior shoulder is mildly tender over the bicipital groove. Elbow flexion strength is near-normal and supination strength is only mildly reduced compared with the left. The distal biceps tendon is palpable and intact in the antecubital fossa, and the hook test is negative. There is no antecubital swelling. Shoulder range of motion is full though slightly painful at end-range. Neurovascular exam of the forearm and hand is normal with intact radial pulse and sensation.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which feature best distinguishes a proximal (long head) biceps rupture from a distal biceps tendon rupture?
options: The muscle bulge migrates proximally (toward the shoulder) and the hook test is positive
There is fixed flexion contracture of the ring and little fingers
The muscle bulge migrates distally and the hook test is negative
There is loss of all elbow flexion and a positive Tinel sign in this clinical context
stages.explanation: When the long head of biceps ruptures proximally, the unanchored muscle belly retracts distally toward the elbow, creating the classic 'Popeye' bulge low in the arm, and the distal tendon remains intact so the hook test is negative, exactly as described. A proximally migrating bulge with a positive hook test (option B) indicates a DISTAL tendon rupture, where the tendon detaches from the radial tuberosity and the muscle retracts up the arm. Complete loss of elbow flexion with a Tinel sign (option C) does not occur because the short head and brachialis preserve flexion. A fixed flexion contracture of the ulnar digits (option D) describes Dupuytren contracture, unrelated to biceps rupture.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Which factor in this patient's history is most relevant to the underlying mechanism of a proximal long head biceps rupture?
options: His occupation as a supervisor as the immediate next step
Chronic anterior shoulder aching suggesting pre-existing rotator cuff/bicipital tendinopathy
His hypertension treated with lisinopril as the immediate next step
His weekend beer consumption as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Proximal long head biceps ruptures almost always occur through a degenerated, tendinopathic tendon; the chronic anterior shoulder aching here points to pre-existing bicipital and likely rotator-cuff degeneration that predisposed the tendon to fail with a modest load. This association is why proximal ruptures are common in older patients and frequently coexist with rotator cuff disease. Hypertension and lisinopril (option A) and alcohol use (option C) are not established mechanisms. His job title (option D) is not itself causative, though repetitive overhead labor can contribute to tendinopathy. Notably, fluoroquinolone exposure is more classically linked to tendon rupture (especially Achilles), but the dominant driver here is chronic tendon degeneration.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management for this 55-year-old with a proximal long head biceps rupture and only mild functional deficit?
options: Urgent surgical reattachment within 2 weeks to preserve strength
Immediate tenodesis regardless of symptoms to correct the deformity
Nonoperative management with analgesia, activity modification,
Long-arm casting in 90° of flexion for 6 weeks as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Proximal long head biceps ruptures are usually managed nonoperatively because the short head and brachialis preserve most elbow flexion and supination strength; the main residual is a cosmetic 'Popeye' bulge and possibly mild fatigue with repetitive supination. Reassurance, analgesia, activity modification, and therapy restore function in most patients. Urgent surgical reattachment within 2 weeks (option A) is the rule for DISTAL biceps ruptures, not proximal. Casting (option C) causes stiffness without benefit. Tenodesis purely for cosmesis (option D) is reserved for younger patients, laborers, or those with persistent pain/cramping who specifically desire it, and is not the default.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Given the proximal rupture is often associated with rotator cuff pathology, what additional evaluation is most appropriate?
options: Bone density (DEXA) scan as the immediate next step
Vascular ultrasound of the subclavian artery in this setting
Nerve conduction studies of the upper extremity in this setting
Examination and, if indicated, imaging of the rotator cuff
stages.explanation: Because proximal long head biceps ruptures so commonly coexist with rotator cuff tendinopathy or tears, the appropriate next step is a focused rotator cuff examination (impingement signs, cuff strength testing) with MRI or ultrasound if cuff pathology is suspected, since a concurrent symptomatic cuff tear may itself warrant treatment. Nerve conduction studies (option A) are for suspected neuropathies, not this tendon injury. Subclavian vascular ultrasound (option C) is unrelated. A DEXA scan (option D) assesses bone density and has no role in evaluating a degenerative tendon rupture in this context.

cases.diagnosis: Proximal (long head) biceps tendon rupture
pearls: A proximal long head biceps rupture produces a 'Popeye' bulge that sits LOW in the arm because the muscle retracts distally; the distal tendon and hook test remain intact.
These ruptures occur through degenerated tendons and frequently signal underlying rotator cuff disease, which should be evaluated.
Management is usually nonoperative: elbow flexion and supination strength are largely preserved by the short head and brachialis.
Contrast with distal biceps rupture, where the bulge migrates proximally, the hook test is positive, and prompt surgery is needed to restore supination strength.

cases.title: Weak Twisting Grip After a Heavy Lift
cases.chiefComplaint: He has pain and weakness at the front of his elbow after his arm was suddenly forced straight while lifting.
cases.history: A 48-year-old construction foreman presents to clinic two days after a heavy load he was lifting unexpectedly dropped, suddenly forcing his flexed right (dominant) elbow into extension against resistance. He felt a sharp tearing sensation and pain in the front of the elbow and a 'pop.' Since then he has bruising in the antecubital fossa and the medial forearm and notes weakness when turning a screwdriver or a doorknob. Past medical history is notable for type 2 diabetes (metformin 1000 mg twice daily) and tobacco use; he smokes one pack daily. He briefly used anabolic steroids in his thirties. He takes no anticoagulants and has no allergies. He drinks alcohol occasionally and denies other drug use. Family history is noncontributory. He is right-hand dominant, works in heavy manual labor, and is highly concerned about returning to full-strength work. He denies numbness or tingling in the hand and reports the elbow still bends, but the twisting weakness is significant. He has had no prior elbow injury or surgery.
cases.exam: There is swelling and ecchymosis in the antecubital fossa extending along the medial forearm. The normal cord-like distal biceps tendon cannot be palpated in the antecubital fossa, and the examiner's finger cannot 'hook' a tendon when the patient actively supinates (positive hook test). There is a proximally retracted, balled-up biceps muscle belly higher in the arm. Resisted supination is markedly weak and painful; resisted elbow flexion is mildly weak. There is no fixed flexion contracture. Distal neurovascular exam is intact with a normal radial pulse, brisk capillary refill, and intact sensation including the lateral antebrachial and median distributions.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which bedside test most specifically confirms a complete distal biceps tendon rupture?
options: Hook test
Speed test (resisted forward flexion of the shoulder) in this clinical context
Spurling maneuver of the cervical spine as the primary diagnosis
Yergason test for biceps stability in the groove as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: The hook test is the most specific bedside maneuver for distal biceps rupture: with the elbow flexed 90° and the forearm supinated, the examiner sweeps a finger from lateral to medial under the distal biceps tendon. An intact tendon allows the finger to 'hook' a taut cord; a complete rupture leaves nothing to hook, as in this patient. The Speed (option A) and Yergason (option C) tests assess the proximal/long head of biceps in the bicipital groove, not the distal insertion. The Spurling maneuver (option D) evaluates cervical nerve root compression and is irrelevant to a distal tendon injury. The positive hook test plus weak supination strongly indicates a complete distal rupture.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Which imaging study best confirms the diagnosis and assesses tendon retraction when the exam is equivocal?
options: CT angiography of the brachial artery
Plain radiograph of the elbow
Bone scan of the upper extremity
MRI of the elbow (or ultrasound)
stages.explanation: MRI (often with the elbow in flexion, abduction, and supination) or ultrasound best visualizes the distal biceps tendon, confirms a complete versus partial tear, and quantifies retraction, which guides surgical planning. Plain radiographs (option A) may show an avulsion fragment from the radial tuberosity or rule out fracture but cannot directly evaluate the tendon. CT angiography (option C) is for vascular injury, not tendon assessment, and the neurovascular exam here is intact. A bone scan (option D) lacks the soft-tissue resolution needed and has no role. When the hook test and history are classic, imaging mainly confirms and characterizes the tear before surgery.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Elbow radiograph: no fracture; possible small avulsion irregularity at the radial tuberosity
MRI elbow: complete rupture of the distal biceps tendon at its insertion on the radial tuberosity
Tendon retracted approximately 3 cm proximally with surrounding hematoma
Brachialis intact; no other ligamentous injury
Neurovascular structures intact

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management for this active 48-year-old laborer with a complete, retracted distal biceps rupture?
options: Early surgical repair , ideally within 2-3 weeks
Corticosteroid injection into the antecubital fossa as initial therapy
Long-term bracing in supination for 12 weeks as initial therapy
Nonoperative management with a sling and physical therapy as monotherapy
stages.explanation: Complete distal biceps ruptures in active patients are repaired surgically, ideally within 2-3 weeks before the tendon retracts and scars, to restore supination strength (lost ~40-50% if untreated) and flexion strength (lost ~30%). This patient is a manual laborer who depends on twisting strength, making early repair the clear choice. Nonoperative care (option A) is reserved for elderly, low-demand, or high-risk surgical patients who accept the strength deficit. Prolonged bracing (option C) does not reattach the tendon and causes stiffness. A corticosteroid injection (option D) is contraindicated, would not heal the rupture, and could worsen tendon quality. Delay beyond a few weeks complicates repair.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: After distal biceps repair, which complication is most characteristically associated with the surgical approach?
options: Axillary nerve palsy as the leading concern
Compartment syndrome of the hand as the leading concern
Lateral antebrachial cutaneous
Ulnar nerve transection as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Distal biceps repair (especially via the anterior or two-incision approaches near the radial tuberosity) carries characteristic risks to the lateral antebrachial cutaneous nerve (sensory) and the posterior interosseous nerve (PIN, motor branch of the radial nerve), plus heterotopic ossification/radioulnar synostosis, particularly with two-incision techniques. The ulnar nerve (option A) lies medially at the elbow and is more at risk in cubital tunnel or medial-sided procedures, not the anterior tuberosity approach. The axillary nerve (option C) is at risk in shoulder surgery, not the elbow. Hand compartment syndrome (option D) is not a typical complication of this repair. Counseling on PIN and sensory nerve risk is standard before surgery.

cases.diagnosis: Distal biceps tendon rupture (complete, retracted)
pearls: Distal biceps rupture follows a sudden eccentric load (forced extension of a flexed, loaded elbow) and produces a positive hook test with weak, painful supination.
In a distal rupture the muscle retracts PROXIMALLY (toward the shoulder); contrast with the proximal long head rupture, where the Popeye bulge sits low near the elbow.
Active patients warrant early surgical reattachment (within ~2-3 weeks) to preserve supination strength, which is lost ~40-50% if untreated.
Counsel patients on risk to the lateral antebrachial cutaneous nerve and the posterior interosseous nerve (PIN), plus heterotopic ossification, after repair.

cases.title: Numb Ring and Little Fingers in a Desk Worker
cases.chiefComplaint: He has numbness and tingling in his ring and little fingers, worse when his elbow is bent.
cases.history: A 50-year-old accountant presents to clinic with several months of intermittent numbness and tingling along the ring and little fingers of his right (dominant) hand. Symptoms are worst at night and when he holds his phone to his ear or rests his bent elbows on his desk for long periods; he often wakes with the hand 'asleep.' He has noticed mild clumsiness, dropping his coffee cup and fumbling small objects, and a vague aching along the inner elbow. Past medical history includes type 2 diabetes (metformin 1000 mg twice daily, glycemic control fair) and obesity. He sustained a childhood fracture of the same elbow. He takes no anticoagulants and has no allergies. He works long hours at a computer with his elbows flexed, drinks alcohol occasionally, and does not smoke. Family history is noncontributory. He denies neck pain, radiation from the neck, thumb or index finger symptoms, and lower-extremity complaints. He is worried about losing hand function for his detailed work and asks whether surgery is necessary.
cases.exam: Sensation is diminished to light touch over the little finger and the ulnar half of the ring finger, including the dorsal ulnar hand (dorsal ulnar cutaneous distribution). Tinel sign is positive over the cubital tunnel (behind the medial epicondyle), and the elbow flexion-compression test reproduces tingling within 30 seconds. There is mild weakness of the first dorsal interosseous and abductor digiti minimi; a faint Froment sign is present (thumb IP flexion when pinching paper). No clawing at rest. The cubital tunnel is mildly tender; the ulnar nerve does not visibly subluxate with flexion. Phalen and median Tinel at the wrist are negative. Cervical spine range of motion is full and Spurling maneuver is negative. Distal pulses intact.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: The distribution of sensory loss INCLUDING the dorsal ulnar hand most strongly localizes the lesion to which site?
options: The median nerve at the carpal tunnel
The ulnar nerve at Guyon's canal (wrist)
The C8-T1 nerve roots in the neck
The ulnar nerve at the elbow
stages.explanation: The dorsal ulnar cutaneous branch arises from the ulnar nerve about 5-7 cm proximal to the wrist, so numbness over the DORSAL ulnar hand indicates the lesion is proximal to the wrist, at the elbow (cubital tunnel). Compression at Guyon's canal (option A) spares the dorsal ulnar hand because that branch has already departed, helping distinguish the two. Carpal tunnel/median nerve (option C) affects the thumb, index, middle, and radial ring finger, not the little finger. A C8-T1 radiculopathy (option D) is possible but would typically involve neck symptoms, a positive Spurling sign, and a broader myotomal pattern; the negative cervical exam and positive cubital-tunnel Tinel point to the elbow.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Which test best confirms the diagnosis and localizes the level of ulnar nerve compromise?
options: Fasting lipid panel in this setting
Plain radiograph of the cervical spine
Nerve conduction studies
MRI of the brain as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Nerve conduction studies with EMG are the confirmatory test: they demonstrate slowed ulnar conduction velocity across the elbow segment, localizing the compression to the cubital tunnel and grading severity, which guides treatment. Cervical spine radiographs (option A) might be considered if radiculopathy were suspected, but the clinical picture and dorsal ulnar involvement point to the elbow. Brain MRI (option C) is irrelevant to a focal peripheral mononeuropathy. A lipid panel (option D) does not evaluate nerve compression. Electrodiagnostics also help exclude a more diffuse polyneuropathy, relevant given his diabetes, and establish a baseline before considering surgery.

stages.label: ELECTRODIAGNOSTICS
stages.content:
Motor nerve conduction: ulnar conduction velocity slowed across the elbow segment (38 m/s) vs forearm segment (52 m/s)
Conduction block/focal slowing localized to the retrocondylar (cubital tunnel) region
Sensory: reduced ulnar sensory nerve action potential amplitude
EMG: mild chronic denervation changes in first dorsal interosseous; abductor pollicis brevis (median) normal
Median studies at the wrist normal (no carpal tunnel syndrome)

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this patient with mild-to-moderate cubital tunnel syndrome and no high-grade motor loss, what is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Wrist splinting in neutral and carpal tunnel release as initial therapy
Corticosteroid injection into the cubital tunnel as first-line therapy as initial therapy
Immediate submuscular ulnar nerve transposition as initial therapy
Activity modification, avoid prolonged/sustained elbow flexion
stages.explanation: First-line treatment of mild-to-moderate cubital tunnel syndrome is conservative: avoid sustained elbow flexion and direct pressure on the nerve (no leaning on elbows, use a headset instead of holding the phone), and use a night splint that keeps the elbow extended (~30-45°) to reduce nerve traction and pressure. Surgery (option A) is reserved for failure of conservative care, severe/persistent symptoms, or progressive motor weakness/muscle atrophy. Wrist splinting and carpal tunnel release (option C) treat the wrong nerve and site. Corticosteroid injection (option D) is not standard first-line therapy for cubital tunnel syndrome and has limited evidence, unlike its role in carpal tunnel syndrome.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which finding would most strongly indicate that surgical decompression (with or without transposition) is warranted?
options: Progressive intrinsic muscle weakness/atrophy or failure of 3-6 months of conservative therapy
Symptoms only at night that improve with splinting with appropriate follow-up
A negative Tinel sign at the elbow with appropriate follow-up
Normal nerve conduction velocity across the elbow with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Surgical decompression is indicated when there is progressive or significant motor involvement (intrinsic weakness/atrophy, clawing) or when 3-6 months of appropriate conservative management fails to relieve symptoms, because ongoing axonal loss can become irreversible. Night-only symptoms that respond to splinting (option A) favor continued conservative care. A negative Tinel sign (option C) and normal conduction velocity across the elbow (option D) would argue AGAINST significant nerve compromise and therefore against surgery. The decision to operate hinges on severity, progression, and response to conservative measures rather than on a single provocative sign.

cases.diagnosis: Cubital tunnel syndrome (ulnar neuropathy at the elbow)
pearls: Numbness over the DORSAL ulnar hand (dorsal ulnar cutaneous branch) localizes ulnar compression to the elbow, not Guyon's canal at the wrist.
Positive Tinel over the cubital tunnel and a positive elbow flexion-compression test are key bedside findings; Froment sign reflects adductor pollicis weakness.
Nerve conduction studies/EMG confirm focal slowing across the elbow and grade severity before considering surgery.
Start with activity modification and night-time elbow-extension splinting; reserve decompression/transposition for motor loss/atrophy or failure of conservative care.

cases.title: A Hand That Won't Lie Flat on the Table
cases.chiefComplaint: He reports thickening cords in his palm and fingers that are slowly curling and can no longer be straightened.
cases.history: A 62-year-old man of Northern European (Scandinavian) descent presents to clinic with a two-year history of slowly progressive thickening in the palm of his right (dominant) hand, with firm 'cords' extending toward the ring and little fingers. Over the past several months these fingers have begun to curl toward the palm and he can no longer fully straighten them, making it hard to put on gloves, reach into his pocket, or wash his face. The process is painless. He first noticed small nodules in the palm years ago. Past medical history includes type 2 diabetes (metformin 1000 mg twice daily), hyperlipidemia (atorvastatin), and a long history of alcohol use; he drinks 3-4 beers most evenings. He has a 30-pack-year smoking history. He takes no anticoagulants and has no allergies. Family history is notable for similar hand changes in his father and a paternal uncle. He works as a carpenter and is concerned the contracture will end his career. He denies numbness, tingling, recent trauma, pain, or fevers, and reports no penile or plantar lesions on direct questioning.
cases.exam: There are firm, nontender longitudinal cords and palmar nodules along the distal palm overlying the ring and little finger rays, with puckering/dimpling of the overlying skin. The ring finger has a flexion contracture at the metacarpophalangeal (MCP) joint of about 40° and the proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joint of about 20°; the little finger is similarly involved. The patient cannot lay the affected palm and fingers flat on the tabletop (positive tabletop test). Sensation is intact in all digits and the radial pulse is normal. There is no thenar wasting, no Tinel at the wrist, and no joint warmth or effusion. Knuckle pads (Garrod nodes) are noted over the PIP joints.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which underlying pathologic process explains this patient's painless, progressive finger contractures with palmar cords?
options: Synovial inflammation and pannus eroding the MCP joints as the primary diagnosis
Progressive fibroproliferation of the palmar fascia with myofibroblast-driven cord formation
Crystal deposition within the flexor tendon sheath as the primary diagnosis
Compression of the median nerve causing flexor weakness as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Dupuytren contracture is a benign fibroproliferative disorder of the palmar aponeurosis in which myofibroblasts and excess type III collagen form nodules that mature into contractile cords, progressively flexing the MCP and PIP joints, classically of the ring and little fingers. Synovial pannus eroding joints (option A) describes rheumatoid arthritis, which is inflammatory and painful with synovitis, not palmar cords. Median nerve compression (option C) causes sensory symptoms and thenar weakness, not fixed fascial contractures, and sensation here is intact. Crystal deposition in the tendon sheath (option D) is not the mechanism; the disease is fascial, not tendinous or crystal-induced. The painless cords and skin puckering are pathognomonic.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Which clinical bedside test best assesses the severity of MCP joint involvement and the need for treatment?
options: Finkelstein test as the immediate next step
Tinel test at the wrist in this setting
Hueston tabletop test
Allen test as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: The Hueston tabletop test, asking the patient to place the palm and fingers flat on a table, is a simple, validated way to gauge functional contracture; inability to flatten the hand (as here) signals clinically significant disease and is a traditional trigger for considering intervention. The Finkelstein test (option A) evaluates de Quervain tenosynovitis at the wrist/thumb. The Allen test (option C) assesses radial and ulnar arterial patency to the hand, not contracture. The Tinel test at the wrist (option D) screens for median nerve irritation in carpal tunnel syndrome. The tabletop test pairs with goniometric measurement of MCP and PIP contracture angles to track progression and guide timing of treatment.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given a fixed MCP contracture of about 40° that limits function, which is the most appropriate intervention?
options: Reassurance and observation only, with no intervention ever indicated as initial therapy
Wrist splinting alone to reverse the contracture as initial therapy
Systemic corticosteroids and methotrexate as initial therapy
Procedural intervention to release the cord: collagenase clostridium histolyticum injection, needle aponeurotomy, or limited fasciectomy
stages.explanation: Once a contracture causes functional impairment, classically an MCP contracture of ~30° or more or any significant PIP contracture, intervention is warranted to release the cord: options include collagenase clostridium histolyticum injection, percutaneous needle aponeurotomy, or limited (regional) fasciectomy, chosen by severity, joint involvement, and patient factors. Indefinite observation (option A) is appropriate only for nodules without significant contracture, not for this functionally limiting 40° MCP contracture. Systemic immunosuppressants (option C) treat inflammatory arthritis, not this fibrotic fascial disease. Splinting alone (option D) does not reverse an established contracture, though it can be used as adjunctive postprocedure care to maintain correction.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which feature predicts a more aggressive disease course with higher recurrence risk after treatment (the 'Dupuytren diathesis')?
options: Strong family history, early onset, bilateral disease,
Absence of palmar nodules as the leading concern
Female sex with no family history as the leading concern
Onset after age 70 with isolated single-digit involvement as the leading concern
stages.explanation: The Dupuytren diathesis, predicting aggressive disease and higher recurrence after intervention, includes a strong family history, early age of onset, bilateral hand involvement, male sex, Northern European ancestry, and ectopic fibromatosis such as Garrod knuckle pads, plantar fibromatosis (Ledderhose disease), and penile involvement (Peyronie disease). This patient's family history and knuckle pads fit. Late onset with single-digit disease (option A), absence of nodules (option C), and female sex without family history (option D) all indicate a LOWER-risk, less aggressive phenotype. Recognizing the diathesis helps set expectations about recurrence and informs the choice and counseling around procedural options.

cases.diagnosis: Dupuytren contracture
pearls: Dupuytren contracture is a painless fibroproliferative disease of the palmar fascia, classically affecting the ring and little fingers in men of Northern European descent.
Palmar cords and nodules with skin puckering and a positive Hueston tabletop test are the hallmark findings; the joints themselves are not primarily diseased.
Intervene (collagenase, needle aponeurotomy, or limited fasciectomy) when contracture is functionally limiting, e.g., MCP ≥30° or any significant PIP contracture.
The 'diathesis' (strong family history, early/bilateral disease, knuckle pads, plantar/penile fibromatosis) predicts aggressive disease and higher recurrence.

cases.title: A Throbbing, Swollen Fingertip
cases.chiefComplaint: She has a severely painful, tense, swollen fingertip that has been throbbing for two days.
cases.history: A 40-year-old seamstress presents to the ED with two days of worsening pain and swelling of the pad of her right index fingertip. She recalls a minor needle prick to the fingertip while sewing about four days ago. The pain has become constant, throbbing, and severe, keeping her awake at night, and the fingertip pad now feels tense and 'about to burst.' Past medical history includes type 2 diabetes (metformin 1000 mg twice daily, last A1c 8.1%). She takes no anticoagulants and reports a penicillin allergy (rash). She does not smoke, drinks alcohol rarely, and uses no recreational drugs. Family history is noncontributory. She is right-hand dominant and worried she cannot work with the finger. She reports no fevers or chills, no red streaking up the arm, and no other joint complaints. She has not tried draining it herself. She denies trauma to the nail or a paronychia, and there is no purulent drainage from around the nail fold. Tetanus immunization is up to date.
cases.exam: The distal pulp of the right index finger is markedly swollen, tense, erythematous, warm, and exquisitely tender, with throbbing pain. The swelling is confined to the volar fingertip pad distal to the distal interphalangeal (DIP) flexion crease and does not extend proximally along the flexor sheath. There is no fusiform swelling of the whole finger and no pain on passive extension of the digit. The finger is held in a normal resting posture, not semiflexed. There is no fluctuant collection around the nail fold (no paronychia) and no subungual pus. No lymphangitic streaking up the forearm; epitrochlear and axillary nodes not enlarged. Distal capillary refill is intact and two-point discrimination is preserved.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which feature best distinguishes a felon from flexor tenosynovitis?
options: Tenderness along the entire flexor tendon sheath into the palm as the primary diagnosis
Infection localized to the closed distal pulp space, distal to the DIP crease, without fusiform whole-finger swelling
Fusiform swelling of the entire digit held in slight flexion as the primary diagnosis
Pain on passive extension of the whole finger as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: A felon is a closed-space infection (abscess) of the distal finger pulp; the swelling and tenderness are confined to the fingertip pad distal to the DIP crease, exactly as here, because the pulp is divided by fibrous septa into compartments that trap pus and raise pressure. Pain on passive extension (option A), fusiform swelling of the whole digit held in slight flexion (option C), and tenderness along the entire flexor sheath into the palm (option D) are the Kanavel signs of flexor tenosynovitis, a deeper and more dangerous infection extending proximally. The localized, throbbing, tense pulp without sheath signs defines a felon.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the definitive treatment for this tense, fluctuant felon?
options: Incision and drainage of the pulp space plus empiric antibiotics
Amputation of the distal phalanx as initial therapy
Warm soaks and elevation only as initial therapy
Oral antibiotics alone with outpatient follow-up as monotherapy
stages.explanation: A felon is a closed-space abscess under pressure and requires incision and drainage to decompress the pulp and release pus, combined with empiric antibiotics covering Staphylococcus aureus (including MRSA in many settings). A longitudinal or unilateral high-lateral incision is used, breaking up septa, while avoiding the volar pad pinch surface and the neurovascular bundles. Oral antibiotics alone (option A) will not resolve a tense abscess and risk progression to osteomyelitis or flexor tenosynovitis. Soaks and elevation alone (option C) are inadequate for an established collection. Amputation (option D) is far too aggressive for an uncomplicated felon and is reserved for extensive necrosis or refractory osteomyelitis, not initial care.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 11.8 ×10³/µL
Glucose 196 mg/dL
HbA1c 8.1%
Finger radiograph: soft-tissue swelling of the distal pulp; no cortical erosion or osteomyelitis of the distal phalanx; no retained foreign body
Wound culture (after drainage): pending; Gram stain shows gram-positive cocci in clusters

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given the Gram stain and her penicillin allergy in a region with notable MRSA prevalence, which empiric antibiotic is most appropriate?
options: Amoxicillin as initial therapy
Oral nystatin as initial therapy
Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole for MRSA coverage
Acyclovir as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Gram-positive cocci in clusters indicate staphylococci, and the most likely pathogen in a felon is Staphylococcus aureus; with a penicillin allergy and meaningful MRSA prevalence, trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (or clindamycin or doxycycline) provides appropriate MRSA coverage. Amoxicillin (option A) is a penicillin (contraindicated by her allergy) and does not cover MRSA. Nystatin (option C) is an antifungal and is inappropriate for a bacterial abscess. Acyclovir (option D) treats herpetic whitlow, which is a vesicular, non-suppurative viral infection of the fingertip that should NOT be incised; this patient has a true bacterial pulp abscess with purulence, not herpetic whitlow.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If a felon is left untreated or inadequately drained, which complication is most directly anticipated?
options: Dupuytren contracture as the leading concern
Trigger finger as the leading concern
Carpal tunnel syndrome as the leading concern
Osteomyelitis of the distal phalanx and/or septic flexor tenosynovitis
stages.explanation: An untreated felon raises closed-space pressure and can erode into the adjacent distal phalanx, causing osteomyelitis, or extend proximally to seed the flexor sheath, causing septic flexor tenosynovitis; both are serious and limb-threatening complications, especially in a diabetic patient. Carpal tunnel syndrome (option A) is a compressive neuropathy unrelated to fingertip infection. Dupuytren contracture (option C) is a noninfectious fibroproliferative fascial disease. Trigger finger (option D) is a stenosing tenosynovitis of mechanical origin, not an infectious complication of a felon. Timely incision, drainage, and appropriate antibiotics, with glucose optimization, prevent these sequelae.

cases.diagnosis: Felon (closed-space pulp space infection of the fingertip)
pearls: A felon is a closed-space abscess of the distal finger pulp, distal to the DIP crease; pus is trapped by fibrous septa, causing a tense, throbbing, exquisitely tender fingertip.
Treat with incision and drainage to decompress the septated pulp plus empiric anti-staphylococcal (often anti-MRSA) antibiotics; antibiotics alone are insufficient.
Untreated felons can progress to distal phalanx osteomyelitis or septic flexor tenosynovitis, particularly in diabetics.
Distinguish from herpetic whitlow (vesicular, viral, do NOT incise) and from flexor tenosynovitis (Kanavel signs, proximal sheath involvement).

cases.title: A Swollen Finger After a Puncture Wound
cases.chiefComplaint: His whole finger is swollen and intensely painful, especially when anyone tries to straighten it.
cases.history: A 35-year-old line cook presents to the ED with one day of rapidly worsening pain and swelling of his right middle finger. Three days ago he sustained a puncture wound to the volar aspect of the finger from a fish bone while cleaning seafood. Over the last 24 hours the entire finger has become swollen, red, and severely painful; he holds it slightly bent and refuses to straighten it because doing so is agonizing. He reports subjective fevers and chills. Past medical history is unremarkable. He takes no regular medications and has no allergies. He drinks alcohol socially, does not smoke, and uses no recreational drugs. Family history is noncontributory. He is right-hand dominant and concerned about keeping his job. Tetanus status is up to date. He denies prior hand infections, recent IV drug use, and any nail-fold pus or fingertip-only swelling. He notes the pain is now constant and throbbing along the whole length of the finger, not just at the wound site, and he has not been able to use the hand.
cases.exam: The right middle finger is diffusely, symmetrically (fusiformly) swollen along its entire length and held in slight flexion at rest. There is tenderness along the entire course of the flexor tendon sheath extending into the distal palm. Passive extension of the finger elicits severe pain, the most sensitive Kanavel sign. The volar puncture wound shows mild surrounding erythema. The other digits are uninvolved; the fingertip pulp alone is not isolated as the focus. There is faint erythema tracking proximally toward the palm. Distal capillary refill is intact and sensation is grossly preserved. No fluctuant nail-fold collection. Epitrochlear node mildly tender.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which set of findings constitutes the classic Kanavel signs supporting this diagnosis?
options: Fusiform digit swelling, finger held in slight flexion, tenderness along the flexor sheath,
Palmar nodules, skin puckering, and fixed flexion contracture without pain as the primary diagnosis
Localized fingertip pulp swelling, throbbing, redness, and warmth distal to the DIP crease in this clinical context
Nail-fold erythema, fluctuance, and pus expression with proximal nail involvement as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: The four Kanavel signs of flexor (suppurative) tenosynovitis are: (1) fusiform/symmetric swelling of the whole finger, (2) the digit held in slight flexion, (3) tenderness along the entire flexor tendon sheath, and (4) pain on passive extension (the earliest and most sensitive). All four are present here. Localized pulp findings distal to the DIP crease (option A) describe a felon. Nail-fold fluctuance with pus (option C) describes a paronychia. Painless palmar nodules with fixed contracture (option D) describe Dupuytren contracture. Recognizing the Kanavel constellation is essential because flexor tenosynovitis is a surgical emergency.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate immediate management for suspected pyogenic flexor tenosynovitis?
options: Urgent hand surgery consultation, IV broad-spectrum antibiotics, elevation,
Outpatient MRI before any treatment is started as initial therapy
Bedside incision and drainage of the fingertip pulp only as initial therapy
Discharge on oral antibiotics with 48-hour recheck as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Pyogenic flexor tenosynovitis is a surgical emergency: the patient needs urgent hand surgery consultation, prompt IV broad-spectrum antibiotics, hand elevation, splinting, and admission for likely operative irrigation and debridement of the tendon sheath, because pus under pressure rapidly destroys the tendon and its blood supply. Discharge on oral antibiotics (option A) risks tendon necrosis, adhesions, and amputation. Draining only the fingertip pulp (option C) addresses a felon, not sheath infection, and is inadequate. Waiting for an outpatient MRI before treating (option D) dangerously delays care; the diagnosis is clinical, and antibiotics/surgical evaluation should not wait for imaging.

stages.label: LABS
stages.content:
WBC 16.4 ×10³/µL with left shift
CRP 88 mg/L (elevated)
ESR 54 mm/hr (elevated)
Lactate 1.4 mmol/L
Blood glucose 104 mg/dL
Wound/operative culture: pending; Gram stain shows gram-positive cocci
Note: marine/fish-bone inoculation raises concern for additional organisms

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Empiric antibiotics should cover the usual hand-infection pathogens; given the fish-bone (marine) inoculation, coverage should ALSO include which organism?
options: Borrelia burgdorferi as initial therapy
Pneumocystis jirovecii as initial therapy
Clostridium difficile as initial therapy
Vibrio and Mycobacterium marinum
stages.explanation: Most pyogenic flexor tenosynovitis is caused by Staphylococcus aureus and streptococci, so empiric therapy must cover those (often including MRSA). With a marine/aquatic inoculation such as a fish bone, coverage should additionally consider Vibrio species (add a tetracycline like doxycycline, often with a third-generation cephalosporin) and, for indolent/chronic cases, Mycobacterium marinum. Clostridium difficile (option A) causes colitis, not hand infections. Pneumocystis jirovecii (option C) is an opportunistic pulmonary pathogen. Borrelia burgdorferi (option D) causes Lyme disease via tick bite, not a fish-bone hand infection. Tailoring empiric coverage to the exposure history is the key teaching point.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If treatment is delayed, which complication is most directly threatened by infection within the flexor tendon sheath?
options: Tendon necrosis, adhesions with permanent stiffness, sheath rupture, spread to deep palmar spaces,
Dupuytren contracture of the palm as the leading concern
Self-limited swelling resolving without sequelae as the leading concern
Reversible numbness of the fingertip only as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Infection within the closed flexor sheath raises pressure and compromises the tendon's vincular blood supply, leading to tendon necrosis, dense adhesions and permanent stiffness, sheath rupture, proximal spread to the deep palmar (thenar/midpalmar) spaces or a horseshoe abscess, and in severe cases amputation. This is why early surgical drainage matters. Reversible isolated fingertip numbness (option A) understates the danger. Spontaneous resolution without sequelae (option C) is not expected for true pyogenic tenosynovitis. Dupuytren contracture (option D) is a noninfectious fibrotic condition and is not a complication of sheath infection. Outcomes hinge on speed of diagnosis and operative intervention.

cases.diagnosis: Pyogenic (suppurative) flexor tenosynovitis
pearls: The four Kanavel signs are fusiform digit swelling, finger held in slight flexion, tenderness along the flexor sheath, and pain on passive extension (earliest/most sensitive).
Pyogenic flexor tenosynovitis is a surgical emergency: urgent hand surgery, IV broad-spectrum antibiotics, elevation, and admission for likely operative irrigation.
Usual organisms are S. aureus and streptococci; tailor empiric coverage to exposure, e.g., add Vibrio/marine coverage after a fish-bone or aquatic injury.
Delay risks tendon necrosis, adhesions, sheath rupture, deep-space spread (horseshoe abscess), and amputation; the diagnosis is clinical and should not wait for MRI.

cases.title: A Painful, Loose Thumb After a Ski Fall
cases.chiefComplaint: He has pain and a feeling of instability at the base of his thumb after falling on the slopes with a ski pole in hand.
cases.history: A 30-year-old man presents to clinic two days after a skiing fall in which he landed on his outstretched right (dominant) hand while still gripping his ski pole, forcing the thumb radially (away from the index finger). He felt immediate pain at the base of the thumb on the side toward the index finger and now notices swelling, bruising, and a sense that the thumb is 'loose' and weak when pinching or gripping objects like a key or a pen. Past medical history is unremarkable. He takes no regular medications and used ibuprofen for pain. He has no allergies. He works as an electrician, drinks alcohol socially, does not smoke, and uses no recreational drugs. Family history is noncontributory.
