He is right-hand dominant and worried about losing pinch strength for his job. He denies numbness or tingling and reports the thumb tip moves normally. There was no skin laceration. He has had no prior thumb injury or surgery and his tetanus status is current.
cases.exam: There is swelling, tenderness, and ecchymosis over the ulnar aspect of the thumb metacarpophalangeal (MCP) joint. A small, firm, tender mass is palpable just proximal to the ulnar MCP joint. Valgus (radially directed) stress testing of the thumb MCP joint reveals laxity: there is more than 30° of radial deviation with stress and no firm endpoint, compared with a stable contralateral thumb. Pinch strength is reduced and painful. The thumb interphalangeal joint and tip move normally. Distal sensation and capillary refill are intact. There is no snuffbox tenderness and no scaphoid tenderness; the wrist is nontender.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which structure is injured in this thumb MCP instability, and what does the mechanism suggest?
options: The radial collateral ligament from forced ulnar deviation in this clinical context
The ulnar collateral ligament of the thumb MCP joint from forced radial deviation
The scapholunate ligament of the wrist as the primary diagnosis
The A1 pulley of the flexor tendon as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: A forced radial (valgus) deviation of the thumb, classically a fall while gripping a ski pole ('skier's thumb') or chronic stress ('gamekeeper's thumb'), injures the ulnar collateral ligament (UCL) at the thumb MCP joint, producing ulnar-sided tenderness and valgus laxity with weak, painful pinch, as here. The radial collateral ligament (option A) is injured by the opposite (ulnar deviation) mechanism. The scapholunate ligament (option C) is a wrist structure, and the wrist exam is normal. The A1 pulley (option D) is involved in trigger finger, a different mechanical condition. The UCL is critical for pinch stability, making this injury functionally important.

stages.label: WORKUP
stages.question: Before stress-testing or after equivocal exam, which imaging step is most appropriate first?
options: Stress radiographs only, without any plain films as the immediate next step
Immediate MRI without any radiographs as the immediate next step
Plain radiographs of the thumb BEFORE aggressive stress testing to exclude an avulsion fracture
CT angiography of the hand as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Obtain plain radiographs of the thumb first to look for an avulsion fracture at the UCL insertion before applying vigorous valgus stress, because stressing a displaced bony avulsion can worsen displacement. Radiographs also guide whether a bony fragment needs fixation. Stress radiographs alone without baseline films (option A) skip the important step of identifying a fracture first. Going straight to MRI (option C) is reasonable to assess the ligament and detect a Stener lesion when needed, but it is not the FIRST step before radiographs. CT angiography (option D) evaluates vasculature and has no role here, as the neurovascular exam is intact.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Thumb radiographs: no avulsion fracture at the UCL insertion; joint congruent without subluxation
Valgus stress exam (after fracture excluded): >30° radial laxity with no firm endpoint, and >15° side-to-side difference vs the contralateral thumb
Ultrasound/MRI: complete UCL rupture with the torn proximal ligament displaced superficial to the adductor aponeurosis (Stener lesion)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The imaging shows the torn ligament displaced superficial to the adductor aponeurosis. What is this finding called, and why does it matter?
options: A SLAP lesion; it is treated with corticosteroid injection
A Bankart lesion; it heals well with casting as the primary diagnosis
A Hill-Sachs lesion; it requires only a thumb spica splint
A Stener lesion; the interposed aponeurosis prevents ligament healing
stages.explanation: A Stener lesion occurs when the torn proximal end of the UCL retracts and becomes displaced superficial to the adductor aponeurosis, which then interposes between the ligament ends and physically blocks healing; this mandates surgical repair to restore pinch stability. A Bankart lesion (option A) is a glenoid labral injury in shoulder instability, not a thumb finding. A Hill-Sachs lesion (option C) is a humeral head impaction defect from shoulder dislocation. A SLAP lesion (option D) is a superior labral tear of the shoulder. Only the Stener lesion describes this thumb pathology, and its presence is the key indication for operative repair.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given a complete UCL rupture with a confirmed Stener lesion, what is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Surgical repair of the ulnar collateral ligament
Corticosteroid injection into the MCP joint as monotherapy
Thumb spica cast for 4-6 weeks with no surgery
Buddy-taping to the index finger and early mobilization
stages.explanation: A complete UCL tear with a Stener lesion requires surgical repair (or reconstruction) because the interposed adductor aponeurosis prevents the ligament from healing back to bone; restoring UCL integrity is necessary to preserve pinch and grip strength. A thumb spica cast (option A) is appropriate for partial tears or complete tears WITHOUT a Stener lesion (stable, with a firm endpoint), not for this displaced complete tear. A corticosteroid injection (option D) does not repair a torn ligament and could impair healing. Buddy-taping (option D, listed as option E) is used for minor finger sprains and provides no stability for a complete thumb UCL rupture with a Stener lesion.

cases.diagnosis: Gamekeeper's/skier's thumb (ulnar collateral ligament rupture) with Stener lesion
pearls: Skier's/gamekeeper's thumb is a UCL injury of the thumb MCP joint from forced radial (valgus) deviation; suspect it with ulnar-sided tenderness and weak, painful pinch.
Get plain radiographs BEFORE vigorous valgus stress testing to exclude an avulsion fracture; >30° laxity or no firm endpoint suggests a complete tear.
A Stener lesion (torn ligament displaced superficial to the adductor aponeurosis) blocks healing and mandates surgical repair.
Partial tears or complete tears without a Stener lesion (stable, firm endpoint) can be treated nonoperatively in a thumb spica cast.

cases.title: A Child's Painful, Deformed Forearm After a Fall
cases.chiefComplaint: His mother brings him in for forearm pain, swelling, and refusal to move the elbow after a fall.
cases.history: An 8-year-old boy is brought to the ED by his mother after falling from monkey bars at the playground about an hour ago, landing on his outstretched right arm. He has pain and swelling of the right forearm and is holding the arm protectively, refusing to bend or use the elbow. He cried immediately and has not wanted to move the arm since. Past medical history is unremarkable; he is fully immunized and developmentally normal. He takes no medications and has no allergies. He lives with both parents, attends third grade, and is otherwise healthy. Family history is noncontributory. The mother witnessed the fall, and the story is consistent. There is no head injury, no loss of consciousness, and no other injuries. He denies numbness but has not been tested. He last ate two hours ago. The mother is anxious and asks whether it is 'just a sprain.' He is right-hand dominant and very tearful but consolable, and he points mainly to the proximal forearm near the elbow.
cases.exam: There is swelling, tenderness, and a palpable deformity over the proximal third of the right ulna. The elbow is held in slight flexion and is painful; there is fullness and tenderness over the lateral elbow at the radiocapitellar region. Forearm rotation (pronation/supination) and elbow flexion are limited by pain. The skin is intact with no open wound. Distal neurovascular exam: radial pulse 2+, brisk capillary refill, fingers warm. The examiner specifically tests the posterior interosseous nerve (thumb/finger extension) and finds the patient reluctant but able to extend; sensation in median, ulnar, and radial distributions appears intact. The wrist is nontender. Compartments are soft.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given a proximal ulna fracture with lateral elbow fullness, which associated injury must be specifically excluded?
options: Radial head dislocation
Scaphoid fracture as the first step today
Supracondylar humerus fracture
Distal radius (Colles) fracture
stages.explanation: A proximal ulna fracture combined with a radial head DISLOCATION is a Monteggia fracture-dislocation; the lateral elbow fullness and tenderness at the radiocapitellar joint here should prompt deliberate evaluation for a dislocated radial head, which is notoriously and dangerously easy to overlook. A distal radius (Colles) fracture (option A) involves the wrist, which is nontender here. A scaphoid fracture (option C) causes snuffbox tenderness at the wrist, also absent. A supracondylar humerus fracture (option D) is a distal humerus injury, not a proximal ulna fracture; while common in children, it does not explain the ulnar deformity plus radiocapitellar findings. Missing the radial head dislocation leads to chronic disability.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: On every elbow/forearm radiograph, which line, if disrupted, confirms radial head dislocation?
options: The anterior humeral line as the primary diagnosis
Shenton's line as the primary diagnosis
The radiocapitellar line
The scapholunate interval as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: The radiocapitellar line, drawn along the axis of the radial neck/shaft, should pass through the center of the capitellum on EVERY view; if it does not, the radial head is dislocated, the key to catching Monteggia injuries. The anterior humeral line (option A) is used to detect subtle supracondylar (distal humerus) fractures, not radial head position. Shenton's line (option C) assesses the hip. The scapholunate interval (option D) evaluates the wrist for ligamentous injury. Because an isolated ulna fracture should prompt scrutiny of the radiocapitellar relationship, always image the elbow with the forearm and check this line to avoid missing the dislocation.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Forearm and elbow radiographs (AP and lateral): displaced fracture of the proximal third of the ulna with apex-anterior angulation
Radiocapitellar line disrupted: the radial head is dislocated anteriorly relative to the capitellum
Findings consistent with a Bado type I Monteggia fracture-dislocation
No distal radius or wrist fracture; growth plates otherwise intact
Neurovascular status documented as intact

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management of this pediatric Monteggia fracture-dislocation?
options: Wrist splinting alone with no elbow immobilization as initial therapy
Discharge with a sling and outpatient follow-up in 2 weeks as initial therapy
Reduction of the ulna which typically restores the radial head, followed by immobilization, with orthopedic involvement
Immediate radial head excision as initial therapy
stages.explanation: In children, restoring ulnar length and alignment (frequently achievable with closed reduction because the fracture is often plastic/greenstick) usually reduces the radial head as well; the limb is then immobilized (commonly a long-arm cast in a position that maintains radial head reduction) with orthopedic management, and open reduction is reserved for unstable, irreducible, or complete fractures. Discharge without reduction (option A) leaves a dislocated radial head and risks permanent dysfunction. Wrist splinting alone (option C) fails to address the proximal ulna and elbow. Radial head excision (option D) is inappropriate in a growing child and is not the treatment for an acute reducible dislocation. Post-reduction films must confirm the radiocapitellar line is restored.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which nerve is most commonly injured in a Monteggia fracture-dislocation, and how does it typically present?
options: The posterior interosseous nerve , presenting with weak finger/thumb extension
The ulnar nerve, presenting with clawing of the ring and little fingers as the leading concern
The median nerve, presenting with thenar atrophy as the leading concern
The axillary nerve, presenting with deltoid weakness as the leading concern
stages.explanation: The posterior interosseous nerve (PIN), a motor branch of the radial nerve, is most commonly injured in Monteggia fracture-dislocations because of its proximity to the radial head; it presents as weakness of finger and thumb extension (finger-drop) with PRESERVED wrist extension (radial wrist extensors are innervated proximally) and preserved sensation (PIN is purely motor). The median nerve with thenar atrophy (option A), ulnar nerve with clawing (option C), and axillary nerve with deltoid weakness (option D) are not the characteristic injury here. Most PIN palsies in Monteggia injuries are neurapraxic and resolve after reduction, but documenting and following the deficit is essential.

cases.diagnosis: Monteggia fracture-dislocation (proximal ulna fracture with radial head dislocation)
pearls: Monteggia = proximal ulna fracture + radial head dislocation; an 'isolated' ulna fracture should always prompt scrutiny of the radial head.
Check the radiocapitellar line on every view: a line through the radial neck/shaft must bisect the capitellum, or the radial head is dislocated.
In children, reducing and stabilizing the ulna usually restores the radial head; image the whole forearm including the elbow and confirm reduction on post-reduction films.
The posterior interosseous nerve (PIN) is the most commonly injured nerve, causing finger-drop with preserved wrist extension and sensation; it is usually neurapraxic.

cases.title: Elbow Pain After a Fall on the Outstretched Hand
cases.chiefComplaint: Right elbow pain and stiffness after falling onto her outstretched hand.
cases.history: A 36-year-old recreational tennis player presents to the ED after tripping on a curb and falling forward onto her outstretched right hand with the elbow extended. She felt immediate lateral elbow pain and has had difficulty fully straightening and rotating the arm since. There was no loss of consciousness, no wrist or shoulder pain, and no numbness or tingling in the hand. She denies prior elbow injury or surgery. PMH is unremarkable; she takes only a daily oral contraceptive and a multivitamin. No anticoagulants. NKDA. She works as a graphic designer and is right-hand dominant. Social history: drinks socially, never smoked, exercises regularly. Family history is noncontributory. She has taken ibuprofen 400 mg twice since the fall with minimal relief. She is otherwise healthy and up to date on vaccinations. She is anxious about whether she will need surgery and how long she will be out of work.
cases.exam: Alert, comfortable at rest. Right elbow held in slight flexion with a small effusion fullness in the lateral infracondylar recess. Tenderness is maximal over the radial head/lateral elbow. Active and passive supination and pronation are markedly limited by pain; flexion-extension is reduced near terminal extension. No skin tearing, no deformity, no crepitus over the olecranon. Distal neurovascular exam intact: radial pulse 2+, brisk capillary refill, normal sensation in median, ulnar, and radial distributions, intact thumb interphalangeal flexion and finger abduction. Wrist and shoulder nontender with full range of motion. No forearm compartment tenderness.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first imaging study to obtain?
options: Plain radiographs of the elbow
Ultrasound of the elbow joint
MRI of the elbow as the first step today
CT of the elbow with 3D reconstruction
stages.explanation: Plain radiographs (AP and lateral) are the correct first-line study for acute elbow trauma after a FOOSH; they are fast, cheap, and detect most fractures and the fat pad signs that suggest occult fracture. MRI is unnecessary up front and rarely available emergently; it is reserved for suspected ligamentous or chondral injury after normal radiographs. CT is a reasonable second step to characterize displacement or comminution before surgical planning but is not the initial test. Ultrasound can show effusion but is operator-dependent and does not reliably define fracture lines, so it is not the standard initial study for suspected fracture.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Elbow radiographs (AP/lateral):
Posterior fat pad sign present (positive "sail sign")
Anterior fat pad elevated
Nondisplaced fracture line through the radial head
<2 mm displacement, no articular step-off
No associated olecranon or coronoid fracture
No elbow dislocation

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The posterior fat pad (sail) sign on the lateral radiograph most directly indicates which of the following?
options: An intra-articular elbow effusion/hemarthrosis suggesting occult fracture
Olecranon bursitis as the primary diagnosis
A torn ulnar collateral ligament as the primary diagnosis
Normal anatomy seen in most adults as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: A visible posterior fat pad means the posterior fat, normally hidden in the olecranon fossa, has been displaced by an intra-articular effusion (usually hemarthrosis from an occult fracture)—classically a nondisplaced radial head fracture in adults. A torn UCL produces valgus instability but does not by itself create the sail sign. Olecranon bursitis is a superficial, extra-articular swelling over the olecranon and does not displace intra-articular fat pads. A small anterior fat pad can be normal, but a posterior fat pad is always pathologic—so it is not a normal finding.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Her fracture is a nondisplaced radial head fracture (Mason type I) with no mechanical block to motion. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Long-arm cast immobilization for 6 weeks as initial therapy
Open reduction and internal fixation as initial therapy
Sling for comfort with early active range of motion
Radial head excision as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Nondisplaced Mason type I radial head fractures without a mechanical block are treated nonoperatively with brief sling immobilization for comfort and early active range of motion to prevent the stiffness elbows are prone to; this yields excellent outcomes. Prolonged long-arm casting promotes elbow contracture and is discouraged. ORIF is reserved for displaced (type II with block or type III) fractures. Radial head excision/replacement is considered for unreconstructable comminuted (type III) fractures, not nondisplaced ones. Early motion is the key principle in radial head fractures.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Before discharge, which examination maneuver is most important to confirm there is no mechanical block requiring surgical referral?
options: Obtain stress radiographs of the elbow as the immediate next step
Check Tinel sign at the cubital tunnel as the immediate next step
Aspirate the hemarthrosis
Assess grip strength with a dynamometer as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Aspirating the hemarthrosis (often with intra-articular local anesthetic) relieves pain and allows the examiner to gently rotate the forearm through a full arc; a smooth, unblocked pronation/supination confirms no mechanical block from a displaced fragment, supporting nonoperative care. A persistent block mandates orthopedic/surgical evaluation. Grip dynamometry does not assess intra-articular mechanical block. Tinel at the cubital tunnel evaluates ulnar nerve irritation, not a fracture block. Stress radiographs assess ligamentous instability and are painful and unnecessary in an acute nondisplaced radial head fracture.

cases.diagnosis: Nondisplaced radial head fracture (Mason type I) with hemarthrosis
pearls: A posterior fat pad (sail) sign on a lateral elbow film implies intra-articular hemarthrosis and an occult fracture—most often a nondisplaced radial head fracture in adults.
Mason type I radial head fractures are treated with a sling for comfort and EARLY range of motion; prolonged immobilization risks elbow contracture.
Always test for a mechanical block to forearm rotation—aspiration with lidocaine can facilitate this exam and guide surgical referral.
Document distal neurovascular status; the posterior interosseous nerve courses near the radial neck and can rarely be injured.

cases.title: Elbow Swelling and Trouble Straightening the Arm After a Fall
cases.chiefComplaint: Left elbow pain and inability to straighten the arm after falling directly onto the elbow.
cases.history: A 60-year-old woman slipped on a wet kitchen floor and fell directly onto the point of her left elbow. She reports immediate posterior elbow pain, rapid swelling, and an inability to actively extend or hold the arm out against gravity. She felt a 'pop' at the time. No numbness or tingling, though she notes mild tingling in the small finger that has since resolved. PMH: hypertension and osteoporosis. Medications: amlodipine 5 mg daily, alendronate 70 mg weekly, calcium-vitamin D supplement. NKDA. She is a retired teacher, lives with her husband, drinks one glass of wine nightly, never smoked. Family history notable for maternal hip fracture. She last had a DEXA scan 2 years ago showing osteopenia. She takes no anticoagulants. She is concerned because she relies on her arms for gardening and caring for grandchildren. She came in promptly because the elbow looked 'caved in' and she could not push a door open with that arm.
cases.exam: Left elbow with posterior swelling and ecchymosis over the olecranon. A palpable gap is appreciated over the proximal ulna with focal tenderness. The patient cannot actively extend the elbow against gravity from a flexed position, though passive extension is full. Flexion is intact but painful. Skin is intact with no open wound. Distal pulses 2+ and symmetric; capillary refill brisk. Sensation intact in median, radial, and ulnar distributions at exam; intact intrinsic hand function and finger abduction. No forearm compartment firmness. Wrist and shoulder nontender with full motion.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Her inability to actively extend the elbow against gravity, with a palpable gap over the proximal ulna, most specifically suggests injury to which structure?
options: Triceps mechanism/olecranon
Ulnar collateral ligament as the first step today
Annular ligament of the radial head
Biceps tendon insertion at the radial tuberosity
stages.explanation: Active elbow extension is powered by the triceps inserting on the olecranon; loss of active extension against gravity with a palpable proximal-ulna gap indicates disruption of the extensor mechanism, classically a displaced olecranon fracture. A distal biceps rupture impairs supination/flexion strength, not extension. UCL injury causes valgus instability without loss of active extension. The annular ligament stabilizes the radial head and its injury does not abolish extension. The extension-loss finding localizes the problem to the triceps-olecranon unit.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Elbow radiographs (AP/true lateral):
Transverse fracture through the mid-olecranon
Fracture gap 4 mm with articular involvement of the trochlear notch
Proximal fragment displaced proximally by triceps pull
Radial head intact, no dislocation
No distal humerus fracture

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which radiographic feature best explains why this displaced olecranon fracture causes loss of active extension?
options: The triceps pulls the proximal fragment away, disrupting extensor continuity
There is articular involvement of the trochlear notch in this clinical context
The radial head is intact as the primary diagnosis
The fracture line is transverse rather than oblique as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: The triceps inserts on the olecranon; when the fracture displaces, the triceps retracts the proximal fragment proximally, breaking the continuity of the extensor lever so the patient cannot actively extend against gravity. An intact radial head is reassuring but irrelevant to extension loss. Articular involvement of the trochlear notch matters for joint congruity and surgical indication but is not the mechanism of extension loss. The transverse versus oblique orientation affects fixation strategy, not whether extension is lost—displacement and triceps pull are the operative reason.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given a displaced (>2 mm gap) intra-articular olecranon fracture with loss of active extension in this active patient, what is the most appropriate definitive treatment?
options: Sling and early range of motion only as initial therapy
Operative fixation
Closed reduction and percutaneous pinning of the radial head
Long-arm cast in full extension for 6 weeks as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Displaced intra-articular olecranon fractures with extensor mechanism disruption require operative fixation—tension-band wiring for simple transverse patterns or plate fixation for comminuted/oblique patterns—to restore the extensor lever and joint congruity. Casting in extension will not reduce a displaced intra-articular fracture and risks malunion and stiffness. Sling with early motion alone is appropriate only for nondisplaced fractures with intact extension. Percutaneous pinning of the radial head addresses the wrong bone entirely; the radial head is intact here.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which nerve is most at risk given the location of this injury and its surgical approach, warranting careful documentation and protection?
options: Anterior interosseous nerve
Axillary nerve
Median nerve in this presentation
Ulnar nerve
stages.explanation: The ulnar nerve runs in the cubital tunnel directly posteromedial to the elbow, immediately adjacent to the olecranon, so it is the nerve most at risk from both the fracture and operative dissection/hardware—consistent with this patient's transient small-finger tingling. The median nerve lies anteriorly and is rarely involved in posterior olecranon injuries. The anterior interosseous nerve is a median branch in the proximal forearm, not at the olecranon. The axillary nerve is at the shoulder and unrelated to elbow trauma. Pre- and postoperative ulnar nerve documentation is essential.

cases.diagnosis: Displaced intra-articular olecranon fracture with extensor mechanism disruption
pearls: Loss of active elbow extension against gravity after a direct blow to the elbow = extensor mechanism disruption, classically a displaced olecranon fracture.
Displaced (>2 mm) intra-articular olecranon fractures need operative fixation—tension-band wiring for simple transverse patterns, plating for comminution.
The ulnar nerve is immediately adjacent to the olecranon; document its function before and after intervention.
Obtain a true lateral radiograph—the degree of displacement and articular involvement drives the surgical decision.

cases.title: Severe Hip Pain and a Shortened Leg After a Car Crash
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe right hip pain and inability to move the leg after a high-speed motor vehicle collision.
cases.history: A 32-year-old man is brought in by EMS after a head-on motor vehicle collision in which he was the restrained driver and his knees struck the dashboard. He has severe right hip pain and cannot move the right leg. He denies head injury or loss of consciousness and has no chest or abdominal pain. He reports numbness over the dorsum of the right foot. PMH: none. Medications: none. NKDA. Social: occasional alcohol, vapes nicotine, works in construction. No prior surgeries. Family history noncontributory. On arrival he is in significant distress from pain. Primary survey: airway patent, breathing unlabored, hemodynamically stable, GCS 15. Secondary survey reveals the right lower extremity in an abnormal position. He received fentanyl en route. He asks repeatedly whether he will be able to walk again. There is no obvious external bleeding and the abdomen is soft.
cases.exam: The right lower extremity is held flexed at the hip, adducted, and internally rotated, with the limb appearing shortened compared to the left. Severe pain with any attempted hip motion; the hip is effectively immobile. Skin intact, no open wound. Distal pulses (dorsalis pedis, posterior tibial) 2+ and symmetric, foot warm. Sensation diminished over the dorsum of the foot and first web space; weak ankle dorsiflexion and great-toe extension on the right. Plantar flexion and sensation over the sole are intact. Knee without effusion or instability; no obvious patellar injury. Contralateral limb normal.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The right leg is flexed, adducted, and internally rotated after a dashboard injury. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Femoral neck fracture
Anterior cruciate ligament rupture
Anterior hip dislocation
Posterior hip dislocation
stages.explanation: The classic posture of posterior hip dislocation is a limb that is flexed, adducted, internally rotated, and shortened—exactly the dashboard mechanism, where axial force through a flexed, adducted hip drives the femoral head posteriorly. Anterior dislocations present abducted and externally rotated. A femoral neck fracture typically shows a shortened, externally rotated limb, not the adducted/internally rotated position. ACL rupture causes knee instability and effusion, not this fixed hip deformity. The specific flexion-adduction-internal-rotation triad is the hallmark of posterior hip dislocation.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
AP pelvis radiograph:
Right femoral head displaced superolaterally, lying superior to the acetabulum
Femoral head appears smaller than the left (closer to the plate)
No displaced acetabular wall fragment seen on this view
Femoral neck and shaft intact
Left hip normal
Neurovascular note: decreased sensation dorsal foot, weak dorsiflexion

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: His decreased dorsal foot sensation and weak ankle/great-toe dorsiflexion indicate injury to which nerve, a recognized complication of this dislocation?
options: Superior gluteal nerve
Sciatic nerve
Obturator nerve
Femoral nerve in this presentation
stages.explanation: The sciatic nerve lies just posterior to the hip and is stretched or compressed by a posteriorly displaced femoral head; its peroneal division is most vulnerable, producing foot drop (weak dorsiflexion/great-toe extension) and sensory loss over the dorsum of the foot and first web space—exactly this patient's findings. The femoral nerve is anterior and governs knee extension/anterior thigh sensation. The obturator nerve supplies the medial thigh adductors. The superior gluteal nerve innervates hip abductors and causes a Trendelenburg gait, not foot drop. Documenting sciatic function before and after reduction is essential.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After confirming no displaced femoral neck fracture, what is the most appropriate next step?
options: Immediate total hip arthroplasty as initial therapy
Schedule elective open reduction within 1 week as initial therapy
Apply skeletal traction and admit for observation without reduction
Urgent closed reduction under procedural sedation, ideally within 6 hours
stages.explanation: A posterior hip dislocation is an orthopedic emergency; urgent closed reduction under sedation (ideally within about 6 hours) minimizes the risk of avascular necrosis of the femoral head, whose blood supply is compromised by the dislocation. Delaying reduction to an elective timeframe markedly increases AVN risk. Traction without reduction leaves the head dislocated and does not address the ischemia. Total hip arthroplasty is not the acute treatment for a simple dislocation in a young patient; reduction with later evaluation for associated fractures is standard. Confirming no femoral neck fracture first avoids displacing such a fracture during reduction.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After successful closed reduction with restored alignment, what is the most appropriate imaging to evaluate for associated acetabular/femoral head fracture and joint congruity?
options: Repeat plain AP pelvis only and discharge
CT of the hip/pelvis
Lower-extremity venous duplex
Bone scan as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: Post-reduction CT is standard to assess for associated posterior acetabular wall fractures, marginal impaction, intra-articular fragments, and concentric joint reduction that plain films can miss; these findings determine the need for surgery and weight-bearing status. A bone scan is too slow and nonspecific for acute fracture detection. Repeating a plain AP film alone and discharging would miss occult intra-articular fragments and incongruity. Venous duplex evaluates DVT, which is not the immediate diagnostic priority after reduction. CT after reduction is the guideline-endorsed step.

cases.diagnosis: Posterior hip dislocation with sciatic (peroneal division) nerve injury
pearls: Flexed, adducted, internally rotated, shortened limb after a dashboard injury = posterior hip dislocation until proven otherwise.
The sciatic nerve (peroneal division) is at risk—check and document dorsiflexion, great-toe extension, and dorsal foot sensation before and after reduction.
Posterior hip dislocation is an emergency: reduce within ~6 hours to lower the risk of femoral head avascular necrosis.
Obtain a post-reduction CT to detect acetabular wall fractures, loose fragments, and confirm concentric reduction.

cases.title: Thigh Deformity and Pain After a Motorcycle Crash
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe right thigh pain and deformity after a high-speed motorcycle crash.
cases.history: A 23-year-old man is brought in by EMS after losing control of his motorcycle at highway speed and being thrown. He complains of severe right thigh pain with obvious deformity and swelling. He denies head or neck pain and was wearing a helmet. No chest or abdominal complaints, but he feels lightheaded. PMH: none. Medications: none. NKDA. Social: smokes occasionally, drinks on weekends, college student. No prior surgeries. Family history noncontributory. Primary survey: airway patent, breathing equal, GCS 15. A traction splint was placed in the field. He received IV fentanyl en route. He is pale and diaphoretic. There is no external arterial bleeding visible, but the thigh is grossly swollen and tense over the mid-shaft. He has had no prior fractures. He reports the pain is the worst he has ever felt and is anxious about his leg.
cases.exam: Young man, pale and diaphoretic, in significant pain. Right thigh is markedly swollen, shortened, and angulated at the mid-shaft with a traction splint in place; severe tenderness and crepitus over the mid-femur. Skin intact, no open wound or bone protrusion. Distal pulses: dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial 2+ and symmetric, foot warm with brisk capillary refill. Sensation and motor function intact distally. Compartments of the leg soft. Pelvis stable to compression, no perineal hematoma or blood at the meatus. Abdomen soft and nontender. Contralateral limb and remainder of secondary survey normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: His HR is 122 and BP 104/64 with a swollen, deformed thigh. What is the most appropriate immediate priority?
options: Obtain an MRI of the femur to characterize the fracture as the first step today
Apply ice and elevate the leg, then reassess in an hour as the first step today
Begin resuscitation with IV access
Send him directly to the operating room before any imaging or labs as the first step today
stages.explanation: A femoral shaft fracture can sequester 1–1.5 L of blood into the thigh; with tachycardia and a falling-normal BP, the priority is hemorrhage control and resuscitation—two large-bore IVs, type and cross, and blood/fluids per ATLS—while searching for other bleeding sources. MRI is irrelevant to acute trauma resuscitation and delays care. Rushing to the OR without resuscitation or evaluating for concomitant injuries is unsafe. Ice and elevation are inadequate for a major long-bone fracture with signs of hypovolemia. Resuscitation and hemorrhage assessment come first.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Femur radiograph: transverse, mildly comminuted mid-shaft femur fracture, displaced
Hemoglobin 11.2 g/dL (from baseline ~15)
Lactate 3.1 mmol/L
FAST exam: negative for free fluid
Pelvis radiograph: no fracture
Type and cross sent; 2 units PRBC available

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Two days later he develops acute dyspnea, confusion, and a petechial rash over the chest and axillae, with new hypoxia. What is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Acute compartment syndrome of the thigh
Tension pneumothorax in this clinical context
Anaphylaxis to a blood transfusion
Fat embolism syndrome
stages.explanation: The triad of hypoxia/respiratory distress, neurologic change, and petechiae (often axillary/conjunctival) appearing 24–72 hours after a long-bone fracture is classic for fat embolism syndrome, driven by marrow fat entering the circulation. Anaphylaxis would occur during/just after transfusion with urticaria, wheeze, and hypotension, not a delayed petechial-neurologic-pulmonary triad. Tension pneumothorax causes acute unilateral absent breath sounds and tracheal deviation, not petechiae or confusion. Thigh compartment syndrome causes severe local pain and a tense compartment, not pulmonary/neurologic findings with petechiae. The timing and triad point to fat embolism.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the cornerstone of management for fat embolism syndrome in this patient?
options: High-dose IV heparin anticoagulation as initial therapy
Supportive care with supplemental oxygen
Emergent placement of an IVC filter as initial therapy
Systemic thrombolysis as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Fat embolism syndrome is managed supportively—supplemental oxygen, and mechanical ventilation if needed—because it is largely self-limited; early operative fixation of the long-bone fracture reduces incidence and ongoing marrow fat showering. Heparin is not indicated; fat emboli are not thrombi, and anticoagulation adds bleeding risk in a trauma patient. Thrombolysis treats thromboembolism, not fat embolism, and is dangerous post-trauma. IVC filters prevent lower-extremity DVT from reaching the lungs but do nothing for marrow fat already in the circulation. Supportive care plus early fixation is the standard.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Regarding definitive treatment of the femoral shaft fracture itself, which is the standard of care in this young adult?
options: Long-leg cast immobilization for 12 weeks
Intramedullary nailing of the femur
Skeletal traction as definitive treatment
Below-knee amputation based on current risk
stages.explanation: Intramedullary nailing is the gold standard for adult femoral shaft fractures, providing stable fixation, early mobilization, and high union rates; early fixation also helps reduce fat embolism risk. Casting cannot control a displaced femoral shaft fracture and leads to malunion and prolonged immobilization complications. Skeletal traction is a temporizing measure, not definitive treatment in modern practice. Amputation is reserved for non-salvageable mangled limbs, which is not the case here with intact distal neurovascular status. IM nailing is the answer.

cases.diagnosis: Femoral shaft fracture (high-energy) complicated by fat embolism syndrome
pearls: A femoral shaft fracture can sequester 1–1.5 L of blood—resuscitate and watch for hypovolemic shock.
Fat embolism syndrome: hypoxia + neurologic changes + petechiae appearing 24–72 hours after a long-bone fracture; management is supportive plus early fixation.
Intramedullary nailing is the definitive treatment of choice for adult femoral shaft fractures.
Always reassess distal pulses and compartments—high-energy femur fractures can have associated vascular injury or compartment syndrome.

cases.title: Knee Pain and Swelling After Being Struck by a Car Bumper
cases.chiefComplaint: Right knee pain, swelling, and inability to bear weight after being hit by a car at low speed.
cases.history: A 54-year-old man was struck on the lateral aspect of his right knee by the front bumper of a slowly moving car in a parking lot. He had immediate pain, rapid swelling, and cannot bear weight. He denies hip or ankle pain and has no other injuries. He reports the knee feels 'tight' and he has tingling in the foot that comes and goes. PMH: type 2 diabetes and hyperlipidemia. Medications: metformin 1000 mg twice daily, atorvastatin 40 mg nightly. NKDA. Social: former smoker (quit 5 years ago), drinks rarely, works as an accountant. No prior knee surgery. Family history noncontributory. He took acetaminophen before arrival with little relief. He is anxious because the knee is very swollen and he is worried about a fracture. He has had no fevers and the skin is intact.
cases.exam: Right knee with a large tense effusion and lateral soft-tissue swelling/ecchymosis. Marked tenderness over the lateral tibial plateau. Unable to perform a straight-leg raise due to pain; active and passive flexion severely limited. Skin intact, no open wound. Distal pulses (dorsalis pedis, posterior tibial) 2+ and symmetric; foot warm. Sensation intact but the patient reports intermittent paresthesias in the foot. Anterior compartment of the leg is firm but not rock-hard at presentation; pain with passive toe extension is present. No knee instability testable due to guarding. Contralateral leg normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Knee radiographs show a depressed lateral tibial plateau fracture with a fat-fluid level (lipohemarthrosis) on the cross-table lateral view. What does the lipohemarthrosis indicate?
options: Septic arthritis of the knee as the first step today
An intra-articular fracture allowing marrow fat
A normal incidental finding in adults in this clinical context
A purely ligamentous knee injury as the first step today
stages.explanation: A lipohemarthrosis—a fat-fluid level seen on a horizontal-beam (cross-table) lateral radiograph—occurs when marrow fat and blood escape from an intra-articular fracture into the joint, so it strongly implies an occult or visible intra-articular fracture even when the fracture line is subtle. A purely ligamentous injury bleeds into the joint (hemarthrosis) but does not release marrow fat, so no fat-fluid level forms. Septic arthritis produces purulent effusion, not a layered fat-fluid level, and is not trauma-related here. It is never a normal finding. The fat-fluid layer is a fracture marker.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Knee radiographs: depressed lateral tibial plateau fracture
Cross-table lateral: fat-fluid level (lipohemarthrosis) present
CT knee: lateral plateau articular depression ~6 mm with split fragment
No fibular neck fracture
Neurovascular: pulses intact; intermittent foot paresthesias
Anterior compartment firm, pain with passive toe extension

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Given his firm anterior compartment, pain with passive toe extension, and intermittent paresthesias, which complication must be actively monitored?
options: Deep vein thrombosis
Acute compartment syndrome of the leg
Complex regional pain syndrome
Septic arthritis in this presentation
stages.explanation: Tibial plateau fractures (especially high-energy bumper injuries) carry a real risk of acute compartment syndrome; the earliest, most sensitive sign is pain out of proportion and pain with passive stretch of the muscles in the compartment, along with a tense compartment and paresthesias—exactly the early picture here. DVT is a later risk and would not cause passive-stretch pain. Septic arthritis takes days to develop and presents with fever and warmth, not acute post-traumatic compartment findings. CRPS is a chronic late pain syndrome. The combination demands serial compartment exams and possible pressure measurement.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: His pain worsens, the compartment becomes rock-hard, and passive toe extension is exquisitely painful. Measured anterior compartment pressure is 45 mmHg with a diastolic BP of 90 (delta pressure 45). What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Elevate the leg above the heart and observe
Apply a circumferential compressive dressing
Start therapeutic anticoagulation in this setting
Emergent fasciotomy
stages.explanation: Wait—reassess the numbers: a delta pressure (diastolic minus compartment) at or below 30 mmHg, or unequivocal clinical compartment syndrome, mandates emergent fasciotomy. Here the clinical picture (rock-hard compartment, severe passive-stretch pain) is diagnostic regardless of the exact delta, and limb viability depends on prompt decompression. Elevating the leg lowers the perfusion gradient and worsens ischemia—the limb should be kept at heart level, not raised, and certainly not just observed. A compressive dressing further raises compartment pressure and is contraindicated. Anticoagulation treats DVT, not compartment syndrome, and adds bleeding risk. Emergent fasciotomy is the answer.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Regarding the displaced, depressed lateral tibial plateau fracture itself, what is the most appropriate definitive treatment?
options: Hinged knee brace and immediate full weight-bearing as initial therapy
Open reduction and internal fixation to restore the articular surface
Long-leg cast in extension for 8 weeks as initial therapy
Total knee arthroplasty as initial therapy
stages.explanation: A displaced intra-articular plateau fracture with significant articular depression/step-off is treated with ORIF to restore joint congruity and limb alignment, frequently elevating the depressed segment and supporting it with bone graft or substitute; this reduces post-traumatic arthritis. Immediate full weight-bearing in a brace would collapse the articular surface. Long-leg casting cannot restore the depressed articular surface and risks malunion plus stiffness. Total knee arthroplasty is not the acute treatment for a fresh fracture in a 54-year-old; it is a salvage option for severe late arthritis. ORIF restoring the articular surface is correct.

cases.diagnosis: Lateral tibial plateau fracture with lipohemarthrosis, complicated by acute compartment syndrome
pearls: A fat-fluid level (lipohemarthrosis) on a cross-table lateral knee film signals an intra-articular fracture—look hard for a subtle plateau fracture.
Tibial plateau fractures carry a high risk of compartment syndrome; the earliest sign is pain with passive stretch—do serial exams.
Compartment syndrome with a delta pressure ≤30 mmHg or clear clinical signs warrants emergent fasciotomy; never elevate the limb above the heart.
Displaced intra-articular plateau fractures need ORIF to restore the articular surface and reduce post-traumatic arthritis.

cases.title: Knee Pain and Trouble Lifting the Leg After a Fall
cases.chiefComplaint: Anterior right knee pain, swelling, and inability to straighten or lift the leg after falling onto the knee.
cases.history: A 45-year-old man tripped on a loose stair and fell directly onto his flexed right knee on a concrete step. He felt a crack and immediate anterior knee pain, with rapid swelling and inability to extend the knee or lift the leg off the bed. He denies hip or ankle pain. No numbness or tingling distally. PMH: well-controlled hypertension. Medications: lisinopril 10 mg daily. NKDA. Social: drinks a few beers weekly, never smoked, works in warehouse logistics. No prior knee surgery. Family history noncontributory. He took ibuprofen at home without relief. He is worried because he cannot straighten the leg and walking is impossible. He has had no fevers. The skin over the knee is intact but abraded from the fall. He is otherwise healthy and active and wants to know if this needs surgery.
cases.exam: Right knee with a moderate effusion/hemarthrosis and anterior tenderness. A palpable defect is appreciated over the patella with focal bony tenderness. The patient cannot perform a straight-leg raise and cannot actively extend the knee against gravity, though passive extension is possible. Skin abraded but intact—no open laceration communicating with the joint on probing. Distal pulses 2+ and symmetric; foot warm. Sensation and motor function distally intact. No ligamentous laxity testable due to pain/guarding. Contralateral knee normal with intact extensor mechanism.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: His inability to perform a straight-leg raise or actively extend the knee against gravity most directly indicates what?
options: An anterior cruciate ligament tear
Disruption of the knee extensor mechanism
Patellar tendinopathy (jumper's knee)
A meniscal tear as the first step today
stages.explanation: Active knee extension and the straight-leg raise depend on an intact extensor mechanism (quadriceps muscle → quadriceps tendon → patella → patellar tendon → tibial tubercle). Inability to extend against gravity or perform an SLR signals a break in that chain—here, a patellar fracture. An ACL tear causes instability and a hemarthrosis but preserves active extension. A meniscal tear causes mechanical symptoms and joint-line pain, not loss of active extension. Patellar tendinopathy is a chronic overuse pain syndrome, not an acute loss of extension after trauma. Loss of active extension localizes to the extensor mechanism.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Knee radiographs (AP/lateral):
Transverse fracture through the mid-patella
Fracture gap of 4 mm with articular step-off
Patella in normal vertical height (no patella alta or baja)
No femoral condyle or tibial plateau fracture
No bipartite patella (smooth corticated superolateral ossicle absent)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which finding distinguishes a displaced transverse patellar fracture requiring surgery from a nondisplaced fracture that can be managed nonoperatively?
options: Extensor mechanism disruption with fracture gap >3–4 mm or articular step-off >2 mm
Presence of a hemarthrosis as the primary diagnosis
Pain with weight-bearing as the primary diagnosis
A positive posterior fat pad sign as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: The surgical thresholds for a patellar fracture are loss of active extension (extensor mechanism disruption), fracture displacement/gap greater than about 3–4 mm, or articular step-off greater than about 2 mm—all of which this patient has. A hemarthrosis is common to most intra-articular knee injuries and does not by itself indicate surgery. Pain with weight-bearing is nonspecific. The posterior fat pad sign is an elbow finding (olecranon fossa), not a knee criterion. The key discriminators are extensor competence and the degree of displacement/step-off.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given a displaced transverse patellar fracture (4 mm gap, articular step-off) with loss of active extension, what is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Knee immobilizer in extension for 6 weeks with no surgery as initial therapy
Aspiration of the hemarthrosis and discharge with early flexion exercises
Operative fixation to restore the extensor mechanism
Total knee arthroplasty as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Displaced patellar fractures with extensor mechanism disruption require operative fixation—classically tension-band wiring (or screws ± wire)—to restore extensor continuity and the articular surface, enabling active extension and reducing post-traumatic arthritis. A knee immobilizer alone is appropriate only for nondisplaced fractures with an intact extensor mechanism. Total knee arthroplasty is not indicated for an acute fracture in a healthy 45-year-old. Aspiration and early flexion would leave the extensor mechanism disrupted and the fragments displaced, worsening the injury. Operative fixation is the correct choice.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Before operative planning, which step is most important to exclude an open fracture that would change urgency and antibiotic management?
options: Order inflammatory markers (ESR/CRP) as the immediate next step
Measure compartment pressures as the immediate next step
Carefully inspect the abraded skin
Obtain an MRI of the knee as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: With abrasions over the fracture, you must exclude an open (intra-articular) fracture: inspect all wounds and, if any wound could communicate with the joint, perform a saline load test (intra-articular saline injection; extravasation confirms an open joint). An open fracture mandates urgent irrigation, debridement, and antibiotics. MRI evaluates soft tissue but does not establish whether the joint is open and delays care. Compartment syndrome is uncommon with isolated patellar fractures and is not the priority here. ESR/CRP assess infection over time, not an acute open fracture. Confirming joint integrity is the key step.

cases.diagnosis: Displaced transverse patellar fracture with extensor mechanism disruption
pearls: Loss of active knee extension or inability to do a straight-leg raise = extensor mechanism disruption; in a patellar fracture this is a surgical indication.
Operative criteria for patellar fracture: extensor disruption, gap >3–4 mm, or articular step-off >2 mm—tension-band wiring is the classic fixation.
Always inspect overlying wounds and use a saline load test to exclude an open joint when skin is broken near the fracture.
Do not mistake a bipartite patella (smooth, corticated superolateral ossicle, often bilateral) for an acute fracture.

cases.title: Knee Buckling and a Gap Above the Kneecap After a Stumble
cases.chiefComplaint: Right knee pain, giving-way, and inability to straighten the knee after stumbling off a curb.
cases.history: A 58-year-old man stumbled stepping off a curb and felt a sudden 'pop' and tearing sensation just above his right kneecap as he tried to catch himself with the leg bent. The knee buckled and he could not straighten it or bear weight afterward. He denies a direct blow to the knee. He recalls intermittent anterior knee aches for months. PMH: type 2 diabetes, chronic kidney disease stage 2, hyperlipidemia. Medications: metformin 1000 mg twice daily, atorvastatin 40 mg, and a course of ciprofloxacin finished last week for a UTI. NKDA. Social: drinks occasionally, ex-smoker. Works as a school custodian. Family history noncontributory. He is overweight (BMI 31). He notes the leg feels 'unstable' and he cannot lift it straight off the bed. He is worried because he has to be on his feet for work and cannot walk now.
cases.exam: Right knee with suprapatellar swelling and ecchymosis. A palpable gap is felt just proximal to the superior pole of the patella. The patella sits lower than the contralateral side (patella baja relative position). The patient cannot actively extend the knee against gravity and cannot perform a straight-leg raise; passive extension is full. Skin intact. Distal pulses 2+ and symmetric, foot warm, sensation intact. No bony tenderness over the patella itself. Contralateral knee normal with intact extensor mechanism and no gap.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: A palpable suprapatellar gap, inability to extend the knee, and a low-riding patella after a stumble are most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Tibial plateau fracture
Quadriceps tendon rupture
Patellar fracture
Patellar tendon rupture
stages.explanation: A quadriceps tendon rupture produces a palpable gap ABOVE the patella, loss of active knee extension, and a low-riding patella (patella baja) because the unopposed patellar tendon pulls the patella distally. A patellar tendon rupture instead causes an infrapatellar gap and patella ALTA (high-riding patella). A patellar fracture would show focal patellar bony tenderness and a fracture on film, not a soft-tissue suprapatellar gap. A tibial plateau fracture causes a hemarthrosis and proximal tibial tenderness, not a suprapatellar tendon gap. The suprapatellar gap plus patella baja is classic for quadriceps tendon rupture.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Knee radiograph (lateral): patella baja (low-riding patella); no patellar or plateau fracture
Suprapatellar soft-tissue swelling
Ultrasound of the extensor mechanism: full-thickness defect of the quadriceps tendon at the superior patellar pole with retraction
Patellar tendon intact
Note: recent fluoroquinolone course (ciprofloxacin)

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which element of his history is a recognized risk factor for spontaneous/low-energy tendon rupture and should be noted?
options: His occasional alcohol use as the immediate next step
His occupation as a custodian before discharge planning
His use of atorvastatin only as the immediate next step
Recent fluoroquinolone use along with diabetes
stages.explanation: Fluoroquinolones (e.g., ciprofloxacin) are associated with tendinopathy and tendon rupture, and the risk is amplified by diabetes and chronic kidney disease—all present here, explaining a rupture from a low-energy stumble. Statins are far less strongly associated with tendon rupture and atorvastatin alone is not the key factor. His occupation involves standing but is not a defined rupture risk factor. Occasional alcohol use is not a recognized cause of tendon rupture. Recognizing the fluoroquinolone-plus-comorbidity association is the high-yield point and informs counseling.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this complete quadriceps tendon rupture with loss of active extension in an active 58-year-old, what is the most appropriate treatment?
options: Corticosteroid injection into the tendon defect
Knee immobilizer in extension and outpatient physical therapy only
Early surgical repair of the tendon
Total knee arthroplasty as initial therapy
stages.explanation: A complete quadriceps tendon rupture with loss of active extension requires early surgical repair (ideally within a few weeks) to restore the extensor mechanism and function; delay leads to retraction and poorer outcomes. Immobilization alone is appropriate only for partial tears with an intact extensor mechanism (preserved active extension). Corticosteroid injection into a ruptured tendon further weakens it and is contraindicated. Total knee arthroplasty addresses arthritis, not an acute tendon rupture. Early operative repair is the standard for complete tears.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which physical exam finding best distinguishes a quadriceps tendon rupture from a patellar tendon rupture?
options: Tenderness of the joint line as the primary diagnosis
Position of the patella
Presence of a knee effusion as the primary diagnosis
Ability to flex the knee as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Patellar position is the key discriminator: in a quadriceps tendon rupture the unopposed patellar tendon pulls the patella down (patella baja, low-riding), with the gap above the patella; in a patellar tendon rupture the unopposed quadriceps pulls the patella up (patella alta, high-riding), with the gap below it. A knee effusion can occur with either and is nonspecific. Knee flexion is generally preserved in both. Joint-line tenderness reflects meniscal pathology, not extensor tendon level. Patellar height (and gap location) is the distinguishing finding.

cases.diagnosis: Complete quadriceps tendon rupture
pearls: Suprapatellar gap + loss of active extension + patella baja = quadriceps tendon rupture (gap is ABOVE the patella).
Patellar tendon rupture is the mirror image: infrapatellar gap and patella alta (high-riding patella).
Fluoroquinolones, diabetes, CKD, and corticosteroids predispose to low-energy tendon rupture—ask about recent antibiotics.
Complete extensor tendon ruptures need early surgical repair; partial tears with intact extension can be braced.

cases.title: Knee Instability and a Vague Ache After a Dashboard Injury
cases.chiefComplaint: Persistent right knee discomfort and a feeling of instability weeks after a car crash.
cases.history: A 28-year-old man presents to clinic 3 weeks after a motor vehicle collision in which his flexed knee struck the dashboard. The acute pain has improved, but he has a lingering deep, vague ache and a sense that the knee feels 'loose,' especially going down stairs and decelerating. He had transient swelling that resolved. He denies locking or true giving-way. No numbness or tingling. PMH: none. Medications: occasional ibuprofen. NKDA. Social: recreational soccer player, drinks socially, never smoked, works in IT. No prior knee injury or surgery. Family history noncontributory. He was evaluated in the ED after the crash with normal radiographs and discharged. He is frustrated that the knee 'isn't right' and wants to return to soccer. He has full strength in the leg and walks without a limp but notices the instability with cutting maneuvers.
cases.exam: Right knee with no effusion now and full range of motion. With the knee flexed to 90°, the tibia rests posteriorly relative to the femur compared to the other side (posterior sag sign). Posterior drawer at 90° shows increased posterior tibial translation with a soft endpoint. Lachman and anterior drawer are normal. No varus/valgus laxity at 0° and 30°. McMurray negative; joint lines nontender. Distal pulses 2+, sensation intact. The 'quadriceps active test' reduces the posteriorly sagging tibia anteriorly. Skin intact, no tenderness over the proximal tibia.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: A posterior sag of the tibia at 90° of flexion and a positive posterior drawer after a dashboard injury indicate injury to which structure?
options: Posterior cruciate ligament
Lateral meniscus
Anterior cruciate ligament
Medial collateral ligament
stages.explanation: The posterior cruciate ligament prevents posterior translation of the tibia on the femur; a dashboard injury drives the proximal tibia posteriorly, tearing the PCL. The classic findings are a posterior sag sign and a positive posterior drawer, both present here. An ACL tear produces a positive Lachman/anterior drawer (normal in this patient). The MCL produces valgus laxity, which is absent. A lateral meniscus tear causes mechanical symptoms and joint-line tenderness, not posterior tibial translation. The posterior sag and posterior drawer localize the injury to the PCL.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: Which test helps confirm the posterior sag is due to PCL injury rather than masking an ACL injury?
options: McMurray test as the immediate next step
Quadriceps active test
Ober test as the immediate next step
Patellar apprehension test as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: The quadriceps active test confirms PCL deficiency: with the knee at 90° and tibia posteriorly subluxed, an active quadriceps contraction pulls the tibia anteriorly (reduces the sag), demonstrating posterior laxity. This also avoids the pitfall of misreading a posteriorly sagging tibia as a falsely 'positive' anterior drawer (which can mimic ACL injury). McMurray tests for meniscal tears. The patellar apprehension test evaluates patellar instability. The Ober test evaluates iliotibial band tightness. The quadriceps active test specifically corroborates PCL injury.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
MRI right knee:
Isolated complete tear of the posterior cruciate ligament
Intact ACL, MCL, LCL, and posterolateral corner
Menisci intact
No bone bruise pattern of concern, no fracture
Grade II posterior laxity clinically (tibia 5–10 mm posterior)

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this isolated grade II PCL tear in an active patient, what is the most appropriate initial management?
options: Long-leg cast for 8 weeks as initial therapy
Immediate PCL reconstruction as initial therapy
Nonoperative management: protected rehabilitation emphasizing quadriceps strengthening, often with a PCL/dynamic brace
Arthroscopic meniscectomy as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Isolated grade I–II PCL injuries are typically managed nonoperatively with a structured rehab program emphasizing quadriceps strengthening (the quadriceps dynamically counteracts posterior tibial sag), frequently with a PCL-specific brace; most athletes return to play. Immediate reconstruction is reserved for high-grade (grade III) injuries, combined ligament/posterolateral-corner injuries, or bony avulsions—not an isolated grade II tear. A long-leg cast promotes stiffness and quadriceps atrophy, worsening dynamic stability. Meniscectomy is irrelevant with intact menisci. Quadriceps-focused rehabilitation is the correct first-line approach.

cases.diagnosis: Isolated posterior cruciate ligament (PCL) tear, grade II
pearls: Dashboard injury → posteriorly directed force on the tibia → PCL tear; look for the posterior sag sign and a positive posterior drawer.
The quadriceps active test confirms PCL injury and prevents misreading the sag as a false-positive anterior drawer.
Isolated low-grade PCL tears are managed nonoperatively with quadriceps-dominant rehab, often with a PCL brace.
Surgery is reserved for grade III, combined ligament/posterolateral-corner injuries, or bony avulsions.

cases.title: Inner Knee Pain After a Hit During Football
cases.chiefComplaint: Medial right knee pain and a sense of looseness after being hit on the outside of the knee during a football game.
cases.history: A 19-year-old college football player presents to clinic the day after taking a direct blow to the lateral aspect of his planted right knee during a tackle. He felt immediate medial-sided pain and a brief 'opening' sensation. He was able to walk off the field and continued limited play. There was mild swelling overnight. He denies locking, true giving-way, or numbness. No hip or ankle pain. PMH: none. Medications: occasional ibuprofen. NKDA. Social: nonsmoker, occasional alcohol, student-athlete. No prior knee injury or surgery. Family history noncontributory. He iced the knee and used a compression sleeve overnight. He is eager to return to play and asks whether he needs surgery or an MRI. He reports the pain is localized to the inner side of the knee and worsens when he pushes the leg outward.
cases.exam: Right knee with mild medial soft-tissue swelling and tenderness over the medial femoral epicondyle/MCL origin. Valgus stress at 30° of flexion reproduces medial pain with increased laxity but a firm endpoint; valgus stress at full extension (0°) is stable. Lachman and anterior drawer are normal; posterior drawer normal. Varus stress stable. McMurray negative; joint lines otherwise nontender. No effusion appreciated on ballottement. Full active extension and intact straight-leg raise. Distal pulses 2+, sensation intact. Gait mildly antalgic.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Medial pain with valgus laxity at 30° but stability at full extension after a lateral blow is most consistent with which injury?
options: Combined MCL and ACL/PCL (multiligament) injury
Isolated medial collateral ligament sprain
Anterior cruciate ligament tear
Medial meniscus tear in this clinical context
stages.explanation: A valgus (lateral-to-medial) blow stresses the MCL; pain and laxity with valgus stress at 30° of flexion, with a firm endpoint and stability in full extension, indicate an isolated MCL sprain (the MCL is the primary valgus restraint at 30°). An ACL tear would show a positive Lachman, which is normal here. An isolated medial meniscus tear causes joint-line tenderness and mechanical symptoms, not valgus laxity. Laxity to valgus at full extension (0°) would suggest a more severe combined injury involving the cruciates/posteromedial corner; this patient is stable at 0°, arguing against a multiligament injury.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: On valgus stress at 30°, there is increased medial opening compared to the other knee but a definite firm endpoint with about 6 mm more laxity. How is this MCL injury best graded?
options: Grade III (complete tear, gross laxity with a soft/no endpoint)
Grade I (sprain, no instability) before discharge planning
It cannot be graded clinically as the immediate next step
Grade II
stages.explanation: MCL sprains are graded clinically: grade I has pain but minimal/no laxity (a few mm) with a firm endpoint; grade II has increased laxity (roughly 5–10 mm more opening) with a firm endpoint, indicating a partial tear—matching this exam; grade III has gross laxity (>10 mm) with a soft or absent endpoint, indicating a complete tear. A firm endpoint with moderate increased opening is the hallmark of grade II. The presence of a definite endpoint argues against a complete (grade III) tear, and the increased laxity is beyond a grade I sprain. MCL injuries can be graded clinically.

stages.label: IMAGING
stages.content:
Knee radiographs: no fracture; medial joint space symmetric, no avulsion at the MCL origin
MRI (obtained for grading/return-to-play): grade II (partial) sprain of the superficial MCL near the femoral origin
ACL, PCL, LCL intact
Menisci intact
No bone contusion of concern

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this isolated grade II MCL sprain, what is the most appropriate management?
options: Nonoperative care: hinged knee brace, early protected range of motion,
Long-leg cast in extension for 6 weeks as initial therapy
Surgical MCL reconstruction now as initial therapy
Immediate return to contact play without protection as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Isolated MCL sprains—including grade III—heal well nonoperatively because the MCL has excellent vascular healing potential; treatment is a hinged knee brace, early protected motion, and progressive strengthening, with return to sport when strength and stability are restored. Surgery is reserved for select cases such as combined ligament injuries, distal tibial-sided (Stener-like) avulsions, or persistent instability after rehab—not an isolated grade II tear. A long-leg cast causes stiffness and atrophy and is unnecessary. Returning to contact immediately without protection risks conversion to a worse injury. Functional bracing and rehab is the standard.

cases.diagnosis: Isolated grade II medial collateral ligament (MCL) sprain
pearls: A valgus blow to the lateral knee injures the MCL; test valgus stress at 30° (MCL is the primary restraint) and at 0° (laxity at 0° suggests combined injury).
Grade by endpoint and laxity: I = firm/minimal, II = firm/increased (partial), III = soft or absent endpoint/gross laxity (complete).
Isolated MCL injuries—even grade III—heal well nonoperatively with a hinged brace and early protected motion.
Surgery is reserved for combined ligament injuries, tibial-sided avulsions, or persistent instability after rehab.

cases.title: Sudden Hot, Swollen Knee in a Hospitalized Patient
cases.chiefComplaint: Acute right knee pain, swelling, and warmth that developed overnight on the medical ward.
cases.history: A 72-year-old woman admitted 2 days ago for community-acquired pneumonia develops acute right knee pain, swelling, warmth, and stiffness overnight. She cannot bear weight. She has had similar but milder knee flares in the past attributed to 'arthritis.' She denies recent knee trauma. PMH: osteoarthritis, hypertension, hypothyroidism, and a remote episode of similar acute joint swelling in the wrist. Medications: amlodipine 5 mg, levothyroxine 75 mcg, and now IV ceftriaxone/azithromycin for pneumonia. NKDA. Social: lives independently, never smoked, rare alcohol. Family history noncontributory. She is afebrile to low-grade. She is worried this is 'the gout' her sister has, but she has never had a great-toe attack. She has had no recent diuretic use. The flare began without an obvious trigger during her admission. She is otherwise recovering well from the pneumonia.
cases.exam: Right knee is warm, diffusely swollen with a tense effusion, and tender; range of motion is markedly limited by pain. Overlying skin is erythematous without a portal of entry or fluctuance. No periarticular tophi. Other joints currently nontender; mild bony enlargement of both knees consistent with osteoarthritis. No great-toe (first MTP) involvement. Distal pulses intact, sensation normal. Lungs with improving right basilar crackles. No new murmurs. No lymphangitic streaking. She guards the knee strongly against any movement.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: For this acute monoarticular hot, swollen knee, what is the single most important next diagnostic step?
options: Obtain serum uric acid level and treat if elevated in this clinical context
Order an MRI of the knee as the first step today
Arthrocentesis with synovial fluid analysis
Start empiric colchicine and observe the response in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Any acute monoarticular arthritis—especially in an inpatient with fever—requires arthrocentesis to send synovial fluid for cell count, Gram stain and culture, and crystal analysis, primarily to exclude septic arthritis, which is a joint-threatening emergency. Empiric colchicine without aspiration could mask or delay diagnosis of infection. MRI does not distinguish crystal disease from infection and delays definitive sampling. Serum uric acid is neither sensitive nor specific during an acute attack (it can be normal in acute gout and elevated without gout) and does not address crystal type or infection. Joint aspiration is the decisive step.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Synovial fluid analysis:
WBC 28,000 /µL (78% neutrophils) — inflammatory range
Gram stain: no organisms
Crystals: rhomboid-shaped, positively birefringent crystals seen
Knee radiograph: chondrocalcinosis (calcification of the meniscal/articular cartilage)
Serum WBC 11.5 ×10³/µL
Synovial fluid culture: pending

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Rhomboid, positively birefringent crystals with chondrocalcinosis on radiograph indicate which diagnosis?
options: Calcium pyrophosphate deposition disease
Rheumatoid arthritis as the primary diagnosis
Gout (monosodium urate) in this clinical context
Septic arthritis as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Calcium pyrophosphate dihydrate crystals are rhomboid (or rod-shaped) and POSITIVELY birefringent (blue when parallel to the compensator), and CPPD characteristically shows chondrocalcinosis—cartilage calcification—on radiographs; this is pseudogout. Gout crystals are needle-shaped and NEGATIVELY birefringent (yellow when parallel). Septic arthritis is excluded here by a negative Gram stain and the identification of crystals (though culture is pending, the inflammatory but not frankly purulent count plus crystals favors CPPD). Rheumatoid arthritis is typically symmetric and polyarticular without crystals. The crystal shape, positive birefringence, and chondrocalcinosis define CPPD.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Once septic arthritis is reasonably excluded (negative Gram stain, crystals present, culture pending), what is the most appropriate first-line treatment for this acute CPPD flare in a 72-year-old with hypertension and recovering pneumonia?
options: Intra-articular corticosteroid injection or a short course of oral corticosteroids/colchicine
High-dose systemic NSAIDs as monotherapy regardless of comorbidities
Urgent surgical synovectomy as initial therapy
Begin urate-lowering therapy with allopurinol as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Acute CPPD flares are treated with anti-inflammatory therapy; in an elderly patient with comorbidities, an intra-articular corticosteroid injection into the single accessible knee (after infection is excluded) is excellent, and short-course oral corticosteroids or low-dose colchicine are alternatives. NSAIDs are effective but risky as monotherapy in an older adult with hypertension and possible renal/GI concerns, so they are not the best blanket choice here. Allopurinol lowers urate for gout and has no role in CPPD. Synovectomy is not a treatment for an acute crystal flare. Targeted anti-inflammatory therapy, favoring intra-articular steroids, is best.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: CPPD/pseudogout can be triggered by or associated with underlying metabolic conditions. Which workup is most appropriate, particularly in younger or recurrent cases?
options: No further workup is ever indicated as the immediate next step
Routine bone marrow biopsy as the immediate next step
Screen for associations such as hyperparathyroidism, hemochromatosis, hypomagnesemia,
Empiric lifelong allopurinol for all patients as the immediate next step
stages.explanation: CPPD is associated with metabolic disorders—classically hyperparathyroidism (hypercalcemia), hemochromatosis, hypomagnesemia, and hypophosphatasia (also hypothyroidism)—so screening (calcium, PTH, iron studies/ferritin, magnesium, alkaline phosphatase) is appropriate, especially in younger patients or recurrent/polyarticular disease. A bone marrow biopsy is unrelated to crystal arthropathy. Allopurinol treats gout, not CPPD, and is not given empirically to all. Saying no workup is ever needed is incorrect, because identifying a treatable metabolic driver can change management. Screening for the classic CPPD associations is the right step.

cases.diagnosis: Acute calcium pyrophosphate deposition disease (pseudogout, CPPD) of the knee
pearls: Any acute hot, swollen joint requires arthrocentesis first to exclude septic arthritis before assuming crystal disease.
CPPD crystals are rhomboid and positively birefringent; gout crystals are needle-shaped and negatively birefringent.
Chondrocalcinosis (cartilage calcification) on radiograph supports CPPD/pseudogout.
Screen for hyperparathyroidism, hemochromatosis, hypomagnesemia, and hypophosphatasia—especially in young or recurrent CPPD; allopurinol has no role in CPPD.

cases.title: Stiff, Swollen Knuckles in the Morning
cases.chiefComplaint: Several months of painful, swollen finger joints with prolonged morning stiffness.
cases.history: A 45-year-old woman presents with a 5-month history of gradually worsening pain and swelling in both hands, particularly the knuckles. She reports stiffness lasting about 90 minutes each morning that loosens with activity. The symptoms are symmetric, involving the same joints on both hands, and she has noticed the same in both wrists. She feels fatigued and occasionally has low-grade subjective fevers. There is no rash, no recent tick bite, and no preceding diarrheal or genitourinary illness. PMH is notable only for hypothyroidism on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. She takes ibuprofen 400 mg as needed with partial relief. No other medications. NKDA. She works as an accountant, has never smoked, drinks wine occasionally. Family history is significant for a mother with rheumatoid arthritis and an aunt with lupus. She denies dry eyes or dry mouth, photosensitivity, oral ulcers, or Raynaud phenomenon. She is concerned because typing has become difficult and her wedding ring no longer fits.
cases.exam: Bilateral, symmetric swelling and tenderness of the second and third metacarpophalangeal (MCP) joints and proximal interphalangeal (PIP) joints with boggy synovial thickening and warmth. Early ulnar deviation of the fingers at the MCPs is noted. Both wrists are tender with reduced range of motion. The distal interphalangeal (DIP) joints are spared.
