Grip strength is reduced bilaterally. No subcutaneous nodules over the extensor surfaces yet. No skin rash, no nail pitting, no dactylitis. Feet are nontender. No lymphadenopathy.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature of this presentation most strongly favors an inflammatory arthritis over osteoarthritis?
options: Bony, hard joint enlargement without warmth as the first step today
Pain that worsens with use throughout the day as the first step today
Involvement of the distal interphalangeal joints as the first step today
Morning stiffness lasting more than one hour with symmetric MCP/PIP synovitis
stages.explanation: Prolonged morning stiffness (>1 hour) that improves with activity, plus warm, boggy, symmetric synovitis of the MCP and PIP joints, is the classic signature of an inflammatory arthritis such as rheumatoid arthritis. DIP involvement (option A) and hard bony enlargement without warmth (option D) are hallmarks of osteoarthritis, which characteristically spares the MCPs and produces Heberden/Bouchard nodes. Pain that worsens with use and improves with rest (option B) is typical of a mechanical/degenerative process; inflammatory arthritis classically improves with use and is worst after inactivity. The combination of duration, symmetry, joint distribution, and warmth here points firmly toward inflammation rather than degeneration.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which combination of serologic tests is most appropriate to order to support the suspected diagnosis?
options: Anti-dsDNA and anti-Smith antibodies as the primary diagnosis
HLA-B27 and anti-Ro/anti-La antibodies as the primary diagnosis
Antistreptolysin O titer and uric acid as the primary diagnosis
Rheumatoid factor and anti-cyclic citrullinated peptide antibodies
stages.explanation: Rheumatoid factor (RF) and anti-CCP antibodies are the cornerstone serologies for rheumatoid arthritis. Anti-CCP is highly specific (~95%) and predicts erosive disease; RF supports the diagnosis and correlates with extra-articular manifestations. Anti-dsDNA and anti-Smith (option A) are specific for SLE, which would more often cause a nonerosive arthritis with other systemic features. HLA-B27 and anti-Ro/La (option C) suggest spondyloarthropathy or Sjogren, not the symmetric small-joint picture here. ASO and uric acid (option D) evaluate post-streptococcal arthritis and gout, neither of which fits a symmetric, chronic, small-joint synovitis. ESR/CRP should also be obtained as markers of inflammatory activity.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
ESR 48 mm/hr
CRP 22 mg/L
Rheumatoid factor 96 IU/mL (high)
Anti-CCP antibody 140 U (strongly positive)
ANA negative
Hemoglobin 11.4 g/dL (normocytic)
Uric acid 4.8 mg/dL
Hand radiographs: periarticular osteopenia and early marginal erosions at the second and third MCP joints; soft-tissue swelling; joint spaces otherwise preserved

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which is the most appropriate disease-modifying therapy to initiate first?
options: Long-term oral prednisone monotherapy
Acetaminophen scheduled around the clock
An anti-TNF biologic as initial monotherapy
Methotrexate
stages.explanation: Methotrexate is the recommended first-line disease-modifying antirheumatic drug (DMARD) for newly diagnosed RA; it slows radiographic progression and is the anchor drug for the disease. Early DMARD initiation within the 'window of opportunity' improves long-term outcomes. Long-term prednisone monotherapy (option A) controls inflammation but does not adequately prevent erosions and carries cumulative toxicity; low-dose steroids may be used as a short bridge only. Acetaminophen (option C) provides symptomatic relief but no disease modification. Biologics such as anti-TNF agents (option D) are typically reserved for patients who fail methotrexate or have high disease activity with poor prognostic features; they are not standard first-line monotherapy and require TB screening. Methotrexate is given with folic acid to reduce toxicity.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Before starting the chosen therapy and during follow-up, which monitoring is most important to prevent a serious adverse effect?
options: Serial fasting lipid panels as the leading concern
Frequent serum potassium measurements as the leading concern
Periodic CBC and liver function tests, plus baseline hepatitis serologies and a chest film
Routine bone densitometry every 3 months as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Methotrexate can cause hepatotoxicity and myelosuppression, so periodic CBC and liver enzymes are essential, along with baseline hepatitis B/C serologies and a chest radiograph (to screen for pulmonary disease before this potentially pneumotoxic drug). Patients must also avoid alcohol and take folic acid. Lipid panels (option A) are relevant to cardiovascular risk in RA but are not the key safety monitor for methotrexate. Bone densitometry (option C) matters for chronic steroid users but is not a methotrexate safety parameter and is not done every 3 months. Potassium monitoring (option D) is not relevant to methotrexate toxicity. Methotrexate is teratogenic and contraindicated in pregnancy, so contraception counseling is also required.

cases.diagnosis: Rheumatoid arthritis of the hands
pearls: RA causes symmetric MCP/PIP/wrist synovitis with morning stiffness >1 hour and characteristically spares the DIP joints (unlike osteoarthritis).
Anti-CCP is highly specific for RA and predicts erosive disease; RF is supportive but less specific.
Methotrexate is the first-line DMARD; start early within the 'window of opportunity' and monitor CBC and LFTs.
Early radiographic clues are periarticular osteopenia and marginal erosions; ulnar deviation and swan-neck/boutonniere deformities are late findings.

cases.title: A Limping Boy With Fever and Leg Pain
cases.chiefComplaint: Four days of worsening lower-leg pain, limp, and fever.
cases.history: A 9-year-old boy is admitted with 4 days of progressively worsening pain in the lower right leg and 2 days of fever to 39 °C. He now refuses to bear weight. The pain is localized near the knee and is constant, worse at night, and not clearly related to a single injury, though he plays soccer regularly. There was a minor scraped knee about 2 weeks ago that healed. He has had no cough, dysuria, diarrhea, or rash. PMH is unremarkable; immunizations are up to date. No chronic medications; he took acetaminophen with minimal relief. NKDA. He lives with both parents and a younger sibling, attends elementary school, and has no sick contacts or recent travel. Family history is noncontributory. On review of systems he reports decreased appetite and fatigue but no joint swelling elsewhere. There is no history of sickle cell disease, immunodeficiency, or recent dental work. Parents deny any penetrating wound to the foot or recent surgery.
cases.exam: Ill-appearing but nontoxic boy. Point tenderness over the distal femoral metaphysis just above the right knee with overlying warmth and mild soft-tissue swelling; he guards markedly with palpation. The knee joint itself has a small effusion but only mild pain with gentle passive range of motion, and he tolerates some flexion/extension once distracted. No erythematous fluctuant abscess. Distal pulses intact, sensation normal, capillary refill brisk. No other joints involved. Skin without portal of entry currently. Cardiac and pulmonary exams normal; no heart murmur.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which is the most likely diagnosis given metaphyseal point tenderness, fever, and refusal to bear weight?
options: Septic arthritis of the knee as the first step today
Transient (toxic) synovitis of the hip
Acute hematogenous osteomyelitis of the distal femur
Growing pains as the first step today
stages.explanation: Focal tenderness over the distal femoral metaphysis with fever and constant night pain in a child is classic for acute hematogenous osteomyelitis, which seeds the metaphysis because of its sluggish sinusoidal blood flow. Septic arthritis (option A) would typically produce severe pain with any joint motion and resistance to all passive movement; here the joint tolerates gentle motion while the bone is exquisitely tender. Growing pains (option C) are bilateral, occur in the evening, never cause fever or focal tenderness, and resolve by morning. Toxic synovitis (option D) affects the hip, follows a viral illness, and causes a mildly painful limp without high fever or focal bony tenderness. The localized metaphyseal signs point to bone.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Initial plain radiographs of the femur are normal. What is the most appropriate next imaging study?
options: Non-contrast CT of the femur
MRI with contrast of the affected bone
DEXA bone densitometry
Repeat plain radiographs in 6 hours
stages.explanation: MRI with contrast is the imaging modality of choice for suspected osteomyelitis because it detects marrow edema and early infection days before plain films show changes; it also delineates subperiosteal or soft-tissue abscess that may need drainage. Plain radiographs are typically normal for 7-14 days (lytic changes and periosteal reaction appear late), so repeating them immediately (option A) is low yield early on. CT (option C listed as option B) shows cortical destruction and sequestra but is less sensitive than MRI for early marrow involvement and adds radiation. DEXA (option D) measures bone density and has no role in infection. MRI's early sensitivity is precisely why it is preferred when films lag behind the clinical picture.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 16.8 ×10³/µL (82% neutrophils)
ESR 76 mm/hr
CRP 98 mg/L
Blood cultures: pending (drawn before antibiotics)
Femur radiograph: no lytic lesion or periosteal reaction
MRI right femur: marrow edema in the distal metaphysis with enhancement and a small subperiosteal fluid collection consistent with osteomyelitis

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which is the most appropriate empiric antibiotic choice while cultures are pending in this otherwise healthy, immunized child?
options: IV vancomycin alone as initial therapy
IV ceftriaxone monotherapy targeting Gram-negative rods
Oral amoxicillin as initial therapy
IV antistaphylococcal coverage such as cefazolin
stages.explanation: Staphylococcus aureus is by far the most common cause of acute hematogenous osteomyelitis in healthy children, so empiric IV therapy should target it: cefazolin or nafcillin for methicillin-susceptible disease, with vancomycin (or clindamycin) added/substituted where community MRSA is prevalent. Obtaining blood cultures first—and bone aspirate when feasible—guides de-escalation. Oral amoxicillin (option A) lacks reliable staphylococcal coverage and is inadequate as initial therapy for acute osteomyelitis. Vancomycin alone (option B) covers MRSA but is not first choice when MSSA is likely, as beta-lactams are more effective against susceptible strains. Ceftriaxone monotherapy (option D) misses staphylococci and is aimed at the wrong organisms. Therapy is later tailored to culture results and can transition to oral once the patient improves.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: After 48 hours of appropriate IV antibiotics, the boy still has high fevers, worsening pain, and an enlarging subperiosteal collection. What is the best next step?
options: Start high-dose corticosteroids to reduce inflammation
Continue the same antibiotics and reassess in another 72 hours
Switch immediately to oral antibiotics and discharge
Surgical drainage/debridement of the subperiosteal abscess
stages.explanation: Failure to improve on appropriate antibiotics with an enlarging subperiosteal or intraosseous abscess is an indication for surgical drainage and debridement; pus under pressure will not be sterilized by antibiotics alone and threatens further bone destruction. Simply waiting another 72 hours (option A) risks progression to chronic osteomyelitis, sequestrum formation, or growth-plate injury. Switching to oral antibiotics and discharging (option C) is appropriate only once the child is clinically improving with falling inflammatory markers—not during clinical deterioration. Corticosteroids (option D) are not indicated and would impair the immune response to infection. Source control plus continued culture-directed antibiotics is the correct combined approach when the patient is not responding.

cases.diagnosis: Acute hematogenous osteomyelitis of the distal femur
pearls: Acute hematogenous osteomyelitis in children localizes to the metaphysis of long bones due to sluggish sinusoidal blood flow; S. aureus is the most common pathogen.
MRI detects osteomyelitis days earlier than plain films, which can stay normal for 1-2 weeks before showing lytic change and periosteal reaction.
Empiric therapy targets S. aureus (cefazolin/nafcillin, add vancomycin or clindamycin if MRSA is likely) after blood cultures are drawn.
Persistent fever or an enlarging abscess despite antibiotics warrants surgical drainage; untreated disease can become chronic with sequestrum formation.

cases.title: A Painful, Growing Lump Above the Knee
cases.chiefComplaint: Several weeks of deep thigh pain and a firm, enlarging mass just above the knee.
cases.history: A 15-year-old boy presents with about 6 weeks of progressively worsening pain in the lower right thigh, now associated with a palpable firm swelling just above the knee. The pain is deep, aching, present at rest, and notably worse at night, sometimes waking him. He initially attributed it to a basketball injury, but the pain and swelling have steadily increased despite rest and ibuprofen. He denies fevers, chills, night sweats, or weight loss. There is no preceding significant trauma or fracture. PMH is unremarkable. No regular medications other than as-needed ibuprofen 400 mg. NKDA. He is a sophomore in high school, plays basketball, does not use tobacco, alcohol, or drugs. Family history is noncontributory; no known hereditary cancer syndromes or prior radiation exposure. Review of systems is otherwise negative, including no respiratory symptoms. He is tall for his age and has had a recent growth spurt. He is anxious about the lump and reports the overlying skin sometimes feels warm.
cases.exam: A firm, tender, fixed mass over the distal femur just proximal to the knee, with mild overlying warmth and dilated superficial veins. The mass is approximately 6 cm and does not transilluminate. Knee range of motion is mildly limited by pain but the joint is stable with no effusion. Distal neurovascular exam is intact: pulses, sensation, and motor function normal. No regional lymphadenopathy. No other masses. Lungs clear to auscultation. No clubbing.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which feature of this adolescent's mass is most concerning for malignancy and warrants urgent evaluation?
options: Symptoms that began after a sports practice as the first step today
A deep, fixed, enlarging mass with constant night pain in a teenager
Mild warmth over the skin as the first step today
Pain that improves completely with ibuprofen as the first step today
stages.explanation: A deep-seated, fixed, progressively enlarging mass with constant pain that is worse at night in an adolescent is a red-flag presentation for a primary bone malignancy such as osteosarcoma, which peaks during the adolescent growth spurt and favors the metaphysis around the knee. Pain that resolves with NSAIDs (option A) is reassuring rather than alarming—though notably, osteoid osteoma classically responds dramatically to NSAIDs, which is not the case here. A history of sports activity (option C) is a common red herring; many bone tumors are discovered after minor trauma draws attention to a preexisting lesion. Mild skin warmth (option D) is nonspecific. The progressive, deep, night-predominant pain with an enlarging fixed mass is the combination that mandates prompt imaging.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial imaging study for this suspected bone lesion?
options: Ultrasound of the thigh
Immediate open surgical excision
Whole-body bone scan in this clinical context
Plain radiographs of the femur in two views
stages.explanation: Plain radiographs in two orthogonal views are the appropriate first imaging step for any suspected primary bone lesion; they characterize the lesion's location, margins, matrix, and periosteal reaction and often suggest aggressiveness. Ultrasound (option A) is useful for soft-tissue masses but poorly evaluates intraosseous lesions and periosteal reaction. A bone scan (option C) helps with staging and detecting skip/metastatic lesions but is not the initial study to characterize the primary. Immediate open excision (option D) is never appropriate before imaging and biopsy planning, because an improperly placed incision can contaminate compartments and compromise future limb-salvage surgery; biopsy should be done at the treating sarcoma center. After radiographs, MRI of the whole bone and tissue diagnosis follow.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Alkaline phosphatase 410 U/L (elevated)
LDH 320 U/L (elevated)
Calcium 9.6 mg/dL
Femur radiograph: aggressive mixed lytic and sclerotic lesion in the distal femoral metaphysis with cortical destruction, a 'sunburst' pattern of periosteal new bone, and a Codman triangle at the lesion margin
MRI: large soft-tissue extension with marrow involvement; growth plate abutted

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Given the radiographic 'sunburst' pattern and Codman triangle in this age group, what is the most likely diagnosis, and what is the essential next step to confirm it?
options: Osteosarcoma; confirm with biopsy performed at the treating orthopedic oncology center
Giant cell tumor; confirm with excisional curettage in clinic as the primary diagnosis
Osteomyelitis; confirm with a trial of antibiotics as the primary diagnosis
Ewing sarcoma; confirm with serum tumor markers as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: The combination of an aggressive metaphyseal lesion with a sunburst (spiculated) periosteal reaction and Codman triangle in a 15-year-old, plus elevated alkaline phosphatase and LDH, is classic for osteosarcoma, the most common primary bone malignancy of adolescents. Definitive diagnosis requires biopsy, and the biopsy must be planned and performed by the orthopedic oncology team that will do the resection, because a poorly placed tract can preclude limb salvage. Ewing sarcoma (option A) is on the differential but classically arises in the diaphysis with onion-skin periosteal reaction, and serum markers do not confirm it. Osteomyelitis (option C) can mimic tumor but should not be 'confirmed' by an empiric antibiotic trial when imaging shows an aggressive matrix-forming tumor. Giant cell tumor (option D) occurs in skeletally mature patients at the epiphysis and is not curetted blindly when malignancy is suspected.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: After biopsy confirms osteosarcoma and staging CT chest is obtained, what is the standard treatment approach?
options: Radiation therapy alone, as osteosarcoma is highly radiosensitive as initial therapy
Immediate amputation with no chemotherapy as initial therapy
Neoadjuvant chemotherapy, then limb-salvage surgery , followed by adjuvant chemotherapy
Observation with serial imaging given the patient's young age as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Standard care for osteosarcoma is multi-agent neoadjuvant (preoperative) chemotherapy to shrink the tumor and treat micrometastases, followed by wide surgical resection—usually limb-salvage when margins allow—and then adjuvant chemotherapy, with the degree of tumor necrosis in the resected specimen informing prognosis. Radiation alone (option A) is inadequate because osteosarcoma is relatively radioresistant; radiation is reserved for unresectable sites. Immediate amputation without chemotherapy (option C) ignores micrometastatic disease and yields poor survival; chemotherapy dramatically improved outcomes. Observation (option D) is unacceptable for an aggressive malignancy. The lungs are the most common site of metastasis, which is why CT chest staging and systemic chemotherapy are integral to management.

cases.diagnosis: Osteosarcoma of the distal femur
pearls: Osteosarcoma peaks in adolescence around the knee (distal femur/proximal tibia) and shows a sunburst periosteal reaction and Codman triangle with elevated ALP and LDH.
Plain films first, then MRI of the whole bone; biopsy must be done by the orthopedic oncology team that will resect to preserve limb-salvage options.
Lungs are the most common metastatic site—stage with CT chest.
Treatment is neoadjuvant chemotherapy, wide resection (limb salvage when feasible), then adjuvant chemotherapy; osteosarcoma is relatively radioresistant.

cases.title: Leg Pain and Swelling Mistaken for an Infection
cases.chiefComplaint: Weeks of worsening mid-thigh pain, swelling, and intermittent low-grade fevers.
cases.history: An 11-year-old girl presents with about 7 weeks of deep, aching pain in the mid-right thigh with a tender swelling over the shaft of the bone. The pain is worse at night and has gradually intensified despite NSAIDs. Over the same period she has had intermittent low-grade fevers and feels generally tired. She was seen at an urgent care 3 weeks ago, treated empirically for presumed 'cellulitis/bone infection' with a course of oral antibiotics, with no improvement. She denies recent significant trauma. PMH is unremarkable; she takes only ibuprofen. NKDA. She lives with her parents, attends middle school, and plays the violin. No travel, no sick contacts, no animal exposures, and no penetrating injuries. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is notable for the night pain, intermittent fever, and recent decreased appetite; she denies cough or other localized infectious symptoms. Parents are frustrated that the antibiotic course did nothing and that the swelling has slowly grown.
cases.exam: Tender, firm, mildly warm fusiform swelling over the mid-shaft (diaphysis) of the right femur. The overlying skin is intact without fluctuance or a clear portal of entry. Mild soft-tissue fullness is present. The adjacent hip and knee joints have full, painless range of motion with no effusion. Distal neurovascular status is intact. No regional lymphadenopathy. No rash. The remainder of the musculoskeletal exam is normal. Lungs are clear.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given a diaphyseal lesion with fever and failure to respond to antibiotics, which is the most important next step?
options: Aspirate the nearby knee joint as the first step today
Prescribe a longer course of broader-spectrum oral antibiotics
Obtain plain radiographs of the femur
Reassure and treat as a muscle strain with rest as the first step today
stages.explanation: A diaphyseal bone lesion with constant night pain, systemic symptoms, and—critically—no response to a course of antibiotics should not be assumed to be infection. The next step is plain radiographs and a work-up for a primary bone tumor, since Ewing sarcoma classically arises in the diaphysis of long bones in children and notoriously mimics osteomyelitis (fever, elevated inflammatory markers, periosteal reaction). Escalating antibiotics (option A) delays diagnosis when the clinical course already argues against simple infection. Reassurance as a strain (option C) ignores red flags of progressive night pain and systemic symptoms. Aspirating the knee (option D) is unhelpful because the joint is uninvolved and the pathology is in the bone shaft. Imaging followed by biopsy is the path to diagnosis.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 9.8 ×10³/µL
ESR 60 mm/hr
CRP 40 mg/L
LDH 480 U/L (elevated)
Femur radiograph: permeative, 'moth-eaten' lytic lesion in the mid-diaphysis with multilayered 'onion-skin' (lamellated) periosteal reaction and an associated soft-tissue mass
MRI: large extraosseous soft-tissue component extending beyond the cortex

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which diagnosis best fits a diaphyseal permeative lytic lesion with onion-skin periosteal reaction in an 11-year-old?
options: Chronic osteomyelitis
Osteochondroma
Osteosarcoma in this clinical context
Ewing sarcoma
stages.explanation: Ewing sarcoma classically presents in the diaphysis of long bones (or flat bones like the pelvis) in children and adolescents as a permeative, moth-eaten lytic lesion with a lamellated 'onion-skin' periosteal reaction and a prominent soft-tissue mass; systemic symptoms and elevated ESR/LDH frequently mimic infection. Osteosarcoma (option A) favors the metaphysis around the knee and produces a sunburst pattern and Codman triangle, usually in older adolescents. Chronic osteomyelitis (option C) is the key mimic but is excluded here by progression despite antibiotics and the imaging features; biopsy will distinguish them definitively. Osteochondroma (option D) is a benign, painless metaphyseal bony outgrowth with a cartilage cap—nothing like this aggressive permeative lesion. Definitive diagnosis requires biopsy with molecular testing.

stages.label: NEXT STEP
stages.question: What molecular/pathologic finding would confirm the suspected diagnosis on biopsy?
options: Malignant osteoid produced directly by tumor cells
t(11;22) EWSR1-FLI1 translocation in small round blue cells
Cartilage cap continuous with normal medullary bone
Positive bacterial cultures with neutrophilic infiltrate
stages.explanation: Ewing sarcoma is characterized histologically by sheets of small round blue cells and molecularly by the t(11;22)(q24;q12) translocation producing the EWSR1-FLI1 fusion gene; cells are typically CD99 positive. Malignant osteoid produced by tumor cells (option B) defines osteosarcoma, not Ewing sarcoma. Positive bacterial cultures with a neutrophilic infiltrate (option C) would indicate osteomyelitis, the principal clinical mimic that biopsy is meant to exclude. A cartilage cap continuous with the medullary cavity (option D) is the defining feature of osteochondroma, a benign lesion entirely unlike this aggressive tumor. Identifying the EWSR1 rearrangement both confirms Ewing sarcoma and guides enrollment in appropriate treatment protocols.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the standard treatment framework for localized Ewing sarcoma?
options: Antibiotics and observation as initial therapy
Radiation therapy alone, avoiding systemic treatment
Multi-agent chemotherapy combined with local control
Surgery alone with wide local excision as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Ewing sarcoma is treated with multi-agent systemic chemotherapy (it is a chemosensitive tumor with frequent micrometastases at diagnosis) combined with local control by surgery, radiation, or both depending on tumor location and resectability. Unlike osteosarcoma, Ewing sarcoma is radiosensitive, so radiation is a legitimate component of local control when resection is not feasible. Surgery alone (option A) ignores micrometastatic disease and yields poor survival. Antibiotics and observation (option B) would be appropriate only if this were infection, which has been excluded. Radiation alone without chemotherapy (option D) fails to address systemic disease. The combined modality approach—chemotherapy plus local control—is what produces the best outcomes.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which staging study is essential because it identifies the most common site of metastatic spread in this tumor?
options: Carotid ultrasound
Echocardiography
Abdominal MRI for liver lesions
CT of the chest
stages.explanation: Like other primary bone sarcomas, Ewing sarcoma metastasizes most commonly to the lungs (and also to bone and bone marrow), so CT of the chest is an essential staging study, complemented by bone marrow evaluation and PET or bone scan for skeletal spread. Carotid ultrasound (option B) evaluates the cervical vessels and has no role here. Echocardiography (option C) assesses cardiac function—relevant to monitoring anthracycline chemotherapy toxicity, but not for staging tumor spread. Abdominal MRI for liver lesions (option D) is not a standard primary staging study for Ewing sarcoma, whose principal distant sites are lung, bone, and marrow. Accurate staging directs prognosis and treatment intensity.

cases.diagnosis: Ewing sarcoma of the femoral diaphysis
pearls: Ewing sarcoma arises in the diaphysis of long bones (and pelvis) in children and classically mimics osteomyelitis with fever, high ESR/LDH, and an onion-skin periosteal reaction.
Failure of a bone lesion to respond to antibiotics should prompt tumor work-up with imaging and biopsy rather than antibiotic escalation.
Diagnosis is confirmed by small round blue cells with the t(11;22) EWSR1-FLI1 fusion and CD99 positivity.
Treatment is multi-agent chemotherapy plus local control; unlike osteosarcoma, Ewing sarcoma is radiosensitive, and lungs are the chief metastatic site.

cases.title: A Hard Bump Near the Knee
cases.chiefComplaint: A painless, slowly enlarging hard bump just below the knee noticed over the past year.
cases.history: A 17-year-old boy presents at his mother's urging because of a firm bump near the top of his shin that he first noticed about a year ago. It has slowly grown but does not hurt; he denies night pain, fevers, weight loss, or systemic symptoms. Occasionally the bump rubs against his shin guard during soccer and feels mildly irritated, but otherwise it is asymptomatic. There is no history of significant trauma. He has had no prior similar lumps and no surgeries. PMH is unremarkable; he takes no regular medications. NKDA. He is a high-school junior, plays soccer, and denies tobacco, alcohol, or drug use. Family history is unremarkable, with no known multiple bony growths in relatives. He has been growing normally and is near his expected adult height. The mass is hard, fixed to bone, and points away from the knee joint. He is otherwise healthy and active, with no limitation in his sports participation aside from occasional rubbing discomfort.
cases.exam: A nontender, hard, bony, fixed mass arising from the proximal tibial metaphysis, projecting away from the adjacent knee joint, measuring roughly 3 cm. The overlying skin is normal without warmth or erythema. There is mild local tenderness only where a shin guard rubs. Knee range of motion is full and painless; the joint is stable with no effusion. Distal pulses, sensation, and motor function are normal. No additional bony masses are palpable elsewhere. No regional lymphadenopathy.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which features of this mass are most reassuring for a benign process?
options: Associated fever and weight loss as the first step today
Fixed mass with overlying skin ulceration as the first step today
Rapid growth with night pain as the first step today
Painless, slow-growing, hard mass pointing away from the joint in a skeletally maturing teen
stages.explanation: A painless, slowly growing, hard mass that arises from the metaphysis and points away from the nearby joint in an adolescent is the classic benign profile of an osteochondroma, the most common benign bone tumor. Rapid growth with night pain (option A), fever and weight loss (option C), and skin ulceration over a fixed mass (option D) are red flags for malignancy or infection and would argue against a simple benign exostosis. The directionality—growth away from the joint, in line with the pull of the adjacent tendons—and the slow, painless course are hallmark reassuring features. Plain radiographs typically confirm the diagnosis, and most osteochondromas need no intervention unless symptomatic.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most appropriate initial diagnostic test?
options: Plain radiograph of the proximal tibia
Bone marrow aspiration
Excisional biopsy in this clinical context
PET-CT scan as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Plain radiographs are the appropriate first—and usually definitive—test for an osteochondroma. The diagnosis is often made on imaging alone because of a pathognomonic finding: the lesion's cortex and medullary cavity are continuous with those of the underlying bone. Excisional biopsy (option B) is unnecessary for a classic asymptomatic osteochondroma and is reserved for symptomatic lesions or when malignant transformation is suspected. PET-CT (option C) is a staging tool for malignancy and is not indicated for a benign-appearing exostosis. Bone marrow aspiration (option D) evaluates marrow and hematologic disease, with no role here. If radiographs are equivocal or the cartilage cap thickness is a concern, MRI can characterize the cap, but plain films come first.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Tibia radiograph: a sessile bony outgrowth from the proximal tibial metaphysis with cortical and medullary continuity with the parent bone, projecting away from the knee joint
No aggressive periosteal reaction
No soft-tissue mass
Cartilage cap thin on correlative imaging

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The radiograph shows a bony projection whose cortex and marrow are continuous with the tibia. What is the diagnosis?
options: Osteochondroma
Unicameral bone cyst
Chondrosarcoma in this clinical context
Osteoid osteoma in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Continuity of both the cortex and the medullary cavity of the lesion with the parent bone is pathognomonic for osteochondroma, a benign cartilage-capped bony outgrowth and the most common benign bone tumor. Osteoid osteoma (option A) is a small nidus with surrounding sclerosis that causes night pain relieved by NSAIDs—not a bony projection continuous with marrow. A unicameral (simple) bone cyst (option C) is a centrally located lucent lesion, often in the proximal humerus, not an exophytic outgrowth. Chondrosarcoma (option D) is malignant and would show an aggressive, thick cartilage cap or destructive features; while osteochondromas can rarely transform, the thin cap and benign morphology here are reassuring. The marrow continuity seals the benign diagnosis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management for this asymptomatic lesion, and what should prompt concern for malignant transformation?
options: Radiation therapy to shrink the lesion as initial therapy
Immediate wide resection regardless of symptoms as initial therapy
Observation; new pain or a growing/thickening cartilage cap after skeletal maturity raises concern for transformation
Long-term bisphosphonates as initial therapy
stages.explanation: An asymptomatic osteochondroma is managed with observation; surgical excision is reserved for lesions causing pain, mechanical symptoms, nerve or vessel impingement, or cosmetic concern. The key teaching point is monitoring for malignant transformation to chondrosarcoma: new pain or growth after skeletal maturity, and a thickening cartilage cap (generally >1.5-2 cm on MRI), are warning signs warranting further work-up. Immediate wide resection (option A) is overtreatment for an asymptomatic benign lesion. Radiation (option C) has no role and could even promote malignancy. Bisphosphonates (option D) treat bone density and certain bone diseases but do nothing for an exostosis. Patients with multiple hereditary exostoses carry a higher transformation risk and need closer surveillance.

cases.diagnosis: Osteochondroma of the proximal tibia
pearls: Osteochondroma is the most common benign bone tumor: a painless metaphyseal bony outgrowth that points away from the adjacent joint.
Cortical and medullary continuity with the parent bone on radiograph is pathognomonic and usually obviates biopsy.
Asymptomatic lesions are observed; excise for pain, mechanical/neurovascular symptoms, or cosmesis.
Watch for malignant transformation to chondrosarcoma—new pain, growth after skeletal maturity, or a cartilage cap >1.5-2 cm, especially in multiple hereditary exostoses.

cases.title: A Lytic Lesion at the Wrist in a Young Woman
cases.chiefComplaint: Several months of aching wrist pain with a vague swelling near the wrist.
cases.history: A 30-year-old woman presents with about 4 months of dull, aching pain at the right wrist, gradually worsening and now interfering with gripping and twisting tasks. She has noticed mild fullness on the back of the wrist. There is no clear history of trauma. The pain is present with use and also lingers at rest, but there is no fever, night sweats, or weight loss. PMH is unremarkable. She takes only an oral contraceptive and occasional naproxen. NKDA. She works as a graphic designer (heavy keyboard and mouse use), does not smoke, drinks socially, and has no family history of bone disease or hereditary cancer syndromes. Review of systems is otherwise negative; she denies hypercalcemia symptoms such as constipation or polyuria, and there is no history of kidney stones to suggest hyperparathyroidism (a brown-tumor mimic). She is skeletally mature. She is worried because over-the-counter measures and a wrist brace have not resolved her symptoms, and the fullness seems slightly more prominent than a few months ago.
cases.exam: Tenderness and mild fullness over the distal radius near the wrist, with a vaguely palpable firm swelling. Range of motion of the wrist is mildly limited by pain, particularly with dorsiflexion. The overlying skin is normal without warmth or erythema. Distal neurovascular exam is intact: radial and ulnar pulses normal, sensation intact in median/ulnar/radial distributions, finger movement preserved. No regional lymphadenopathy. No other bony masses. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: What is the most appropriate first diagnostic step for persistent localized wrist pain with a palpable bony fullness?
options: Empiric corticosteroid injection in this clinical context
Reassurance and continued bracing for presumed overuse
Nerve conduction studies for carpal tunnel syndrome
Plain radiographs of the wrist
stages.explanation: Persistent, progressive localized bone pain with a palpable firm fullness should be imaged with plain radiographs first to characterize any underlying lesion. A corticosteroid injection (option A) without a diagnosis could be harmful and delays identifying a bone tumor. Nerve conduction studies (option C) evaluate carpal tunnel syndrome, which causes sensory symptoms in the median distribution rather than a localized bony swelling and lingering structural pain—and her neurologic exam is normal. Continued reassurance and bracing (option D) is inappropriate when symptoms have failed conservative measures and a mass is developing; that course risks missing a neoplasm. Radiographs are inexpensive, fast, and frequently point to the diagnosis, after which MRI and biopsy follow if a lesion is found.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Calcium 9.5 mg/dL
Phosphate 3.4 mg/dL
PTH 38 pg/mL (normal)
Alkaline phosphatase 78 U/L (normal)
Wrist radiograph: expansile, eccentric lytic lesion in the epiphysis/metaphysis of the distal radius extending to the subchondral bone, with a 'soap-bubble' appearance and thinned cortex; no internal matrix mineralization
MRI: lesion abuts the articular surface; no large soft-tissue mass

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: An eccentric, epiphyseal 'soap-bubble' lytic lesion at the distal radius in a skeletally mature 30-year-old is most consistent with which diagnosis?
options: Simple (unicameral) bone cyst
Giant cell tumor of bone
Osteosarcoma in this clinical context
Brown tumor of hyperparathyroidism
stages.explanation: Giant cell tumor of bone classically occurs in skeletally mature young adults (20-40 years), arises at the epiphysis extending to the subchondral bone of long bones, and shows an eccentric, expansile, lytic 'soap-bubble' lesion—exactly as seen at this distal radius, a typical site. Osteosarcoma (option B) is a matrix-forming malignancy that favors the metaphysis of adolescents and shows aggressive periosteal reaction, not a purely lytic epiphyseal soap-bubble pattern. A simple bone cyst (option C) is a centrally located lucency in children, often in the proximal humerus, not eccentric and epiphyseal. A brown tumor (option D) is a key mimic but requires biochemical hyperparathyroidism—here calcium, phosphate, and PTH are normal, effectively excluding it. The location, age, and morphology fit giant cell tumor.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the expected histopathology of this lesion?
options: Small round blue cells with EWSR1 rearrangement as the primary diagnosis
Malignant osteoid produced by anaplastic cells as the primary diagnosis
A cartilage cap continuous with marrow as the primary diagnosis
Multinucleated osteoclast-like giant cells scattered among mononuclear stromal cells
stages.explanation: Giant cell tumor of bone is composed of numerous multinucleated osteoclast-like giant cells dispersed within a background of neoplastic mononuclear stromal cells—the stromal cells are the true neoplastic component (overexpressing RANKL, which drives the giant-cell osteoclast activity). Malignant osteoid (option A) defines osteosarcoma. Small round blue cells with an EWSR1 rearrangement (option C) define Ewing sarcoma. A cartilage cap continuous with marrow (option D) is the hallmark of osteochondroma. Although GCT is classified as benign, it is locally aggressive and can rarely metastasize to the lung; biopsy confirms the histology before definitive treatment. The RANKL biology also underlies the use of denosumab in selected cases.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the standard treatment for a localized giant cell tumor of bone, and why is close follow-up essential?
options: Radiation therapy as first-line treatment as initial therapy
Amputation in all cases as initial therapy
Intralesional curettage with adjuvant
Observation alone with no intervention as initial therapy
stages.explanation: The standard treatment for localized giant cell tumor is intralesional curettage combined with a local adjuvant (high-speed burring, phenol, hydrogen peroxide, or cementation with PMMA) to reduce residual tumor; wide resection is reserved for extensive or recurrent disease. Close follow-up is essential because GCT has a notable local recurrence rate, and rare pulmonary 'benign metastases' can occur. Radiation (option A) is avoided when possible because of the risk of malignant transformation and is reserved for unresectable lesions. Amputation in all cases (option C) is excessive for a benign locally aggressive tumor that is usually amenable to joint-sparing surgery. Observation alone (option D) is inadequate because the lesion is expansile and locally destructive. Denosumab (a RANKL inhibitor) is an option for unresectable or recurrent disease.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Despite its benign classification, which surveillance imaging is warranted because of GCT's rare metastatic potential?
options: Brain MRI with appropriate follow-up
Annual mammography with appropriate follow-up
Periodic chest imaging to screen for pulmonary metastases
Renal ultrasound with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Giant cell tumor, although histologically benign, can rarely produce pulmonary 'benign metastases,' so periodic chest imaging is warranted as part of surveillance, in addition to local imaging to detect recurrence at the treated site. Brain MRI (option B) is unnecessary, as the CNS is not a site of GCT spread. Annual mammography (option C) is unrelated to this bone tumor. Renal ultrasound (option D) has no role here; while one might consider parathyroid/renal work-up for a brown-tumor mimic, that was already excluded by normal calcium, phosphate, and PTH. Monitoring the lungs and the local site addresses GCT's two principal follow-up concerns: distant lung deposits and frequent local recurrence.

cases.diagnosis: Giant cell tumor of the distal radius
pearls: Giant cell tumor occurs in skeletally mature young adults (20-40), at the epiphysis extending to subchondral bone, as an eccentric expansile 'soap-bubble' lytic lesion.
Histology shows osteoclast-like giant cells among neoplastic mononuclear stromal cells; RANKL overexpression underlies denosumab use.
Rule out a brown tumor of hyperparathyroidism with calcium, phosphate, and PTH before committing to the diagnosis.
Treat with intralesional curettage plus a local adjuvant; follow closely for local recurrence and screen the chest for rare benign pulmonary metastases.

cases.title: Forefoot Pain in a New Recruit
cases.chiefComplaint: Two weeks of gradually worsening pain in the front of the foot with marching.
cases.history: A 20-year-old female military recruit presents with 2 weeks of insidious, progressively worsening pain over the front of her right foot. She is 5 weeks into basic training, which markedly increased her daily marching and running on hard surfaces compared with her prior sedentary lifestyle. The pain began as a dull ache during long marches and now hurts with most weight-bearing activity, improving with rest. There is no specific injury or twisting event and no acute 'pop.' She denies numbness, tingling, or color change. PMH is unremarkable; she has regular menstrual periods and takes only as-needed ibuprofen. NKDA. She does not smoke and drinks rarely. There is no family history of metabolic bone disease or early osteoporosis. Her diet during training has been adequate. She is highly motivated to keep training and has been 'pushing through' the pain, which has made it gradually worse. She reports mild swelling over the top of the forefoot at the end of training days.
cases.exam: Focal, reproducible tenderness over the shaft of the second metatarsal with mild dorsal forefoot swelling. Pain is reproduced by direct palpation of the bone and by a tuning-fork/percussion test over the metatarsal. There is no skin breakdown, erythema, or significant ecchymosis. The forefoot is neurovascularly intact: dorsalis pedis pulse present, sensation normal, and capillary refill brisk. There is no midfoot instability, and the ankle has full range of motion. No calf tenderness or swelling to suggest DVT.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given insidious forefoot pain after a sharp rise in training load, which is the most likely diagnosis?
options: Metatarsal stress fracture
Acute traumatic metatarsal fracture
Plantar fasciitis as the first step today
Gout of the first metatarsophalangeal joint
stages.explanation: Insidious, activity-related forefoot pain that worsens with repetitive loading—classically after a sudden increase in marching or running—and focal tenderness over a metatarsal shaft is the textbook picture of a metatarsal stress (march) fracture, most often of the second or third metatarsal. An acute traumatic fracture (option A) follows a discrete injury with sudden pain, which she denies. Plantar fasciitis (option C) causes inferior heel pain that is worst with the first steps in the morning, not focal dorsal metatarsal-shaft tenderness. Gout (option D) presents as acute, red, hot, exquisitely painful first-MTP arthritis, not a gradual stress-related ache over the metatarsal shaft. The training history and focal bony tenderness point to a stress fracture.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Initial plain radiographs of the foot are normal. What is the best next step?
options: Treat clinically as a stress fracture and/or obtain MRI
Begin empiric antibiotics for osteomyelitis as the primary diagnosis
Order an immediate CT angiogram of the foot as the primary diagnosis
Conclude there is no fracture and clear her for full activity
stages.explanation: Early plain films are frequently normal in stress fractures because it takes 2-3 weeks for periosteal callus or a fracture line to become visible. The correct approach is to treat clinically as a stress fracture with activity modification while either obtaining MRI (the most sensitive early test, showing marrow/periosteal edema) or repeating radiographs in 2-3 weeks to catch the developing callus. Clearing her for full activity (option A) based on normal early films would risk progression to a complete fracture. A CT angiogram (option C) evaluates vasculature and is irrelevant here. Empiric antibiotics for osteomyelitis (option D) are unwarranted without signs of infection; her presentation is mechanical, not infectious. The key concept is that normal early films do not exclude a stress fracture.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Foot radiograph (initial): no visible fracture line or callus
MRI right foot: periosteal and marrow edema along the second metatarsal shaft with a faint linear stress fracture line
Follow-up radiograph at 3 weeks: periosteal new bone (callus) along the second metatarsal
Vitamin D 28 ng/mL
Calcium 9.4 mg/dL

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the most appropriate management for this low-risk metatarsal stress fracture?
options: Immediate open reduction and internal fixation as initial therapy
Continue full training and 'push through' the pain as initial therapy
Relative rest with activity modification, protected weight-bearing ,
Strict non-weight-bearing in a cast for 12 weeks as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Metatarsal shaft stress fractures (other than the proximal fifth metatarsal) are low-risk fractures that heal well with relative rest: reducing the offending load, protected weight-bearing in a stiff-soled shoe or walking boot, pain-guided activity, and a gradual, progressive return to training once symptoms resolve. Continuing to push through pain (option A) is exactly what causes progression to a complete fracture. Surgical fixation (option C) is reserved for high-risk or displaced fractures, not a typical second-metatarsal stress fracture. Twelve weeks of strict non-weight-bearing casting (option D) is excessive for a low-risk metatarsal stress fracture and risks deconditioning. Addressing contributory factors—training progression, footwear, and correcting low vitamin D—optimizes healing and prevents recurrence.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which counseling point best helps prevent recurrent stress fractures as she returns to training?
options: Progress training load gradually, ensure adequate calcium/vitamin D,
Avoid all weight-bearing exercise permanently with appropriate follow-up
Take daily prophylactic NSAIDs to mask pain during marches based on current risk
Increase training volume as rapidly as possible to make up lost time
stages.explanation: Stress fractures arise from repetitive overload outpacing the bone's capacity to remodel, so prevention centers on gradual progression of training load, adequate calcium and vitamin D intake, and proper footwear/orthotics, along with attention to recovery. Rapidly increasing volume to 'make up time' (option A) recreates the exact mechanism that caused the injury. Avoiding all weight-bearing exercise permanently (option C) is neither necessary nor healthy; bone responds positively to graded loading. Prophylactic NSAIDs to mask pain (option D) is dangerous because it removes the warning signal of overload and can let a fracture progress unnoticed; some evidence also suggests NSAIDs may impair bone healing. Sensible load management and nutritional optimization are the durable preventive strategy.

cases.diagnosis: Second metatarsal stress fracture (march fracture)
pearls: March fractures are stress fractures of the metatarsal shaft (usually 2nd/3rd) from a sudden increase in repetitive loading, classically in military recruits and runners.
Early plain films are often normal; MRI is most sensitive, and callus appears on radiographs at 2-3 weeks.
Most metatarsal shaft stress fractures are low-risk and heal with relative rest and protected weight-bearing—pushing through pain risks completion.
Prevent recurrence with gradual load progression, adequate calcium/vitamin D, and proper footwear; beware the higher-risk proximal fifth metatarsal.

cases.title: Groin Pain in a Distance Runner
cases.chiefComplaint: Several weeks of deepening groin pain with running, now hurting even when walking.
cases.history: A 24-year-old competitive distance runner presents with 4 weeks of progressively worsening right groin pain. It began as an ache only during long runs and now occurs with walking and at the end of the day. She recently increased her weekly mileage substantially while preparing for a marathon. She denies a discrete injury. On focused questioning she reports that her menstrual periods stopped about 8 months ago, she restricts her diet to stay lean, and she has lost weight over the past year. There is no fever or night sweats. PMH is otherwise unremarkable; she takes no regular medications and no hormonal contraception. NKDA. She does not smoke, drinks rarely, and has no family history of early osteoporosis. She is highly driven and has continued training through the pain. She denies prior stress fractures but acknowledges chronic fatigue and feeling cold. Review of systems is notable for amenorrhea, dietary restriction, and high training volume—features she had not connected to her bone pain.
cases.exam: Slender, lean young woman. Pain in the right groin reproduced by active straight-leg raise and by axial loading (heel strike/hop test, which she is reluctant to perform due to pain). Internal and external rotation of the hip provoke deep anterior groin pain near the end of range. There is no visible deformity, no leg-length discrepancy, and no ecchymosis. Gait is mildly antalgic. Distal neurovascular exam is intact. There is no inguinal hernia, no lymphadenopathy, and no adductor-origin tenderness to suggest a simple muscle strain. The contralateral hip exam is normal.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Which constellation in this runner most raises concern for a bone stress injury rather than a muscle strain?
options: A sudden 'pop' during sprinting with immediate bruising as the first step today
Pain only after activity that resolves quickly with stretching as the first step today
Bilateral symmetric knee pain after sitting as the first step today
Insidious load-related groin pain with amenorrhea, dietary restriction,
stages.explanation: Insidious, progressively load-related groin pain in a lean female runner with amenorrhea and dietary restriction is the female athlete triad (low energy availability, menstrual dysfunction, and low bone density), which dramatically raises the risk of a femoral neck stress fracture. The triad impairs bone health, so a stress injury must be excluded. Pain that resolves quickly with stretching (option A) is more typical of a benign musculotendinous issue. A sudden pop with bruising during sprinting (option C) describes an acute muscle/tendon strain or avulsion, not a stress fracture. Bilateral knee pain after sitting (option D) suggests patellofemoral pain, an unrelated entity. The triad plus mechanical groin pain demands urgent imaging because femoral neck stress fractures can displace.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Initial hip radiographs are unremarkable. Given the suspicion, what is the best next step?
options: Obtain MRI of the hip and, until cleared, make her non-weight-bearing/protected
Order a DEXA scan and do nothing else as the primary diagnosis
Inject the hip with corticosteroid for pain relief as the primary diagnosis
Reassure and allow continued running with pain as tolerated
stages.explanation: When a femoral neck stress fracture is suspected and plain films are negative (as they often are early), MRI is the most sensitive test and should be obtained promptly; meanwhile the patient should be protected (made non-weight-bearing or kept on crutches) until the bone is cleared, because a femoral neck stress fracture can progress to a displaced fracture with devastating consequences. Reassurance with continued running (option A) risks catastrophic displacement and avascular necrosis. A DEXA alone (option C) addresses bone density but does not diagnose or protect the acutely injured neck. A corticosteroid injection (option D) provides no benefit, may worsen bone health, and dangerously masks a warning sign. Protection plus MRI is the safe, correct path.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Hip radiograph: no visible fracture
MRI right hip: marrow edema in the femoral neck with a fracture line involving the tension (superolateral) side of the femoral neck
LH and FSH low; estradiol low (consistent with functional hypothalamic amenorrhea)
Vitamin D 18 ng/mL (low)
DEXA: Z-score -2.3 at the lumbar spine

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: The MRI shows a fracture line on the tension (superolateral) side of the femoral neck. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Urgent orthopedic referral for surgical fixation, as tension-side femoral neck stress fractures are high-risk for displacement
Immediate total hip arthroplasty as initial therapy
Continue running with a compression brace as initial therapy
Protected weight-bearing and outpatient follow-up only as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Femoral neck stress fractures are classified by location: compression-side (inferomedial) fractures are lower-risk and may be managed with strict protected weight-bearing, but tension-side (superolateral) fractures are high-risk for displacement and require urgent orthopedic referral for surgical fixation (e.g., percutaneous screws). Protected weight-bearing with outpatient follow-up alone (option A) is appropriate for compression-side, not tension-side, injuries. Total hip arthroplasty (option C) is not the treatment for a young patient's stress fracture; it is reserved for end-stage arthritis or failed fixation. Continuing to run with a brace (option D) is dangerous and could precipitate complete displacement, which carries a high risk of avascular necrosis and nonunion. Tension-side location is the key trigger for surgical urgency.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If a tension-side femoral neck stress fracture displaces, which serious complication is most likely?
options: Sciatic nerve transection
Compartment syndrome of the thigh
Deep vein thrombosis only
Avascular necrosis of the femoral head
stages.explanation: Displacement of a femoral neck fracture disrupts the retinacular blood supply (largely from the medial femoral circumflex artery) to the femoral head, leading to avascular necrosis and nonunion—the feared complications that make these injuries surgical emergencies in the right context. Compartment syndrome (option A) typically follows high-energy trauma to fascial compartments, not a femoral neck stress fracture. DVT (option C) can complicate immobilization but is not the hallmark consequence of displacement and is preventable. Sciatic nerve transection (option D) is not a typical sequela of a femoral neck fracture. Protecting the blood supply by preventing displacement—and fixing high-risk fractures promptly—is precisely why tension-side injuries are treated urgently.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Beyond treating the fracture, what is essential for this athlete's long-term bone health?
options: Increase training intensity immediately after fixation with appropriate follow-up
Recommend permanent cessation of all athletic activity with appropriate follow-up
Start long-term opioids for pain control with appropriate follow-up
Address the female athlete triad: optimize energy availability, restore menstruation,
stages.explanation: Sustainable recovery requires treating the underlying female athlete triad (now framed as Relative Energy Deficiency in Sport): increasing caloric/energy availability, restoring menstrual function (often through improved nutrition and reduced energy deficit, with multidisciplinary input), and correcting low vitamin D and calcium—addressing the root cause of her low bone density and fracture. Immediately increasing training intensity (option A) ignores the cause and invites reinjury. Long-term opioids (option C) do not heal bone and carry dependence risk. Permanent cessation of all sport (option D) is unnecessary and counterproductive; the goal is safe return once energy balance, bone health, and healing are restored. A team approach (sports medicine, nutrition, sometimes endocrinology/psychology) optimizes both recovery and prevention.

cases.diagnosis: Femoral neck stress fracture (tension-side) with female athlete triad
pearls: Suspect a femoral neck stress fracture in a runner with insidious groin pain, especially with the female athlete triad (low energy availability, amenorrhea, low bone density).
Early plain films are often normal—obtain MRI and protect the limb (non-weight-bearing) until the neck is cleared.
Tension-side (superolateral) fractures are high-risk and need urgent surgical fixation; compression-side (inferomedial) fractures may be managed nonoperatively.
Displacement risks avascular necrosis and nonunion; long-term management must correct the underlying triad/RED-S.

cases.title: Hip Pain in a Patient on Long-Term Steroids
cases.chiefComplaint: A few months of deep, worsening groin and hip pain with weight-bearing.
cases.history: A 39-year-old man presents with 3 months of progressively worsening deep right groin and hip pain that radiates toward the buttock and thigh. The pain is worse with weight-bearing and at the extremes of hip motion, and it now disturbs his sleep. There is no discrete injury. PMH is significant for severe asthma and a prior episode of immune thrombocytopenia, for which he has required multiple courses of high-dose corticosteroids over the past 2 years, including a prolonged taper. Current medications include prednisone 20 mg daily (recently tapering), an inhaled corticosteroid, and a PPI. NKDA. He drinks alcohol moderately to heavily on weekends and does not smoke. Family history is noncontributory. Review of systems is otherwise negative—no fevers, no recent infections, no rash. He is worried because the pain is steadily worsening despite rest and over-the-counter analgesics, and he has begun to limp. He has no history of sickle cell disease but is aware that his steroid use and alcohol intake are risk factors his physician has mentioned.
cases.exam: Antalgic gait favoring the right leg. Deep groin pain reproduced by passive internal rotation and flexion of the right hip, with restricted and painful range of motion, particularly internal rotation. There is no erythema, warmth, or effusion suggestive of septic arthritis, and no point tenderness over the greater trochanter to suggest bursitis. Leg lengths are equal and there is no fixed deformity. Distal neurovascular exam is intact. The lumbar spine exam is unremarkable, and straight-leg raise does not reproduce radicular pain. The contralateral hip has full, painless motion at this time.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Given chronic high-dose steroid use and heavy alcohol intake, which diagnosis should be highest on the differential for this hip pain?
options: Avascular necrosis of the femoral head
Septic arthritis of the hip
Lumbar radiculopathy
Trochanteric bursitis
stages.explanation: Chronic corticosteroid use and heavy alcohol consumption are the two most common nontraumatic risk factors for avascular necrosis (osteonecrosis) of the femoral head, which classically presents in this age group with insidious, progressive deep groin pain worsened by weight-bearing and limited, painful internal rotation. Trochanteric bursitis (option A) causes lateral hip pain with point tenderness over the greater trochanter, not deep groin pain with restricted internal rotation. Lumbar radiculopathy (option C) produces radicular leg pain with a positive straight-leg raise, absent here. Septic arthritis (option D) typically presents acutely with fever, warmth, and an effusion, none of which are present. The risk-factor profile plus the pain pattern make femoral head osteonecrosis the leading concern.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Plain radiographs of the hip are normal. What is the most appropriate next imaging study?
options: Bone densitometry (DEXA)
Repeat plain radiographs in 6 months
Hip joint aspiration
MRI of the hips
stages.explanation: MRI is the most sensitive and specific test for early avascular necrosis and is the next step when radiographs are normal; it detects marrow changes and the characteristic 'double-line sign' and crescent before any plain-film abnormality appears. Waiting 6 months for repeat radiographs (option A) would allow potential progression to subchondral collapse, missing the window for joint-preserving treatment. DEXA (option C) measures bone density for osteoporosis and does not diagnose osteonecrosis. Hip aspiration (option D) is indicated when septic arthritis is suspected—but there are no signs of infection here. Because early AVN is radiographically occult and early intervention can preserve the joint, MRI is essential. Imaging both hips is wise, as AVN is frequently bilateral.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Hip radiograph: no subchondral lucency or collapse; joint space preserved
MRI both hips: serpiginous subchondral signal change in the right femoral head with a 'double-line sign' and an early subchondral 'crescent'; the femoral head contour is still preserved (no collapse)
Left femoral head: small early area of marrow signal change (asymptomatic)
ESR 14 mm/hr; CRP 4 mg/L (normal)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The MRI shows a subchondral crescent with a preserved femoral head contour. What does this finding indicate about staging?
options: Pre-collapse osteonecrosis
Advanced disease with established arthritis as the primary diagnosis
Complete femoral head collapse requiring arthroplasty as the primary diagnosis
A normal variant of no clinical significance as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: A subchondral crescent sign with a still-preserved (uncollapsed) femoral head contour indicates pre-collapse osteonecrosis: the bone has infarcted and a subchondral fracture is forming, but the articular surface has not yet flattened. This is the critical window in which joint-preserving interventions (e.g., core decompression, with or without bone grafting) can be considered before irreversible collapse and secondary arthritis. Advanced disease with established arthritis (option A) and complete collapse requiring arthroplasty (option C) would show flattening of the head and joint-space loss, which are absent here. A normal variant (option D) is incorrect—the double-line and crescent signs are pathologic markers of osteonecrosis. Recognizing the pre-collapse stage drives the urgency of joint-preserving management.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For this pre-collapse osteonecrosis of the femoral head, which management is most appropriate?
options: High-dose corticosteroids to reduce inflammation as initial therapy
Immediate total hip arthroplasty as initial therapy
Reduce/modify steroid
Long-term bed rest alone as initial therapy
stages.explanation: In pre-collapse femoral head osteonecrosis, management aims to preserve the native joint: minimize or modify the inciting risk factors (taper steroids as the underlying disease allows and reduce alcohol), protect the joint with reduced weight-bearing, and consider joint-preserving surgery such as core decompression (often with bone grafting or biologics) to relieve intraosseous pressure and promote revascularization. Immediate total hip arthroplasty (option A) is reserved for collapsed heads or advanced arthritis, not a pre-collapse joint in a young patient. More high-dose corticosteroids (option C) would worsen the very process causing the necrosis. Bed rest alone (option D) neither halts progression nor preserves the joint. Addressing the cause plus joint-preserving intervention offers the best chance to avoid arthroplasty.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: The MRI also noted an asymptomatic early lesion in the contralateral femoral head. What does this most importantly imply?
options: It confirms metastatic cancer with appropriate follow-up
It is an artifact and can be ignored with appropriate follow-up
It mandates immediate bilateral hip replacement with appropriate follow-up
Osteonecrosis is frequently bilateral, so the other hip should also be monitored
stages.explanation: Nontraumatic osteonecrosis—especially from steroids or alcohol—is frequently bilateral, so an asymptomatic contralateral lesion is an important finding that warrants monitoring of the second hip and reinforces systemic management of shared risk factors (steroid minimization, alcohol reduction). Dismissing it as artifact (option A) ignores a real, treatable early lesion. Immediate bilateral hip replacement (option C) is inappropriate, particularly for an asymptomatic, pre-collapse or even pre-symptomatic hip in a young patient. It does not confirm metastatic cancer (option D); the imaging pattern (double-line sign, serpiginous marrow change) is characteristic of osteonecrosis, not metastasis. Recognizing bilaterality changes surveillance and underscores that the underlying systemic risk factors must be controlled.

cases.diagnosis: Avascular necrosis of the femoral head (pre-collapse)
pearls: Chronic corticosteroids and heavy alcohol use are the leading nontraumatic causes of femoral head osteonecrosis; presentation is insidious groin pain with painful, limited internal rotation.
MRI is the most sensitive test when radiographs are normal—look for the double-line sign and the subchondral crescent before plain-film collapse.
A crescent sign with preserved head contour marks the pre-collapse stage, the window for joint-preserving treatment like core decompression.
Osteonecrosis is often bilateral—image and monitor both hips and control systemic risk factors.

cases.title: A Hot, Swollen Foot in a Diabetic Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Several weeks of a warm, swollen, increasingly deformed foot without much pain.
cases.history: A 60-year-old man with long-standing poorly controlled type 2 diabetes presents with 3 weeks of a warm, red, swollen right foot. He noticed the swelling gradually and reports surprisingly little pain given how dramatic the foot looks—he attributes this to his known diabetic neuropathy. There was no clear injury, though he walks a lot for work. He was started on oral antibiotics elsewhere for presumed 'cellulitis,' with no improvement. He denies fever, chills, or drainage. PMH includes type 2 diabetes for 18 years (recent A1c 9.4%), peripheral neuropathy, hypertension, and chronic kidney disease stage 3. Medications include metformin, insulin glargine, lisinopril, and atorvastatin. NKDA. He is a retired delivery driver, former smoker (quit 5 years ago), and drinks rarely. Family history is notable for diabetes. Review of systems is negative for systemic infection symptoms. He reports the redness improves somewhat when he elevates the leg, and the foot has begun to look 'flatter' in the middle, with the arch collapsing.
cases.exam: A markedly warm, erythematous, swollen right midfoot with early collapse of the longitudinal arch producing a 'rocker-bottom' deformity. The skin is intact with no ulcer, no drainage, and no fluctuant abscess or sinus tract. There is profound loss of protective sensation on monofilament testing and absent ankle reflexes; pedal pulses are palpable. Crucially, the erythema and swelling notably decrease with limb elevation. There is no lymphangitic streaking and no inguinal lymphadenopathy. The contralateral foot also shows neuropathy but no deformity. No bony exposure on probing.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A warm, swollen, deforming foot with minimal pain in a neuropathic diabetic—out of proportion to systemic illness—most suggests which diagnosis?
options: Cellulitis as the first step today
Gout as the first step today
Deep vein thrombosis
Charcot neuroarthropathy
stages.explanation: An acutely warm, red, swollen, and progressively deforming foot with disproportionately little pain in a patient with diabetic peripheral neuropathy is the classic presentation of acute Charcot neuroarthropathy. Loss of protective sensation allows repetitive microtrauma and inflammatory bone/joint destruction, producing collapse (rocker-bottom deformity). Cellulitis (option A) is the leading mimic, but it usually causes more pain and systemic signs and would not produce progressive bony collapse; importantly, Charcot erythema characteristically diminishes with elevation, whereas cellulitis does not. Gout (option C) causes an exquisitely painful, red joint—not a relatively painless deforming process. DVT (option D) causes calf swelling and pain, not midfoot collapse with deformity. The neuropathy, minimal pain, deformity, and elevation-dependent erythema point to Charcot.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which simple bedside finding best helps distinguish Charcot neuroarthropathy from cellulitis?
options: A positive monofilament test as the primary diagnosis
Presence of pedal pulses as the primary diagnosis
Reduction of erythema
The patient's blood glucose level as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: A practical bedside discriminator is that the erythema and swelling of acute Charcot neuroarthropathy typically decrease with limb elevation (the redness is dependent/vasomotor in origin), whereas the erythema of cellulitis persists despite elevation. This elevation test, combined with the disproportionately mild pain and the deforming bony process, helps separate the two. Pedal pulses (option A) may be present in both and reflect perfusion rather than the diagnosis. A positive monofilament test (option C) confirms neuropathy—a necessary substrate for Charcot—but neuropathy can coexist with cellulitis, so it does not distinguish them. Blood glucose (option D) reflects diabetes control, not which acute foot process is occurring. The elevation response is a quick, high-yield clue at the bedside.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 8.6 ×10³/µL (normal)
ESR 30 mm/hr
CRP 12 mg/L (mildly elevated)
Hemoglobin A1c 9.4%
Foot radiograph: fragmentation and dislocation of the midfoot (tarsometatarsal) joints with bony debris and early collapse of the longitudinal arch (rocker-bottom)
MRI: bone marrow edema and joint disorganization without a contiguous ulcer or sinus tract to bone; findings favor neuroarthropathy over osteomyelitis

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the cornerstone of treatment for acute Charcot neuroarthropathy?
options: Prolonged IV antibiotics as initial therapy
Encourage normal walking to maintain mobility as initial therapy
Immediate offloading
Urgent below-knee amputation as initial therapy
stages.explanation: The cornerstone of acute Charcot management is immediate offloading and immobilization—classically a total-contact cast with strict non-weight-bearing—to protect the inflamed, destructible foot and prevent further collapse until the active (inflammatory) phase resolves and the foot consolidates. Prolonged IV antibiotics (option A) are for osteomyelitis, which the imaging and intact skin make unlikely here; antibiotics do not treat the neuroarthropathic process. Encouraging normal walking (option C) is exactly wrong, since continued weight-bearing drives progressive destruction and deformity. Amputation (option D) is a last resort for unsalvageable feet, not initial management of acute Charcot. Early recognition and offloading are critical because timely treatment can prevent the severe deformities and subsequent ulceration that lead to amputation.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If untreated, what is the most important downstream complication of the resulting deformity?
options: Development of gouty tophi as the leading concern
Conversion to rheumatoid arthritis as the leading concern
Plantar ulceration over bony prominences that can become infected
Spontaneous resolution with no sequelae as the leading concern
stages.explanation: The feared downstream complication of untreated Charcot is collapse into a rocker-bottom deformity that creates abnormal plantar pressure points; over insensate skin, these prominences ulcerate, and ulcers can become infected, progressing to osteomyelitis and ultimately limb-threatening infection requiring amputation. This is why preventing deformity through early offloading matters so much. Spontaneous resolution without sequelae (option A) is not the natural history of inadequately treated active Charcot. It does not convert to rheumatoid arthritis (option C), which is a distinct autoimmune disease. Gouty tophi (option D) result from urate deposition, unrelated to neuroarthropathy. The chain from deformity to ulcer to infection to amputation is the central reason for aggressive, timely management.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Once the acute phase consolidates, which long-term strategy best protects this patient's foot?
options: Daily high-impact exercise on the foot to strengthen it with appropriate follow-up
Custom therapeutic footwear/orthoses, regular podiatric foot care, glycemic optimization,
Return to regular shoes and normal activity without follow-up with appropriate follow-up
Lifelong systemic antibiotics with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: After the active phase consolidates, long-term protection centers on custom therapeutic footwear and orthoses to accommodate deformity and redistribute pressure, regular podiatric foot care and patient education on daily foot inspection, glycemic optimization, and ongoing surveillance—including of the contralateral foot, which is also at risk. Returning to regular shoes without follow-up (option A) invites ulceration and recurrence. Lifelong systemic antibiotics (option C) are unnecessary absent infection and risk resistance and toxicity. Daily high-impact exercise on the foot (option D) would re-traumatize a vulnerable, deformed, insensate foot. A comprehensive protective program—proper footwear, foot care, diabetes control, and monitoring—is what prevents ulcers and preserves the limb over the long term.

cases.diagnosis: Charcot neuroarthropathy of the foot
pearls: Acute Charcot presents as a warm, red, swollen, deforming foot with disproportionately mild pain in a neuropathic diabetic—commonly misdiagnosed as cellulitis.
A helpful bedside clue: Charcot erythema decreases with limb elevation, whereas cellulitis erythema persists.
Treatment cornerstone is immediate offloading/immobilization (total-contact cast, non-weight-bearing) until the active phase consolidates.
Untreated collapse causes rocker-bottom deformity with plantar ulceration, osteomyelitis, and amputation; long-term care needs custom footwear, foot surveillance, and glycemic control.

cases.title: Non-Healing Foot Ulcer in a Diabetic Man
cases.chiefComplaint: A non-healing ulcer on the bottom of his foot that has worsened over six weeks.
cases.history: A 64-year-old man with type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 9.4% three weeks ago) is admitted for a plantar foot ulcer beneath the first metatarsal head that has been present for six weeks despite home dressing changes. Over the last few days the surrounding skin became red and the wound began draining yellow fluid with a foul odor. He denies fever or chills but notes he can no longer fit into his work boot. He has diabetic peripheral neuropathy and felt no pain from the ulcer until recently. PMH: type 2 diabetes for 14 years, hypertension, prior right great-toe amputation.
