Medications: metformin 1000 mg twice daily, insulin glargine 30 units nightly, lisinopril 20 mg daily, aspirin 81 mg daily. Allergies: none. Social: former smoker (30 pack-years, quit 5 years ago), lives alone, drinks alcohol rarely. He works as a warehouse supervisor and is on his feet much of the day. Family history: father had diabetes and coronary disease.
cases.exam: A 2.5 cm full-thickness ulcer is present over the plantar first metatarsal head with surrounding erythema, warmth, and malodorous purulent drainage. Sterile blunt metal probe inserted into the ulcer base reaches a hard, gritty surface consistent with bone. Pedal pulses are diminished but palpable; capillary refill is 3 seconds. Monofilament sensation is absent over both forefeet. There is no fluctuance, crepitus, or proximal tracking cellulitis up the leg. The contralateral foot shows a healed first-ray amputation site. Lymphangitic streaking is absent and the patient is hemodynamically stable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A sterile metal probe inserted into the ulcer base contacts hard, gritty bone. In this patient, what does a positive probe-to-bone test most strongly suggest?
options: Foreign body retention
Plantar fascial rupture
Underlying osteomyelitis
Charcot neuroarthropathy
Deep venous thrombosis
stages.explanation: In a patient with a diabetic foot ulcer and clinical signs of infection, a positive probe-to-bone test (palpable bone or a hard, gritty surface at the wound base) substantially raises the probability of underlying osteomyelitis; in high-prevalence inpatient settings its positive predictive value is high. Charcot neuroarthropathy causes a deformed, swollen but typically non-ulcerated midfoot and would not by itself explain purulent drainage probing to bone. Plantar fascial rupture produces acute pain and bruising, not a chronic draining ulcer. A retained foreign body is possible but far less likely than osteomyelitis given the chronicity and exposed bone. DVT causes calf swelling and tenderness, not a probing plantar ulcer.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which initial imaging study is most appropriate to evaluate for osteomyelitis in this foot?
options: Plain radiographs of the foot
Immediate MRI with contrast
Lower-extremity venous duplex
Triple-phase bone scan
CT angiogram of the leg
stages.explanation: Plain radiographs are the appropriate first imaging step: they are inexpensive, fast, and can reveal cortical erosion, periosteal reaction, or bony destruction, and they establish a baseline. Early osteomyelitis may not show changes for 10-14 days, so a normal film does not exclude it and MRI is reserved for when plain films are nondiagnostic but suspicion remains. MRI with contrast is the most sensitive test but is not the first study obtained. A triple-phase bone scan is less specific in a neuropathic foot with prior surgery. Venous duplex evaluates DVT, and CT angiogram assesses arterial supply, neither of which answers the osteomyelitis question first.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 13.8 ×10³/µL (neutrophil predominant)
Hemoglobin 11.6 g/dL
Glucose 248 mg/dL
HbA1c 9.4%
ESR 78 mm/hr
CRP 96 mg/L
Creatinine 1.1 mg/dL
Foot radiograph: cortical erosion and lucency of the first metatarsal head with periosteal reaction
ABI (affected limb): 0.82

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which specimen provides the most reliable identification of the causative organism(s) to guide therapy?
options: Empiric coverage without any cultures
Blood cultures alone in this clinical context
Superficial wound swab of the ulcer surface
Sinus tract drainage culture
Bone biopsy with culture
stages.explanation: Bone biopsy with culture and histopathology is the gold standard for diagnosing diabetic foot osteomyelitis and identifying the true pathogen(s) with susceptibilities, ideally obtained before or guiding long-term antibiotic selection. Superficial wound swabs and sinus tract cultures correlate poorly with deep bone pathogens because they reflect colonizing flora. Blood cultures are useful when bacteremia is suspected but are frequently negative in chronic foot osteomyelitis and do not localize bone involvement. Treating empirically without cultures risks inadequate or excessively broad therapy; targeted therapy guided by bone culture improves cure rates and antibiotic stewardship in this prolonged-treatment disease.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Bone culture grows methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus. After surgical debridement, what is the most appropriate antibiotic strategy?
options: Oral metronidazole for 2 weeks as initial therapy
Topical antibiotics to the wound only as initial therapy
Single dose of IV cefazolin as initial therapy
IV vancomycin guided by cultures for approximately 6 weeks
Oral cephalexin for 7 days as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Culture-directed therapy for MRSA osteomyelitis requires an agent with reliable MRSA activity such as IV vancomycin, with a typical duration of about 6 weeks when infected bone remains after debridement; duration can be shortened if all infected bone is resected. Cephalexin and cefazolin are beta-lactams that do not cover MRSA. Topical antibiotics cannot penetrate bone and are inadequate for osteomyelitis. Metronidazole covers anaerobes only and has no staphylococcal activity. Debridement of necrotic bone plus appropriately targeted systemic antibiotics, along with glycemic control and offloading, gives the best chance of limb salvage.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Beyond antibiotics and debridement, which intervention is most important to promote healing and prevent ulcer recurrence?
options: Strict pressure offloading of the affected foot
Prolonged bed rest with the leg elevated continuously
Compression stockings to both legs
Daily hydrogen peroxide soaks based on current risk
Routine hyperbaric oxygen for all diabetic ulcers
stages.explanation: Offloading pressure from the wound (total contact cast or specialized offloading footwear) is essential because repetitive plantar pressure on an insensate foot perpetuates tissue breakdown and is a leading cause of non-healing and recurrence. Continuous bed rest is impractical and causes deconditioning and complications. Hydrogen peroxide is cytotoxic to granulation tissue and impairs healing. Compression stockings treat venous disease and can worsen perfusion if arterial supply is marginal. Hyperbaric oxygen is an adjunct considered selectively for refractory wounds, not a first-line measure for all diabetic ulcers; glycemic control, offloading, and revascularization when indicated take priority.

cases.diagnosis: Diabetic foot ulcer with underlying osteomyelitis (probe-to-bone positive)
pearls: A positive probe-to-bone test in an infected diabetic foot ulcer strongly predicts underlying osteomyelitis.
Plain films are first-line imaging but may be normal early; MRI is most sensitive when films are nondiagnostic.
Bone biopsy culture, not superficial swabs, identifies the true osteomyelitis pathogen and guides therapy.
Limb salvage depends on debridement, culture-directed antibiotics (often ~6 weeks), glycemic control, offloading, and revascularization when indicated.

cases.title: Rapidly Worsening Leg Pain and Swelling in the ICU
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe, escalating left leg pain with fever after a minor skin scratch two days ago.
cases.history: A 55-year-old man with type 2 diabetes presents to the emergency department with 36 hours of rapidly worsening left lower-leg pain and swelling, now admitted to the ICU. He recalls scratching his shin on a fence two days ago. The pain has become excruciating and 'out of proportion' to the modest skin changes, requiring escalating opioids with little relief. Over the last several hours he has felt feverish, lightheaded, and increasingly confused. PMH: type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 10.1%), obesity, hypertension, peripheral arterial disease. Medications: metformin, insulin, amlodipine. Allergies: none. Social: drinks alcohol heavily on weekends, does not smoke. He has not had recent surgery or IV drug use. Family history: noncontributory. On arrival he is tachycardic and hypotensive. The team is concerned that the clinical picture is evolving faster than typical cellulitis, and his analgesic requirement is striking relative to the visible findings.
cases.exam: The patient is ill-appearing, diaphoretic, and confused. The left lower leg is tensely swollen with dusky, mottled skin and several tense hemorrhagic bullae. There is palpable crepitus over the calf. Pain on palpation extends well beyond the area of visible erythema, and there are patches of cutaneous anesthesia over the most affected skin. Pulses are difficult to palpate due to swelling. There is no obvious fluctuant abscess. The remainder of the exam is notable for dry mucous membranes and delayed capillary refill consistent with early septic shock.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Pain out of proportion to exam, crepitus, hemorrhagic bullae, and skin anesthesia in a septic diabetic point most urgently to which diagnosis?
options: Stasis dermatitis
Acute gout flare
Uncomplicated cellulitis
Necrotizing fasciitis
Deep vein thrombosis
stages.explanation: The combination of pain out of proportion to visible findings, crepitus (gas in tissue), hemorrhagic bullae, skin anesthesia (from destruction of cutaneous nerves), and systemic toxicity is the classic presentation of necrotizing fasciitis, a surgical emergency. Uncomplicated cellulitis does not cause crepitus, bullae, anesthesia, or this degree of systemic collapse. DVT causes swelling and tenderness but not crepitus, bullae, or sepsis of this severity. Gout produces a hot, painful joint, not spreading necrosis with gas. Stasis dermatitis is chronic and non-toxic. Recognizing necrotizing infection immediately is critical because delay markedly increases mortality.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 26.4 ×10³/µL with bandemia
Hemoglobin 12.8 g/dL
Sodium 128 mEq/L
Glucose 312 mg/dL
Creatinine 2.1 mg/dL
CRP 215 mg/L
Lactate 4.6 mmol/L
Platelets 96 ×10³/µL
Plain radiograph of leg: soft-tissue gas tracking along fascial planes

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Using the LRINEC score (laboratory risk indicator for necrotizing fasciitis), which lab pattern in this patient most increases suspicion?
options: Normal lactate with normal WBC in this clinical context
Normal CRP with low WBC as the primary diagnosis
Elevated CRP, leukocytosis, hyponatremia,
Hypernatremia with low glucose in this clinical context
Isolated thrombocytosis as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: The LRINEC score assigns points for elevated CRP, leukocytosis, low hemoglobin, hyponatremia, elevated creatinine, and hyperglycemia; this patient's markedly elevated CRP, high WBC with bandemia, sodium of 128, and creatinine of 2.1 all push the score into the high-risk range, supporting necrotizing infection. A normal CRP with low WBC would argue against it. Isolated thrombocytosis is not a LRINEC component. Hypernatremia and low glucose are the opposite of the concerning pattern. Importantly, LRINEC is an adjunct only; a low score never excludes necrotizing fasciitis, and surgical exploration should not be delayed when clinical suspicion is high.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the single most important intervention to reduce mortality in this patient?
options: Emergent surgical debridement of necrotic tissue
Bedside incision and drainage of a bulla
MRI of the leg before any intervention
Lower-extremity Doppler ultrasound as monotherapy
Immediate broad-spectrum IV antibiotics alone
stages.explanation: Emergent surgical exploration and aggressive debridement of all necrotic tissue is the definitive, mortality-reducing treatment for necrotizing fasciitis; source control cannot wait. Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (including coverage for gram-positives, gram-negatives, anaerobes, and clindamycin for antitoxin effect) are essential adjuncts but cannot penetrate dead, avascular tissue and do not substitute for surgery. Obtaining MRI before intervention dangerously delays the operating room when the diagnosis is already clinically and radiographically apparent. Draining a single bulla is inadequate source control. Doppler ultrasound evaluates clot, not necrotizing infection, and would waste critical time.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which empiric antibiotic regimen best covers the likely polymicrobial pathogens while adding toxin suppression?
options: Ceftriaxone monotherapy as initial therapy
Azithromycin plus doxycycline as initial therapy
Metronidazole alone as initial therapy
Vancomycin plus piperacillin-tazobactam plus clindamycin
Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Broad empiric coverage with vancomycin (for MRSA), piperacillin-tazobactam (gram-negatives and anaerobes), and clindamycin (which suppresses streptococcal and staphylococcal toxin production via protein synthesis inhibition) is the recommended regimen until cultures return. Oral amoxicillin-clavulanate provides inadequate spectrum and route for a critically ill, hypotensive patient. Ceftriaxone alone misses MRSA, anaerobes, and Pseudomonas and lacks antitoxin effect. Azithromycin plus doxycycline does not provide reliable coverage for this surgical sepsis. Metronidazole covers only anaerobes. Antibiotics are adjunctive to surgical debridement, which remains the cornerstone of management.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: After initial debridement, what is the expected operative course for this patient?
options: Amputation is contraindicated in all cases with appropriate follow-up
Avoidance of further surgery once antibiotics are started
Wound closure at the first operation regardless of viability
Planned serial re-explorations every 24-48 hours until viable margins
A single operation is curative and no re-exploration is needed
stages.explanation: Necrotizing fasciitis characteristically requires planned serial re-explorations (typically every 24-48 hours) to debride additional necrotic tissue as the process declares itself, because a single operation rarely removes all nonviable tissue. Closure is deferred until the wound is clean and margins are viable, so primary closure at the first operation is inappropriate. Antibiotics do not eliminate the need for repeat surgery; ongoing source control is paramount. Amputation, while a last resort, is sometimes life-saving when limb tissue is unsalvageable, so it is not absolutely contraindicated. Supportive ICU care for septic shock continues throughout.

cases.diagnosis: Necrotizing fasciitis of the lower extremity
pearls: Pain out of proportion to exam plus crepitus, bullae, and skin anesthesia signals necrotizing fasciitis until proven otherwise.
LRINEC supports the diagnosis but a low score does not exclude it; never let imaging delay the OR.
Emergent surgical debridement is the definitive, mortality-reducing treatment; antibiotics are adjunctive.
Add clindamycin to broad-spectrum coverage for its antitoxin (protein-synthesis-inhibiting) effect, and plan serial re-explorations.

cases.title: Sudden Breathlessness and Confusion After a Thigh Fracture
cases.chiefComplaint: New shortness of breath and confusion two days after fracturing his femur.
cases.history: A 21-year-old previously healthy man was admitted 48 hours ago after a high-speed motorcycle crash that caused a closed midshaft femur fracture. He was placed in skeletal traction awaiting intramedullary nailing. Overnight the nursing staff noted he became progressively short of breath, restless, and confused, and he is transferred to the ICU. He has no chest pain, leg swelling out of proportion, or hemoptysis. PMH: none. Medications: only acetaminophen and as-needed opioids since admission. Allergies: none. Social: occasional alcohol, no tobacco, no drugs. Family history: no clotting disorders. He had no respiratory symptoms on admission, and his oxygenation was normal for the first 24 hours. The team notes the temporal pattern of deterioration roughly two days after a long-bone fracture and is constructing a differential that includes pulmonary embolism, pneumonia, and a fracture-related embolic syndrome.
cases.exam: The patient is tachypneic and agitated, oriented only to person. Conjunctivae and oral mucosa show scattered petechiae, and a fine petechial rash is visible over the anterior chest wall and axillae. Lung exam reveals diffuse crackles bilaterally. The fractured thigh is splinted and in traction with intact distal pulses and no calf cord or asymmetric swelling. Neurologic exam shows global confusion without focal deficit. Cardiac exam reveals tachycardia without murmur, and there is no jugular venous distension. The petechiae and the timing relative to the long-bone fracture are the most striking findings.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Hypoxia, confusion, and a petechial rash 48 hours after a femur fracture are most consistent with which syndrome?
options: Acute opioid overdose
Tension pneumothorax
Pulmonary embolism from DVT
Aspiration pneumonia
Fat embolism syndrome
stages.explanation: The classic triad of fat embolism syndrome (FES) is hypoxemia, neurologic changes, and a petechial rash, characteristically developing 24-72 hours after a long-bone or pelvic fracture, exactly this patient's course. Thromboembolic PE can cause hypoxia and tachycardia but does not produce the conjunctival/axillary petechial rash and is less associated with this triad. Aspiration pneumonia typically follows an aspiration event and is more focal. Opioid overdose causes hypoventilation and miosis with depressed, not agitated, mental status and would not cause petechiae. Tension pneumothorax causes unilateral absent breath sounds and hemodynamic collapse, not bilateral crackles with petechiae.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
ABG (4 L): pH 7.47, PaO2 52 mmHg, PaCO2 30 mmHg
Hemoglobin 9.8 g/dL (was 13.0 on admission)
Platelets 88 ×10³/µL
WBC 12.1 ×10³/µL
Chest X-ray: bilateral diffuse patchy infiltrates
CT pulmonary angiogram: no filling defect; bilateral ground-glass opacities
Fundoscopy: scattered retinal hemorrhages and cotton-wool spots

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Fat embolism syndrome is primarily a clinical diagnosis. Which set of criteria is most commonly cited to support it?
options: Ranson criteria
Centor criteria
Gurd and Wilson criteria
Wells criteria
Light criteria
stages.explanation: Gurd and Wilson criteria are the classic framework for diagnosing fat embolism syndrome, combining major features (petechial rash, hypoxemia, CNS depression, pulmonary edema) with minor features (tachycardia, fever, retinal changes, thrombocytopenia, anemia, fat in urine/sputum). Wells criteria estimate pretest probability of venous thromboembolism, not FES. Light criteria distinguish transudative from exudative pleural effusions. Ranson criteria assess acute pancreatitis severity. Centor criteria estimate the likelihood of streptococcal pharyngitis. This patient meets multiple Gurd and Wilson major and minor criteria (petechiae, hypoxemia, CNS changes, retinal hemorrhages, thrombocytopenia, anemia, fever, tachycardia).

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the cornerstone of treatment for fat embolism syndrome?
options: Systemic thrombolysis as initial therapy
Therapeutic anticoagulation with heparin
High-dose corticosteroids as definitive cure
Emergency IVC filter placement as initial therapy
Supportive care with oxygenation
stages.explanation: Management of fat embolism syndrome is primarily supportive: supplemental oxygen, and mechanical ventilation when hypoxemia is severe, plus hemodynamic support and treatment of the underlying fracture. There is no proven specific pharmacologic cure. Thrombolysis treats thrombotic clot, not fat globules, and adds bleeding risk in a trauma patient. Therapeutic anticoagulation is not indicated for fat embolism itself and is hazardous post-trauma. Corticosteroids have been studied mainly for prophylaxis with inconsistent evidence and are not a definitive treatment. An IVC filter prevents lower-extremity venous clots from reaching the lungs and does nothing for marrow fat embolism. Most patients recover with supportive care.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which intervention most effectively prevents fat embolism syndrome in long-bone fractures?
options: Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics
Routine prophylactic corticosteroids for all fractures
Early operative fixation of the fracture
Prolonged traction with delayed surgery
Strict bed rest for two weeks
stages.explanation: Early operative fixation (stabilization) of long-bone fractures reduces the incidence of fat embolism syndrome by limiting ongoing release of marrow fat from fracture motion. Prolonged traction with delayed surgery increases the window during which marrow contents embolize and is associated with higher FES rates. Routine prophylactic corticosteroids have shown only limited, inconsistent benefit and are not standard for all fractures. Bed rest does not address the source of embolization. Antibiotics treat infection and have no role in FES prevention. Timely stabilization, careful surgical technique, and supportive care remain the practical mainstays.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If this patient's hypoxemia worsens despite high-flow oxygen and he develops bilateral infiltrates with poor compliance, what complication has likely developed?
options: Pleural effusion
Pulmonary fibrosis
Spontaneous pneumothorax
Acute respiratory distress syndrome
Lobar pneumonia
stages.explanation: Progressive refractory hypoxemia with diffuse bilateral infiltrates and reduced lung compliance after fat embolism indicates acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS), a recognized severe consequence of FES requiring lung-protective mechanical ventilation. Lobar pneumonia produces focal consolidation, not diffuse bilateral injury, and would have a different time course. Spontaneous pneumothorax causes unilateral findings and tracheal shift, not diffuse infiltrates. Pulmonary fibrosis is a chronic process that does not develop acutely over hours. A pleural effusion causes basilar dullness and is not the cause of diffuse ground-glass injury here. Recognizing ARDS prompts low-tidal-volume ventilation and meticulous supportive care.

cases.diagnosis: Fat embolism syndrome
pearls: Fat embolism syndrome classically presents 24-72 hours after long-bone fracture with hypoxia, neurologic change, and a petechial rash.
It is a clinical diagnosis supported by Gurd and Wilson criteria; CT angiogram is typically negative for clot.
Treatment is supportive (oxygen, ventilatory and hemodynamic support); no specific drug cures it.
Early operative fixation of long-bone fractures reduces FES incidence; severe cases can progress to ARDS.

cases.title: Bone Protruding Through a Leg Wound After a Crash
cases.chiefComplaint: Severe lower-leg pain with bone visible through an open wound after a motorcycle collision.
cases.history: A 33-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by ambulance after a motorcycle versus car collision approximately 50 minutes ago. He has severe right lower-leg pain and reports he saw bone through the skin at the scene. He was wearing a helmet and denies head injury or loss of consciousness. He has no chest, abdominal, or back pain. PMH: none. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Social: smokes half a pack daily, drinks socially. He works in construction. Family history: noncontributory. His last tetanus vaccination was 'sometime in college,' more than 10 years ago and he is unsure of the exact date. Primary survey shows an intact airway, normal breathing, and stable circulation; the secondary survey is notable only for the right leg injury. The wound was contaminated with roadway debris and dirt at the scene, and EMS applied a sterile saline dressing during transport.
cases.exam: There is a 4 cm transverse laceration over the anteromedial right tibia with exposed bone fragment and ongoing moderate venous oozing; the wound is grossly contaminated with dirt and asphalt. The leg is deformed and tender with limited active motion. Distal neurovascular exam: dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses palpable, capillary refill brisk, sensation intact over the foot, and the patient can wiggle his toes. The compartments are soft and non-tense. There is no other open wound. No knee effusion or hip tenderness is appreciated. The contralateral leg and the remainder of the trauma survey are unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: In the trauma bay, after completing the primary and secondary survey, which immediate intervention is most important for this open fracture?
options: Apply a tourniquet to stop all bleeding
Obtain an MRI of the leg first as the first step today
Scrub the bone vigorously with povidone-iodine in the ED
Reduce and splint the fracture before any other step
Begin IV antibiotics promptly
stages.explanation: Early IV antibiotics are a time-sensitive, mortality- and morbidity-reducing intervention for open fractures and should be given as soon as possible to lower infection risk, along with assessing and updating tetanus prophylaxis. Reduction and splinting are important but come after neurovascular assessment and antibiotic initiation; gross repositioning is done with care. Vigorous ED scrubbing or povidone-iodine scrubbing of exposed bone is not recommended and is reserved for formal operative irrigation and debridement. MRI is not part of acute open-fracture management; plain films suffice initially. A tourniquet is reserved for uncontrolled, life-threatening hemorrhage, not moderate venous oozing.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Right tibia/fibula radiograph: comminuted midshaft tibial fracture with associated fibular fracture; no foreign metallic body
Wound: ~4 cm, gross contamination, exposed bone, no extensive soft-tissue stripping or large skin loss
Hemoglobin 13.4 g/dL
WBC 11.2 ×10³/µL
Distal neurovascular exam: intact
Last tetanus toxoid: >10 years ago, primary series complete

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on a wound greater than 1 cm with moderate contamination but without extensive soft-tissue loss, which Gustilo-Anderson type best fits?
options: Type IIIC as the primary diagnosis
Type IIIB as the primary diagnosis
Type II
Type IIIA as the primary diagnosis
Type I as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Gustilo-Anderson Type II is a wound greater than 1 cm with moderate soft-tissue damage and contamination but without extensive soft-tissue loss, periosteal stripping, or vascular injury, matching this 4 cm contaminated wound with adequate coverage and intact perfusion. Type I is a clean wound under 1 cm with minimal injury. Type IIIA involves extensive soft-tissue laceration but with adequate bone coverage. Type IIIB involves extensive soft-tissue loss with periosteal stripping requiring flap coverage. Type IIIC includes an arterial injury requiring repair. Classification guides antibiotic choice and informs prognosis, though definitive grading is finalized in the operating room.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Given this Gustilo-Anderson Type II tibial fracture, which antibiotic and tetanus plan is most appropriate?
options: Vancomycin alone, defer tetanus as initial therapy
No antibiotics; tetanus immunoglobulin only as initial therapy
Antifungal therapy plus tetanus immunoglobulin as monotherapy
Oral amoxicillin only, no tetanus as initial therapy
First-generation cephalosporin plus tetanus toxoid booster
stages.explanation: For Gustilo Type I-II open fractures, a first-generation cephalosporin such as cefazolin (gram-positive coverage) is the standard prophylactic antibiotic; an aminoglycoside is added for Type III injuries. Because his primary tetanus series is complete but his last booster was more than 10 years ago and the wound is dirty, he needs a tetanus toxoid-containing booster (Tdap/Td); tetanus immunoglobulin is added only when the primary series is incomplete or unknown. Giving immunoglobulin alone is incorrect when a booster suffices. Oral amoxicillin and deferring tetanus undertreats this dirty open fracture. Vancomycin alone is not first-line prophylaxis here, and antifungals have no role.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the definitive surgical management this patient requires?
options: Observation and repeat imaging in one week as initial therapy
Bedside suture closure of the wound in the ED as monotherapy
Casting without surgery as initial therapy
Outpatient follow-up with oral antibiotics as initial therapy
Urgent operative irrigation
stages.explanation: Open fractures require urgent operative irrigation and debridement to remove contamination and devitalized tissue, plus fracture stabilization (commonly intramedullary nailing for tibial shaft fractures), to minimize infection and promote healing. Bedside ED suture closure traps contamination and risks deep infection. Outpatient management with oral antibiotics is inappropriate for an open, contaminated fracture. Casting alone does not address the contaminated wound or provide adequate stabilization for a comminuted open injury. Observation with delayed imaging neglects the urgent infection and stabilization needs. Timely debridement, appropriate antibiotics, and fixation are the pillars of open-fracture care.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Postoperatively the patient develops increasing pain, a tense swollen leg, and pain on passive toe extension. What complication must be excluded immediately?
options: Acute compartment syndrome
Hardware allergy
Superficial wound dehiscence
Fat embolism syndrome
Deep vein thrombosis
stages.explanation: Worsening pain out of proportion, a tense swollen compartment, and pain with passive stretch of the toes are hallmark signs of acute compartment syndrome, a surgical emergency that can complicate tibial fractures and requires emergent fasciotomy. Superficial wound dehiscence does not cause a tense compartment or pain on passive stretch. DVT can cause swelling but not the passive-stretch pain pattern, and is not the immediate must-exclude diagnosis here. Fat embolism causes hypoxia and confusion, not isolated limb compartment findings. Hardware allergy is rare and not an acute postoperative emergency. Prompt recognition and fasciotomy prevent irreversible muscle and nerve damage.

cases.diagnosis: Open tibial fracture (Gustilo-Anderson Type II)
pearls: Open fractures need prompt IV antibiotics and tetanus assessment as time-sensitive priorities, plus urgent debridement.
Gustilo-Anderson grading (I-III) drives antibiotic choice: cefazolin for I-II, add an aminoglycoside for III.
A complete tetanus series with last booster >10 years ago and a dirty wound needs a toxoid booster, not immunoglobulin alone.
Watch for compartment syndrome after tibial fractures: pain out of proportion and pain on passive stretch mandate emergent fasciotomy.

cases.title: Sudden Thigh Pain After a Minor Stumble
cases.chiefComplaint: Sudden severe thigh pain and inability to bear weight after a minor trip at home.
cases.history: A 68-year-old woman is admitted after sudden severe right thigh pain and inability to bear weight following a minor trip over a rug at home; the mechanism seemed far too trivial for such a dramatic injury. For the past three months she has had a deep, dull, progressively worsening thigh ache, worse at night, that she attributed to arthritis. She also reports unintentional 12-pound weight loss and increasing fatigue. PMH: hypertension, prior smoking, and a 'breast lump' she never had evaluated. Medications: amlodipine, hydrochlorothiazide. Allergies: none. Social: former smoker (20 pack-years), occasional wine, lives with her husband. Family history: mother had breast cancer. She has not seen a physician in several years and has never had a mammogram. The combination of a fracture after trivial trauma, antecedent rest and night pain, constitutional symptoms, and an unevaluated breast lump prompts concern for an underlying pathologic process rather than a simple fall.
cases.exam: The right lower extremity is shortened and externally rotated with marked tenderness over the proximal femur and inability to perform a straight-leg raise. Distal pulses are intact and sensation is preserved. Breast exam reveals a firm, fixed 2 cm mass in the right upper outer quadrant with overlying skin dimpling and a palpable firm right axillary lymph node. There is no spinal tenderness. The contralateral hip is non-tender with full range of motion. Lungs are clear and the abdomen is soft without organomegaly. The breast and axillary findings are the most concerning extraskeletal signs.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A femur fracture from minimal trauma plus preceding rest/night pain and weight loss should raise suspicion for what?
options: Trochanteric bursitis as the first step today
Pathologic fracture through a metastatic lesion
Septic arthritis of the hip
Muscle strain as the first step today
Simple osteoporotic fracture only
stages.explanation: A fracture caused by trivial trauma, especially with antecedent rest/night pain and constitutional symptoms (weight loss, fatigue), strongly suggests a pathologic fracture through a bone weakened by a metastatic lesion. While osteoporosis lowers fracture threshold, it does not explain the months of progressive night pain or systemic symptoms, so attributing this solely to osteoporosis is inadequate. A muscle strain cannot cause a displaced femur fracture with shortening and external rotation. Septic arthritis presents with fever and a hot, restricted joint, not an acute completed fracture. Trochanteric bursitis causes lateral hip tenderness without fracture. The breast mass and axillary node further point to malignancy.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Right femur radiograph: displaced subtrochanteric fracture through a permeative lytic lesion
Hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL
Calcium 11.8 mg/dL
Albumin 3.8 g/dL
Alkaline phosphatase 240 U/L
ESR 64 mm/hr
Chest X-ray: multiple small pulmonary nodules
Mammogram: spiculated right breast mass, BI-RADS 5

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which carcinoma most commonly metastasizes to bone and best fits this woman's presentation?
options: Basal cell carcinoma
Pancreatic carcinoma
Medullary thyroid carcinoma
Breast carcinoma
Glioblastoma in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Breast carcinoma is among the most common cancers to metastasize to bone (along with prostate, lung, kidney, and thyroid) and fits perfectly here given the spiculated breast mass, axillary node, lytic femoral lesion, and pulmonary nodules. Pancreatic carcinoma rarely presents first with isolated bone metastasis and is not suggested by the breast findings. Glioblastoma essentially does not metastasize outside the CNS. Basal cell carcinoma is a locally invasive skin cancer that almost never metastasizes to bone. Medullary thyroid carcinoma can spread to bone but is far less common and unsupported by this clinical picture. Tissue diagnosis will confirm the primary.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: The corrected calcium is elevated. What is the most appropriate initial treatment for symptomatic hypercalcemia of malignancy?
options: Oral calcium restriction alone as the primary diagnosis
Thiazide diuretic as the primary diagnosis
Loop diuretics as first-line monotherapy in this clinical context
Aggressive IV isotonic saline hydration, then a bisphosphonate
Immediate hemodialysis for all patients in this clinical context
stages.explanation: The cornerstone of treating hypercalcemia of malignancy is aggressive IV isotonic saline to restore volume and promote calciuresis, followed by a bisphosphonate (e.g., zoledronic acid) or denosumab to inhibit osteoclastic bone resorption. Loop diuretics are not first-line and are added only after adequate volume repletion if needed; using them up front in a dehydrated patient worsens hypercalcemia. Oral calcium restriction alone is insufficient for symptomatic hypercalcemia. Thiazides actually increase serum calcium and are contraindicated. Hemodialysis is reserved for severe, refractory cases or renal failure, not routine first-line therapy. Calcitonin can be added for rapid short-term lowering.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Before fixing the femur, which step is essential to establish the diagnosis and guide oncologic care?
options: Biopsy to obtain tissue diagnosis
Long-term casting only as initial therapy
Watchful waiting for 6 weeks as initial therapy
Immediate amputation as initial therapy
Empiric chemotherapy without tissue as monotherapy
stages.explanation: Obtaining tissue via biopsy, frequently performed at the time of surgical stabilization, is essential to confirm metastatic carcinoma, identify the primary, and obtain receptor status (e.g., ER/PR/HER2) that directs systemic therapy. Amputation is not indicated for a metastatic pathologic fracture amenable to stabilization. Empiric chemotherapy without a tissue diagnosis is inappropriate because treatment depends on tumor type and receptor profile. Casting alone does not stabilize an unstable subtrochanteric pathologic fracture or treat the cancer. Watchful waiting delays both fracture care and cancer treatment. Definitive operative fixation (e.g., intramedullary nailing) plus oncologic workup and often postoperative radiation optimize outcomes.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which adjuvant local therapy is commonly used after surgical fixation of a metastatic long-bone lesion?
options: Routine limb amputation as initial therapy
Hyperbaric oxygen as initial therapy
No further local therapy is ever indicated
Topical chemotherapy as initial therapy
Postoperative radiation therapy to the lesion
stages.explanation: Postoperative radiation therapy to the stabilized metastatic lesion is commonly used to control local tumor, relieve pain, and reduce the risk of further bony destruction and hardware failure. Routine amputation is not appropriate for a stabilizable metastatic fracture. Hyperbaric oxygen has no established role in treating bone metastases. Topical chemotherapy cannot penetrate bone and is not used for skeletal metastases. Saying no further local therapy is indicated ignores the well-established benefit of adjuvant radiation. Combined with systemic therapy guided by the primary tumor and its receptor status, this multimodal approach improves pain control and function.

cases.diagnosis: Pathologic femur fracture from metastatic breast carcinoma
pearls: A fracture from trivial trauma with preceding rest/night pain and weight loss suggests a pathologic fracture until proven otherwise.
Breast, prostate, lung, kidney, and thyroid cancers are the classic bone-metastasizing primaries.
Treat hypercalcemia of malignancy with aggressive IV saline first, then a bisphosphonate or denosumab; avoid thiazides.
Obtain tissue diagnosis (often during fixation) and consider postoperative radiation plus receptor-directed systemic therapy.

cases.title: Bone Pain and Fatigue in an Older Man
cases.chiefComplaint: Persistent back and rib bone pain with worsening fatigue over several months.
cases.history: A 66-year-old man presents to clinic with three months of progressive low back and rib pain that is worse with movement and not relieved by acetaminophen. He also reports increasing fatigue, intermittent constipation, excessive thirst, and a recent episode of confusion. He has had two upper respiratory infections in the past two months. PMH: hypertension, well-controlled. Medications: lisinopril 10 mg daily, acetaminophen as needed. Allergies: none. Social: retired accountant, never smoked, rare alcohol. Family history: noncontributory. He denies trauma. Over the same period he has noticed that minor bumps cause more discomfort than expected and that he feels generally unwell. Review of systems is positive for decreased appetite and a 9-pound weight loss. The clustering of bone pain, fatigue, constitutional symptoms, neurologic change, and frequent infections in an older adult prompts a workup for a systemic marrow process rather than isolated musculoskeletal disease.
cases.exam: The patient appears pale and tired. There is point tenderness over several thoracic ribs and the lumbar spine without overlying erythema or deformity. Neurologic exam is intact without focal weakness, sensory level, or saddle anesthesia, though he is mildly inattentive. Conjunctivae are pale. There is no lymphadenopathy, hepatosplenomegaly, or peripheral edema. Cardiopulmonary exam is unremarkable. Skin shows no rash or petechiae. The combination of diffuse skeletal tenderness and pallor, with otherwise unremarkable focal findings, is most notable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Bone pain, anemia, fatigue, constipation/thirst (suggesting hypercalcemia), and recurrent infections in an older adult most suggest which process?
options: Vitamin D deficiency osteomalacia
Osteoarthritis
Multiple myeloma
Fibromyalgia in this clinical context
Mechanical low back strain
stages.explanation: The constellation of bone pain, anemia, symptoms of hypercalcemia (constipation, thirst, confusion), and recurrent infections in an older adult is classic for multiple myeloma, a clonal plasma-cell malignancy. The mnemonic CRAB (hyperCalcemia, Renal insufficiency, Anemia, Bone lesions) captures its features. Osteoarthritis causes mechanical joint pain without anemia, hypercalcemia, or recurrent infection. Osteomalacia causes diffuse bone pain but not anemia with hypercalcemia and immune dysfunction in this pattern. Fibromyalgia causes widespread soft-tissue pain with normal labs and no anemia or hypercalcemia. Mechanical strain does not explain systemic symptoms, anemia, or hypercalcemia. Laboratory and imaging studies will confirm the diagnosis.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Hemoglobin 9.0 g/dL (normocytic)
Calcium 11.6 mg/dL
Creatinine 1.9 mg/dL
Total protein 9.6 g/dL, albumin 3.4 g/dL (elevated globulin gap)
Peripheral smear: rouleaux formation
Serum protein electrophoresis: monoclonal (M) spike
Skull radiograph: multiple well-defined punched-out lytic lesions
Beta-2 microglobulin: elevated

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Skull films show multiple punched-out lytic lesions. Which additional test best confirms the underlying plasma-cell disorder?
options: Serum ferritin as the primary diagnosis
Bone marrow biopsy showing clonal plasma cells
Radionuclide bone scan in this clinical context
Rheumatoid factor as the primary diagnosis
Erythrocyte sedimentation rate alone
stages.explanation: Bone marrow biopsy demonstrating clonal plasma-cell infiltration is central to diagnosing multiple myeloma and quantifies plasma-cell burden, complementing serum/urine protein electrophoresis and free light chains. A radionuclide bone scan is notably insensitive for myeloma because the lytic lesions are purely osteolytic with little osteoblastic activity; whole-body low-dose CT, MRI, or PET-CT are preferred imaging. Serum ferritin assesses iron status, not plasma-cell disease. Rheumatoid factor evaluates autoimmune arthritis and is irrelevant here. ESR is nonspecifically elevated and cannot confirm the diagnosis. Together, the M spike, clonal marrow plasma cells, and CRAB features establish symptomatic myeloma.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which imaging modality is preferred over a traditional radionuclide bone scan for detecting myeloma bone disease?
options: Technetium-99m bone scan
Plain abdominal film
Carotid ultrasound
Whole-body low-dose CT, MRI, or PET-CT
DEXA scan only in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Whole-body low-dose CT, MRI, or PET-CT are preferred for detecting myeloma bone disease because they detect the purely lytic lesions and marrow involvement that conventional technetium bone scans miss; the osteolytic lesions lack the osteoblastic activity that bone scans rely on. A technetium bone scan is therefore insensitive in myeloma. A plain abdominal film does not assess the skeleton comprehensively. Carotid ultrasound evaluates vascular disease, unrelated here. A DEXA scan measures bone density for osteoporosis screening and does not characterize focal lytic lesions or marrow disease. Appropriate cross-sectional or metabolic imaging guides staging and treatment planning.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which medication class reduces skeletal-related events and bone pain in multiple myeloma?
options: Oral corticosteroids alone as bone protection
Calcium and vitamin D megadoses as monotherapy
Broad-spectrum antibiotics for bone lesions
Bisphosphonates (e.g., zoledronic acid)
Loop diuretics for bone health as monotherapy
stages.explanation: Bisphosphonates such as zoledronic acid (or denosumab) inhibit osteoclast-mediated bone resorption, reduce skeletal-related events (fractures, cord compression, need for radiation), and help control bone pain and hypercalcemia in myeloma. Corticosteroids are part of antimyeloma chemotherapy regimens but are not given as standalone bone protection. Megadose calcium and vitamin D do not prevent skeletal events and could worsen hypercalcemia. Loop diuretics treat volume and hypercalcemia acutely but do not protect bone. Antibiotics treat infection, not the lytic bone disease. Bone-targeted agents are given alongside systemic antimyeloma therapy and supportive care, with attention to renal dosing and osteonecrosis-of-the-jaw risk.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: The patient develops acute kidney injury. Which myeloma-related mechanism is the most likely cause?
options: Light-chain (cast) nephropathy
Minimal change disease
Renal artery stenosis
Acute pyelonephritis
Contrast-induced nephropathy from the skull film
stages.explanation: Light-chain (cast) nephropathy, in which filtered monoclonal free light chains precipitate with Tamm-Horsfall protein to form obstructing tubular casts, is the most common cause of renal failure in multiple myeloma; hypercalcemia and dehydration contribute. Acute pyelonephritis would present with fever, flank pain, and pyuria, not fitting this picture. Renal artery stenosis causes renovascular hypertension and chronic, not abrupt myeloma-related, injury. Minimal change disease causes nephrotic syndrome unrelated to myeloma light chains. A plain skull radiograph uses no iodinated contrast, so contrast nephropathy is impossible here. Management includes hydration, treating hypercalcemia, and rapid reduction of the light-chain burden with antimyeloma therapy.

cases.diagnosis: Multiple myeloma with skeletal involvement
pearls: Think myeloma with CRAB features: hyperCalcemia, Renal insufficiency, Anemia, and lytic Bone lesions.
Skull shows punched-out lytic lesions; technetium bone scans miss them, so use low-dose CT, MRI, or PET-CT.
Diagnosis rests on a monoclonal protein plus clonal plasma cells on marrow biopsy.
Bisphosphonates/denosumab reduce skeletal events; light-chain cast nephropathy is the classic cause of renal failure.

cases.title: A Bowed Leg, Hearing Loss, and an Odd Lab Result
cases.chiefComplaint: Aching in a progressively bowing lower leg, found alongside an abnormal blood test.
cases.history: A 70-year-old man presents to clinic because his right lower leg has gradually bowed outward over the past two years and now aches deeply, especially with weight-bearing. He has also noticed worsening hearing in both ears and that his hats feel tighter than they used to. He was referred after a routine panel showed an isolated elevation of alkaline phosphatase. PMH: hypertension, mild osteoarthritis. Medications: amlodipine, occasional ibuprofen. Allergies: none. Social: retired postal worker, drinks coffee daily, no tobacco or significant alcohol. Family history: his father had a 'deformed leg' and used a cane late in life. He denies fever, weight loss, or night sweats. He notes the bone pain is constant and dull rather than sharp, and the bowing has slowly altered his gait. The combination of focal bone deformity, conductive/sensorineural hearing changes, increasing head size, and an isolated alkaline phosphatase elevation suggests a localized disorder of bone remodeling.
cases.exam: The right tibia is anterolaterally bowed, warm to the touch over the deformity, and tender to palpation; the overlying skin is intact. Gait is mildly antalgic with a broad base. The skull appears enlarged with frontal bossing. Hearing is reduced bilaterally. Cardiovascular exam reveals no signs of high-output failure (no elevated JVP, no displaced apex, no flow murmur at rest). Neurologic exam is otherwise nonfocal. There is no joint effusion. The localized warmth and tenderness over a bowed long bone, together with skull enlargement, are the most distinctive findings.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A bowed, warm long bone with skull enlargement, hearing loss, and an isolated elevated alkaline phosphatase in an older adult suggests which diagnosis?
options: Paget disease of bone
Renal osteodystrophy
Primary hyperparathyroidism
Osteomalacia in this clinical context
Osteoporosis in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Paget disease of bone is characterized by localized accelerated, disorganized bone remodeling that produces enlarged, bowed, warm bones, skull enlargement with hearing loss (from cranial nerve compression or otic capsule involvement), and an isolated elevation of alkaline phosphatase with otherwise normal calcium and phosphate. Osteomalacia causes diffuse bone pain with low vitamin D and abnormal calcium/phosphate, not focal bowing with normal mineral levels. Primary hyperparathyroidism raises calcium and PTH, which are not the isolated finding here. Osteoporosis causes low bone mass and fractures without elevated alkaline phosphatase or bone enlargement. Renal osteodystrophy occurs in chronic kidney disease with deranged mineral metabolism, absent here.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Alkaline phosphatase 642 U/L (markedly elevated)
Calcium 9.5 mg/dL (normal)
Phosphate 3.4 mg/dL (normal)
PTH 42 pg/mL (normal)
25-OH vitamin D 34 ng/mL (normal)
Creatinine 1.0 mg/dL
Tibia radiograph: cortical thickening, coarsened trabeculae, bowing deformity
Reported on prior workup: elevated urinary hydroxyproline (bone turnover marker)

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which laboratory pattern is most characteristic of Paget disease of bone?
options: Elevated calcium and elevated PTH as the primary diagnosis
Pancytopenia with monoclonal spike as the primary diagnosis
Elevated phosphate with low alkaline phosphatase
Isolated elevated alkaline phosphatase with normal calcium
Low calcium and low phosphate as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Paget disease typically produces an isolated, often markedly elevated alkaline phosphatase (reflecting high osteoblastic activity) with normal serum calcium and phosphate, exactly as seen here. Elevated calcium with elevated PTH indicates primary hyperparathyroidism. Low calcium and low phosphate suggest osteomalacia or vitamin D deficiency. Elevated phosphate with low alkaline phosphatase does not fit any of these bone diseases and is not Pagetic. Pancytopenia with a monoclonal spike points to multiple myeloma. The normal calcium, phosphate, PTH, and vitamin D alongside a high alkaline phosphatase and classic radiographic remodeling confirm the biochemical signature of Paget disease.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which imaging study best assesses the overall extent and distribution of Pagetic bone involvement?
options: Carotid Doppler
Abdominal ultrasound
Echocardiogram
Radionuclide bone scan
Mammography in this clinical context
stages.explanation: A radionuclide (technetium) bone scan is the best test to map the extent and distribution of Paget disease because the highly active, remodeling Pagetic bone avidly takes up tracer, revealing all involved sites for monitoring and treatment planning; plain radiographs characterize individual lesions. A carotid Doppler evaluates vascular stenosis, unrelated to bone mapping. An echocardiogram assesses cardiac function (relevant only if high-output failure is suspected) but does not localize bone disease. Mammography screens breast tissue. Abdominal ultrasound images viscera, not the skeleton. Using a bone scan to survey disease burden complements the focal radiographic and biochemical findings.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: For symptomatic Paget disease with bone pain, what is the first-line pharmacologic therapy?
options: Bisphosphonates
Methotrexate as initial therapy
Allopurinol as initial therapy
Long-term systemic corticosteroids
Calcitriol megadoses as monotherapy
stages.explanation: Bisphosphonates, particularly a single IV infusion of zoledronic acid, are first-line for symptomatic Paget disease; they potently inhibit the overactive osteoclasts driving the disordered remodeling, reduce bone pain, and normalize alkaline phosphatase. Long-term corticosteroids are not a treatment for Paget disease and cause bone loss. Megadose calcitriol does not address the osteoclastic overactivity and risks hypercalcemia. Allopurinol treats gout/hyperuricemia, irrelevant here. Methotrexate is used for inflammatory and neoplastic conditions, not Paget disease. Adequate calcium and vitamin D should accompany bisphosphonate therapy to prevent hypocalcemia, and alkaline phosphatase is followed to gauge response.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: Which rare but serious complication should prompt concern if a patient with longstanding Paget disease develops new, worsening localized pain and a soft-tissue mass?
options: Osteosarcoma
Simple bone cyst
Gouty tophus in this presentation
Stress reaction
Bursitis as the leading concern
stages.explanation: Sarcomatous transformation, most often osteosarcoma, is a rare but feared complication of Paget disease and should be suspected when a patient develops new or rapidly worsening localized pain, swelling, or a soft-tissue mass over involved bone, sometimes with a sudden further rise in alkaline phosphatase. A gouty tophus is a crystal deposit associated with hyperuricemia, not malignant transformation. A simple bone cyst is a benign childhood lesion, not a complication of Paget disease. A stress reaction follows repetitive loading and would not form a soft-tissue mass. Bursitis causes periarticular pain without a destructive mass. New imaging and biopsy are warranted to evaluate for malignancy.

cases.diagnosis: Paget disease of bone
pearls: Paget disease: focal bone enlargement/bowing, warmth, skull growth, hearing loss, and isolated high alkaline phosphatase with normal calcium/phosphate.
A radionuclide bone scan best maps disease extent; radiographs show cortical thickening and coarse trabeculae.
IV bisphosphonates (zoledronic acid) are first-line for symptomatic disease; supplement calcium and vitamin D.
New focal pain or a soft-tissue mass raises concern for osteosarcomatous transformation.

cases.title: A Toddler With Repeated Fractures and Blue Eyes
cases.chiefComplaint: Recurrent fractures from seemingly minor incidents, prompting questions about the cause.
cases.history: A 4-year-old girl is brought to clinic by her mother for evaluation after a third fracture in 18 months, the latest a forearm fracture from a low fall onto carpet. Each fracture has occurred with what the family describes as minimal trauma, and they feel they are repeatedly questioned about how the injuries happened. The child is otherwise developmentally appropriate, active, and well-cared-for. PMH: two prior long-bone fractures, mild hearing concerns noted by preschool. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Social: lives with both parents and an older sibling; no concerns from school regarding the home environment, and the story is consistent across caregivers and visits. Family history: the mother reports she had 'several broken bones' as a child and has bluish eyes; a maternal uncle is short with bowed legs. The recurrent low-energy fractures combined with a positive family history and distinctive eye findings raise the question of an inherited connective-tissue/bone disorder versus non-accidental trauma.
cases.exam: The child is well-appearing and interactive. The right forearm is splinted and tender. The sclerae have a distinct blue-gray hue bilaterally. The teeth appear slightly grayish and translucent. There is mild ligamentous laxity at the wrists and elbows, and the limbs show subtle bowing. There are no bruises in unusual patterns, no retinal hemorrhages, and no signs of neglect; growth parameters are at the lower percentiles but tracking. The remainder of the exam is unremarkable. The blue sclerae, dental changes, joint laxity, and family history are the most distinctive findings supporting an intrinsic bone disorder.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: Recurrent low-trauma fractures with blue sclerae and a positive family history most strongly suggest which diagnosis?
options: Osteogenesis imperfecta
Leukemia as the first step today
Ehlers-Danlos syndrome
Non-accidental trauma exclusively
Rickets as the first step today
stages.explanation: Osteogenesis imperfecta (OI), a heritable defect of type I collagen, classically presents with recurrent fractures from minimal trauma, blue sclerae, dentinogenesis imperfecta (gray/translucent teeth), hearing loss, joint laxity, and a family history of fractures, all present here. Non-accidental trauma must always be considered with multiple fractures, but the blue sclerae, dental and collagen findings, and clear familial pattern point to OI; the two can coexist and require careful, non-judgmental evaluation. Rickets causes metaphyseal changes and bowing from vitamin D/calcium deficiency, not blue sclerae with this inheritance. Ehlers-Danlos primarily affects skin/joints, with fractures less prominent. Leukemia can cause bone pain but not this collagen phenotype.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Calcium 9.6 mg/dL (normal)
Phosphate 4.8 mg/dL (normal)
Alkaline phosphatase 210 U/L (normal for age)
25-OH vitamin D 32 ng/mL (normal)
PTH 30 pg/mL (normal)
Forearm radiograph: transverse fracture with diffusely osteopenic, thin (gracile) bones; old healed fractures and mild bowing
Skeletal survey: multiple healed fractures of varying ages; wormian bones in the skull

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Osteogenesis imperfecta most commonly results from a defect in which protein?
options: Type IV collagen
Elastin as the primary diagnosis
Type I collagen
Dystrophin in this clinical context
Fibrillin-1 in this clinical context
stages.explanation: Most cases of osteogenesis imperfecta result from mutations affecting type I collagen (genes COL1A1/COL1A2), the principal structural protein of bone, leading to brittle bones and the associated blue sclerae, dental and hearing abnormalities. Fibrillin-1 defects cause Marfan syndrome. Dystrophin defects cause Duchenne/Becker muscular dystrophy. Type IV collagen mutations cause Alport syndrome (kidney, ear, eye disease), not brittle bones. Elastin abnormalities are seen in conditions such as supravalvular aortic stenosis/Williams and cutis laxa. The type I collagen defect explains why bone, sclera (thin collagen allowing the bluish choroid to show through), teeth, and the auditory ossicles are all affected.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which finding helps distinguish osteogenesis imperfecta from isolated non-accidental trauma in this child?
options: Retinal hemorrhages as the primary diagnosis
A single fracture with a clear mechanism
Blue sclerae, wormian bones,
Normal sclerae with normal collagen testing
Patterned bruising in the shape of an object
stages.explanation: Blue sclerae, wormian (intrasutural) skull bones, generalized osteopenia with gracile bones, and a strong family history of low-trauma fractures support a heritable bone fragility disorder such as osteogenesis imperfecta and help distinguish it from isolated abuse. A single fracture with a clear mechanism argues against either pathology. Normal sclerae with normal collagen testing would point away from OI. Retinal hemorrhages and patterned bruising are concerning for inflicted injury, not OI. Importantly, OI and abuse are not mutually exclusive, so evaluation must remain thorough and compassionate, often involving genetics and, when indicated, child-protection assessment.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which pharmacologic therapy is used to increase bone density and reduce fracture frequency in moderate-to-severe osteogenesis imperfecta?
options: Chronic NSAID therapy
Systemic corticosteroids
High-dose vitamin A as monotherapy
Growth hormone for all patients
Bisphosphonates
stages.explanation: Bisphosphonates such as IV pamidronate (or other agents) reduce osteoclastic bone resorption, increase bone mineral density, decrease bone pain, and reduce fracture frequency in children with moderate-to-severe osteogenesis imperfecta. Systemic corticosteroids worsen bone density and are not a treatment for OI. Growth hormone is not a universal therapy for OI and is reserved for selected situations under specialist care. High-dose vitamin A can actually impair bone and cause toxicity. Chronic NSAIDs manage pain symptomatically but do not strengthen bone. Comprehensive care also includes physical therapy, fracture management, orthopedic surgery (e.g., rodding for deformity), and ensuring adequate calcium and vitamin D.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Beyond medication, which component is most important in the long-term management of this child?
options: Routine prophylactic limb amputation based on current risk
Avoidance of any calcium or vitamin D based on current risk
Strict immobilization and avoidance of all activity
Multidisciplinary care including physical therapy
A high-protein-only diet with appropriate follow-up
stages.explanation: Long-term management of osteogenesis imperfecta is multidisciplinary: physical therapy to build muscle strength and safe mobility, orthopedic follow-up for fractures and deformity correction (e.g., intramedullary rodding), audiology, dental care, and genetic counseling. Strict immobilization and avoiding all activity worsen disuse osteopenia and muscle weakness, increasing fracture risk. A high-protein-only diet is not a treatment. Prophylactic amputation is never appropriate. Avoiding calcium and vitamin D is harmful because adequate intake supports bone mineralization, especially alongside bisphosphonate therapy. A coordinated team approach optimizes function, growth, and quality of life while minimizing fractures.

cases.diagnosis: Osteogenesis imperfecta
pearls: Recurrent low-trauma fractures with blue sclerae, gray teeth, hearing loss, and a family history suggest osteogenesis imperfecta.
OI usually stems from a type I collagen (COL1A1/COL1A2) defect; wormian bones and gracile osteopenic bones support it.
OI and non-accidental trauma can coexist; evaluate thoroughly and compassionately.
Bisphosphonates reduce fractures; care is multidisciplinary (PT, orthopedics, audiology, dental, genetics).

cases.title: A Limping Boy After a Recent Cold
cases.chiefComplaint: A limp and hip discomfort after recovering from a viral upper respiratory illness.
cases.history: A 5-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department for a two-day history of limping and right hip/thigh discomfort. About one week ago he had a runny nose, cough, and low-grade fever that resolved. He has been eating and playing, though he favors the right leg and complains the hip hurts, sometimes referring pain to the knee. He has had no high fevers since the cold, no rash, and no recent trauma. PMH: healthy, immunizations up to date. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Social: attends kindergarten; well-appearing at home. Family history: noncontributory. His parents note he was reluctant to walk this morning but improved slightly after a dose of ibuprofen. The central clinical task is to distinguish a benign, self-limited post-viral hip process from septic arthritis, which is an emergency, using systematic clinical and laboratory criteria.
cases.exam: The child is well-appearing and consolable, lying with the right hip held in mild flexion and slight external rotation. He has a mildly antalgic gait and will bear weight when encouraged, though reluctantly. There is a soft end-feel with mild discomfort at the extremes of internal rotation and abduction of the right hip, but he tolerates gentle range of motion. There is no warmth, erythema, or effusion of the knee, and no spinal tenderness. He is afebrile in the ED, and the rest of the exam, including the abdomen and contralateral hip, is unremarkable.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A well-appearing afebrile child who limps after a recent viral illness but still bears weight most likely has which condition?
options: Transient synovitis of the hip
Legg-Calve-Perthes disease
Osteomyelitis of the femur
Septic arthritis of the hip
Slipped capital femoral epiphysis
stages.explanation: Transient synovitis is the most common cause of acute hip pain and limp in young children, typically following a viral illness, in a well-appearing child who is afebrile or low-grade febrile and able to bear weight, exactly this presentation. Septic arthritis must be excluded but usually involves high fever, refusal to bear weight, and a toxic appearance. Legg-Calve-Perthes (avascular necrosis) causes a more insidious limp, often in slightly older children, without a viral prodrome. SCFE typically occurs in older, overweight adolescents. Femoral osteomyelitis presents with fever and focal bony tenderness. Clinical criteria and labs help differentiate transient synovitis from septic arthritis.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
Temperature (peak, by history): 37.4 °C (no fever >38.5 °C)
WBC 9.8 ×10³/µL
ESR 22 mm/hr
CRP 14 mg/L
Weight-bearing status: able to bear weight
Hip ultrasound: small right hip joint effusion
Hip radiograph: no fracture, no widened joint space, normal epiphysis

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Which set of clinical criteria is used to estimate the probability of septic arthritis versus transient synovitis in a child with hip pain?
options: Ottawa criteria
Centor criteria
Alvarado score
Kocher criteria
Wells criteria
stages.explanation: The Kocher criteria estimate the likelihood of septic arthritis of the hip using four predictors: non-weight-bearing, fever >38.5 C, ESR >40 mm/hr, and WBC >12,000/uL (CRP is often included in modified versions). Ottawa rules guide ankle/knee radiography after trauma. Wells criteria assess venous thromboembolism probability. The Alvarado score estimates appendicitis likelihood. Centor criteria estimate streptococcal pharyngitis probability. Applying Kocher here, the child is weight-bearing, afebrile, with ESR 22 and WBC 9.8, so zero major criteria are met, making septic arthritis very unlikely and supporting transient synovitis. Higher Kocher counts sharply raise septic-arthritis probability and prompt aspiration.

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: Based on the Kocher criteria, how many predictors does this child meet, and what does that imply?
options: Zero criteria; septic arthritis is very unlikely
Four criteria; septic arthritis is nearly certain
Three criteria; aspiration is mandatory
Cannot be assessed without an MRI
Two criteria; immediate surgery is required
stages.explanation: This child is weight-bearing, afebrile (no fever >38.5 C), with ESR 22 (<40) and WBC 9.8 (<12,000), meeting zero of the four Kocher criteria, which corresponds to a very low predicted probability of septic arthritis and supports a diagnosis of transient synovitis. Four criteria would imply a very high probability, not zero. Three criteria would raise concern and often prompt aspiration, but that is not this patient. Two criteria do not mandate immediate surgery. MRI is not required to apply the Kocher criteria, which use clinical and basic lab data; the small effusion seen on ultrasound is consistent with transient synovitis.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: What is the appropriate management for this child with likely transient synovitis?
options: Six weeks of IV antibiotics as monotherapy
Immediate hip spica casting as monotherapy
Long-term systemic corticosteroids
Rest, NSAIDs, and close outpatient follow-up
Emergent surgical drainage of the hip
stages.explanation: Transient synovitis is self-limited, so management is supportive: rest, NSAIDs for pain and inflammation, and close follow-up with clear return precautions if fever, worsening pain, or inability to bear weight develops (which would prompt reassessment for septic arthritis). Emergent surgical drainage is reserved for confirmed septic arthritis, not transient synovitis. Six weeks of IV antibiotics treat osteomyelitis or confirmed joint infection, not a sterile reactive synovitis. Hip spica casting is for certain fractures or dysplasia, not synovitis. Long-term corticosteroids are inappropriate and unnecessary. Most children improve within days to a week; persistent or worsening symptoms warrant re-evaluation and possible joint aspiration.

stages.label: DISPOSITION
stages.question: Which development during follow-up would most strongly prompt re-evaluation for septic arthritis?
options: Gradual resolution of the limp over a few days
Return to normal play after rest based on current risk
Mild residual stiffness in the morning
New high fever with refusal to bear weight
A single low-grade temperature that resolves
stages.explanation: New high fever, refusal to bear weight, and escalating hip pain are red flags that shift the probability toward septic arthritis and warrant urgent re-evaluation, including possible joint aspiration, because untreated septic arthritis rapidly destroys the joint. Gradual resolution of the limp is the expected, reassuring course of transient synovitis. Mild morning stiffness during recovery is not alarming. Return to normal play indicates improvement. A single low-grade temperature that resolves is consistent with the benign post-viral process. Clear return precautions and timely reassessment are essential because the early presentations of transient synovitis and septic arthritis can overlap.

cases.diagnosis: Transient (toxic) synovitis of the hip
pearls: Transient synovitis is the most common cause of childhood hip pain, often after a viral illness, in a well-appearing child who can bear weight.
Kocher criteria (non-weight-bearing, fever >38.5 C, ESR >40, WBC >12,000) estimate septic-arthritis probability.
Management is supportive with rest, NSAIDs, and close follow-up; it is self-limited.
New high fever, refusal to bear weight, or worsening pain should trigger reassessment for septic arthritis.

cases.title: A Toddler Who Won't Move His Leg
cases.chiefComplaint: A feverish toddler who refuses to move or bear weight on one leg.
cases.history: A 2-year-old boy is brought to the emergency department because he has refused to move his right leg since yesterday and screams when it is touched or when his diaper is changed. He has had a fever to 39.2 C at home and has been irritable, eating poorly, and inconsolable. There is no known trauma. PMH: a minor skin infection on the leg two weeks ago that was treated with topical care; otherwise healthy, vaccines up to date. Medications: none. Allergies: none. Social: lives with parents; no concerns at daycare. Family history: noncontributory. The parents note he held the leg still in a flexed position and cried whenever they tried to stand him up. Unlike a child with a benign limp, this toddler appears systemically ill and exhibits pseudoparalysis of the limb, which, combined with fever, raises immediate concern for a joint-space infection requiring urgent diagnosis and treatment.
cases.exam: The toddler is ill-appearing, irritable, and inconsolable. The right hip is held in flexion, abduction, and external rotation, the position that maximizes intracapsular volume and minimizes pain. He refuses to bear weight (pseudoparalysis) and cries with any passive movement of the hip, especially internal rotation. There is fullness and warmth in the groin without overlying skin breakdown. The knee and ankle move without distress, and there is no spinal tenderness. He is tachycardic and febrile. The systemic toxicity, pseudoparalysis, and pain with hip motion are the key findings.

stages.label: INITIAL APPROACH
stages.question: A febrile, toxic-appearing toddler with pseudoparalysis and a hip held in flexion-abduction-external rotation most likely has which diagnosis?
options: Transient synovitis
Growing pains in this clinical context
Septic arthritis of the hip
Developmental dysplasia of the hip
Muscular strain
stages.explanation: A febrile, ill-appearing toddler with pseudoparalysis (refusal to move the limb) and the hip held in flexion, abduction, and external rotation (which maximizes joint capsule volume to reduce pain) is the classic presentation of septic arthritis of the hip, an orthopedic emergency. Transient synovitis occurs in a well-appearing, usually afebrile child who can often bear weight. Developmental dysplasia presents in infancy with a positive Barlow/Ortolani or limb-length asymmetry, not acute fever and toxicity. Growing pains are bilateral, nocturnal, and benign without fever or pseudoparalysis. A muscular strain does not cause systemic toxicity. Prompt recognition is critical because delayed treatment causes irreversible joint destruction.

stages.label: LABS & IMAGING
stages.content:
WBC 18.6 ×10³/µL with left shift
ESR 68 mm/hr
CRP 110 mg/L
Temperature: 39.2 °C
Weight-bearing: refuses (non-weight-bearing)
Blood cultures: pending
Hip ultrasound: moderate right hip joint effusion
Hip radiograph: soft-tissue swelling, no osteomyelitis or fracture

stages.label: DIAGNOSIS
stages.question: What is the most important diagnostic test to confirm septic arthritis of the hip?
options: Repeat plain radiographs in 24 hours as the primary diagnosis
Urgent ultrasound-guided joint aspiration with synovial fluid analysis
Bone densitometry as the primary diagnosis
MRI of the pelvis as the primary diagnosis
Empiric antibiotics without sampling as the primary diagnosis
stages.explanation: Urgent joint aspiration (arthrocentesis), often ultrasound-guided for the deep hip, is the definitive diagnostic test: synovial fluid is sent for cell count, Gram stain, and culture, with a high WBC count (typically tens of thousands with neutrophil predominance) and positive Gram stain/culture confirming septic arthritis. MRI can show effusion and adjacent osteomyelitis but should not delay aspiration and drainage when septic arthritis is suspected. Repeat radiographs in 24 hours dangerously delay diagnosis. Empiric antibiotics are started but only after attempting to obtain fluid and cultures, and antibiotics alone do not replace source control. Bone densitometry is irrelevant to acute infection.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Synovial fluid shows a markedly elevated WBC with neutrophil predominance and positive Gram stain. What is the definitive treatment?
options: Hip spica cast alone as initial therapy
Emergent surgical drainage plus IV antibiotics
Oral antibiotics and discharge home as initial therapy
Intra-articular corticosteroid injection as initial therapy
NSAIDs and observation as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Septic arthritis of the hip is a surgical emergency requiring emergent drainage and irrigation of the joint (open arthrotomy or arthroscopic washout) combined with IV antibiotics; the hip is especially vulnerable because pus under pressure compromises the femoral head blood supply and rapidly destroys cartilage. Oral antibiotics with discharge are unsafe and inadequate for a deep septic joint. NSAIDs and observation neglect a destructive infection. A hip spica cast does not drain infection. Intra-articular corticosteroids would worsen infection. Empiric IV antibiotics (tailored to age and likely organisms such as Staphylococcus aureus, then adjusted by culture) accompany prompt surgical source control.

stages.label: MANAGEMENT
stages.question: Which empiric antibiotic choice best targets the most likely organism in this age group while awaiting cultures?
options: Oral amoxicillin for typical ear-infection pathogens
Acyclovir as initial therapy
Antifungal therapy as initial therapy
An agent active against Staphylococcus aureus
Metronidazole alone as initial therapy
stages.explanation: Staphylococcus aureus is the most common cause of pediatric septic arthritis across age groups, so empiric therapy should reliably cover S. aureus (e.g., cefazolin, or anti-MRSA coverage like vancomycin/clindamycin where MRSA is prevalent), later tailored to culture and local resistance patterns; coverage is broadened in specific situations (e.g., neonates or Kingella considerations). Oral amoxicillin targeting otitis pathogens does not provide adequate antistaphylococcal coverage or the IV route a deep joint infection requires. Antifungals treat fungal infections, not typical bacterial septic arthritis. Metronidazole covers only anaerobes. Acyclovir is an antiviral with no role here. Antibiotics complement, not replace, surgical drainage.

stages.label: COMPLICATION
stages.question: If diagnosis and drainage are delayed, which serious complication is most concerning in septic arthritis of the hip?
options: Avascular necrosis of the femoral head
Reversible muscle cramping as the leading concern
Mild transient stiffness only in this presentation
Isolated skin scarring as the leading concern
Self-resolution without sequelae
stages.explanation: Delayed treatment of hip septic arthritis can cause avascular necrosis of the femoral head, cartilage destruction, growth disturbance, chronic dislocation, and permanent disability, because increased intracapsular pressure and inflammatory enzymes compromise blood supply and destroy articular cartilage rapidly. Mild transient stiffness alone underestimates the destructive nature of the disease. Septic arthritis does not reliably self-resolve and can be limb- and life-threatening. Isolated skin scarring is not the primary risk of an intra-articular infection. Reversible muscle cramping does not capture the irreversible joint damage that delay causes. This is why urgent aspiration, drainage, and antibiotics are imperative.

cases.diagnosis: Septic arthritis of the hip (pediatric)
pearls: Septic hip in a toddler: fever, toxic appearance, pseudoparalysis, and the hip held in flexion-abduction-external rotation.
Urgent joint aspiration (often ultrasound-guided) with synovial fluid analysis confirms the diagnosis.
Treatment is emergent surgical drainage plus IV antibiotics; S. aureus is the most common organism.
Delay risks avascular necrosis of the femoral head and permanent joint destruction.
